register.name: shadcn/ui + Radix UI primitives
register.what: Copy-paste component distribution built on Radix headless primitives + Tailwind. Already partially adopted in @curaos/ui (10+ Radix packages installed). Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes`: provides battle-tested accessible primitives (Dialog, Select, Tabs, Tooltip, DropdownMenu, Command/cmdk, Combobox, DatePicker, etc.) that become the foundation for all 16 missing archetypes. Components are copied into the repo - no external runtime dependency.

register.stack_fit: Native fit: Tailwind already in stack, Radix already installed, React 19, TypeScript 5. ui-app-emit already emits shadcn-style components. Zero wrapper needed.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (code copied into repo = zero external runtime). No 3rd-party SaaS. Fully air-gap safe.
register.generator_first_target: ui-kit package (@curaos/ui) - already the integration point; shadcn CLI pulls components into src/components/
register.person_centric_fit: Command palette (cmdk), accessible dialogs, drawers, and date pickers are person-facing UX primitives used in patient-app, personal-* apps, and clinician surfaces equally. RTL-capable via Radix internals.
register.verify_notes: Three gap packages to add for missing archetypes: cmdk (Command/command-palette), vaul (Drawer), react-day-picker + date-fns (DatePicker). All MIT. Effort S as claimed. Recommend adding Base UI as the primitive layer for any NET-NEW compone
register.simpler_alternative: Already adopted - ponytail ladder rung 2 applies. The ui-kit package at curaos/frontend/packages/ui-kit already has 11 @radix-ui/* packages installed and wraps them in shadcn-style CVA+Radix components (button.tsx comment explicitly says "shadcn-style cva + Radix Slot"). cmdk, vaul (drawer), react-day-picker are NOT yet in package.json - those are the gap packages covering the missing archetypes (Command palette, Drawer, DatePicker). Adding them is extending the already-adopted pattern, not a new dependency class.

register.name: TanStack Table v9 + TanStack Virtual
register.what: Headless table/grid engine (sorting, filtering, grouping, pagination, column pinning, row selection, server-side) + row virtualization. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` DataTable and `platform-reporting-builder` grids. v9 (beta as of 2026) uses prototype-chain row/column APIs - 90% less memory vs v8, handles 10-16M rows before hitting 4 GB. TanStack Virtual pairs for virtualized rendering of those rows.
register.taxonomy: Headless table/grid engine (sorting, filtering, grouping, pagination, column pinning, row selection, server-side) + row virtualization. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` DataTable and `platform-reporting-builder` grids. v9 (beta as of 2026) uses prototype-chain row/column APIs - 90% less memory vs v8, handles 10-16M rows before hitting 4 GB. TanStack Virtual pairs for virtualized rendering of those rows.
register.stack_fit: React 19 compatible. No wrapper needed. Already aligns with existing data-table.tsx in ui-kit. TanStack Query v5 (server state) pairs naturally with Table for server-side fetch patterns used across NestJS APIs.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (npm package, no SaaS). Air-gap safe.
register.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit (codegen emitter) - archetype hint 'data-grid' maps to TanStack Table wrapper in @curaos/ui
register.person_centric_fit: Person-facing apps (patient-app, personal-finance) need paginated/virtualized lists of clinical results, invoices, and transactions. Same table primitives serve both management grid and person timeline list.
register.verify_notes: Verdict is confirmed-trial not confirmed-adopt for three reasons: (1) v8 has a confirmed React 19 Compiler incompatibility with no clean fix - 'use no memo' is acceptable short-term but is a smell for a stack that will adopt the Compiler wi
register.simpler_alternative: Yes, and it already exists. The codebase has a hand-rolled DataTable at curaos/frontend/packages/ui-kit/src/components/data-table.tsx (147 lines, zero deps). It covers: sortable columns, row selection (controlled + uncontrolled), aria-sort, numeric alignment, generic over row type. It does NOT cover: pagination, server-side fetch, column pinning, grouping, virtualization of large row counts. For the 24 web apps in current scope the existing component likely covers 80% of tables. The gap cases are: (a) platform-reporting-builder grids needing 1M+ row virtualization, (b) management grids needing column pinning + grouping. Those two use cases justify TanStack Table - but NOT across all 24 apps immediately. Ponytail ladder: rung 2 applies for most tables (existing DataTable). TanStack Table is rung 4/7 only for the virtualization + advanced grid subset.

register.name: TanStack Query v5 + TanStack Form v1
register.what: Query: async server-state cache (fetch, cache, sync, background refetch, optimistic updates). Form: headless cross-framework form state with Standard Schema (Zod 4 native, no extra resolver package). Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` form binding. @curaos/ui already ships react-hook-form v7 + @hookform/resolvers; TanStack Form v1 is an alternative for new complex forms with field-level async validation and nested arrays.
register.taxonomy: Query: async server-state cache (fetch, cache, sync, background refetch, optimistic updates). Form: headless cross-framework form state with Standard Schema (Zod 4 native, no extra resolver package). Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` form binding. @curaos/ui already ships react-hook-form v7 + @hookform/resolvers; TanStack Form v1 is an alternative for new complex forms with field-level async validation and nested arrays.
register.stack_fit: React 19, TypeScript 5. Query integrates directly with NestJS REST/SSE endpoints. Form v1 supports Zod 4 natively via Standard Schema - exact match with curaos/ui's Zod 4.4.3 dep. react-hook-form already installed; Form v1 is additive for new wizard/multi-step forms.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted npm. Air-gap safe.
register.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit - codegen emits useQuery hooks from @curaos/*-sdk + TanStack Query as the data-fetching layer
register.person_centric_fit: Query powers person-facing real-time data (patient vitals, invoice status, appointment schedules). Form v1 handles multi-step patient intake wizards, consent forms, and preference screens with async field validation against NestJS endpoints.
register.verify_notes: SECURITY FLAG (resolved): May 11 2026 supply chain attack (CVE-2026-45321, GHSA-g7cv-rxg3-hmpx) hit 42 @tanstack/* packages via GitHub Actions cache poisoning. @tanstack/query* and @tanstack/form* are confirmed clean per multiple independen
register.simpler_alternative: For Query: no simpler alternative - already the standard, no change needed. For Form v1 specifically: RHF v7 (already installed) covers multi-step wizards via useFormContext + FormProvider. 2026 independent consensus from Formisch, splitforms, LogRocket, MakersDen: default to RHF; reach for TanStack Form only when nested dynamic field arrays + per-field async validation state are the core requirement. Bundle sizes nearly identical (RHF ~10.7 KB gzipped, TanStack Form ~9.6 KB gzipped). Ponytail ladder: Form v1 does not clear rung 5 for general use - RHF covers it. It clears rung 7 only for the specific multi-step wizard archetype where RHF's form-wide validation timing is a real constraint.

register.name: Recharts
register.what: Declarative SVG chart library built on D3 and React components. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` ReportViewer + `platform-reporting-builder` chart widgets. Chart types: Line, Bar, Area, Pie, Radar, Scatter, Treemap, RadialBar, ComposedChart. Composable component API; ResponsiveContainer for layout. Needed for: vitals trends (patient-app), billing AR aging, quality dashboards, KPI panels, financial GL reports.
register.taxonomy: Declarative SVG chart library built on D3 and React components. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` ReportViewer + `platform-reporting-builder` chart widgets. Chart types: Line, Bar, Area, Pie, Radar, Scatter, Treemap, RadialBar, ComposedChart. Composable component API; ResponsiveContainer for layout. Needed for: vitals trends (patient-app), billing AR aging, quality dashboards, KPI panels, financial GL reports.
register.stack_fit: Pure React + SVG, zero canvas complexity. ~290KB bundle (SVG path rendering included). Tree-shakeable per chart type. No server component conflict. Direct drop-in to @curaos/ui ReportViewer wrapper.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted npm. Air-gap safe. No telemetry.
register.generator_first_target: ui-kit package (@curaos/ui) - ReportViewer + KpiCard components wrap Recharts; ui-app-emit emits chart archetype for contract fields typed as time-series
register.person_centric_fit: Person-facing: vitals trend chart (patient-app), spending over time (personal-finance-app), donation history (fundraising-app). Management: quality measure population dashboards, AR aging bars, revenue cycle funnel.
register.verify_notes: DOWNGRADE from adopt to trial. Three reasons: (1) Candidate description is stale - v2 is deprecated, not stable; adoption must be v3-pinned from day one. (2) WCAG 2.1 AA gap: HealthStack patient-app and clinician-app face legal healthcare a
register.simpler_alternative: No chart utility exists in stdlib, Radix, Tailwind, or any already-installed dep (@curaos/ui has none). Scratch SVG is non-trivial for the required types (RadialBar, ComposedChart, Radar). visx (@visx/* packages, ~15KB modular) is the lower-level alternative but requires D3 knowledge and 2-3x implementation time per chart - not appropriate for generator-first emission. Nivo has better out-of-box WCAG 2.1 AA (all SVG charts include ARIA + keyboard nav) but heavier bundle and less ergonomic wrapper pattern. Recharts v3 accessibilityLayer is now default-on, closes most of the gap, though ARIA role choices (img vs graphics-symbol) remain imperfect. For CuraOS HealthStack (patient-app vitals, clinical dashboards) WCAG AA is a legal compliance requirement (ADA Title II healthcare deadline active as of 2026). This is the one real risk: Recharts accessibility is opt-in-improved, not guaranteed AA, requiring per-component augmentation.

register.name: RJSF (react-jsonschema-form) v6

Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` schema-driven form generation and `platform-reporting-builder` dynamic field configuration. Direct path to emitting forms from TypeSpec-derived JSON Schema contracts: TypeSpec -> OpenAPI schema -> JSON Schema subset -> RJSF renders the form automatically. Needed for: dynamic workflow form steps, admin config screens, report builder field selectors.

register.name: AG Grid Community

Community = MIT/Apache-2; Enterprise = $999/dev/yr (Excel export, range select, advanced pivot, row grouping tree).

Fills taxonomy_id: workflow-automation.catalog.connector-registry - the confirmed gap where local connector-catalogue.fixture.ts is a static list with no live registry. The MIT-licensed piece SDK (outside packages/ee/) is port-adapt safe; the EE embed builder is iframe-only and excluded.

register.stack_fit: Pure TS, no wrapper needed for Bun/NestJS. createPiece() shape ports directly into @curaos/connector-sdk. Auth strategies map to integrations-core-service OAuth vault. Per-action Zod prop schemas align with CuraOS Zod validation rule.

register.person_centric_fit: Connector presented as 'apps I connect to my life' (my bank, my pharmacy) with consent-gated OAuth; management surface = org OAuth vault + piece marketplace.
register.verify_notes: Downgrade from adopt to confirmed-trial on three grounds: (A) Zod claim is overstated - TypeBox-to-Zod bridge is unproven effort; validate one piece end-to-end through the existing ConnectorRegistry before broad adoption. (B) The candidate
register.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is already built. integrations-core-service/src/connectors/connector-framework.ts exists in main, defines ConnectorRegistry + Connector descriptor, and its own docstring says "The host app registers real Activepieces pieces against this registry at boot." The static connector-catalogue.fixture.ts (4 entries) is the acknowledged gap placeholder. The abstraction layer does NOT need to be rebuilt; it needs to be populated. Ponytail ladder stops at rung 2 (already in this codebase). The trial verdict is: validate that @activepieces/pieces-framework pieces can be mapped into the existing ConnectorRegistry descriptor shape before committing to the full port.

register.name: Mastra (@mastra/core + @mastra/nestjs + @mastra/mcp)
register.what: Apache 2.0 TypeScript AI orchestration framework with graph-based workflow engine (.then/.branch/.parallel), agents + tool calling, MCPClient/MCPServer, memory, RAG, and a first-class NestJS adapter that exposes agents/workflows/MCPs as NestJS endpoints with native DI/guards/interceptors. Fills taxonomy_ids: workflow-automation.catalog.ai-llm-steps (fully absent locally - zero mcp/llm/aiagent matches in automation+workflow+plugin services) and workflow-automation.catalog.mcp-tooling-integration (also absent).

register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: @mastra/core imported into automation-core-service; contract: add AiAgentStep + AgentTool union to automation.tsp then regenerate @curaos/automation-sdk; codegen-emitter: mcp-server entity emitter in tools/codegen
register.person_centric_fit: AI step framed as consent-gated assistant; person sees intent + plain results; management surface = model config, tool bindings, guardrails, Langfuse observability.
register.verify_notes: Four compounding blockers beyond the supply chain incident: (1) @mastra/nestjs is Express-only in v1 - hard bootstrap failure on any other adapter, constraining CuraOS adapter flexibility forward. (2) Confirmed Bun+Turborepo monorepo breaka
register.simpler_alternative: Yes. CuraOS already has Temporal in workflow-core-service. AI steps as Temporal Activities + direct @ai-sdk/* (Vercel AI SDK) packages wired through LiteLLM cover the stated gap without a new framework layer. @ai-sdk/* is Zod-native, BYO-provider, TypeSpec-contract-friendly, and has no supply chain incidents. This satisfies the ponytail ladder at rung 4 (already-installed-adjacent) rather than importing a new framework with known friction and a fresh state-actor targeting history.

Primary use in CuraOS: the provider-agnostic LLM call layer inside Mastra agents or standalone automation-core AI steps. Fills the BYO-model requirement for workflow-automation.catalog.ai-llm-steps when paired with LiteLLM for self-hosted model routing.

register.stack_fit: Zero-friction NestJS integration (official cookbook example). Works in Bun. MCP tool interface aligned with @modelcontextprotocol/sdk. Mastra uses ai SDK internally - so adopting Mastra implicitly adopts this. Direct import also valid for lighter use-cases (single generateText call in a service).

register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: imported into automation-core-service AiAgentStep handler and plugin-runtime-service LLM code-step; no codegen target (it is a call-time SDK, not a contract owner)
register.person_centric_fit: Enables plain-language AI responses on person-facing surfaces; streaming to React frontend via NestJS SSE; tool calls drive automation steps.
register.verify_notes: Trial (not adopt) because of two real gates that must be resolved first: (1) ESM migration of automation-core-service (or scoping ai SDK to a new ESM-only package within the monorepo) must be planned as explicit work, not assumed free. (2)
register.simpler_alternative: Ponytail check: the codebase has no existing LLM call layer (automation-core-service has zero ai/mastra deps). The actual use case is generateText/streamText calls inside AiAgentStep handlers. If Mastra is already planned as the agent runtime, Mastra wraps ai SDK internally - direct ai SDK import in automation-core-service may be redundant for the agentic path. For non-agentic LLM steps (single generateText), fetch() against LiteLLM's OpenAI-compatible endpoint with a Zod-validated response is a zero-dep option that avoids the ESM migration cost entirely. That is the ponytail ladder rung to test first before committing to ai SDK in CJS services. For the full streaming + tool-call + structured-output surface (the full value prop), fetch() is not a substitute - ai SDK earns its keep there.

register.person_centric_fit: Enables AI agents (internal and external) to invoke CuraOS workflows on behalf of a person; exposes person-consented tools to agent ecosystem.
register.verify_notes: THREE active security concerns that must be mitigated before production: (1) CVE-2026-25536 (CVSS High, cross-client data leak via StreamableHTTPServerTransport instance reuse; fixed in >=1.26.0 - pin to >=1.26.0, currently satisfied by 1.2
register.simpler_alternative: Not applicable for the core use case (protocol interop with the MCP ecosystem is the point; you cannot substitute it). However, ponytail challenge: if the actual need is internal workflow automation addressability, a simpler REST/OpenAPI endpoint from NestJS already handles it with zero new deps and works today. MCP adoption is justified ONLY if external AI agents (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.) need to invoke CuraOS workflows - i.e., the protocol interop value is real. If the near-term use is internal-only, defer. If Mastra is adopted as the automation orchestrator, @mastra/mcp already wraps this SDK - direct adoption of the raw SDK is then redundant (YAGNI). Resolve the Mastra decision first.

register.name: node-x12
register.what: ASC X12 parser, generator, query engine, and mapper with stream support. Covers the entire @curaos/x12-sdk gap (taxonomy: x12-revenue-cycle): ISA/GS envelope, 837P/837I generation, 835 ERA parsing, 270/271 eligibility round-trip. The query language (dot-path selectors) can extract segment values without writing loop code. JS-EDI-Notation provides a JSON-friendly interchange format that maps cleanly to TypeSpec contracts.
register.taxonomy: ASC X12 parser, generator, query engine, and mapper with stream support. Covers the entire @curaos/x12-sdk gap (taxonomy: x12-revenue-cycle): ISA/GS envelope, 837P/837I generation, 835 ERA parsing, 270/271 eligibility round-trip. The query language (dot-path selectors) can extract segment values without writing loop code. JS-EDI-Notation provides a JSON-friendly interchange format that maps cleanly to TypeSpec contracts.
register.stack_fit: Pure TypeScript, ESM-compatible; works under Bun. No NestJS adapter needed - import into @curaos/x12-sdk package directly. Drizzle schema sits upstream of it. Zod can validate the parsed JS objects. Generator-first: wrap in sdk-emit output so service code never calls node-x12 directly.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (library). No payer connectivity. Must pair with a clearinghouse adapter (Stedi/Claim.MD/Availity) for the 3rd-party leg.
register.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/x12-sdk) - node-x12 is a vendored dep inside the SDK; sdk-emit exposes typed TS facades, never raw X12 API.
register.person_centric_fit: Underlying transport only. Person surfaces (claim-status timeline, up-front copay estimate, EOB plain-language statement) are built above it in claims-service. The library enables real 270 eligibility checks that power patient cost estimates before service.
register.verify_notes: Verdict rationale: downgrade from "adopt" to "confirmed-trial". The candidate is correct that node-x12 fills the gap and the generator-first wrapping isolates risk. But three adversarial findings push this to trial-gated: (1) The successor
register.simpler_alternative: No stdlib or already-installed dep covers ASC X12 wire format parsing - this is a domain-specific binary-adjacent format with loop/segment/element grammar that requires a real parser. The ponytail ladder dead-ends at rung 5: no already-installed dep covers it. Scratch implementation of a compliant 5010 X12 parser + generator is non-trivial (weeks, not days) and would itself need the same maintenance. The real alternative comparison is: (A) node-x12 v1.7.1 locked + tightly wrapped, (B) EDIFlow @ediflow/x12 + @ediflow/hipaa-x12-005010 (April 2026, v0.3.0, actively maintained but ~3 months old, higher API churn risk), (C) tastypackets/x12-parser (parser only, no generator, no 837 emit), (D) buy clearinghouse SDK (Stedi/Claim.MD) that abstracts X12 entirely. Option D is worth flagging: if the clearinghouse adapter layer (already required for payer connectivity per the candidate's own note) can handle envelope assembly, node-x12 scope shrinks to internal canonical representation only. EDIFlow is the strongest emerging alternative but v0.3.0 at 3 months old is not adoption-ready for a healthcare billing lane.

register.name: x12-parser (tastypackets)
register.what: Streaming X12 parser built on Node Transform streams - no generation, query, or mapping. Tested against 1M+ 835 records in production. Pure stream-in / JSON-out: ideal for high-volume 835 ERA ingestion pipelines (taxonomy: x12-revenue-cycle, 835 parse leg only). Zero non-dev dependencies.
register.taxonomy: Streaming X12 parser built on Node Transform streams - no generation, query, or mapping. Tested against 1M+ 835 records in production. Pure stream-in / JSON-out: ideal for high-volume 835 ERA ingestion pipelines (taxonomy: x12-revenue-cycle, 835 parse leg only). Zero non-dev dependencies.
register.stack_fit: Works under Bun (Node streams compatible). No native TS types - need a thin .d.ts wrapper. Does not generate X12, so must pair with node-x12 or scratch-written generator for 837 emission. Best as the 835 stream ingestion path inside @curaos/x12-sdk.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (library). Parse leg only.
register.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/x12-sdk, 835 stream parser path).
register.person_centric_fit: Enables ERA auto-posting speed - faster 835 processing directly benefits patient EOB delivery and balance reconciliation on the person-app.
register.verify_notes: Downgrade from "adopt" to "trial" on two real grounds: (1) 4+ year stale codebase on a Node.js streaming library - Bun stream compat gaps could surface at scale and there is no upstream to fix them; (2) no TS types requires SDK-level wrappe
register.simpler_alternative: No stdlib or already-installed dep covers X12 835 parsing. X12 is a proprietary delimiter-variable binary-ish EDI format with HIPAA-mandated loop structure (CLP/SVC implied context, ISA-declared delimiters, companion-guide variance per payer). Scratch implementation is non-trivial: delimiter sniffing, loop-context state machine, segment schema, and 1M+ record throughput. Not a one-liner. The ponytail ladder stops at rung 5 (already-installed dep) - but nothing is installed yet. This is a legitimate external dep slot. Simpler alternative: node-x12 has TS types and parse+generate, but is also in maintenance mode since 2021 and has lower weekly downloads than x12-parser. No clearly superior maintained option exists in the JS/TS ecosystem as of 2026.

Drizzle would store a serialized FHIR blob or a relational projection. Generator-first: codegen emitter can output Medplum-typed DTOs instead of custom ones.

CoverageEligibilityResponse powers up-front copay estimates surfaced in the patient app. ClaimResponse provides the denial reason in structured form that enables patient-readable denial explanations.
register.verify_notes: Verdict rationale: The candidate's recommendation to "adopt @medplum/core" is wrong on the package boundary. The right adoption is @medplum/fhirtypes (downgrade from core to the types-only package). This is not a rejection of the Medplum ec
register.simpler_alternative: Yes - @medplum/fhirtypes (types-only subset of the same monorepo, no runtime, no MedplumClient, zero dependencies) covers the entire stated need: Claim, ClaimResponse, ExplanationOfBenefit, CoverageEligibilityRequest/Response as strongly-typed R4 interfaces. This is the correct ponytail rung - use the types package, not the full SDK. Secondary alternative for Zod-native validation: @solarahealth/fhir-r4 (MIT), but maintenance is slower. No stdlib or already-installed package covers FHIR R4 contract shapes - an external types package IS the right call here; the question is which one.

Wrapper needed to translate accounting-core domain events into TB account/transfer calls.

register.generator_first_target: accounting-core-service SDK (wraps TB client behind accounting.tsp Transfer operation); posting-rules.ts maps domain events to TB createTransfers calls.

register.verify_notes: Downgraded from "trial" to "downgrade-reference" (keep as a named option in the ADR, do not integrate now) for three compounding reasons: (1) ponytail ladder kills it - the need is already met by the existing implementation; (2) curaos-loca
register.simpler_alternative: YES - already installed and fully implemented. accounting-core-service already has a complete double-entry engine on Drizzle + Postgres (CNPG). Ponytail ladder rung 2: the codebase already has it. Adding TB introduces: a second stateful binary to operate and upgrade (TB version must match client exactly - tight coupling), a required Postgres mirror bridge for all reporting (every query goes to Postgres anyway), unconfirmed Bun napi compatibility, a bespoke NestJS wrapper not supported by any generator, and a two-DB consistency problem (TB + Postgres must stay in sync across crashes). The local-vs-3rdparty duality rule (curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule) requires both a self-hosted AND a BYO-3rd-party option. TB has no BYO-3rd-party/SaaS path today (cloud offering is invitation-only and immature as of mid-2026). The fallback being "Postgres Drizzle-based double-entry" is not a BYO lane - it is the same self-hosted stack. The rule is not met.

register.name: dinero.js v2
register.what: Immutable, tree-shakeable, bigint-native money arithmetic library with 168 ISO 4217 currencies and compile-time currency mismatch detection. Directly fills @curaos/currency package gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.multi-currency) and replaces hand-rolled SafeMinorNonNeg/int64 patterns throughout accounting-core, sales-core, commerce-core, and healthstack-billing.
register.taxonomy: Immutable, tree-shakeable, bigint-native money arithmetic library with 168 ISO 4217 currencies and compile-time currency mismatch detection. Directly fills @curaos/currency package gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.multi-currency) and replaces hand-rolled SafeMinorNonNeg/int64 patterns throughout accounting-core, sales-core, commerce-core, and healthstack-billing.
register.stack_fit: Zero-dep npm import, full TypeScript generics, tree-shakeable. Bun-native. Drizzle stores int64 minor units (unchanged); Dinero wraps at service boundary for arithmetic and formatting. FX gain/loss computed as Dinero difference before posting to accounting-core GL. No wrapper needed - import directly into @curaos/currency package.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (offline, no external API). FX rate feed is BYO (ECB/Frankfurter free API or Open Exchange Rates). Satisfies both lanes.
register.generator_first_target: @curaos/currency package (new); accounting.tsp FX gain/loss entry generation; all money-path services import via @curaos/currency.
register.person_centric_fit: Enables accurate multi-currency display in 'my invoices', 'my balance', 'my copay estimate' across all person-facing apps with locale-aware formatting built in.
register.verify_notes: VERDICT RATIONALE - trial not full adopt: The value proposition is real (compile-time currency safety, allocation math, FX scaffolding) and the integration is clean. But three friction points prevent immediate 'adopt': (1) bus factor 1 with
register.simpler_alternative: REAL ALTERNATIVE EXISTS and partially covers the use case. The codebase already stores amounts as bigint minor units in Drizzle (amount_minor BIGINT + text currency_code). For arithmetic: native BigInt operators cover add/subtract/compare at service boundaries with zero deps. For locale formatting: Intl.NumberFormat (stdlib, Bun-native) covers all 168 ISO 4217 currencies with locale-aware display - no library needed. What dinero adds on top: (1) currency-mismatch type safety at compile time, (2) allocation/splitting (banker's rounding), (3) FX conversion scaffolding. Those three are real gaps stdlib cannot close. So the honest ladder answer: stdlib + native BigInt covers ~70% of the claimed value; adopt dinero only if compile-time currency safety and allocation math are explicit requirements for the @curaos/currency package.

register.name: node-sales-tax (sales-tax npm)
register.what: Offline international tax rate calculator covering EU VAT (MOSS), Canada GST, 50+ countries with optional online VAT number fraud check. Partial fill for @curaos/tax-engine gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.tax). Covers simple flat-rate VAT/GST look-up; compound/repartition/withholding from Odoo account_tax model still needs port-adapt on top.
register.taxonomy: Offline international tax rate calculator covering EU VAT (MOSS), Canada GST, 50+ countries with optional online VAT number fraud check. Partial fill for @curaos/tax-engine gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.tax). Covers simple flat-rate VAT/GST look-up; compound/repartition/withholding from Odoo account_tax model still needs port-adapt on top.
register.stack_fit: Direct npm import, TypeScript types built-in, zero deps, Bun-compatible. Plugs into @curaos/tax-engine as the rate-lookup primitive; compound/repartition/withholding logic wraps on top. No Drizzle interaction needed at rate-lookup layer.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (offline JSON rates, no API key). BYO lane: swap for TaxJar/Avalara SDK when tenant needs real-time nexus calculation. Satisfies both lanes via pluggable rate-provider pattern.
register.generator_first_target: @curaos/tax-engine package; accounting.tsp tax computation step; sales-core invoice line tax resolution.
register.person_centric_fit: Enables accurate tax display on 'my invoice' before payment; correct copay tax on patient billing estimate.
register.verify_notes: Adopt as trial with three hard conditions: (1) Wrap inside @curaos/tax-engine so no service ever calls sales-tax directly - the pluggable rate-provider pattern the candidate describes is mandatory, not optional, both for BYO-TaxJar/Avalara
register.simpler_alternative: Partially. For EU-only coverage: ibericode/vat-rates or benbucksch/eu-vat-rates are pure static JSON with no runtime dep and no code surface. They are narrower (EU only, no Canada GST, no 50+ country coverage) and have no computation logic - callers must implement rate resolution themselves. For CuraOS scope (ERP + HealthStack patient billing across multiple jurisdictions), sales-tax's rate coverage and the minimal computation API are worth the marginal weight over a raw JSON file, provided cross-fetch is quarantined. The ponytail ladder stops here: no already-installed dep covers 50-country tax rate lookup + Canadian GST; stdlib does not; the package is justified but must be adopted with the cross-fetch caveat addressed.

register.name: Lago (self-hosted billing engine)
register.what: Open-source metering + usage-based billing + subscription management + invoicing platform. Fills erp-invoice-subscription-recurring gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.invoicing, erp.accounting.subscriptions). Covers: event ingestion, usage metering, plan/charge definitions, subscription lifecycle, invoice generation, dunning, payment orchestration, revenue analytics.
register.taxonomy: Open-source metering + usage-based billing + subscription management + invoicing platform. Covers: event ingestion, usage metering, plan/charge definitions, subscription lifecycle, invoice generation, dunning, payment orchestration, revenue analytics.
register.stack_fit: AGPL: never import Lago code into CuraOS build. Integration via REST SDK (@getlago/lago-node). NestJS accounting-core-service calls Lago REST API as a billing-state sidecar. Helm chart available for K8s air-gap deployment. Lago manages billing state; CuraOS maps Lago invoices back to accounting-core GL via event subscription. Zod validation on Lago response DTOs needed.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Both: self-host Lago pod in same K8s cluster (self-hosted lane) OR configure to call Lago Cloud (BYO-3rd-party lane). Satisfies dual requirement from [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]].
register.generator_first_target: accounting.tsp (subscription/invoice contract wraps Lago events); @curaos/lago-adapter package (thin NestJS module wrapping Lago Node SDK with Zod validation); codegen emitter for subscription-core-service.
register.person_centric_fit: Person sees clean invoice + subscription self-management ('my plan', 'my usage this month', 'cancel or upgrade'). Lago's invoice PDF + webhook feed CuraOS personal-app billing view.
register.verify_notes: Corrections to candidate before trial: (1) SDK package name: lago-javascript-client (not @getlago/lago-node). Update codegen generator target. (2) ClickHouse and Kafka are scaling add-ons, not baseline requirements - trial starts with Postg
register.simpler_alternative: No credible simpler alternative covers the same surface. Flexprice (Go backend, AGPL-3.0 backend / Apache 2.0 frontend, ~3.5k stars) is younger and thinner. Kill Bill is Java/Apache 2.0 but architecturally heavier and decade-old design. Building usage metering + subscription lifecycle + invoice generation + dunning from scratch in NestJS is a multi-month L effort with high correctness risk (billing math, proration, tax, idempotency). Lago at trial scope (REST sidecar + adapter module) is the minimum-viable path. Ponytail ladder stops at rung 5: already-available dependency that solves it, integrated via thin adapter. No custom billing engine needed.

register.name: EDIFlow (@ediflow/core + @ediflow/hipaa-x12-005010)
register.what: TypeScript-native X12/HIPAA EDI library: parse + generate 837P, 837I, 835, 270/271 and 11 more HIPAA transaction sets. Fills gap taxonomy_ids: healthcare.revenue.insurance-claims, healthcare.revenue.eligibility, healthcare.revenue.revenue-cycle. Covers the @curaos/x12-sdk package gap identified in gap-analysis.json (ABSENT). Clean Architecture: Domain layer is format-agnostic; X12 is an adapter, so adding new transaction sets requires no Domain changes.

register.stack_fit: Direct npm-import into Bun/NestJS. Zero external dependencies. Wrap in @curaos/x12-sdk: TypeSpec contract -> EDIFlow emitter/parser -> service. No NestJS-specific wrapper needed; call as a pure library from service methods.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host only (pure library, no SaaS)
register.generator_first_target: sdk-package (@curaos/x12-sdk wrapping EDIFlow emitters/parsers, folded into codegen mold so every claim service variant extends the SDK)
register.person_centric_fit: Back-office EDI; person surface = claim status timeline ('submitted to payer', 'adjudicated, you owe $X'). EDIFlow supplies the engine; CuraOS contracts + events supply the person-facing projection.
register.verify_notes: Verdict rationale: downgrade to reference, not adopt. Three converging reasons. (1) Internal prior art wins: gap-analysis.json already recommended port-adapt from OpenEMR as the implementation path. That decision pre-dates this candidate an
register.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is already documented in the codebase. The gap-analysis.json (/.ai-analysis/gap-analysis.json, id: x12-revenue-cycle) explicitly recommends: best_source = OpenEMR (GPL) X125010837P.php + X125010837I.php + ParseERA.php + EDI270.php; suggested_mode = 'E port-adapt (X12 logic) into a NEW @curaos/x12-sdk generator-first package'. X12 segments are ANSI standard (not copyrightable), so porting logic from OpenEMR to fresh TypeScript carries no GPL contamination. This path: (a) was already evaluated and chosen before this candidate appeared, (b) produces the same @curaos/x12-sdk generator-first package, (c) starts from battle-tested segment loop logic used in production EHRs, (d) eliminates the v0.x API-change risk entirely since the SDK owns the contract, (e) eliminates bus-factor dependency on a 5-month-old solo project. EDIFlow is a reference for how to structure the TypeScript adapter layer (clean architecture: Domain format-agnostic, X12 as adapter) - not a dependency to adopt. Additionally, for 270/271 eligibility specifically, Stedi offers a production-grade managed API option that satisfies the local-vs-3rdparty duality rule as the BYO-3rd-party-provider path, while the @curaos/x12-sdk covers the self-hosted path.

register.name: Twenty CRM (self-hosted API sidecar)
register.what: Full CRM pipeline system: leads, contacts, accounts/households, deals/opportunities, pipelines, activities, email sync, reporting. Covers crm-insurance-broker.sales.* and crm-insurance-broker.service.* taxonomy IDs. Fills the CRM gaps in crm-core-service (leads absent, thin deal model, no pipeline catalog, no cases). Two usage modes: (A) self-host Twenty alongside CuraOS and call its GraphQL/REST API from crm-core (api-adapter, BYO-3rd-party path); (B) reference its NestJS + metadata-driven architecture to accelerate crm-core-service build (reference-only).

register.stack_fit: Twenty backend = NestJS + GraphQL + PostgreSQL - identical to CuraOS stack. twenty-sdk npm package exists for extending Twenty's own model. As an API adapter: thin NestJS service wraps Twenty GraphQL calls behind a crm.tsp-defined interface. No Bun-specific issues (REST/GraphQL client). Wrapper needed to bridge TypeSpec contracts to Twenty's metadata-driven schema.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: BYO-3rd-party (run Twenty as a sidecar, call its API; or self-host for the operator's own CRM use)
register.generator_first_target: api-adapter (NestJS crm-core-service wraps Twenty REST/GraphQL as BYO-3rd-party CRM backend; crm.tsp contract is the stable internal interface)
register.person_centric_fit: Twenty is entirely org/rep-centric (Salesforce alternative framing). Person-centric reshape requires the same re-centering as the source-map analysis - mine its entity completeness, not its UX. Its metadata-driven object model is a strong reference for CuraOS crm-core contract design.
register.verify_notes: Mode B (reference-only) is confirmed-adopt: Twenty's entity completeness (leads, pipeline stages, activities, cases) is strong prior art for crm-core TypeSpec contract design. Mine it. Mode A (api-adapter sidecar) requires: (1) legal sign-o
register.simpler_alternative: Yes. Generator-first extension of crm-core-service (emit leads + pipeline catalog + cases via curaos/tools/codegen producing NestJS service + TypeSpec contract + Drizzle schema + seed) avoids license ambiguity, CVE monitoring obligation, sidecar ops overhead, and schema-translation glue entirely. Ponytail ladder: the generator is already installed; the missing entities are well-defined domain objects; Mode A sidecar fails rungs 1 and 5 (YAGNI complexity + existing capability path). Mode B (reference-only) passes rung 5: mine Twenty's entity model to accelerate TypeSpec contract design for crm-core, do not run it.

register.verify_notes: Three corrections required in source-to-local-map.json before this proceeds to implementation: (1) license field: GPL-3.0 -> AGPLv3. (2) js-client claim: "official Node.js client" -> "documentation-only code snippet, no npm package, write a
register.simpler_alternative: Checked ponytail ladder. No already-installed CuraOS module covers Lead/Opportunity/Case BYO-CRM sidecar integration today. The crm-core-service is a gap in the current service inventory (crm-insurance lane). Rolling a generic REST adapter for ANY external CRM is ~100-150 lines of NestJS module + Zod response shapes. That is likely comparable effort to an EspoCRM-specific adapter given the JS client is not packaged. However, the entityDefs reference value (best_source for TypeSpec contract design) is real and hard to replicate without a reference implementation. Alternative: skip EspoCRM as a sidecar target entirely and design crm.tsp contracts from first principles using industry-standard CRM field taxonomies (Salesforce SOQL, HubSpot v3 API, or IATA standards). That path removes the AGPLv3 dependency on the reference entirely and produces a more portable contract. That is worth noting but is a design question above this candidate's scope.

The back-office case management is the management surface. Strong fit for person-centric lens - the person owns their document submission journey.
register.verify_notes: Research value: high -- Three disqualifying findings confirmed from independent sources that the candidate's summary understated or missed. (1) Maintenance risk is acute: the OSS repo is in an officially-unsupported rebuild phase while the
register.simpler_alternative: Yes, for the actual CuraOS use case (insurance policy onboarding document collection + lead qualification review workflow). The CuraOS workflow/BPM engine (the core platform primitive per AGENTS.md §5.0) is the correct host for KYC document-step orchestration - it already owns human task + automation + SLA. Document upload steps are form steps in a BPM flow, not a separate KYC infrastructure. The back-office review surface is the crm-insurance-broker app built on the App/Site Builder. The 3rd-party provider slot (per curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule) is better filled by a purpose-built identity verification vendor API (Persona, Onfido, Sumsub, or their self-hosted equivalents) called from a thin NestJS adapter in crm-core, rather than running a second full NestJS service with a competing Prisma schema. Insurance-specific document types (proof of insurability, policy applications) would need custom Ballerine flow definitions anyway - that work lands in CuraOS BPM config instead, which is the generator-first target.

register.taxonomy: No viable open-source TypeScript commission calculation engine exists on npm with meaningful adoption. Commercial SaaS (CaptivateIQ, Performio, Core Commissions) dominate; none are self-hostable. The @curaos/commission-engine package must be built from scratch. Gaps covered: commission calculation for sales (crm-insurance-broker.sales.commissions) and insurance broker commissions (premium-based, tiered, split, clawback on cancellation). The aposin openinsuranceplatform commission module and EspoCRM/SuiteCRM commission concepts serve as data model references only.
register.stack_fit: Scratch build: TypeSpec contract (Commission rule: type enum flat/tiered/split, rate, clawback window) -> Drizzle schema -> pure TS calculation engine -> NestJS service. Inputs from sales-core (deal closed, amount), insurance-overlay (policy premium, effective date). Fold every edge case back into the generator per [[curaos-generator-evolution-rule]].
register.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host (build @curaos/commission-engine; BYO-3rd-party = commercial SaaS like CaptivateIQ via API)
register.generator_first_target: sdk-package (@curaos/commission-engine: rule model + calculation + clawback; folded into codegen so crm-core and insurance-overlay services share one canonical calc path)
register.person_centric_fit: Dual surface: person = the agent/broker sees their earned commission statement ('my earnings this month, this quarter, clawback alerts'); management = admin commission console (rule config, batch calc, payout batch, override, audit). Same commission engine, two re-centered surfaces.

register.name: Puck (@puckeditor/core)
register.what: MIT React visual editor that outputs clean JSON to any backend; drag-drop composition of your own React components; multi-zone DropZone/slot model; plugin API; theming via CSS custom properties. Directly covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` (builder-studio surface emission) and `platform-reporting-builder` (dashboard/workspace surface type + SavedLayout). Generator-first target: ui-app-emit / builder-core-service surface type.
register.taxonomy: MIT React visual editor that outputs clean JSON to any backend; drag-drop composition of your own React components; multi-zone DropZone/slot model; plugin API; theming via CSS custom properties. Directly covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` (builder-studio surface emission) and `platform-reporting-builder` (dashboard/workspace surface type + SavedLayout). Generator-first target: ui-app-emit / builder-core-service surface type.
register.stack_fit: Pure React component - drops into any React app with zero wrapper. JSON output stored in any Postgres table via NestJS endpoint. No Bun/Drizzle/Zod friction. Plugin model extends without forking. Slot fields replace old DropZone API (minor migration note in v0.22 docs).
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only - no cloud dependency; data plane is your own backend (NestJS + Drizzle). Satisfies curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule: first-party self-hosted by default, no 3rd-party option needed since there is no vendor cloud to BYO.
register.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit + builder-core-service (SavedLayout entity stores Puck JSON; surface type 'dashboard' added to builder)
register.person_centric_fit: Builder-studio is the management surface for tenant admins authoring pages/layouts. Person-centric surfaces (patient-app, personal-* apps) consume the rendered JSON output. Dual-surface: admin authors with Puck -> person sees generated layout. Satisfies no-feature-loss: full admin control retained, person gets published result.
register.verify_notes: Local-vs-3rdparty rule (curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule) requires every integratable area to offer BOTH a first-party self-hosted option AND a BYO-third-party-provider option. Puck is self-hosted-only by design; there is no third-party manage
register.simpler_alternative: BLOCKING - ponytail ladder rung 2 fires: GrapesJS (v0.22.13) is already installed AND already integrated in builder-studio. A full working SitesCanvas component (storage-adapter.ts + SitesCanvas.tsx + basic-blocks.ts) exists, wired to the live builder-core-service via BuilderClient, with a real grapesjs JSONB field in the SurfaceInput DSL already persisted to Postgres. Adding Puck would introduce a SECOND visual editor with a different JSON schema, a different drag-drop model, and a different component registration API into the same app. That is net negative: more bundle, two data model dialects in SavedLayout, doubled maintenance surface, and a breaking migration for any surface already authored in GrapesJS. GrapesJS also ships a Style Manager, Device Manager, and Asset Manager out of the box - capabilities Puck explicitly does NOT include, requiring @curaos/ui components to supply everything. For the dashboard/reporting-builder surface type gap, GrapesJS's existing plugin API and block registration covers the same composition need. Extend the existing integration; do not add Puck.

Used by Windmill, n8n, Retool for their flow editors. Custom node types map to CuraOS BPM step types (human-approval, connector, code-step, AI-agent). Generator-first target: ui-kit WorkflowCanvas component + workflow-designer surface in builder-core.

register.verify_notes: 1. SCOPE CORRECTION: Candidate names @grapesjs/react (official wrapper, ~3700 downloads/week, last release ~Oct 2024, React 19 NOT confirmed fixed in OSS wrapper). Codebase uses grapesjs-react (community wrapper, v4.0.3 in node_modules). Th
register.simpler_alternative: PONYTAIL LADDER HIT: grapesjs (core) + grapesjs-react are ALREADY INSTALLED in the monorepo (curaos/frontend/apps/builder-studio, grapesjs@^0.22.13, grapesjs-react@^4.0.2) and already in production use for the SitesCanvas surface. This is rung 2 - already in codebase. No new dependency needed. The candidate's @grapesjs/react wrapper (official, ~3700 weekly downloads, lower maturity) should be evaluated against the already-in-use grapesjs-react@4.0.x before switching. EmailBuilder.js (MIT, usewaypoint, 468 stars) is a simpler JSON-first alternative specifically for email/notification templates - lighter, no interop bugs, clean JSON output schema that fits notify-service better than HTML/CSS blob. Worth evaluating for the notify-service use case specifically before extending GrapesJS there.

JSON schema form definition aligns with TypeSpec contract-first: form schema derived from entity contract. Generator-first target: ui-app-emit Wizard archetype + workflow-core FormTrigger.
register.taxonomy: MIT JSON-powered form renderer + drag-drop form builder React component. JSON schema form definition aligns with TypeSpec contract-first: form schema derived from entity contract. Generator-first target: ui-app-emit Wizard archetype + workflow-core FormTrigger.
register.license: MIT (formio.js renderer); OSLv3 (formio server); Enterprise Form Builder module is commercial
register.stack_fit: formio/react provides React component wrapper. JSON schema maps cleanly to Zod-validated entity models (generator can emit form schema from .tsp entity). NestJS backend serves form definitions from Drizzle. No Bun friction on renderer side. Server (OSLv3) can be run as a self-hosted sidecar or replaced with NestJS endpoint.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host renderer (MIT) + own NestJS backend. Server sidecar optional. Enterprise Form Builder module = commercial BYO option if richer builder needed.
register.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit Wizard/FormTrigger archetype (codegen emits form schema from entity model) + workflow-core FormTrigger binding

Person-centric: journey-style wizard. Management: full form designer with conditional logic, validation, calculated fields.
register.verify_notes: Primary kill reasons: (1) License ambiguity on the renderer itself (OSLv3 vs MIT unresolved; network-copyleft is a hard blocker for CuraOS's self-hosted SaaS distribution model). (2) The drag-drop builder - the stated use-case for the 'work
register.simpler_alternative: YES - already installed. @curaos/ui already ships react-hook-form 7.79.0 + @hookform/resolvers 5.4.0 + zod 4.4.3 + Radix UI primitives. Multi-step wizard = a state machine over RHF steps + Radix Tabs/Dialog - no new dependency. TypeSpec entity -> Zod schema -> RHF form is already the natural generator target and is TypeScript-native, tree-shakeable, and zero OSLv3 risk. RJSF (react-jsonschema-form, MIT, 46k weekly downloads, v5 stable, v6 in beta 2025) is the correct JSON-schema-to-form drop-in if a schema-driven renderer is truly needed - it does NOT require a commercial module for the builder, and its TypeScript types are complete. SurveyJS Form Library is MIT for rendering but survey-creator (drag-drop builder) requires a commercial developer license - same commercial trap as formio's Enterprise Form Builder Module.

register.name: Martin (maplibre/martin)
register.what: Blazing-fast PostGIS-native vector tile server (auto-discovers geometry tables/functions, serves MVT from PostGIS, PMTiles, MBTiles). Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.tiles: the fleet/map UI needs real-time vector overlays from the same PostGIS instance geospatial-core-service already owns.
register.taxonomy: Blazing-fast PostGIS-native vector tile server (auto-discovers geometry tables/functions, serves MVT from PostGIS, PMTiles, MBTiles). register.stack_fit: No Node wrapper needed. Add as Docker sidecar (ghcr.io/maplibre/martin) reading the same CNPG PostGIS DB via DATABASE_URL. NestJS geospatial-core-service emits geometry tables; Martin auto-discovers them. Zero-config for most setups. Helm chart available; Zarf-compatible static binary for air-gap.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host; BYO tile CDN (MapTiler, Esri) covered by same MapLibre frontend pointing at a different tile URL
register.generator_first_target: service/geospatial-core-service: generator emits Drizzle geometry columns + GiST index + publishes Martin config snippet; ui-app-emit MapView archetype references tile URL env var
register.person_centric_fit: Enables real-time map rendering (patient location, delivery tracking, facility finder, geofence overlays) in both personal-* apps and business management dashboards without building a tile pipeline from scratch.
register.verify_notes: Hard reject on architectural grounds, not quality grounds. Martin is an excellent tool - fast (2-3x faster than nearest competitor in benchmarks), well-maintained, correct license. The rejection is because ADR-0206 (Accepted 2026-05-24) alr
register.simpler_alternative: Already decided. ADR-0206 (Accepted 2026-05-24) explicitly resolved tile serving as: PMTiles file archive + HTTP range-request proxy from MinIO/S3 + NestJS thin proxy + MapLibre GL JS client-side render. The existing Requirements.md for geospatial-core-service encodes this. The ponytail ladder stops at rung 2: the solution already exists in the codebase/ADR. Martin is a new process, new sidecar, new operational surface - none of which is needed given the resolved architecture.

register.name: MapLibre GL JS + react-map-gl
register.what: MapLibre GL JS (BSD-3, npm: maplibre-gl) is the open-source WebGL map renderer; react-map-gl v8 (MIT, npm: react-map-gl) wraps it with idiomatic React/TypeScript components. Together they cover taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.maps for the ui-kit MapView archetype and all 24 web + 2 mobile apps that need interactive maps.
register.taxonomy: MapLibre GL JS (BSD-3, npm: maplibre-gl) is the open-source WebGL map renderer; react-map-gl v8 (MIT, npm: react-map-gl) wraps it with idiomatic React/TypeScript components. register.local_vs_3rdparty: serves both: self-hosted tiles from Martin/PMTiles/SeaweedFS OR BYO 3rd-party tile provider (MapTiler, Esri, HERE) by swapping tile URL - zero code change

register.person_centric_fit: Powers patient/customer-facing location UX (find nearby facility, live delivery tracking on personal-shop, SOS location pin on personal-tracking app) AND management fleet map dashboard. Same component, two surfaces.
register.verify_notes: ADOPT web layer (MapLibre GL JS v5.24 + react-map-gl v8.1.1 from react-map-gl/maplibre) as confirmed-trial, not full adopt, because: (1) react-map-gl v8 is relatively young (v8.0 released within the last ~6 months) and the MapLibre v5 API s
register.simpler_alternative: No. Ponytail ladder check: no map library exists in the codebase (grep confirms zero references to maplibre, leaflet, openlayers, mapbox, deck.gl). Leaflet is the only lighter alternative - it IS simpler but disqualified for fleet/health use cases requiring 10k+ real-time markers; Leaflet DOM rendering degrades sharply above ~1k markers per published benchmarks. Native platform (<iframe> embed, static image tiles) does not cover interactive vector maps with geofence overlays, live tracking pins, and zoom/filter interaction. MapLibre GL is the minimum viable choice for the stated use cases; no installed dep covers it.

register.what: WebGL data visualization layers (ScatterplotLayer, TripsLayer, HeatmapLayer, PathLayer) composited over MapLibre GL. Covers the dense-dataset visualization gap in curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.fleet-tracking: fleet live positions (hundreds of vehicles), delivery route paths, geofence heatmaps in the management dashboard.
register.taxonomy: WebGL data visualization layers (ScatterplotLayer, TripsLayer, HeatmapLayer, PathLayer) composited over MapLibre GL. register.local_vs_3rdparty: frontend rendering only - agnostic to tile source; works with self-hosted or BYO
register.generator_first_target: ui-kit MapView archetype variant 'fleet-density' or 'trip-layer' -> emits DeckGL layers atop MapLibre base
register.person_centric_fit: Primarily management surface (fleet dispatcher heatmap, route density analytics). Person surface limited to own trip history replay - marginal but non-zero.
register.verify_notes: Staged adoption path: (1) Add MapLibre to ui-kit as the MapView base - this is the real first decision. (2) Implement fleet live positions and geofence heatmaps using MapLibre native layers. (3) If live dispatcher repaint rate jank is measu
register.simpler_alternative: MapLibre GL JS native heatmap layer and native clustering cover the low-to-mid density scenario (hundreds of vehicles as markers, geofence heatmaps via style layers) without deck.gl. MapLibre is uninstalled too, so both options have the same zero-dep baseline today. The ponytail ladder says: install MapLibre first and use its native layers. Escalate to deck.gl only when live-update repaint rate causes measurable jank (MapLibre is documented as slow on frequent repaints) or when TripsLayer animated replay is a hard requirement. deck.gl earns its keep at the animated/high-density tier only.

register.name: Valhalla routing engine
register.what: Open-source OSM routing engine with multi-modal routing, isochrones, time-aware turn restrictions, map matching, matrix queries, and optimized routes. Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.location (routing leg of location service) and fleet-core dispatch (ETA, route optimization). `@iwpnd/valhalla-ts` (MIT, npm) is a typed HTTP client.
register.taxonomy: Open-source OSM routing engine with multi-modal routing, isochrones, time-aware turn restrictions, map matching, matrix queries, and optimized routes. Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.location (routing leg of location service) and fleet-core dispatch (ETA, route optimization). `@iwpnd/valhalla-ts` (MIT, npm) is a typed HTTP client.
register.stack_fit: Deploy as Docker sidecar. NestJS geospatial-core-service calls Valhalla HTTP API via `@iwpnd/valhalla-ts` (v1.0.29, MIT, 71 weekly downloads - thin but typed). Wrap into @curaos/routing-sdk to abstract self-host vs BYO provider. fleet-core-service emits dispatch.route.calculated events from routing results.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: self-host via Docker; BYO via HERE Routing, Google Routes, Mapbox Directions API - same @curaos/routing-sdk interface
register.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/routing-sdk) + service geospatial-core-service adds /routing endpoint wired to Valhalla; fleet-core-service consumes routing-sdk for ETA
register.person_centric_fit: Powers patient/customer journey: 'get directions to clinic', 'track my delivery ETA', 'how long until driver arrives'. Management surface: fleet dispatcher sees optimal routes, driver app gets turn-by-turn. Isochrones enable 'show facilities within 30 min' person surface.
register.verify_notes:
**Research value: high** - Multiple independent sources confirm prior art and real deployment patterns.

ADVERSARIAL FINDINGS (net verdict: confirm-trial, not full adopt yet):

1. LICENSE - PASS. MIT confirmed, compatible with self-host + a
register.simpler_alternative: Two alternatives worth flagging: (1) @routingjs/valhalla (from nilsnolde/routingjs, gis-ops maintained) is a better-maintained multi-engine TypeScript client with a unified interface across Valhalla, OSRM, GraphHopper, and ORS - it directly enables the BYO-provider duality the curaos-local-vs-3rdparty rule requires, making it a stronger pick than @iwpnd/valhalla-ts. (2) OSRM is faster for pure A-to-B ETA queries with no dynamic costing, but lacks isochrones and multi-modal - insufficient for 'show facilities within 30 min' requirement. No already-installed CuraOS dependency or stdlib covers geospatial routing. Valhalla is the correct OSS engine; the client library choice should be upgraded.

register.name: Traccar
register.what: Open-source GPS tracking server supporting 200+ protocols and 2000+ GPS hardware device models. REST API + WebSocket for live position events. PostgreSQL backend. Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.fleet-tracking: the hardware-ingestion layer that fleet-core-service cannot replicate without enormous protocol effort.
register.taxonomy: Open-source GPS tracking server supporting 200+ protocols and 2000+ GPS hardware device models. REST API + WebSocket for live position events. PostgreSQL backend. Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.fleet-tracking: the hardware-ingestion layer that fleet-core-service cannot replicate without enormous protocol effort.
register.stack_fit: Deploy as Docker sidecar with its own PG DB (separate from CuraOS CNPG tenants). NestJS fleet-core-service consumes Traccar REST API (GET /api/positions, GET /api/events, WebSocket /api/socket) via a thin @curaos/traccar-adapter. Position events forwarded as fleet.position.updated Kafka events. fleet-core geofence logic in PostGIS owns geofence evaluation (Traccar geofences are duplicates - skip). No Bun/NestJS changes needed beyond the adapter.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: self-host (Docker, PostgreSQL backend); BYO-3rd-party: Samsara, Here Fleet, Teltonika Cloud, Google Maps Fleet - same adapter contract
register.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/traccar-adapter) adapter between Traccar REST+WebSocket and fleet-core-service position ingestion contract
register.person_centric_fit: Driver app (personal-tracking-service) sends positions to Traccar via existing protocol OR directly to fleet-core API. Traccar handles hardware GPS devices (ambulance, delivery van, field worker device) that personal-tracking cannot handle. Person (patient/customer) sees derived delivery ETA and SOS response location, not raw Traccar data.

register.name: Photon (komoot)
register.what: OpenStreetMap geocoder built on OpenSearch (search-as-you-type, typo-tolerant, forward + reverse geocode). Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.location: the geocoding leg of geospatial-core-service (convert address <-> coordinates). GraphHopper publishes weekly updated Photon database dumps, drastically reducing import burden.
register.taxonomy: OpenStreetMap geocoder built on OpenSearch (search-as-you-type, typo-tolerant, forward + reverse geocode). GraphHopper publishes weekly updated Photon database dumps, drastically reducing import burden.
register.stack_fit: CuraOS already runs Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for search-service. Photon uses the same OpenSearch index. NestJS geospatial-core-service calls Photon HTTP REST via thin @curaos/geocoder-sdk wrapper. Zarf-bundleable. No npm package needed - plain HTTP.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: self-host via Docker + OpenSearch; BYO: Google Geocoding API, HERE Geocoder, Mapbox Geocoding - same @curaos/geocoder-sdk interface
register.generator_first_target: service geospatial-core-service: generator emits /geocode and /reverse endpoints wired to @curaos/geocoder-sdk; geocoder-sdk has self-hosted Photon + BYO provider implementations
register.person_centric_fit: Powers 'find my nearest clinic', facility search, delivery address autocomplete, SOS address resolution in person apps. Management surface: driver address lookup, geofence definition by address.

register.name: PMTiles (protomaps)
register.what: Single-file archive format for vector/raster tiles served via HTTP range requests from any S3-compatible object store (including SeaweedFS). Zero tile server needed for bounded/static datasets. Covers the same curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.tiles taxonomy_id as Martin but as a simpler deployment path for pre-built tile archives (country basemaps, regional overlays).
register.taxonomy: Single-file archive format for vector/raster tiles served via HTTP range requests from any S3-compatible object store (including SeaweedFS). Zero tile server needed for bounded/static datasets. Covers the same curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.tiles taxonomy_id as Martin but as a simpler deployment path for pre-built tile archives (country basemaps, regional overlays).
register.stack_fit: npm package `pmtiles` for MapLibre integration (no import on NestJS needed). SeaweedFS already deployed - upload .pmtiles basemap file, set CORS headers, done. Martin handles dynamic PostGIS tiles; PMTiles handles static basemap archives. Complementary, not competing.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host on SeaweedFS; BYO-3rd-party: Protomaps CDN, MapTiler CDN - same PMTiles URL in MapLibre style
register.generator_first_target: ui-kit MapView archetype: tile source switches between Martin (dynamic) and PMTiles (static) via env config; no code change
register.person_centric_fit: Enables offline-capable basemap for mobile apps (personal-tracking, personal-shop delivery view). Air-gap deployments get a bundled basemap Zarf artifact with zero runtime tile server.

register.name: Drizzle ORM PostGIS geometry type (built-in + wkx)
register.what: Drizzle ORM v0.31+ ships native `geometry` column type for PostGIS points (SRID 4326, mode: xy). Polygon/multipolygon/linestring requires a `customType` wrapper with `wkx` npm package for WKB-to-GeoJSON conversion. Together this covers the data-layer contract gap in geospatial-core-service: no external library needed beyond what is already in the stack.

register.stack_fit: Direct drop-in for geospatial-core-service Drizzle schema. geometry('location', { type: 'point', srid: 4326 }) for vehicle/facility positions; customType + wkx for geofence polygons. GiST index via sql template in drizzle migration. Generator folds geometry column + GiST index into codegen mold for any entity with 'location' field hint.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party only (DB layer)
register.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter: add geometry column + GiST index emission when contract entity has @geo decorator; wkx added to SDK package template
register.person_centric_fit: Enables proper spatial storage and queries (ST_DWithin for 'nearby', ST_Contains for geofence entry) backing both person (find nearby clinic) and management (geofence alert) surfaces.

register.name: Fleetbase (AGPL-3.0)
register.what: Full open-source logistics OS (dispatch, order management, driver app, warehouse, proof-of-delivery, routing). Much broader than Traccar. Laravel/PHP backend. Would cover fleet-core dispatch workflow surface comprehensively.
register.taxonomy: Full open-source logistics OS (dispatch, order management, driver app, warehouse, proof-of-delivery, routing). Much broader than Traccar. Laravel/PHP backend. Would cover fleet-core dispatch workflow surface comprehensively.
register.stack_fit: AGPL means any modification must be open-sourced under AGPL or a commercial license purchased. PHP/Laravel stack does not compose into the NestJS/Bun monorepo. Would need full REST API adapter layer. Weight far exceeds the dispatch workflow value CuraOS needs.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: both (self-host or managed Fleetbase cloud)
register.generator_first_target: reference-only
register.person_centric_fit: Driver app (Navigator) is strong; but CuraOS personal-tracking-service already handles driver-side positioning. Overlap creates duplication.

register.name: @medplum/fhirtypes + @medplum/core + @medplum/fhirpath
register.what: Decomposable TypeScript npm packages from the Medplum monorepo: @medplum/fhirtypes = generated R4 type definitions (compile-time FHIR schema enforcement); @medplum/core = MedplumClient (FHIR CRUD/search/subscription), resource validators, FHIRPath evaluator client; @medplum/fhirpath = standalone FHIRPath engine. Together they fill gap [interop-fhir-server] (FHIR R4 client + type-safe contract base for @curaos/fhir-client package) and [clinical-fhir-resource-set] (R4 type safety for Observation/Condition/MedicationRequest/AllergyIntolerance etc. contracts). These packages can be npm-imported WITHOUT running the Medplum server.
register.taxonomy: Decomposable TypeScript npm packages from the Medplum monorepo: @medplum/fhirtypes = generated R4 type definitions (compile-time FHIR schema enforcement); @medplum/core = MedplumClient (FHIR CRUD/search/subscription), resource validators, FHIRPath evaluator client; @medplum/fhirpath = standalone FHIRPath engine. Together they fill gap [interop-fhir-server] (FHIR R4 client + type-safe contract base for @curaos/fhir-client package) and [clinical-fhir-resource-set] (R4 type safety for Observation/Condition/MedicationRequest/AllergyIntolerance etc. contracts). These packages can be npm-imported WITHOUT running the Medplum server.
register.stack_fit: Native TS, Bun-compatible, no wrapper needed. @medplum/core client talks to any FHIR R4 server (Medplum, HAPI, or CuraOS own server). @medplum/fhirtypes provides the type layer for TypeSpec-generated FHIR contracts. Zod schemas can wrap fhirtypes for runtime validation. NestJS service can inject MedplumClient as a provider.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Both: first-party self-host (point client at CuraOS interop-service FHIR endpoint) AND BYO third-party (point at external HAPI or cloud FHIR server). Same client, different base URL.
register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/fhir-client): @medplum/fhirtypes as the type layer + @medplum/core client as the HTTP adapter. TypeSpec emitter for healthstack services imports fhirtypes as the canonical R4 shape.
register.person_centric_fit: R4 types enforce correct FHIR resource shape for patient-facing APIs (Patient compartment, Observation, CarePlan); FHIRPath enables declarative 'my record' queries across resources without extra service code.
register.verify_notes:
Verdict is confirmed-trial (not confirmed-adopt) because:
1. ESM-in-NestJS-11 integration path is unproven in the CuraOS repo; needs a one-off spike to validate tsconfig + Bun + NestJS 11 ESM interop before generator templates are written a
register.simpler_alternative: @types/fhir (DefinitelyTyped) for type-only use: active, zero-dep, no version lock to client. REJECTED: known open defects (circular FhirResource reference, missing constraint profiles), not co-evolved with a FHIR client, would require a separate FHIR HTTP client anyway. Savings do not outweigh the completeness gap in a HIPAA-adjacent codebase. @medplum/fhirtypes wins on correctness. For the HTTP client layer: hand-rolling a thin fetch wrapper against a FHIR R4 OpenAPI spec is feasible but produces a maintenance surface that @medplum/core already solved with weekly patches and validator parity. Ponytail verdict: adopt the library, not the scratch implementation.

register.name: @medplum/hl7 (+ panates/hl7v2 as fallback)
register.what: @medplum/hl7 v4.3.13 (Apache-2.0) provides HL7v2 parsing, serialization, and utilities as part of the Medplum monorepo. Fills gap [interop-fhir-server] HL7v2 ADT ingestion sub-gap and [healthcare.interop.hl7] taxonomy_id. Ingests ADT (A01/A02/A03/A08), ORU (lab results), ORM (orders) messages and converts to normalized domain events. panates/hl7v2 (MIT, v1.8.0, April 2026, 762 stars) is the standalone alternative with full parser + serializer + TCP server + client classes if Medplum dependency is unwanted.
register.taxonomy: @medplum/hl7 v4.3.13 (Apache-2.0) provides HL7v2 parsing, serialization, and utilities as part of the Medplum monorepo. Fills gap [interop-fhir-server] HL7v2 ADT ingestion sub-gap and [healthcare.interop.hl7] taxonomy_id. Ingests ADT (A01/A02/A03/A08), ORU (lab results), ORM (orders) messages and converts to normalized domain events. panates/hl7v2 (MIT, v1.8.0, April 2026, 762 stars) is the standalone alternative with full parser + serializer + TCP server + client classes if Medplum dependency is unwanted.
register.stack_fit: Both are pure TS, Bun-compatible. NestJS service (healthstack-interop-service) imports parser, wraps in a message-handler that emits normalized FHIR resources as Kafka/Redpanda events via AsyncAPI contract. No wrapper layer needed beyond the message-routing NestJS module.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted only: ingests HL7v2 messages from on-prem hospital systems (Mirth Connect, direct TCP). No 3rd-party cloud variant needed for parsing itself; routing to external clearinghouses handled separately.
register.generator_first_target: Service (healthstack-interop-service): HL7v2 ingest handler wired to AsyncAPI event emitter; codegen-emitter adds HL7MessageHandler scaffold per resource type.
register.person_centric_fit: ADT A08 patient-update messages feed real-time patient record sync to patient-app; ORU results close the care loop for lab/imaging notifications to the person.
register.verify_notes: Reject on two independent grounds: (1) ADR-0115 §4.4.2 is already RESOLVED with HAPI HL7v2 JVM sidecar - adopting a TS npm package for HL7v2 parsing contradicts the locked architecture without opening a new ADR. (2) @medplum/hl7's hard dep
register.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is already the locked decision. ADR-0115 SD-4 (§4.4.2) resolved this: HAPI HL7v2 (JVM, Apache 2.0) + Spring Integration MLLP adapter is the canonical HL7v2 stack. This is reflected verbatim in healthstack-interop-service CONTEXT.md stack table and Requirements.md F-01. The architecture is: MLLP TCP 2575 -> HAPI JVM sidecar -> NATS relay -> NestJS translation worker. The NestJS layer consumes already-parsed NATS messages and never touches raw HL7 bytes. Neither @medplum/hl7 nor panates/hl7v2 is needed in this architecture. The candidate does not mention the ADR lock.

register.name: fhir-validator-js (Outburn) + fhir-validator-wrapper (FHIR/HL7)
register.what: Node.js wrappers around the canonical HL7 Java FHIR Validator CLI. fhir-validator-js (Apache-2.0, v1.4.0, Mar 2025) manages JVM sessions, supports multiple concurrent IG contexts, downloads JAR automatically. Fills gap [clinical-fhir-resource-set] test requirement: 'FHIR-R4 validation per resource (HAPI/fhir.js in CI)' and [interop-fhir-server] 'FHIR-validate every resource in CI'. Runs against US Core, SNOMED-backed IGs, and custom CuraOS profiles.
register.taxonomy: Node.js wrappers around the canonical HL7 Java FHIR Validator CLI. fhir-validator-js (Apache-2.0, v1.4.0, Mar 2025) manages JVM sessions, supports multiple concurrent IG contexts, downloads JAR automatically. Fills gap [clinical-fhir-resource-set] test requirement: 'FHIR-R4 validation per resource (HAPI/fhir.js in CI)' and [interop-fhir-server] 'FHIR-validate every resource in CI'. Runs against US Core, SNOMED-backed IGs, and custom CuraOS profiles.
register.stack_fit: Used in CI (just ci gate) and optionally as a sidecar in healthstack-interop-service for inbound FHIR resource validation. Requires JRE in the pod/CI runner - add openjdk:21-jre to Dockerfile. Bun-compatible for the JS wrapper layer. NestJS service calls it async before persisting inbound resources.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted: JVM runs locally in CI or K8s pod. No external call; IGs loaded locally. Supports air-gap with pre-downloaded JAR + IG packages.
register.generator_first_target: Codegen emitter: every healthstack service's CI test suite gets a FHIR validation step scaffolded by the service-core template.
register.person_centric_fit: Indirect: validated FHIR resources are trustworthy inputs to patient-facing read models; validation failures caught in CI not at patient query time.
register.verify_notes: SECURITY FLAG - three active CVEs in the underlying HL7 Java validator (org.hl7.fhir.validation / org.hl7.fhir.core): CVE-2026-34361 (CVSS 9.3, Critical) and CVE-2026-34360 (blind SSRF) both affect the /loadIG HTTP endpoint - fixed in 6.9.4
register.simpler_alternative: Yes, with a critical scope distinction. @medplum/core validateResource / indexStructureDefinitionBundle provides JVM-free FHIR R4 schema + profile validation in pure TypeScript. It supports US Core and custom StructureDefinitions loaded locally. It is already a plausible dependency given CuraOS HealthStack's FHIR surface. For structural correctness checks and base-spec conformance it is sufficient and has zero JVM cost. The gap: @medplum/core does NOT replicate the HL7 reference validator's terminology-binding checks (SNOMED, LOINC value set expansion), FHIRPath invariant evaluation depth, or gold-standard IG conformance scoring. For interoperability certification-grade CI validation against US Core + CuraOS custom profiles, the HL7 Java validator is the only authoritative tool. The two are complementary, not interchangeable - use @medplum/core for fast unit-level checks and fhir-validator-js for conformance-gate CI runs.

register.name: @asymmetrik/node-fhir-server-core (bluehalo fork)
register.what: MIT-licensed pluggable Express FHIR REST server framework for Node.js. You register resource-typed handler modules (Patient, Observation, etc.) against a spec-enforced FHIR interface; the framework handles routing, capability statement, search parameter parsing, versioning, and auth. Fills gap [interop-fhir-server] (FHIR R4 resource provider framework) as the Node-native alternative to running a full HAPI Java server. Provides the outer FHIR REST shell; CuraOS owns the persistence adapters (Drizzle/Postgres).
register.taxonomy: MIT-licensed pluggable Express FHIR REST server framework for Node.js. You register resource-typed handler modules (Patient, Observation, etc.) against a spec-enforced FHIR interface; the framework handles routing, capability statement, search parameter parsing, versioning, and auth. Fills gap [interop-fhir-server] (FHIR R4 resource provider framework) as the Node-native alternative to running a full HAPI Java server. Provides the outer FHIR REST shell; CuraOS owns the persistence adapters (Drizzle/Postgres).
register.stack_fit: Runs on Express; needs a thin NestJS adapter or can run as a standalone Express app alongside NestJS on a different port. Bun can run Express apps. Resource handlers map 1:1 to NestJS service methods. Weaker TS story than Medplum - types via DefinitelyTyped. Capability statement and search-param parsing are the primary value.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted only: provides the server layer; CuraOS controls storage.
register.generator_first_target: Codegen emitter: healthstack-interop-service resource handler scaffold (one handler class per FHIR resource in .tsp contract). Translator-pair pattern from gap-analysis [interop-fhir-server] maps cleanly to handler->domain-service call.
register.person_centric_fit: Exposes FHIR patient compartment read correctly; supports SMART scoping for person 'my record' access pattern.
register.verify_notes: Candidate recommendation was "trial." Actual finding is reject on three independent grounds: (1) maintenance overstated -- last real release Sep 2024, fragmented into 5+ forks, Express 4 dependency with no ESM path; (2) integration claim op
register.simpler_alternative: Yes -- @medplum/fhir-router (Apache 2.0, pure TypeScript, ESM-native as of Medplum v5, no mongodb dep, framework-agnostic FhirRepository interface, FHIR R4 routing + capability statement, actively maintained through 2025-2026). Implement a NestJS service that satisfies FhirRepository; the router handles FHIR URL dispatch. This is the same architectural pattern the candidate proposes (handler -> domain-service call) but with a cleaner TS contract, no CJS shim, no mongodb baggage, and a healthier upstream. Cost: write a ~50-line NestJS adapter against FhirRepository instead of a parallel Express port.

register.name: fhirclient (SMART on FHIR JS/TS client)
register.what: Official SMART on FHIR JS/TS client library (Apache-2.0). Handles EHR-launch and standalone-launch OAuth2 + PKCE flows, token refresh, FHIR resource fetching with scoping. Fills gap [interop-fhir-server] SMART patient compartment scoping and the 'my record' patient-portal export path. Required for any external EHR integration (Epic, Cerner, Athena) and for CuraOS's patient-app acting as a SMART app against external EHRs.
register.taxonomy: Official SMART on FHIR JS/TS client library (Apache-2.0). Handles EHR-launch and standalone-launch OAuth2 + PKCE flows, token refresh, FHIR resource fetching with scoping. Required for any external EHR integration (Epic, Cerner, Athena) and for CuraOS's patient-app acting as a SMART app against external EHRs.
