register.local_vs_3rdparty: Both: first-party (CuraOS SMART server via identity-service + healthstack-interop-service) AND BYO-3rd-party (Epic/Cerner SMART launch for external EHR connection).

register.person_centric_fit: The primary mechanism for 'my record' patient access: person authenticates via SMART, client fetches their compartment resources from any FHIR R4 server. Directly enables [healthcare.registration.patient-portal-phr] gap.

register.name: Snowstorm (IHTSDO) - already in use as terminology-service backing
register.what: Apache-2.0 FHIR terminology server with deep SNOMED CT support plus LOINC, ICD-10, ICD-10-CM. CuraOS terminology-service is already backed by Snowstorm (verified: 'present-strong', taxonomy_id healthcare.interop.snomed-loinc-terminology). Gap is NOT a new server but value-set API adapter: healthstack-quality-service needs $expand + $validate-code calls for eCQM measure code binding, and healthstack-meds-service needs RxNorm $lookup. Fills [ecqm-quality-engine] value-set binding step and coded-field binding for all FHIR resource services.

register.name: OHIF Viewer v3 + Orthanc DICOM server

Orthanc (GPL-3, C++): lightweight PACS server with DICOMweb plugin, REST API, widely used as self-hosted DICOM store. OHIF iframe-embedded into CuraOS imaging UI; Orthanc runs as K8s sidecar service.

register.name: CDS Hooks spec + sandbox-cds-services (HL7 open)
register.what: CDS Hooks is the HL7-standard REST protocol for embedding clinical decision support into EHR workflows (hook -> prefetch context -> CDS service returns cards/suggestions). cds-hooks/sandbox-cds-services provides Node/Express reference implementations. Fills gap [cds-rules-engine] (taxonomy_id: healthcare.care.clinical-decision-support) integration protocol: CuraOS healthstack-quality-service exposes CDS Hooks endpoints so external EHRs and its own clinician-app can call it. Enables drug-allergy checks, reminders, and risk-score cards to surface in any SMART-enabled EHR.

register.simpler_alternative: No. The observability package is scaffold-only (just a README). Zero OTel deps exist in any curaos service package. Hand-rolling distributed tracing, metrics, and log correlation across 40+ NestJS services from scratch is far worse than OTel. No stdlib or already-installed dep covers this. Ponytail ladder stops at rung 5: OTel is the minimum viable solution for the stated problem.

register.name: nestjs-otel (pragmaticivan/nestjs-otel)
register.what: NestJS DI-aware wrapper around the OTel SDK: OpenTelemetryModule, metric histogram decorators, async context propagation. Fills the same workflow-automation.ops.observability gap - provides the NestJS-idiomatic layer on top of raw OTel so services get metrics/traces without boilerplate per-service.

register.local_vs_3rdparty: Both - OTLP exporter target is configurable at runtime via env vars.
register.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (app-module.ts.hbs template injection)
register.person_centric_fit: Backend infra; reduces per-service instrumentation boilerplate so the team focuses on domain features not observability plumbing.
register.verify_notes: Trial grade, not full adopt, for two reasons: (1) Bun preload integration has not been externally validated against CuraOS's exact bun compile + k3d deploy pipeline - needs one service spike before fleet-wide codegen emission. (2) Single-ma
register.simpler_alternative: Partially yes. Raw @opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core (CNCF-backed, actively published - 0.65.0 released ~11 days ago) covers automatic trace collection from controller handlers with zero wrapper. For traces-only across services, the ponytail ladder says prefer the official package. nestjs-otel earns its keep ONLY for the additive features the official package cannot do: (1) per-request WideEvent attribute bag (structured enrichment from any DI provider in the call chain without request-scoped injection), (2) @Span / @Histogram / @Counter method decorators for domain-level metrics. If CuraOS services need only HTTP-span traces, drop to instrumentation-nestjs-core. If they need metric decorators and structured span enrichment in service methods (the observability-quality lane implies they do), nestjs-otel adds real value with ~5 lines of app module wiring vs. 50+ lines of manual SDK + interceptor boilerplate per service.

register.verify_notes: Three real risks worth tracking: (1) Postgres write saturation above ~20M events/month - acceptable for CuraOS homelab scale; upgrade path is self-hosted Sentry (same DSN swap, zero client change). (2) No pre-built Zarf bundle - must author
register.simpler_alternative: LGTM stack (already in CuraOS observability) does NOT cover structured error grouping, issue deduplication, release tracking, or user-impact correlation the way GlitchTip does. The ponytail ladder check: (1) YAGNI? No - error_tracking_rule already names GlitchTip explicitly. (2) Already in codebase? No error sink exists. (3) Stdlib? Not applicable. (4) Native platform? OTel exceptions exist but no grouping/dedup UI. (5) Already-installed dep? LGTM handles traces/metrics/logs but has no Sentry-protocol error grouping. GlitchTip fills a genuine gap LGTM does not close. Verdict: not replaceable by what is already present.

register.verify_notes: Verdict is trial, not adopt, for three reasons: (1) EE paywall on audit logs is a hard constraint for HealthStack regulated deployments - this must be contractually resolved before production rollout, not deferred; (2) air-gap image-registr
register.simpler_alternative: Ponytail check: OTel already in CuraOS stack. If the only need is LLM span tracing routed into the existing Grafana/Tempo pipeline, zero new service is needed - emit gen_ai.* semantic conventions from LLM call sites and they flow through the existing OTel collector. That covers the observability sub-gap with no new infrastructure. What Langfuse adds beyond that: structured evals (faithfulness, hallucination scoring), prompt management UI, cost tracking per trace, and dataset/experiment management. These are not covered by stdlib OTel + Grafana. So the simpler path handles tracing only; Langfuse is justified only if eval scoring and prompt management are real near-term requirements. Arize Phoenix (Apache 2.0, fully open, no EE paywall, OTel-native) is a cleaner air-gap alternative if audit-log EE requirement is a concern, though it lacks prompt management.

register.stack_fit: Already wired: @temporalio/* v1.17.2 + nestjs-temporal-core 3.2.8 in workflow-core-service package.json. CRITICAL gap: Temporal worker requires authentic Node.js - cannot run inside Bun process. Workaround in prod: run workers as a separate Node subprocess (not Bun) via bun:spawn or a sidecar container. Client/NestJS HTTP layer stays in Bun. No wrapper needed for client path; worker sidecar is the integration seam.

register.verify_notes: Downgrade from 'adopt' to 'confirmed-trial' because the Bun worker gap is a BLOCKING runtime defect in the current integration - the service as wired cannot start under Bun. Conditions for promotion to 'confirmed-adopt': (A) worker extracte
register.simpler_alternative: NO simpler rung covers the full requirement. BullMQ handles queues but not deterministic replay, saga versioning, SLA breach timers with resume, or human-in-loop await without polling. No stdlib or already-installed dep reaches durable execution. Temporal is the correct tool class. Ponytail ladder stops here: build the Node sidecar, not a from-scratch alternative.

Bun-compatible (pure TS, no native modules). No separate server to deploy. Checkpoints steps in Postgres tables co-located with application DB. Drizzle schema coexistence needs coordination on schema namespace.

register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter can scaffold @DBOS.workflow class + @DBOS.step methods from TypeSpec operation sets
register.person_centric_fit: Saga/compensation pattern maps well to multi-step person journeys (e.g. claim submission -> eligibility check -> auth -> submit -> ERA posting as durable steps that resume after crash).
register.verify_notes: Three blocking issues, not risks: (1) local-vs-3rdparty rule violation: DBOS is baked into application code via decorators; it cannot be swapped for Temporal or another provider via runtime config. The rule requires every workflow area to e
register.simpler_alternative: YES - ponytail ladder yields two better-fit options already in stack. (1) Outbox + Kafka/Redpanda + idempotency keys: already installed, covers saga/compensation at service layer without a new library dependency. Sufficient for most multi-step journeys. Ceiling: no step-level replay on crash; requires consumer idempotency discipline. (2) Temporal self-hosted: already named in curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule as the canonical Workflow Execution local option. Provides human-task signals, per-namespace tenant isolation, battle-hardened multi-tenant primitives, and a 3rd-party provider path (Temporal Cloud) via config - exactly what the local-vs-3rdparty rule requires. DBOS fills a niche between these two but does not satisfy the provider-abstraction duality the rule demands.

register.name: Restate (@restatedev/restate-sdk + restate-nest)
register.what: Durable execution runtime: HTTP-native, virtual-object actors with built-in K/V store, exactly-once semantics, single Rust binary deployment. Integrates with XState as persistent event loop. Fills: lighter-weight alternative to Temporal for stateful per-entity workflows (per-patient state machines, per-claim saga). Taxonomy: platform.workflow.durable-execution, platform.workflow.actor-model

register.stack_fit: TypeScript SDK (@restatedev/restate-sdk) is pure TS, Bun-compatible. NestJS adapter via restate-nest (decorator-based). Works as HTTP handler - Restate server calls YOUR NestJS endpoints with durability guarantees. No Node worker_threads dependency - avoids Bun blocker. Drizzle/Postgres can be used inside steps normally. Restate server adds one pod.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: single Restate binary / Docker / Helm - fully self-hostable. BYO-3rd-party: Restate Cloud (managed). BUSL means cannot resell Restate server as a managed service, but using it for CuraOS own workflows is fine.
register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter could scaffold VirtualObject class + handler methods from TypeSpec service definitions
register.person_centric_fit: Virtual objects map naturally to per-person stateful journeys (one actor per patient/claim/order). Built-in K/V avoids polling patterns. XState + Restate combo gives BPMN-grade state modeling with Restate durability.
register.verify_notes: Research value: high - decisive prior art and a codebase blocker found. Key findings: (1) PONYTAIL LADDER FIRES AT RUNG 2: Temporal 1.17.2 is already installed in workflow-core-service. A second durable-execution runtime is YAGNI until Temp
register.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is decisive. CuraOS `workflow-core-service` already ships @temporalio/activity + @temporalio/client + @temporalio/worker + @temporalio/workflow at v1.17.2 plus nestjs-temporal-core 3.2.8. Temporal IS the installed durable-execution engine. Ponytail ladder rung 2 fires: the capability already exists in this codebase. Adding Restate adds a second durable-execution runtime with overlapping semantics, split ownership of actor/saga state, two sets of operational concerns, and a BUSL-licensed binary to every air-gap bundle. That is the opposite of ponytail. The Temporal TypeScript SDK does NOT run on Bun (issue #1334 is open, worker_threads dependency), which is the claimed advantage of Restate. However: CuraOS workflow workers can run under Node while the NestJS services run under Bun - the two runtimes are already separated at the service boundary. This is not a new problem requiring a new tool.

Taxonomy: platform.workflow.background-jobs, platform.workflow.scheduled-jobs
register.taxonomy: PostgreSQL-native job queue with SKIP LOCKED exactly-once delivery, dead-letter queues, scheduled/delayed jobs, ACID guarantees. No Redis. register.stack_fit: Pure PostgreSQL - uses existing CNPG cluster, no extra infra. Drizzle-compatible (both use pg driver). NestJS adapters available. Bun-compatible (pure JS/TS, no native modules). 100-200 jobs/sec throughput ceiling vs BullMQ (thousands/sec) - fine for healthcare workflow volumes. Human-task pattern: pg-workflows extension adds step.waitFor() semantics on top of pg-boss.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party self-host only (runs inside your Postgres). No external provider option - this IS the queue.
register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter can scaffold pg-boss queue handler registrations from TypeSpec async operation definitions
register.person_centric_fit: Scheduled jobs drive proactive patient outreach (appointment reminders, medication refill nudges, care-gap alerts). No Redis ops burden simplifies self-hosted deployments.

State persistence is caller-managed (inject custom state store - Drizzle/Postgres fits naturally). TypeScript types available. Pairs with bpmn-js (frontend designer) for the builder-core-service no-code authoring surface. UserTask suspends execution and exposes signal() / resume() API matching Temporal signal pattern.

register.stack_fit: Pure TypeScript, Bun-compatible, no native dependencies. NestJS: inject actor as a provider, persist snapshot to Drizzle/Postgres on each transition. Deep-persistence snapshot format changed in v5 (migration concern for existing actors). Stately visual editor produces XState machine definitions (visual authoring path for simple state machines).
register.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party only (library in-process). No managed cloud requirement.
register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter can generate XState machine definitions from TypeSpec state enum + transition decorators
register.person_centric_fit: Models patient/encounter lifecycle states (admitted, in-treatment, discharged) + order states (pending, approved, dispensed) as explicit finite automata - makes person journey state visible and auditable. Guards prevent invalid state transitions (safety).

register.name: Inngest (inngest/inngest)
register.what: Event-driven durable step function platform: fan-out, parallel steps, retries, scheduled crons, Postgres or SQLite state. SSPL license (server) with DOSP to Apache-2.0 after 3 years. Fills: event-triggered background workflow alternative if Temporal ops burden is too high. Taxonomy: platform.workflow.event-driven, platform.workflow.background-jobs
register.taxonomy: Event-driven durable step function platform: fan-out, parallel steps, retries, scheduled crons, Postgres or SQLite state. SSPL on server binary means using it for internal CuraOS workflows is fine; offering it as a hosted SaaS to customers without source disclosure would trigger SSPL.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Docker/Helm + Postgres (SSPL, legal review needed for on-prem product distribution). BYO-3rd-party: Inngest Cloud. Hybrid viable.

Flows feature supports parent/child job dependencies (lightweight saga). Drizzle/Postgres used for application data; Redis purely for queue state. CuraOS already uses PostgreSQL-first strategy - adds Redis dependency.

register.name: Documenso (self-hosted e-sign service) + @documenso/sdk-typescript
register.what: Full-featured self-hosted DocuSign alternative with PKCS#12 digital signatures, multi-signer envelopes, embedded signing, webhooks, and eIDAS/UETA compliance. Fills the gap where esign-core-service already owns the contract layer but needs a BYO-3rd-party provider option (local_vs_3rdparty dual requirement). The sdk-typescript (MIT) is the api-adapter target. Covers taxonomy: erp.recurring.contracts esign surface + contract.signed_status projection.

Best fit as the BYO-3rd-party adapter option behind the esign-core provider port, paired with Documenso as the default self-hosted provider.

register.verify_notes: Candidate recommendation was "trial" with self-hosted as primary. Downgraded to reference because: (1) API paywall on self-hosted makes it non-functional as a self-hosted provider without paid seats - violates local-vs-3rdparty-rule's self-
register.simpler_alternative: Documenso is the strictly better self-hosted default: AGPL-3.0 with NO Section 7(b) paywall, full API access free on self-hosted, TypeScript/Next.js native stack (closer to Bun ecosystem), TypeScript SDK exists (github.com/documenso/sdk-typescript), PostgreSQL-native, community Helm chart issue open (#1345). Documenso satisfies the local-vs-3rdparty duality without a license key gate. DocuSeal can remain documented as the BYO-3rd-party cloud option ONLY (point SDK at DocuSeal Cloud for teams that already pay), but should not be the self-hosted provider behind the esign-core port.

register.name: Gotenberg (self-hosted PDF engine)
register.what: Docker-based stateless API wrapping Chromium + LibreOffice for HTML/CSS/Markdown -> PDF, DOCX -> PDF, PDF merge/split. Already the chosen PDF engine per ADR-0113 (InMemoryPdfRendererProvider stub confirms the real Gotenberg adapter is injected at modulith level). MIT license, 12k+ GitHub stars, version 8.32+ active. Fills: reports-service PDF rendering (erp.platform.documents 'present-strong'), AVS PDF render, CMS-1500/UB-04 paper claim forms, invoice PDF.
register.taxonomy: Docker-based stateless API wrapping Chromium + LibreOffice for HTML/CSS/Markdown -> PDF, DOCX -> PDF, PDF merge/split. No wrapper needed - the provider interface is the wrapper.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host primary: Helm chart + Docker image, air-gap safe (Zarf-packaged). BYO-3rd-party: swap the PdfRendererProvider NestJS port to point at any hosted HTML-to-PDF service (PDFBolt, WeasyPrint cloud) - the interface already exists in reports-service.

register.person_centric_fit: Invisible infrastructure. Enables: patient AVS PDF download, invoice PDF email, CMS-1500 download in patient-app (transparency over own billing), clinical document export.

Actively maintained: v6.1.6 released ~Jun 2026. Covers: form-fillable PDF templates for invoices, discharge summaries, consent forms, quotes - the 'template engine' slot separate from Gotenberg's HTML-render-to-PDF path. Taxonomy: erp.platform.documents template authoring + esign document prep.
register.taxonomy: MIT-licensed TypeScript-native PDF generator from JSON templates. Works in Node.js and browser. WYSIWYG template designer UI (@pdfme/ui) for business users to edit templates. register.stack_fit: Direct npm import in NestJS reports-service or a dedicated template-generator-service. `generate(template, inputs)` returns Uint8Array PDF bytes - pipe to SeaweedFS storage via storage-sdk. @pdfme/ui ships as a React component for the admin template designer - plugs directly into the ui-kit / admin web apps.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (npm import - no service dependency). No BYO-3rd-party equivalent needed; the template JSON stays in CuraOS storage.
register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: @pdfme/generator imported in reports-service behind a TemplateRendererProvider port (complement to PdfRendererProvider). Template JSON stored per-tenant in storage-core. @pdfme/ui React component emitted by ui-app-emit for document-template admin screen.
register.person_centric_fit: Enables: tenant-branded invoice PDFs, discharge summary templates, consent form templates with fillable fields. Template designer in admin surface; person gets correctly branded, pre-populated document.
register.verify_notes:
CONDITIONAL TRIAL, not full adopt. Conditions before landing in generator-first codegen:

1. Wrap behind a PdfTemplateProvider interface in reports-service (or template-generator-service). Local impl = pdfme. 3rd-party slot = explicit stub
register.simpler_alternative: Gotenberg (already in CuraOS stack per candidate taxonomy) covers HTML-to-PDF with full CSS pixel-perfect fidelity. If the use case is templated documents, a simpler path is: Handlebars/Mustache HTML templates stored in storage-core, rendered to PDF by the existing Gotenberg service. No new dependency, no expression-eval attack surface, no React designer constraint. Tradeoff: no WYSIWYG admin designer (business users get a code template editor, not a drag-drop canvas). If the WYSIWYG designer is a real user requirement (clinical admins authoring consent form layouts without developer involvement), pdfme fills a genuine gap Gotenberg cannot. If templates are developer-authored and rarely changed, Gotenberg + HTML templates is simpler and already present.

Server-side rendering via renderToStream/renderToBuffer. Fills: programmatic PDF generation where the template is code (e.g., AVS report, complex multi-section clinical document) rather than a JSON template. Complement to pdfme for code-driven documents.

register.name: Meilisearch (Community Edition)
register.what: Rust-based full-text + hybrid (BM25 + vector/semantic) search engine with typo tolerance, facets, tenant tokens for multi-tenancy. Fills taxonomy gaps: platform.search (search-service FHIR-param mapping enrichment from weaker-than-source-enrichments), patient/record search UX (patient-search), and the FilterBar/SearchShell ui-kit archetype contract lint gate (ui-kit-archetypes). Covers the `search-service` local module.
register.taxonomy: Rust-based full-text + hybrid (BM25 + vector/semantic) search engine with typo tolerance, facets, tenant tokens for multi-tenancy. Fills taxonomy gaps: platform.search (search-service FHIR-param mapping enrichment from weaker-than-source-enrichments), patient/record search UX (patient-search), and the FilterBar/SearchShell ui-kit archetype contract lint gate (ui-kit-archetypes). Covers the `search-service` local module.
register.license: MIT (Community Edition, v1.28+ binary). Enterprise Edition (sharding only) = BUSL-1.1, production-requires commercial. CE is safe-permissive for self-host.
register.stack_fit: Official `meilisearch` npm package (TypeScript). `nestjs-meilisearch` v3.0.0 NestJS module available. Self-hosted single binary or Docker. Multi-tenant via signed tenant tokens (JWT, scoped filter_by rules). Hybrid search (BM25 + vector via OpenAI/HuggingFace embeddings) is stable since v1.9+; semanticRatio tunable. Integrates directly with search-service NestJS module via HTTP SDK. Drizzle/Postgres stays source of truth; Meilisearch is the index layer.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted CE (first-party, Zarf/Helm/K8s compatible). BYO: Meilisearch Cloud SaaS at meilisearch.com. Both supported.
register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/search-sdk wrapping meilisearch-js) + codegen-emitter (search-emit: index definition per .tsp entity, FHIR search-param mapping table, FilterBar contract lint gate)
register.person_centric_fit: Patient/user-facing instant search (sub-50ms) with plain-language typo tolerance. Tenant tokens scope each person to their own data. FHIR search-param semantics mappable on top of the index layer.
register.verify_notes: Verdict rationale - confirmed-trial, not confirmed-adopt. Adopt is blocked by: (1) CVE-2026-57823 directly undermines the tenant token isolation claim for patient data - must be confirmed resolved at the pinned version before patient-facing
register.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is already in the stack. PostgreSQL (CNPG, already deployed) with pg_search (ParadeDB BM25 extension) + pgvector delivers hybrid search (BM25 + vector) without adding a new stateful service. Benchmarks show PG GIN/BM25 at P50 4ms latency for boolean queries. ParadeDB pg_search is production-stable as of 2025, runs inside CNPG, and CNPG already has Citus for multi-tenancy. This eliminates: a new binary to air-gap, a new Helm chart, a sync layer, tenant token JWT management, an embeddings sidecar, and the CE/EE binary tracking burden. The ponytail ladder stops here (rung 4: native platform feature / already-installed dependency). The case for Meilisearch must justify why pg_search + pgvector inside existing CNPG is insufficient - specifically typo tolerance and sub-50ms instant-search UX. pg_search does not provide typo tolerance (fuzzy match via trigrams is possible but inferior). For patient-facing instant search with typo tolerance, Meilisearch's UX delta is real. For internal FHIR parameter search and FilterBar contract linting, pg_search likely covers it.

register.name: pgvector (PostgreSQL extension)
register.what: First-class vector search (HNSW + IVFFlat indexes) inside existing CNPG/PostgreSQL. Fills: RAG/semantic search on clinical records, FHIR-param hybrid ranking, terminology similarity, CDS cohort embedding queries. Gaps covered: vector dimension of search-service (weaker-than-source-enrichments FHIR search-param) and future CDS embeddings (cds-rules-engine analog). Taxonomy: platform.search, healthstack.quality (CDS embedding).

register.simpler_alternative: PostgreSQL full-text search (tsvector/tsquery + pg_trgm) already in CNPG covers keyword and fuzzy search without a new extension. For the CDS embedding and FHIR semantic ranking use case described, there is no simpler already-installed alternative - tsvector does not do dense vector similarity. pgvector is the minimum viable addition. Ponytail ladder stops here: no stdlib covers 768-dim cosine similarity at scale.

register.verify_notes:
DOWNGRADE FROM ADOPT TO TRIAL. Three issues make "adopt" premature:

1. RLS security track record is non-trivial in a HIPAA-adjacent system. OX Security found an RLS bypass via subquery injection in v4.1.3 (users can reference tables outsid
register.simpler_alternative: No drop-in simpler alternative exists for this specific use case. Grafana targets ops/metrics, not SQL-explorer BI; its multi-tenant embedded guest story is weaker. Metabase is simpler to operate but carries LGPL (commercial use requires paid Enterprise tier) and its embedded multi-tenant support is less mature. Scratch-build is infeasible at comparable feature depth. Superset is the correct pick on the build-vs-buy axis. The ponytail ladder stops here: no already-installed dep, no stdlib, no native platform feature covers embedded multi-tenant BI dashboards.

register.name: Typesense
register.what: High-QPS in-memory fuzzy search engine with scoped API keys for multi-tenancy, native vector + hybrid search, and Raft-based HA clustering. Alternative to Meilisearch for higher-throughput search (10K+ QPS) or when HA clustering is the primary constraint. Covers same taxonomy as Meilisearch (platform.search, patient-search). Reference/trial over adopt because Meilisearch is the better default for CuraOS's scale and license position.
register.taxonomy: High-QPS in-memory fuzzy search engine with scoped API keys for multi-tenancy, native vector + hybrid search, and Raft-based HA clustering. Reference/trial over adopt because Meilisearch is the better default for CuraOS's scale and license position.
register.stack_fit: Official `typesense` npm client (TypeScript, v3.0.6). NestJS integration via community packages or direct HTTP. Scoped API keys (HMAC-signed with embedded filter_by) = production-grade multi-tenancy. Hybrid search: keyword + vector in one query. HA via Raft (3-node). GPL-3 server: OK to self-host as a service, but must not link server code into CuraOS build.
register.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted binary/Docker (first-party). Typesense Cloud SaaS as BYO option.
register.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (same @curaos/search-sdk abstraction layer that can swap Meilisearch for Typesense backend)
register.person_centric_fit: Same as Meilisearch. Scoped keys let person search only their own records. Higher QPS headroom useful if patient/user concurrency is high.
register.verify_notes: Sole differentiators claimed by candidate -- higher QPS, Raft HA -- are either refuted (QPS) or overstated (Raft gives HA but not write-sharding; Meilisearch has equivalent read HA). Typesense's Raft limitation (entire dataset replicated pe
register.simpler_alternative: Meilisearch CE (MIT, already designated platform.search primary): independent 2026 benchmarks show Meilisearch outperforms Typesense at the relevant QPS range -- 1200 QPS saturation vs 800 QPS for Typesense on identical hardware (c5.xlarge), with 3.2x lower p99 latency at 500 QPS (28ms vs 91ms). The candidate's 10K+ QPS throughput-advantage premise is from Typesense's own marketing page, not third-party data, and is inverted by current evidence. Meilisearch also has scoped API key multi-tenancy (filter_by scope), same HA read-scaling story, and zero GPL distribution overhead. PostgreSQL tsvector on CNPG covers CuraOS's current scale with zero new service.

Covers: search-service FHIR-param mapping, platform-reporting operational logs queries. Best when data volume, complex aggregations, or FHIR-native search behavior (like `_includes`, compartment search) are needed.

No Zod wrapper needed for eval; Zod validates incoming context shape. JDM files are portable JSON - store in DB, edit via GoRules BRMS UI, version-control in repo.
