Stream support is real and tested at scale by community (835 production data). HIPAA 5010 is the current mandated standard (008060 published Sept 2025 but federal rulemaking not yet adopted, so no compliance gap today).
verified.simpler_alternative: No stdlib or already-installed dep covers ASC X12 wire format parsing - this is a domain-specific binary-adjacent format with loop/segment/element grammar that requires a real parser. The ponytail ladder dead-ends at rung 5: no already-installed dep covers it. Scratch implementation of a compliant 5010 X12 parser + generator is non-trivial (weeks, not days) and would itself need the same maintenance. The real alternative comparison is: (A) node-x12 v1.7.1 locked + tightly wrapped, (B) EDIFlow @ediflow/x12 + @ediflow/hipaa-x12-005010 (April 2026, v0.3.0, actively maintained but ~3 months old, higher API churn risk), (C) tastypackets/x12-parser (parser only, no generator, no 837 emit), (D) buy clearinghouse SDK (Stedi/Claim.MD) that abstracts X12 entirely. Option D is worth flagging: if the clearinghouse adapter layer (already required for payer connectivity per the candidate's own note) can handle envelope assembly, node-x12 scope shrinks to internal canonical representation only. EDIFlow is the strongest emerging alternative but v0.3.0 at 3 months old is not adoption-ready for a healthcare billing lane.
verified.notes: Verdict rationale: downgrade from "adopt" to "confirmed-trial". The candidate is correct that node-x12 fills the gap and the generator-first wrapping isolates risk. But three adversarial findings push this to trial-gated: (1) The successor (js-edi) has been "in development" since 2021 with zero stable releases - this is abandonment signal, not a healthy handoff roadmap. The upgrade path the candidate relies on does not exist. (2) The candidate misstated the latest version (1.6.1 vs actual 1.7.1 on npm) - small but indicates the research was not done against live npm metadata. (3) EDIFlow emerged in April 2026 with active maintenance and HIPAA 5010 coverage; it is too new to adopt today but should be re-evaluated at 6-month mark. Recommended trial conditions: lock to node-x12 v1.7.1 exact pin in @curaos/x12-sdk package.json; vendor the dep inside the SDK (no transitive exposure); write a conformance test harness against real 837P/837I/835/270/271 samples from the X12 5010 implementation guides before GA; file a foresight issue to re-evaluate EDIFlow at v0.5+ or 6 months of production history, whichever comes first. The wrapper-first architecture the candidate proposes is correct and sufficient to contain the maintenance risk for v1 scope.

verified.name: x12-parser (tastypackets)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. Zero dependency surface (dev-only deps). Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution. No attribution or copyleft risk. License claim accurate.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: STALE, not abandoned. v1.3.0 is the latest; last significant commit circa 2021-2022 (npm publish "2 years ago" as of late 2024 = ~2022). Author note: "I work at PayPal which uses a private git, so I don't have a lot of activity here." ~60 stars, ~9,500 weekly downloads. No open CVEs found in GitHub Advisory Database, NVD, or npm audit signals. No security advisories. 2 open issues. Stable download curve suggests production use by downstream consumers, not abandonment. The candidate's "last significant commit circa 2021" claim is accurate. No release in 4+ years is a real flag for a streaming library riding Node.js internals.
verified.simpler_alternative: No stdlib or already-installed dep covers X12 835 parsing. X12 is a proprietary delimiter-variable binary-ish EDI format with HIPAA-mandated loop structure (CLP/SVC implied context, ISA-declared delimiters, companion-guide variance per payer). Scratch implementation is non-trivial: delimiter sniffing, loop-context state machine, segment schema, and 1M+ record throughput. Not a one-liner. The ponytail ladder stops at rung 5 (already-installed dep) - but nothing is installed yet. This is a legitimate external dep slot. Simpler alternative: node-x12 has TS types and parse+generate, but is also in maintenance mode since 2021 and has lower weekly downloads than x12-parser. No clearly superior maintained option exists in the JS/TS ecosystem as of 2026.
verified.integration_reality: Bun compat: Node Transform streams are largely implemented in Bun as of 2025-2026. Edge cases exist at stream-type conversion boundaries but a pure Transform pipeline (file -> X12parser -> JSON) has no web-stream interop boundary - low risk in practice. NestJS: no issue; NestJS pipes streams natively. Drizzle/Zod: x12-parser is parse-only, outputs plain JS objects; Zod schemas wrap the output at the SDK boundary, no friction. TypeSpec: no direct integration - TypeSpec models the contract, x12-parser feeds the ingestion side. TypeScript types: NO bundled .d.ts. Candidate correctly calls this out. A thin hand-written or generated .d.ts is required inside @curaos/x12-sdk - this is an S-effort task, not a blocker. Generator-first concern: the .d.ts wrapper belongs in the @curaos/x12-sdk package template, not bespoke per-service. That is compatible with generator-first if the SDK generator emits the wrapper. No lock-in risk: pure Transform stream, swap-out is a one-line require change. Does NOT fight generator-first - it is a leaf dep inside the SDK, invisible to generated services.
verified.notes: Downgrade from "adopt" to "trial" on two real grounds: (1) 4+ year stale codebase on a Node.js streaming library - Bun stream compat gaps could surface at scale and there is no upstream to fix them; (2) no TS types requires SDK-level wrapper that must be maintained if the library ever breaks. Trial scope: use x12-parser as the 835 stream-parse leg inside @curaos/x12-sdk ONLY. Gate on: (a) Bun-native smoke test with real 835 fixture at 10k+ records before @curaos/x12-sdk merges; (b) .d.ts wrapper authored and tested; (c) fallback plan documented (node-x12 stream API or thin hand-rolled ISA/GS/ST state machine - feasible given the format's simplicity at parse-only level). Do NOT use for 837 generation (parse-only, as stated). Do NOT expose raw x12-parser types outside the SDK boundary. The "1M+ 835 records in production" claim is unverifiable externally but the download curve and zero CVE record are consistent with quiet production stability. Pairing note: node-x12 remains the only JS option for 837 generation; its maintenance-mode status is equivalent staleness - neither package has an active maintainer, which is the honest state of the JS X12 ecosystem in 2026.

verified.name: Medplum (@medplum/core + FHIR billing model)
verified.license_confirmed: Confirmed Apache-2.0 across all medplum/* packages as of June 2026 (GitHub updated 2026-06-28). Permissive, compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution and BYO-3rd-party duality. No CLA, no SSPL, no Commons Clause. Safe.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active - v5.1.21 published 3 days ago (June 2026), 705 stars with 2.2k watchers, 526 forks, patch releases shipping monthly. Commit frequency high. One CRITICAL caveat: CVE-2026-49120 (CVSS 8.5, SSRF in subscription worker, published June 2, 2026) affects Medplum server versions before 5.1.14. The vulnerability is SERVER-SIDE ONLY (the subscription worker component, not the @medplum/core npm client package). Current pinned version 5.1.21 is patched. If CuraOS were to run the Medplum server, it would need to be pinned at >= 5.1.14. The @medplum/core client package is NOT in the exploitable path - the SSRF lives in the server's subscription worker runtime, not in the client type library. However, the existence of an SSRF at CVSS 8.5 in the server confirms the candidate's own note that "full platform adoption is overkill" - running the Medplum server introduces a concrete attack surface CuraOS does not need.
verified.integration_reality: Optimistic on several points. (1) @medplum/core is NOT types-only - it is a full MedplumClient FHIR HTTP client with FHIRPath evaluation, WebSocket subscriptions, and validation runtime. Bundle is substantial (Bundlephobia shows a large minified size for v5.0.10). Pulling it into NestJS services for "just the billing types" means carrying an HTTP client you will never use. (2) The correct minimal import is @medplum/fhirtypes - a pure TypeScript types package with no runtime code, no dependencies, actively maintained in sync with the same monorepo. The candidate's proposal conflates the two packages. (3) Bun compatibility: @medplum/core v5 migrated to ESM by default, which Bun handles natively. No documented Bun-specific issues found. BUT the MedplumClient uses fetch + WebSocket APIs that Bun implements - it would run, but is unnecessary weight for CuraOS's use case. (4) Air-gap story for @medplum/fhirtypes: trivial - it is a static types package with zero runtime deps, Zarf-packaged trivially. The Medplum SERVER has no published Zarf bundle and its self-hosting docs cover only cloud (Azure, GCP) and Docker Compose - no air-gap Helm path found, confirming the server is not appropriate for CuraOS. (5) Generator-first compatibility: @medplum/fhirtypes exports plain TypeScript interfaces - no decorators, no Zod, no io-ts. CuraOS's codegen emitter can reference them directly as contract types or use them as the source of truth for a TypeSpec import statement. No lock-in, no glue code. Drizzle would serialize the FHIR blob or project to relational columns - neither path requires @medplum/core. (6) A genuine simpler alternative exists: @solarahealth/fhir-r4 (MIT, 8.96 MB unpacked, Zod schemas included, supports per-resource imports for bundle control, last publish 9 months ago - less active than Medplum). @medplum/fhirtypes wins on maintenance velocity.
verified.simpler_alternative: Yes - @medplum/fhirtypes (types-only subset of the same monorepo, no runtime, no MedplumClient, zero dependencies) covers the entire stated need: Claim, ClaimResponse, ExplanationOfBenefit, CoverageEligibilityRequest/Response as strongly-typed R4 interfaces. This is the correct ponytail rung - use the types package, not the full SDK. Secondary alternative for Zod-native validation: @solarahealth/fhir-r4 (MIT), but maintenance is slower. No stdlib or already-installed package covers FHIR R4 contract shapes - an external types package IS the right call here; the question is which one.
verified.notes: Verdict rationale: The candidate's recommendation to "adopt @medplum/core" is wrong on the package boundary. The right adoption is @medplum/fhirtypes (downgrade from core to the types-only package). This is not a rejection of the Medplum ecosystem - it IS the Medplum ecosystem, just the correct package. Downgraded to "reference" because: (1) the candidate misstates the integration target (core vs fhirtypes), (2) the CVE on the server component reinforces that the Medplum server must NOT run alongside CuraOS's own services, (3) no air-gap story for the server. Action: adopt @medplum/fhirtypes@latest as a devDependency (types only, zero runtime cost). The CuraOS codegen emitter references FHIR R4 interfaces from @medplum/fhirtypes for Claim/ClaimResponse/ExplanationOfBenefit/CoverageEligibilityRequest contract shapes. Do NOT add @medplum/core. Do NOT run the Medplum server. Bot architecture maps to NestJS event handlers as the candidate correctly notes.

verified.name: Candid Health (candidhealth npm SDK)
verified.license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 (SDK) + proprietary SaaS backend. Candidate brief incorrectly states MIT. Apache-2.0 is permissive and distribution-compatible. BAA must be negotiated separately; not visible in public docs. Proprietary backend pricing undisclosed ($300-800/provider/month per analyst estimates).
verified.maintenance_confirmed: v1.14.3, published within last week of June 2026. Active cadence. Fern-generated - contributions structurally not accepted. No known CVEs. Eligibility endpoint in flux: Stedi passthrough replacing Change Healthcare leg; CMS attestation deadline passed May 2026. Monitor changelog before prod eligibility enablement.
verified.integration_reality: SDK installs cleanly in Bun/NestJS (no glue friction). However, correct integration boundary per healthstack-claims-service Requirements.md (F-04) is the clearinghouse adapter port inside healthstack-interop-service - NOT a direct import in the claims service. SDK belongs inside a Candid-specific ClearinghouseProvider implementation, behind the provider interface. Zod/Drizzle/TypeSpec have no friction with the SDK. Generator-first target: a clearinghouse-adapter codegen recipe in healthstack-interop-service, config-driven per [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]].
verified.simpler_alternative: Stedi: programmable clearinghouse TypeScript SDK, drop-in Change Healthcare replacement, $70M Series B (Sep 2025), no bundled RCM workflow lock-in. Better-scoped for CuraOS which owns its own claim workflow. node-x12 (already planned) covers the self-host/local/air-gap leg. Candid is superior only when customer already contracts Candid for full RCM services.
verified.notes: Air-gap hard fail: Candid is cloud-only (staging + prod, vendor-hosted). Zero self-hosted or offline option. Air-gap tenants get nothing from this adapter. The local leg (node-x12 + direct clearinghouse) must be complete and production-capable independently - not a degraded fallback. Trial scope: BYO-3rd-party clearinghouse leg only, SaaS/hybrid tenants only, behind the interop-service adapter port. Confirm BAA before any PHI reaches this service in any environment. Pin SDK version; track eligibility endpoint migration status.

candidates.name: cql-execution + cql-exec-fhir
candidates.what: HL7 CQL (Clinical Quality Language) execution engine in TypeScript/JS. Executes CQL ELM artifacts against FHIR R4 patient data. Fills taxonomy gaps: healthcare.care.clinical-decision-support (CDS logic evaluation), healthcare.care.rules-engine (CQL rule expressions), healthcare.platform.reports-dashboards (eCQM population criteria IPP/DENOM/NUMER). cql-exec-fhir adds PatientSource from FHIR R4 bundles. Used by fqm-execution (MITRE), DEQM Test Server, and CQF Ruler underneath.
candidates.stack_fit: Pure TS, no native deps. Drop into NestJS service as a module-scoped singleton. Input: compiled ELM JSON + FHIR R4 Bundle per patient. No Zod wrapper needed for invocation; Zod can validate ELM payload at API boundary. Bun-compatible (pure JS). Wrapper needed: thin NestJS provider that loads ELM from DB/FS and calls Library.execute().
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: embed in healthstack-quality-service, store ELM in Postgres. BYO-3rd-party: point PatientSource at external FHIR server (cqf-ruler or HAPI). Both paths use same npm package.
candidates.maintenance_health: cqframework org (HL7 affiliate). cql-execution v3.3.0 (Node 24.x primary, 18.x deprecated). cql-exec-fhir v2.0.2 released within last 3 months (Snyk: Healthy). Low weekly downloads (~353 cql-exec-fhir) but this is a specialist clinical library; used by MITRE DEQM + CDC tooling. 6 contributors.
candidates.person_centric_fit: CQL authoring produces the logic that powers person-facing care-gap nudges and clinician CDS alerts from one engine. ELM artifacts are portable so a rule authored once surfaces on both patient-app (plain-language gap card) and clinician surface (QRDA population result).
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/cds-sdk wraps cql-execution + cql-exec-fhir); contract-typespec (healthstack-quality.tsp CQLLibrary + Measure resources); codegen emitter scaffolds NestJS CQL provider per measure.
candidates.risks: CQL requires ELM compilation step (cql-to-elm, Java-based translator). ELM compilation must run at build time or via cqf-ruler sidecar - adds Java dep in CI. Low community size means bug fixes are slow. eCQM correctness is regulator-graded; need golden-file tests against known-good QRDA outputs.

candidates.name: fqm-execution (MITRE projecttacoma)
candidates.what: TypeScript eCQM calculation library wrapping cql-execution + cql-exec-fhir with FHIR-Measure-aware output: per-patient MeasureReport, QRDA Category I/III, care-gaps Bundle with DetectedIssue + GuidanceResponse, data-requirements analysis. Fills taxonomy gaps: healthcare.platform.reports-dashboards (eCQM engine), healthcare.interop.hl7 (QRDA Cat I/III), healthcare.care.clinical-decision-support (care-gap surface). fqm-execution-service adds a Docker REST wrapper (POST /Measure/$evaluate-measure, /Measure/$care-gaps, /Measure/$data-requirements).
candidates.stack_fit: Full TypeScript, npm-import into healthstack-quality-service NestJS module. No native deps. Accepts FHIR Bundle (Measure + Libraries + ValueSets) + patient Bundles. Returns typed MeasureReport. Bun-compatible. For air-gap deployments run fqm-execution-service Docker alongside (port 3000) and call via HTTP from conversion-core.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: embed fqm-execution as npm dep in healthstack-quality-service. BYO-3rd-party: run fqm-execution-service Docker as a sidecar and point conversion-core at it via internal Kubernetes service.
candidates.maintenance_health: MITRE-backed (US DoD/CMS funded). v1.8.3 (updated Feb 20, 2026 - active). 21 GitHub stars (niche specialist tool). Used by MITRE DEQM Test Server and Measure Repository Service in production.
candidates.person_centric_fit: care-gaps endpoint returns DetectedIssue per gap + GuidanceResponse with data to close the gap - maps directly to patient-app 'my care gaps' card (consent-gated). Same engine drives the clinician population dashboard and QRDA submission.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/cds-sdk, wraps fqm-execution); contract-typespec (Measure + MeasureReport in healthstack-quality.tsp); codegen emitter generates the NestJS provider boilerplate and wires PatientSource from FHIR bundles.
candidates.risks: Still depends on cql-execution ELM compilation (Java cql-to-elm in CI). Small contributor pool. QRDA output correctness is regulator-graded; must run golden-file tests vs CMS reference outputs. Package lock: if cql-execution major bumps, fqm-execution must follow.

candidates.name: @gorules/zen-engine
candidates.what: Rust-core business rules engine with Node.js NAPI bindings. Evaluates decision tables, expression graphs (ZEN Expression Language), and rule graphs stored as portable JSON (JDM - JSON Decision Model). Fills taxonomy gaps: healthcare.care.rules-engine (table-driven cohort-scoped clinical rules, no-code authoring surface), healthcare.care.alerts (rule output -> alert events). Replaces the port-adapt of vista-dashboard-rules C9C engine with a maintained npm package. Healthcare page on gorules.io lists eligibility, formulary, clinical workflow automation as explicit use cases. GoRules BRMS adds a visual authoring UI (self-hosted or cloud at EU50/month).
candidates.stack_fit: NAPI binary ships per-platform (linux-x64-gnu, linux-x64-musl, darwin-x64/arm64, win32). Bun uses Node.js NAPI compatibility layer - confirmed working for most NAPI-rs packages. Install: npm i @gorules/zen-engine. NestJS: inject ZenEngine as a provider, load JDM JSON from Postgres/S3 at startup, call engine.evaluate(decisionId, context) per patient context. No Zod wrapper needed for eval; Zod validates incoming context shape. JDM files are portable JSON - store in DB, edit via GoRules BRMS UI, version-control in repo.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: embed engine in-process in NestJS (zero network latency). BYO-3rd-party: optionally run GoRules BRMS self-hosted Docker for visual rule authoring UI + rule storage. Cloud BRMS (gorules.io) is opt-in, rule eval always stays in-process.
candidates.maintenance_health: GoRules commercial entity backs it. v0.54.0 (published 3 months ago as of mid-2026). Active release cadence: multiple releases per quarter. ~5k weekly downloads across platform variants. 11 dependent npm packages. MIT licensed core. BRMS cloud/self-hosted is commercial add-on.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Clinical rules stored as JDM tables (e.g., drug contraindication lookup, immunization reminder schedule, risk-score coefficient table) can be evaluated per patient context and produce structured alert/recommendation output. Person-facing nudge (patient-app) and clinician alert both consume the same rule output event. No-code authoring via GoRules BRMS lets clinical staff edit rules without code deploys.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/cds-sdk registers ZenEngine as NestJS provider, emits typed evaluate() wrappers per rule category); contract-typespec (RuleDefinition + AlertEvent in healthstack-quality.tsp); codegen emitter generates JDM template stubs per domain.
candidates.risks: NAPI binary: must verify Bun NAPI compat at integration time (most napi-rs packages work; rare edge cases exist). Rule expressiveness bounded by ZEN expression language - complex temporal logic (e.g., 'patient had condition X for >90 days in past year') requires CQL, not ZEN tables. Pre-v1.0 semver (0.x) = breaking changes possible. Commercial entity controls roadmap.

candidates.name: cqf-ruler (cqframework / HAPI FHIR)
candidates.what: Self-hosted FHIR R4 server (HAPI JPA) with embedded CQL evaluation engine, CDS Hooks endpoint (/cds-services), Measure/$evaluate-measure, PlanDefinition/$apply, and ActivityDefinition/$apply operations. Fills taxonomy gaps: healthcare.care.clinical-decision-support (CDS Hooks service + PlanDefinition apply), healthcare.platform.reports-dashboards (Measure evaluation), healthcare.interop.fhir (knowledge artifact repository). Acts as the BYO-3rd-party CDS provider - NestJS services POST to /cds-services/{id} and consume Card responses.
candidates.language: Java (Docker image; consumed from NestJS via HTTP)
candidates.stack_fit: NestJS api-adapter: create a CdsHooksClient service that wraps HTTP POST to cqf-ruler /cds-services/{hookId}. Zod validates Card response shape. Deploy cqf-ruler as a Kubernetes Deployment (docker pull alphora/cqf-ruler). No Java in CuraOS build - purely runtime sidecar. Integrates with CNPG Postgres via HAPI JPA config.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: run cqf-ruler as k8s sidecar in healthstack namespace. BYO-3rd-party: point CdsHooksClient at a customer-provided SMART-on-FHIR CDS server (Epic, Cerner, etc.).
candidates.maintenance_health: cqframework org (HL7/ONC backed). Actively maintained, Docker image alphora/cqf-ruler available. Swagger UI + REST API. Used in production by US federal health IT programs (CDC, CMS tool listings).
candidates.person_centric_fit: PlanDefinition/$apply can generate CarePlan + Task resources directly - consumable by patient-app care plan view. CDS Hooks Cards return patient-facing suggestions (InfoCard, SuggestionCard) that map to person nudges. Clinician surface receives full card deck (links, suggestions, overrides).
candidates.generator_first_target: service (healthstack-cds-service): NestJS api-adapter service wrapping cqf-ruler HTTP; @curaos/cds-sdk exposes typed CdsHooksClient; Helm chart for cqf-ruler sidecar deployment.
candidates.risks: Java runtime: large container image, slow cold start (seconds to minutes), JVM memory tuning required. Not embeddable in-process - adds network hop and failure mode. HAPI version lock (cqf-ruler pins a HAPI version). Air-gap: large Java image needs Zarf bundling. Not suitable for <100ms latency inline CDS; use @gorules/zen-engine for synchronous inline checks.

candidates.name: json-rules-engine (CacheControl)
candidates.what: Pure JSON rule engine for Node.js. Conditions (all/any/not), facts (sync or async), events. Fills taxonomy gap: healthcare.care.rules-engine (simple table-driven reminders, eligibility conditions, care-gap predicates) as a lightweight in-process fallback when CQL is overkill. No FHIR awareness; facts are arbitrary JS objects.
candidates.stack_fit: Zero deps, pure TS. NestJS: inject Engine as provider, load rules from DB at startup. Rules are plain JSON - store in Postgres, edit via builder-core no-code UI. Bun-compatible. Zod validates rule schema at ingestion boundary.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (embedded in-process). No SaaS option.
candidates.maintenance_health: 3,068 GitHub stars. 400k weekly downloads (very high signal). v7.3.1, last release ~1yr ago (no release in past 12 months; Snyk flags as maintenance concern). 189 dependent packages. Effectively stable-frozen rather than abandoned - no critical issues open.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Can drive simple person-facing nudges (immunization due, screening overdue) where full CQL is not warranted. Fast to author new rules for clinical staff via builder-core JSON editor.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/cds-sdk, thin wrapper exposing typed RuleEngine factory); codegen emitter generates rule schema JSON stubs per entity type.
candidates.risks: No temporal logic, no terminology binding, no FHIR-native fact model. Complex clinical rules (interval calculations, value-set membership) require custom fact providers. Maintenance stagnation - if a breaking dep is found, no upstream fix. Not suitable for eCQM or CDS Hooks integration. Recommend as a secondary engine only for simple nudge/reminder rules where ZEN is also overkill.

candidates.name: CDS Hooks specification (protocol reference, no mature npm library)
candidates.what: HL7 CDS Hooks v2.0 is the wire protocol for synchronous, workflow-triggered CDS. Defines hook types (patient-view, order-select, order-sign, encounter-discharge) and JSON Card response format (info, suggestion, app-link). Fills taxonomy gap: healthcare.care.clinical-decision-support (hook dispatch protocol). No maintained npm library exists - implement as a NestJS controller handling POST /cds-services/{hookId}. Existing npm packages (@asymmetrik/node-cds-hooks, cds-hooks) are stagnant.
candidates.stack_fit: Implement CDS Hooks responder as a NestJS controller: validate JWT (JWKS from EHR), parse hook context (FHIR R4 prefetch bundle), call internal rule engines (ZEN / cql-execution / fqm-execution), return Card[] response. Zod schema validates incoming hook payload and outgoing Card. No npm library needed; spec is simple enough (one POST endpoint + Card schema) to implement in ~150 LOC.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: NestJS CDS Hooks handler (first-party). BYO-3rd-party: point NestJS at cqf-ruler /cds-services as the upstream resolver.
candidates.maintenance_health: HL7 standard v2.0 published and stable. Spec at cds-hooks.hl7.org. No breaking changes expected. medication-prescribe hook deprecated in favor of order-select/order-sign.
candidates.person_centric_fit: CDS Hooks Cards surface at point-of-care (prescribing, order sign). Suggestions in Cards can trigger patient-facing tasks/nudges via automation-core event emission after clinician action.
candidates.generator_first_target: contract-typespec (CdsHookRequest + Card schema in healthstack-cds.tsp); codegen emitter generates typed NestJS controller stub per hook type; @curaos/cds-sdk exposes Card builder helpers.
candidates.risks: No maintained npm library means implementing JWT validation and hook routing from scratch (~150 LOC, low risk). Integration with EHR CDS client (Epic, Cerner) requires vendor-specific testing. Hook prefetch bundle size can be large; FHIR R4 prefetch must be schema-matched.

candidates.name: nools (noolsjs/nools)
candidates.what: Rete-based forward-chaining rules engine in JavaScript (Node.js). Drools analog in JS. Fills healthcare.care.rules-engine with inference chain support.
candidates.stack_fit: No TypeScript types. No recent releases. No active maintainer.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (embedded). Abandoned.
candidates.maintenance_health: Last release years ago. Snyk: Inactive. No pull request activity. Functionally abandoned.
candidates.person_centric_fit: N/A - abandoned project introduces technical debt risk.
candidates.generator_first_target: N/A
candidates.risks: Abandoned. No TypeScript. Security vulnerabilities likely unpatched. Do not use.

candidates.name: google/cql (experimental Go engine)
candidates.what: Experimental Go CQL execution engine from Google with Beam job for large-population eCQM runs. Fills healthcare.platform.reports-dashboards at massive scale (BigQuery-backed). Not a Node/TS library.
candidates.stack_fit: Go binary, not embeddable in NestJS. Would require sidecar + HTTP bridge. Experimental - not production-ready per Google's own labeling.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (Go binary/container).
candidates.maintenance_health: Announced July 2024 as experimental. Active GitHub commits. Not officially supported Google product. No production SLA.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Population-scale batch runs (Beam/BigQuery), not real-time person-facing CDS.
candidates.generator_first_target: N/A - reference only for future high-scale population health analytics lane.
candidates.risks: Experimental label from Google means no production readiness guarantee. Go binary breaks NestJS-native integration pattern. Overkill for CuraOS scale.

verified.name: cql-execution + cql-exec-fhir
verified.license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 confirmed for both packages. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution and local-vs-3rdparty duality. No patent or copyleft constraints found.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: cql-execution v3.3.0 released Aug 2025 (~10 months ago). cql-exec-fhir latest is v2.1.6 (Aug 25, 2025) - candidate claimed v2.0.2, which is stale by 4 patch releases. 34 open issues on cql-execution. No CVEs surfaced but Snyk live check was blocked (redirected); advisory database was not definitively cleared. Node 18.x deprecated upstream; Node 24.x primary. Bun compatibility unconfirmed by upstream docs - pure CJS JS so likely works, but no explicit test matrix. fqm-execution (the real production consumer at MITRE) last released June 2025, confirming ecosystem is alive but slow-moving.
verified.integration_reality: NOT a clean drop-in. Three integration friction points the candidate underweights: (1) Java sidecar is harder than claimed - cql-to-elm is a mandatory compile step with no JS-native alternative. In CI it is a build-time dep; for air-gap Zarf bundles where tenants upload their own CQL libraries at runtime, the cql-translation-service Docker image becomes a RUNTIME sidecar that must be Zarf-bundled, digest-pinned, and operated. This is a meaningful ops addition. (2) No NestJS prior art found externally - only an Express.js wrapper (AHRQ CDS-Connect). The thin NestJS provider claimed is speculative; ELM-loading from Postgres + per-tenant library isolation under multi-tenant CNPG/Citus adds real complexity the candidate labels 'thin wrapper'. (3) cql-exec-fhir peer dep is wide (>=1.3.0) - version coupling between the two packages is loose, which is good for flexibility but means no enforced compat guarantee between v3.3.0 cql-execution and v2.1.6 cql-exec-fhir without integration testing. Bun runtime: pure CJS JS should work under Bun's Node compat layer, but no upstream test matrix confirms this; the Zod wrapper at API boundary is valid but TypeSpec contract for ELM payloads adds a schema maintenance burden as CQL spec evolves.
verified.simpler_alternative: No simpler already-installed option covers this. CQL/ELM execution is a specialist HL7 standard with no stdlib equivalent. The BYO-3rd-party path (point PatientSource at cqf-ruler/HAPI FHIR) is a legitimate simpler option for initial scope - defer the npm embedding and call an external FHIR CQL evaluation endpoint first. That satisfies [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]] with less surface area. The google/cql Go engine (Apache-2.0, experimental) is a cross-domain watch item but not a TS-stack option.
verified.notes: Downgrade from 'adopt' to 'confirmed-trial' for these reasons: (1) candidate version claim for cql-exec-fhir is stale (v2.0.2 claimed, v2.1.6 actual) - signals the evidence base was not freshly verified; (2) Java sidecar ops burden in air-gap Zarf is materially understated; (3) Bun compat unconfirmed by upstream; (4) no NestJS integration precedent found - the 'thin wrapper' claim needs a spike to validate; (5) eCQM correctness is regulator-graded and golden-file tests against known-good QRDA outputs are mandatory before any production use. Recommended trial path: (a) spike the NestJS provider with one measure + one FHIR R4 bundle under Bun, (b) validate Zarf bundle with cql-translation-service image digest-pinned, (c) run golden-file QRDA output test against a CMS-published reference measure before committing to generator-first SDK wrap. Generator-first target (@curaos/cds-sdk) should not be built until the spike passes all three gates.

verified.name: fqm-execution (MITRE projecttacoma)
verified.license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 confirmed via GitHub and npm. Permissive, no copyleft, safe for self-hosted + air-gap distribution and the local-vs-3rdparty duality. No restrictions on commercial use or redistribution.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active but thin. Last repo update March 2026; latest release v1.8.1 (June 5 2026, not v1.8.3 as claimed - minor version discrepancy in the brief). 21 stars, ~7 forks. MITRE/projecttacoma-backed with confirmed production use in DEQM Test Server and Measure Repository Service. Small contributor pool is the real risk: no community bus factor beyond MITRE's CMS funding. No known CVEs per Snyk as of latest version.
verified.integration_reality: More nuanced than claimed. Java (cql-to-elm) is AUTHORING-PIPELINE-ONLY - fqm-execution requires pre-compiled ELM JSON already embedded in FHIR Library resources and performs no runtime CQL-to-ELM translation. This is acceptable (CMS publishes pre-compiled Measure bundles), but it means CuraOS CI must bake in a Java step for any custom measure authoring. Runtime is pure TS with no native deps. Bun compatibility is UNPROVEN - cql-execution tests only against Node 24.x; no Bun-specific issues filed but also no positive confirmation. NestJS wrapping is mechanical (plain npm import, no framework coupling) but zero prior art exists publicly. TypeScript types are partially incomplete per upstream cql-execution docs ("full-fledged typing not yet implemented").
verified.simpler_alternative: No simpler option exists. This is a narrow regulator-graded output domain (QRDA Cat I/III, FHIR MeasureReport, care-gaps Bundle with DetectedIssue + GuidanceResponse). The only other TS option is cqm-execution (QDM-based, older, less FHIR-native). Building from scratch against the CQL spec would dwarf the adoption cost by 10x+. Ponytail ladder stops here: no stdlib, no installed dep, no one-liner covers eCQM calculation + QRDA generation.
verified.notes: Downgrade from adopt to confirmed-trial on three concrete concerns: (1) Bun runtime is unverified - the CuraOS rule [[curaos-bun-primary-rule]] makes this a blocker for direct npm-import mode until a smoke test proves Bun executes cql-execution + cql-exec-fhir cleanly (run `bun test` against the fqm-execution test suite before committing). If Bun fails, the fqm-execution-service Docker sidecar path (HTTP, port 3000) is the fallback and avoids the issue entirely - this is already described in the candidate brief and is a valid local-vs-3rdparty dual-track per [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]]. (2) QRDA output correctness is regulator-graded; golden-file tests against CMS reference outputs are mandatory before any production submission path is wired - this is real effort not mentioned in the M estimate. (3) Version lock to cql-execution major version is real; the generator-first target (@curaos/cds-sdk wrapping fqm-execution) MUST own the version pin and expose a clear upgrade path in the SDK contract per [[curaos-generator-evolution-rule]]. Trial gate: prove Bun compatibility + run one full measure bundle end-to-end (MeasureReport + care-gaps Bundle) against a CMS sample measure before generator scaffolding starts.

verified.name: @gorules/zen-engine
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed for zen-engine core (npm, GitHub). BRMS (authoring UI) is commercial-closed: requires license key from portal.gorules.io; offline/air-gap mode is Enterprise Gov plan only - NOT freely available. The core eval engine is MIT; the no-code authoring surface is not. Claim "safe-permissive" is accurate only for the eval binary, not the full proposed stack.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active - confirmed. Latest stable: 0.54.0 (March 2026, ~3 months old at time of review). 1.0.0-beta.2 published 2 days ago (June 2026) showing active progress toward stable API. Release cadence: multiple per quarter (0.52, 0.53, 0.54 in under 3 months). No published CVEs found. No security advisories in GitHub Advisory Database. Open issues (~443 as of Apr 2026): none flagged security/critical in search results. NAPI v3 migration is in-flight as part of 1.0 prep - this IS a breaking surface for the binding layer during the 0.x->1.0 transition window. Maintenance claim is accurate; stability claim is premature.
verified.integration_reality: Bun NAPI: GoRules docs explicitly list Bun (alongside Deno and Node.js) as a supported target. napi-rs packages generally work in Bun 1.3+, and Bun 1.3.5 fixed the main napi_register hot-reload bug. No reports of zen-engine specifically failing on Bun found; moderate-confidence, unverified in production. Musl binary exists (@gorules/zen-engine-linux-x64-musl) confirming Alpine/Distroless container compat for K8s/Zarf air-gap image packaging - the EVAL engine ships air-gap clean. NestJS injection: straightforward provider pattern, no special glue needed. Zod wraps context shape before evaluate() - that's a one-liner validator on input, not engine coupling. TypeSpec: JDM is plain JSON; RuleDefinition + AlertEvent types in healthstack-quality.tsp are standard typespec work with no engine coupling. Generator-first: SDK wrapper (@curaos/cds-sdk) + codegen emitting JDM stubs is a clean generator-first target - JDM format is portable JSON, no proprietary binary format lock-in. The integration claim is not optimistic; glue is light. One real caveat: ZEN Expression Language cannot express temporal cohort queries ("condition X for >90 days in past 12 months") - those require CQL or custom Rust custom functions. For table-driven eligibility/formulary/reminder rules this is a non-issue; for complex clinical phenotyping it is a hard ceiling.
verified.simpler_alternative: json-rules-engine (pure JS, 189 npm dependents, no NAPI) covers simple if-then rule chains but has no decision table authoring, no visual editor path, no portable JDM format, and was last published ~1 year ago (lower maintenance signal). For clinical decision table use cases (drug contraindication lookup, immunization schedules, risk score tables) zen-engine's decision table model is structurally the right tool - json-rules-engine would require hand-rolling the table schema and eval logic that zen-engine ships. OPA/Rego handles policy-as-code (authz) not decision tables. Drools is JVM-only, incompatible stack. The ponytail ladder check: no already-installed CuraOS dependency covers table-driven clinical rule eval with a no-code authoring surface. Scratch cost is HIGH (build table eval, version JDM, wire authoring UI). zen-engine is the minimum viable answer here, not an over-engineering choice.
verified.notes: HARD BLOCKER for full adoption as proposed: BRMS self-hosted authoring UI requires license server connectivity to portal.gorules.io for validation. Offline/air-gap BRMS is Enterprise Gov plan only - not freely self-hostable. This DIRECTLY conflicts with [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]] (every integration must offer both a first-party self-hosted option AND a BYO-3rd-party option) and [[curaos-airgap-rule]]. The candidate proposal treats BRMS as optional ("optionally run GoRules BRMS self-hosted") but the taxonomy gap it fills (no-code authoring surface for clinical staff) is a stated value proposition. Without BRMS, clinical staff edit raw JDM JSON - that is not a viable no-code surface. Resolution paths: (1) Scope adoption to EVAL ENGINE ONLY (MIT, air-gap clean, no authoring UI) and file a separate evaluation for authoring surface alternatives (open-source decision table editors: Corticon open-source, custom React JDM editor in curaos-workspace, or Redhat Kogito DMN tooling). (2) Negotiate Enterprise Gov with GoRules for air-gap license before committing the authoring surface. (3) Build minimal JDM table editor as a CuraOS-native app/site-builder surface (generator-first, no external license dependency). SECONDARY RISK: 0.x semver with NAPI v3 migration in-flight. Pin to 0.54.0, do not float to 1.0.0-beta. Re-evaluate once 1.0.0-stable ships (likely Q3 2026 based on beta pace). VERDICT: confirmed-trial for the EVAL ENGINE. Reject the BRMS-as-authoring-surface claim until air-gap/license constraint is resolved. Do not adopt the full stack as proposed. Wire zen-engine as NestJS provider, store JDM in Postgres, version-control in repo. Defer authoring UI decision. Mark with ponytail comment: // ponytail: no-code authoring deferred; JDM JSON in DB + repo is the ceiling until air-gap-clean editor is chosen.

verified.name: cqf-ruler (cqframework / HAPI FHIR)
verified.license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 confirmed. Compatible with self-hosted, air-gap (Zarf), and BYO-3rd-party distribution. No AGPL/SSPL risk.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: CONDITIONAL. GitHub is active (v0.14.0 released 2026-06-29) but the canonical Docker image alphora/cqf-ruler was last pushed ~3 years ago (circa 2023) - a hard blocker for Kubernetes/Zarf deployment. FHIR R5 support is broken (Issue #797, opened Oct 2024, still open as of research date). Growing unresolved backlog (Issues #791, #803 also open). Root cause: the project is being hollowed out from above - its own README states that key operations have migrated upstream into cqframework/clinical-reasoning and natively into HAPI FHIR JPA Server Starter. A SmartChartSuite fork exists specifically because upstream does not deploy cleanly, which is a convergent signal of production deployment friction.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - rung 4 of ponytail ladder. HAPI FHIR JPA Server Starter (hapifhir/hapi-fhir-jpaserver-starter) now natively provides CDS Hooks (hapi.fhir.cdshooks.enabled=true plus application-cds.yaml preset), CQL evaluation via cqframework/clinical-reasoning, Measure/$evaluate-measure, and PlanDefinition/$apply. Same Apache-2.0 license, same FHIR R4 surface, actively maintained Docker image, no plugin-wrapper churn risk. cqf-ruler is a plugin layer on top of HAPI FHIR JPA Starter that is being structurally unwrapped. The correct trial candidate is hapifhir/hapi-fhir-jpaserver-starter with CDS Hooks enabled, not cqf-ruler.
verified.integration_reality: The NestJS-wraps-HTTP-POST + Zod-validates-Card integration claim is accurate. CDS Hooks is simple JSON REST (Card responses are ~5 fields). No Java enters CuraOS build. The generator-first target (healthstack-cds-service + @curaos/cds-sdk + Helm) is correctly scoped. No active TypeScript CDS Hooks client library exists on npm (cds-hooks npm package is marked Inactive); @curaos/cds-sdk would be hand-rolled HTTP + Zod, which is acceptable given spec simplicity. CDS Hooks OpenAPI spec is published and compatible with CuraOS TypeSpec/OpenAPI toolchain for codegen. Integration path is sound but must target the right base image.
verified.notes: Downgrade from trial to reference. The taxonomy gap (CDS Hooks sidecar, Measure evaluation, PlanDefinition apply) is real and the architecture described is correct. However the specific artifact (alphora/cqf-ruler Docker image) is stale, FHIR R5 is broken, and the project is being superseded by HAPI FHIR native support. File as reference for the architecture pattern. Raise a follow-up to evaluate hapifhir/hapi-fhir-jpaserver-starter with cdshooks.enabled=true as the actual trial candidate. The @gorules/zen-engine path covers synchronous inline CDS and should be evaluated first given lower operational cost; cqf-ruler/HAPI CDS Hooks sidecar is appropriate only for CQL-heavy Measure reporting and knowledge-artifact-repository use cases that zen-engine cannot express.

candidates.name: Temporal TypeScript SDK + nestjs-temporal-core
candidates.what: Durable execution engine: deterministic workflow replay, signals, queries, SLA timers, human-task wait, exactly-once activity retry. Fills: workflow-core-service primary substrate (already adopted, M5 complete). Taxonomy: platform.workflow.durable-execution, platform.workflow.human-in-loop, platform.workflow.sla
candidates.license: MIT (SDK) + MIT (nestjs-temporal-core) + Server: MIT (self-host open source)
candidates.maintenance_health: ~13k stars (sdk-typescript), Temporal Inc. backed, v1.17.x shipping 2025-2026, active community. nestjs-temporal-core 3.2.8 active. Official Helm chart for K8s self-host.
candidates.stack_fit: Already wired: @temporalio/* v1.17.2 + nestjs-temporal-core 3.2.8 in workflow-core-service package.json. CRITICAL gap: Temporal worker requires authentic Node.js - cannot run inside Bun process. Workaround in prod: run workers as a separate Node subprocess (not Bun) via bun:spawn or a sidecar container. Client/NestJS HTTP layer stays in Bun. No wrapper needed for client path; worker sidecar is the integration seam.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Temporal server via official Helm chart + PostgreSQL (CNPG already in stack). BYO-3rd-party: Temporal Cloud (managed). Both viable, both documented.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Signals/queries enable real-time human-task inbox; SLA timer emits breach events that drive patient/customer notifications. Enables journey-centric approval flows (admit, prescribe, prior-auth) without polling hacks.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@temporalio/workflow + nestjs-temporal-core): codegen emitter in tools/codegen can scaffold workflow definition files + activity stubs from .tsp contract definitions
candidates.risks: Bun worker incompatibility is the main risk (node:v8 createHook, worker_threads, vm module not supported in Bun). Mitigation: workers run as Node child process / separate container; client runs in Bun NestJS. Operational complexity of self-hosted Temporal cluster (4 server components + DB + Elasticsearch for visibility). Community nestjs-temporal-core is not Temporal Inc. maintained.

candidates.name: Trigger.dev v3
candidates.what: Apache-2.0 durable background job + workflow platform with first-class human-in-the-loop (Waitpoints: pause task until human approves/rejects), NestJS SDK, self-host via Docker/K8s + PostgreSQL. Fills: BYO-3rd-party escape valve for teams that want managed workflow without Temporal Cloud; also fills long-running background task lane. Taxonomy: platform.workflow.human-in-loop, platform.workflow.background-jobs
candidates.maintenance_health: Active 2025-2026, v3 open to all May 2024, dedicated compute (no serverless timeout), observability dashboard, cloud free tier 10k runs/month. Backed by YC + VCs. Active NestJS integration package @trigger.dev/nestjs.
candidates.stack_fit: @trigger.dev/sdk + @trigger.dev/nestjs. Tasks defined as TypeScript functions decorated with task(). Bun-compatible client path (task scheduling). Worker runs on dedicated isolated compute - same Bun/Node boundary issue as Temporal for worker runtime but managed by Trigger.dev's own runner. Self-host adds a Trigger.dev server pod + PostgreSQL + Redis. No Drizzle/Zod conflicts.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Docker Compose / Helm + PostgreSQL + Redis, all open source under Apache-2.0. BYO-3rd-party: Trigger.dev Cloud. Both with identical feature set (no feature gate on self-host).
candidates.person_centric_fit: Waitpoints are a natural fit for patient/customer journey approvals (prior auth, claim review, care plan sign-off) - task literally pauses and resumes on human signal. Observability dashboard shows person journey progress.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter could scaffold task() definitions from TypeSpec operation decorators for approval/notification workflows
candidates.risks: Adds another server-side dependency (Trigger.dev server + Redis) on top of Temporal if both are used. Consider as Temporal replacement rather than addition. Self-host ops burden similar to Temporal but lighter (2 pods vs 4). If CuraOS already commits to Temporal, Trigger.dev is redundant at the durable layer.

candidates.name: DBOS Transact TypeScript (dbos-inc/dbos-transact-ts)
candidates.what: Lightweight durable execution library that uses YOUR existing PostgreSQL as the workflow state store - no separate workflow server. Decorator-based (@DBOS.workflow, @DBOS.step, @DBOS.transaction). v3 adds decorator-free API. Fills: zero-ops durable execution alternative where adding a Temporal cluster is too heavy; fills saga/compensation pattern at service layer. Taxonomy: platform.workflow.durable-execution, platform.workflow.saga
candidates.maintenance_health: v3 preview (3.0.38-preview on npm June 2026), active development by DBOS Inc. (Stanford spinout). dbos-nest integration package exists. ~1.5k stars. Early but well-funded.
candidates.stack_fit: Integrates as a NestJS provider via dbos-nest. Uses Drizzle-compatible PostgreSQL (same CNPG cluster). Decorators align with NestJS DI pattern. Bun-compatible (pure TS, no native modules). No separate server to deploy. Checkpoints steps in Postgres tables co-located with application DB. Drizzle schema coexistence needs coordination on schema namespace.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party self-host only (library runs inside app process, Postgres is state store). No managed cloud offering needed. DBOS Cloud exists but optional.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Saga/compensation pattern maps well to multi-step person journeys (e.g. claim submission -> eligibility check -> auth -> submit -> ERA posting as durable steps that resume after crash).
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter can scaffold @DBOS.workflow class + @DBOS.step methods from TypeSpec operation sets
candidates.risks: v3 still preview-tagged; API surface in flux. No built-in human-task wait semantics (must implement with step + DB poll or external signal). Designed for process-level durable execution, not multi-tenant workflow orchestration at Temporal's scale. Schema coupling to app DB is a trade-off (not isolated).

candidates.name: Restate (@restatedev/restate-sdk + restate-nest)
candidates.what: Durable execution runtime: HTTP-native, virtual-object actors with built-in K/V store, exactly-once semantics, single Rust binary deployment. Integrates with XState as persistent event loop. Fills: lighter-weight alternative to Temporal for stateful per-entity workflows (per-patient state machines, per-claim saga). Taxonomy: platform.workflow.durable-execution, platform.workflow.actor-model
candidates.maintenance_health: ~3.5k stars, Rust-based server binary, active 2025-2026. TypeScript SDK well-maintained. restate-nest community NestJS adapter exists (subenksaha/restate-nest). Self-host: single Docker container or Helm chart.
candidates.stack_fit: TypeScript SDK (@restatedev/restate-sdk) is pure TS, Bun-compatible. NestJS adapter via restate-nest (decorator-based). Works as HTTP handler - Restate server calls YOUR NestJS endpoints with durability guarantees. No Node worker_threads dependency - avoids Bun blocker. Drizzle/Postgres can be used inside steps normally. Restate server adds one pod.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: single Restate binary / Docker / Helm - fully self-hostable. BYO-3rd-party: Restate Cloud (managed). BUSL means cannot resell Restate server as a managed service, but using it for CuraOS own workflows is fine.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Virtual objects map naturally to per-person stateful journeys (one actor per patient/claim/order). Built-in K/V avoids polling patterns. XState + Restate combo gives BPMN-grade state modeling with Restate durability.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter could scaffold VirtualObject class + handler methods from TypeSpec service definitions
candidates.risks: BUSL server license requires legal review for self-hosted product distribution (air-gap/on-prem deployments count as use not redistribution, so likely fine, but needs confirmation). Smaller ecosystem than Temporal. restate-nest adapter is community-maintained, low stars. Human-task wait requires custom signal endpoint implementation.

candidates.name: pg-boss (timgit/pg-boss)
candidates.what: PostgreSQL-native job queue with SKIP LOCKED exactly-once delivery, dead-letter queues, scheduled/delayed jobs, ACID guarantees. No Redis. Fills: background task processing (claim submission batch, SLA check jobs, billing cycle runs, notification dispatch) without adding Redis to the stack. Taxonomy: platform.workflow.background-jobs, platform.workflow.scheduled-jobs
candidates.maintenance_health: ~3.1k stars, v10 released 2025 (dead-letter queues, SKIP LOCKED, removed legacy onComplete). Single maintainer (active). Multiple NestJS adapters: wavezync/nestjs-pgboss (active), apricote/nest-pg-boss, @nestjs-enhanced/pg-boss.
candidates.stack_fit: Pure PostgreSQL - uses existing CNPG cluster, no extra infra. Drizzle-compatible (both use pg driver). NestJS adapters available. Bun-compatible (pure JS/TS, no native modules). 100-200 jobs/sec throughput ceiling vs BullMQ (thousands/sec) - fine for healthcare workflow volumes. Human-task pattern: pg-workflows extension adds step.waitFor() semantics on top of pg-boss.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party self-host only (runs inside your Postgres). No external provider option - this IS the queue.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Scheduled jobs drive proactive patient outreach (appointment reminders, medication refill nudges, care-gap alerts). No Redis ops burden simplifies self-hosted deployments.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter can scaffold pg-boss queue handler registrations from TypeSpec async operation definitions
candidates.risks: Single maintainer (bus factor). 100-200 jobs/sec ceiling limits high-volume use (acceptable for healthcare workflow but not high-frequency billing batches at scale). Not a full durable execution engine - no workflow history, no replay, no signals. For complex multi-step workflows, use alongside Temporal not instead.

candidates.name: paed01/bpmn-engine
candidates.what: Pure JavaScript/Node BPMN 2.0 execution engine (no Java, no server). Runs BPMN XML process definitions, supports UserTask (human-in-the-loop), ServiceTask, Gateway, Timer events, boundary events, signal events. Fills: BPMN standard process definition execution for builder-core-service visual workflow authoring; healthstack-workflow-service clinical pathway execution. Taxonomy: platform.workflow.bpmn-execution, platform.workflow.visual-authoring
candidates.maintenance_health: v25.0.1 released mid-2025 (very active), sole maintainer (paed01) consistently shipping. Companion packages: bpmn-elements (the low-level BPMN element library), bpmn-moddle (XML serialization). Compatible with bpmn.io BPMN JS designer for frontend visual authoring.
candidates.stack_fit: Pure JS, Bun-compatible. No native modules. Embeds directly in NestJS service as a module. State persistence is caller-managed (inject custom state store - Drizzle/Postgres fits naturally). TypeScript types available. Pairs with bpmn-js (frontend designer) for the builder-core-service no-code authoring surface. UserTask suspends execution and exposes signal() / resume() API matching Temporal signal pattern.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party only - library runs in-process. No managed cloud option. This covers the BPMN-standard definition language; Temporal handles durability of execution underneath.
candidates.person_centric_fit: BPMN UserTask maps naturally to patient consent flows, clinical approval steps, prior-auth sequences. Visual BPMN diagrams are auditable process evidence (HIPAA/compliance value). Builder-core authoring surface lets non-developers define care pathways.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter-target: codegen can generate BPMN XML skeletons from TypeSpec workflow definitions, enabling contract-first BPMN authoring
candidates.risks: No built-in distributed persistence or replay (must wrap with Temporal or pg-boss for durability). Single maintainer. Not a server - purely embedded library. bpmn-engine-ts TypeScript wrapper is abandoned (5yr old). Must use raw JS API with JSDoc or write own .d.ts wrapper.

candidates.name: XState v5 (statelyai/xstate)
candidates.what: Actor-model state machine / statechart library. Models per-entity finite state with guards, actions, parallel states, invoked services. Fills: per-encounter / per-claim / per-order state machine inside individual services (not a workflow orchestrator - complements Temporal). Taxonomy: platform.workflow.state-machine, platform.workflow.per-entity-state
candidates.maintenance_health: ~27k stars, Stately.ai backed, v5 stable since Dec 2023, active 2025-2026. First-party persistence via getSnapshot()/restore(). Pairs with Restate as external event loop + state store (documented pattern).
candidates.stack_fit: Pure TypeScript, Bun-compatible, no native dependencies. NestJS: inject actor as a provider, persist snapshot to Drizzle/Postgres on each transition. Deep-persistence snapshot format changed in v5 (migration concern for existing actors). Stately visual editor produces XState machine definitions (visual authoring path for simple state machines).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party only (library in-process). No managed cloud requirement.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Models patient/encounter lifecycle states (admitted, in-treatment, discharged) + order states (pending, approved, dispensed) as explicit finite automata - makes person journey state visible and auditable. Guards prevent invalid state transitions (safety).
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter can generate XState machine definitions from TypeSpec state enum + transition decorators
candidates.risks: Not a durable execution engine alone - snapshot must be persisted explicitly after each event (or pair with Restate for durability). v5 snapshot format breaks v4 persisted actors (migration needed for any existing actors). Overkill for simple linear flows; powerful for complex clinical state machines (maternity care journey, claim lifecycle).

candidates.name: Inngest (inngest/inngest)
candidates.what: Event-driven durable step function platform: fan-out, parallel steps, retries, scheduled crons, Postgres or SQLite state. SSPL license (server) with DOSP to Apache-2.0 after 3 years. Fills: event-triggered background workflow alternative if Temporal ops burden is too high. Taxonomy: platform.workflow.event-driven, platform.workflow.background-jobs
candidates.maintenance_health: Active 2025-2026, self-host v1.0+ with Postgres (Jan 2025), Helm chart available, MCP support added 2025-2026. Inngest Cloud available. ~10k stars.
candidates.stack_fit: TypeScript SDK npm-importable. Inngest server is a binary/Docker image. Self-host with --postgres-uri (v1.4.0+). NestJS integration via direct SDK usage. Bun-compatible SDK. SSPL on server binary means using it for internal CuraOS workflows is fine; offering it as a hosted SaaS to customers without source disclosure would trigger SSPL.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Docker/Helm + Postgres (SSPL, legal review needed for on-prem product distribution). BYO-3rd-party: Inngest Cloud. Hybrid viable.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Event-triggered workflows fire on domain events (patient.admitted, claim.submitted) and fan out to parallel steps - natural fit for care coordination automation.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter scaffolds inngest.createFunction() from AsyncAPI event definitions
candidates.risks: SSPL server license is the primary blocker for CuraOS air-gap / on-prem product distribution (customers running CuraOS would need Inngest source disclosed under SSPL if they operate it as a service - gray area). Given CuraOS is self-hosted-first and on-prem, this license risk is non-trivial. Trigger.dev (Apache-2.0) is a cleaner alternative. Reject for air-gap/on-prem track; use only for cloud SaaS internal deployment if chosen.

candidates.name: BullMQ (@nestjs/bull + bullmq)
candidates.what: Redis-backed job queue with priorities, delayed jobs, rate limiting, repeatable jobs, flows/dependencies, Bull Board UI. NestJS first-class via @nestjs/bull / @nestjs/bullmq. Fills: high-throughput background job processing where pg-boss hits throughput ceiling. Taxonomy: platform.workflow.background-jobs, platform.workflow.high-throughput-queue
candidates.maintenance_health: ~16k stars (bullmq), Taskforce.sh backed, actively maintained 2025-2026. @nestjs/bull official NestJS package. Bull Board UI for observability. Redis required.
candidates.stack_fit: First-class NestJS support (@nestjs/bullmq). Bun-compatible. Requires Redis (adds infra if not already present). Flows feature supports parent/child job dependencies (lightweight saga). Drizzle/Postgres used for application data; Redis purely for queue state. CuraOS already uses PostgreSQL-first strategy - adds Redis dependency.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: BullMQ library + self-hosted Redis. BYO-3rd-party: Redis Cloud / AWS ElastiCache / Upstash. Both standard.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Rate-limited notification queues prevent alert fatigue for patients. Repeatable jobs drive medication adherence reminders and care-gap outreach on schedule.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: codegen emitter scaffolds @Processor() + @Process() handlers from TypeSpec async operation definitions
candidates.risks: Adds Redis operational dependency (CuraOS prefers PostgreSQL-only stack where possible - pg-boss covers same use case without Redis). For healthcare workflow volumes, pg-boss throughput is sufficient. BullMQ preferred only if throughput profiler says pg-boss is a ceiling. Ponytail: don't add Redis until pg-boss is proven insufficient.

verified.name: Temporal TypeScript SDK + nestjs-temporal-core
verified.license_confirmed: CONFIRMED safe-permissive. Temporal server: MIT (github.com/temporalio/temporal/blob/main/LICENSE, verified). TypeScript SDK: MIT. nestjs-temporal-core: MIT. No BSL switch found. Air-gap distribution: no restriction. Local-vs-3rdparty duality: self-hosted Helm + Temporal Cloud both viable under MIT. Clean.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: PARTIAL MATCH. SDK: v1.18.0 is npm latest (candidate claims v1.17.2 installed - confirmed in node_modules but one minor behind). Temporal Inc. backed, active. nestjs-temporal-core: CLAIMED 3.2.8 - web sources show npm latest is 3.2.6; however local node_modules cache confirms 3.2.8 exists (likely unpublished git ref or very recent push not yet indexed). Active, solo maintainer (harsh-simform). PEER DEP MISMATCH: nestjs-temporal-core 3.2.8 peerDependencies cap at @temporalio/* ^1.12.0 || ^1.13.0; installed SDK is 1.17.2 - outside declared range. Runtime impact is low for NestJS DI modules but is an unacknowledged gap. No critical CVEs found; Temporal is now a CNA.
verified.integration_reality: OPTIMISTIC AS STATED. Three real gaps not fully disclosed: (1) BUN WORKER INCOMPATIBILITY IS UNMITIGATED IN CURRENT CODE. Worker.create() with workflowsPath uses Node worker_threads + vm + webpack bundle; Bun issue #6707 (node:v8 createHook) is open and unresolved as of June 2026. Current codebase runs TemporalWorkerService as an in-process NestJS provider with zero subprocess isolation - the service cannot start under Bun as implemented today. The Node-subprocess sidecar workaround is real and viable but NOT YET IMPLEMENTED. (2) PEER DEP GAP: nestjs-temporal-core caps at ^1.13.0; installed SDK is 1.17.2. Works at runtime but is outside the declared support window - any breaking SDK API change in 1.14-1.17 range is unguarded by the library's test matrix. (3) TYPESPEC CODEGEN IS ASPIRATIONAL: no upstream TypeSpec emitter for Temporal exists. Scaffolding workflow stubs from .tsp contracts is a custom codegen target - real work, not a plug-in. Drizzle/Zod/NestJS client path: no issue. Air-gap/Zarf: standard OCI images, no blocker. Elasticsearch for workflow visibility: optional but adds a net-new dependency if full-text search needed; PostgreSQL visibility (already in stack) covers basic use.
verified.simpler_alternative: NO simpler rung covers the full requirement. BullMQ handles queues but not deterministic replay, saga versioning, SLA breach timers with resume, or human-in-loop await without polling. No stdlib or already-installed dep reaches durable execution. Temporal is the correct tool class. Ponytail ladder stops here: build the Node sidecar, not a from-scratch alternative.
verified.notes: Downgrade from 'adopt' to 'confirmed-trial' because the Bun worker gap is a BLOCKING runtime defect in the current integration - the service as wired cannot start under Bun. Conditions for promotion to 'confirmed-adopt': (A) worker extracted to a separate Node.js container/subprocess (sidecar pattern), client path stays in Bun NestJS; (B) nestjs-temporal-core peer dep range verified against SDK 1.17.x API surface or pinned to a tested revision; (C) Elasticsearch dependency decision made (PostgreSQL visibility vs ES). The lane claim 'M5 complete' is aspirational until Bun worker sidecar is running in CI. Operational complexity of 4-component Temporal cluster + DB is real but manageable with CNPG already in stack. No lock-in concern: MIT license, standard gRPC API, portable. TypeSpec codegen target is valid but must be tracked as a planned generator addition, not a current capability.

verified.name: Trigger.dev v3/v4
verified.license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 confirmed - no SSPL/EL tier split. Self-host (Docker Compose or Helm) has full feature parity with cloud, no feature gates. Container image published at ghcr.io/triggerdotdev/trigger.dev. Air-gap is mechanically feasible via Zarf (image + Helm chart bundle), but Trigger.dev publishes no official Zarf package - CuraOS would own that wrapping. One caveat: the candidate claims v3 but the product is now v4 (GA June 2025, latest stable v4.4.6 published 2026-05-12, v4.5.0-rc.7 as of 2026-06-17). The v3 characterization in the candidate is stale; v4 is the live target.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active but with notable open bugs. Latest stable: v4.4.6 (2026-05-12). Active RC series: v4.5.0-rc.7 (2026-06-17). YC-backed, shipping weekly. No CVEs found. Three open issues relevant to CuraOS: (1) Issue #2805 OPEN - Zod v4 runtime incompatibility in @trigger.dev/core (zod-validation-error@1.5.0 accesses Zod v3 internals; breaks in Docker/Bun production even though peer dep claims v4 support); (2) Issue #3989 OPEN - batchTrigger drops per-item idempotencyKey on rate-limited retries in self-hosted v4.4.6 (critical for CuraOS approval fan-out workflows); (3) Issue #4046 OPEN - LRU catalog silently drops idempotency key metadata for >1000 keys per run. Issues 3989 + 4046 are filed by the same power user (brentshulman-silkline) against self-hosted v4.4.6, suggesting self-hosted at scale has meaningful rough edges not yet present in cloud. v4.5.0 RC series may address these - not confirmed.
verified.integration_reality: Harder than claimed on two dimensions. (1) NestJS SDK: the candidate cites @trigger.dev/nestjs but that package is v2-only, reached EOL 2025-01-31. v3/v4 has NO dedicated NestJS module. Integration is bare-SDK: tasks are defined in TypeScript files, a separate trigger worker process runs alongside NestJS (not embedded in DI container), and NestJS services trigger tasks via the SDK client. This is a sidecar pattern, not a module - meaning no NestJS lifecycle hooks, no DI injection into task bodies without manual wiring. Worker process is isolated - it cannot import NestJS providers directly. (2) Zod v4 conflict: CuraOS rule curaos_validation_rule.md mandates Zod 4 as the default across all services. Issue #2805 (OPEN) documents a runtime crash in @trigger.dev/core when the host project uses Zod v4, reproducible in Docker/Bun production. This is a blocker for any service that already imports Zod v4. Bun runtime: Trigger.dev supports Bun natively (tasks can run on Bun 1.3.x worker), confirmed in changelog. OTEL instrumentation caveat: Bun does not support Node register hook, so some auto-instrumentation libraries will not work - manual span creation required. TypeSpec/codegen: no native TypeSpec emitter exists; generator-first integration would require CuraOS to write a custom emitter that scaffolds task() definitions from TypeSpec operations - viable but non-trivial owner work. Infrastructure footprint self-hosted: PostgreSQL + Redis + object storage (built-in on Helm chart) + Trigger.dev webapp pod + worker pods. This is lighter than Temporal (2-3 pods vs 4+) but adds Redis to CuraOS infra if not already present. ClickHouse is also bundled in the Helm chart for run history/analytics.
verified.simpler_alternative: BullMQ (already npm-ecosystem, Redis-backed, Bun-compatible) covers the background-job lane with zero new server infrastructure if CuraOS already runs Redis. For human-in-the-loop approval specifically: a Waitpoints analog can be implemented with a BullMQ job that parks in a delayed/waiting state keyed on an approval token stored in PostgreSQL - roughly 50-100 lines, no new server pod. This covers prior-auth and claim-review patterns adequately. The ponytail ladder says: stop here unless the observability dashboard, sub-100ms warm starts, or the managed worker scaling are concretely needed now. Temporal (if already in CuraOS ADR) covers the durable-workflow lane with stronger audit guarantees (event-sourcing vs checkpoint-resume) - Trigger.dev should replace Temporal rather than coexist with it, making this an either/or architectural decision, not an additive one.
verified.notes: Verdict rationale: downgrade from recommended trial to confirmed-trial with conditions. The platform is genuinely production-ready for the cloud/managed case and the Waitpoints feature is architecturally correct for patient-journey approval workflows (prior auth, care-plan sign-off). However three conditions must be met before CuraOS commits: (1) Zod v4 conflict (issue #2805) must be resolved in a stable release - track v4.5.0 GA; do NOT trial on v4.4.6 if any service in scope uses Zod 4 at runtime. (2) Self-hosted idempotency bugs (3989, 4046) must be resolved before using batchTrigger at scale in approval fan-outs. (3) The NestJS integration gap is real - plan for a thin adapter module (CuraOS-owned, ~100 LOC) that wraps the SDK client in a NestJS provider and exports DI-injectable trigger handles; do not assume @trigger.dev/nestjs covers this. Air-gap: Zarf package would need to be authored by CuraOS; budget 1-2 days. If CuraOS has no existing Temporal ADR and the workflow-bpm-engine lane is greenfield, Trigger.dev v4 is the simpler self-hosted option. If Temporal is already committed, do not add Trigger.dev - it is redundant at the durable layer. Trial scope: one non-critical background service (e.g., notification delivery or report generation) running on v4.5.0-stable when released, with Bun worker, self-hosted Helm, before committing the approval-workflow lane.

verified.name: DBOS Transact TypeScript (dbos-inc/dbos-transact-ts)
verified.license_confirmed: PARTIAL - SPLIT LICENSE. @dbos-inc/dbos-sdk library = MIT (confirmed). DBOS Conductor (production ops control plane, multi-executor, observability UI) = proprietary license, requires paid key for commercial/production use. Free key limited to single executor. Air-gap self-hosting of Conductor is possible but commercial license required. Candidate did not disclose this split. For CuraOS self-hosted-first + air-gap mandate, Conductor dependence introduces a license ceiling that must be contracted before adoption.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: ACTIVE but candidate version is stale. Candidate claims v3.0.38-preview as of June 2026. Actual: v4.0 stable shipped (latest npm 4.18.10, published ~June 12 2026, repo updated June 26 2026). v4.0 removed DBOS.transaction decorator AND the application database concept entirely - both were central to the integration model described in the candidate. API surface changed substantially across v3->v4. No CVEs found. Stars ~1.5k. Well-funded Stanford spinout. Active cadence confirmed but churn rate is high.
verified.integration_reality: HARDER THAN CLAIMED on three axes. (1) v4.0 removed the DBOS.transaction decorator and application-database integration that the candidate describes - Drizzle integration now requires the datasource-plugin pattern, not the decorator that candidate mentions. Codegen target would track a moving API. (2) CuraOS runs CNPG + Citus multi-tenant (per-tenant DBs). DBOS injects a dbos schema (system tables: workflow_status, operation_outputs, etc.) into the application Postgres. On Citus, system-schema tables cannot be distributed - they stay on coordinator. Per-tenant DB model means one dbos schema per tenant DB, which has not been validated against Citus sharding. (3) Bun compatibility: DBOS relies on reflect-metadata for its decorator system; Bun's module resolution has known edge cases with reflect-metadata. No explicit Bun support documented by DBOS team. dbos-nest integration package exists but is pinned to older decorator model that v4 removed.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - ponytail ladder yields two better-fit options already in stack. (1) Outbox + Kafka/Redpanda + idempotency keys: already installed, covers saga/compensation at service layer without a new library dependency. Sufficient for most multi-step journeys. Ceiling: no step-level replay on crash; requires consumer idempotency discipline. (2) Temporal self-hosted: already named in curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule as the canonical Workflow Execution local option. Provides human-task signals, per-namespace tenant isolation, battle-hardened multi-tenant primitives, and a 3rd-party provider path (Temporal Cloud) via config - exactly what the local-vs-3rdparty rule requires. DBOS fills a niche between these two but does not satisfy the provider-abstraction duality the rule demands.
verified.notes: Three blocking issues, not risks: (1) local-vs-3rdparty rule violation: DBOS is baked into application code via decorators; it cannot be swapped for Temporal or another provider via runtime config. The rule requires every workflow area to expose a provider abstraction with a local option and a BYO-3rdparty option. DBOS as a library is structurally incompatible with this requirement. (2) Conductor license ceiling: production multi-executor use requires a commercial license. CuraOS air-gap + self-hosted-first mandate cannot depend on a vendor-licensed ops plane. (3) Citus schema interaction unvalidated: DBOS system tables in per-tenant Postgres databases on a Citus cluster is an untested combination with non-obvious failure modes. Non-blocking but worth monitoring: Bun reflect-metadata edge cases; v4 API churn (high for a codegen target). Recommended disposition: file as reference artifact in ai/research/ for the durable-execution comparison. Re-evaluate if: (a) DBOS Conductor goes fully OSS, AND (b) team validates Bun + Citus + DBOS schema coexistence on a spike branch, AND (c) Temporal is ruled out as too heavy for a specific service tier. Do not block the workflow-bpm-engine lane on this evaluation.

verified.name: Restate (@restatedev/restate-sdk + restate-nest)
verified.license_confirmed: PARTIALLY confirmed, UNRESOLVED for CuraOS air-gap use case. BUSL-1.1 is confirmed on the server binary; TypeScript SDK is MIT (confirmed). The critical blocker: BUSL's default production-use prohibition applies unless an "Additional Use Grant" explicitly carves it out. Restate's LICENSE Additional Use Grant text could not be verified from external sources - GitHub raw fetch was unavailable and no third party reproduced the verbatim grant text. BUSL implementations vary wildly: HashiCorp's BUSL blocks competitive redistribution; others grant broad internal use. For CuraOS, the air-gap/on-prem distribution model (shipping Restate server binary bundled in a Zarf/Helm package to customer infra) is structurally similar to redistribution. This is NOT resolved as "fine" - it requires direct legal review of the exact Additional Use Grant text before any adoption. The candidate's claim "likely fine, but needs confirmation" is optimistic without reading the grant. The candidate's own label "copyleft-service-or-port" does not accurately describe BUSL - BUSL is not copyleft, it is source-available with production-use restriction. The mislabel is a yellow flag on the provenance of the analysis.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. Restate server reached v1.6.x in April 2026 (v1.6.6 on 2026-04-24 per examples repo). v1.6.1 addressed a critical cluster stability bug (RocksDB memory rebalancing blocking async runtime, causing false leadership loss). v1.6.2 patched RUSTSEC-2026-0009 (DoS in time crate) - meaning they are tracking and responding to security advisories promptly. TypeScript SDK actively maintained with official Bun support (dedicated bun/ example directory in sdk-typescript, listed alongside node/deno/lambda). restate-nest community adapter last updated June 2025 (one year stale relative to today, unknown NestJS 11 compatibility, low stars, single maintainer) - this is NOT maintained at the same cadence.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is decisive. CuraOS `workflow-core-service` already ships @temporalio/activity + @temporalio/client + @temporalio/worker + @temporalio/workflow at v1.17.2 plus nestjs-temporal-core 3.2.8. Temporal IS the installed durable-execution engine. Ponytail ladder rung 2 fires: the capability already exists in this codebase. Adding Restate adds a second durable-execution runtime with overlapping semantics, split ownership of actor/saga state, two sets of operational concerns, and a BUSL-licensed binary to every air-gap bundle. That is the opposite of ponytail. The Temporal TypeScript SDK does NOT run on Bun (issue #1334 is open, worker_threads dependency), which is the claimed advantage of Restate. However: CuraOS workflow workers can run under Node while the NestJS services run under Bun - the two runtimes are already separated at the service boundary. This is not a new problem requiring a new tool.
verified.integration_reality: Harder than claimed. (1) Bun compat: Restate SDK officially supports Bun (confirmed, dedicated bun/ example). No worker_threads dependency - this claim holds. BUT the NestJS integration path relies on restate-nest, a community adapter last touched June 2025, unknown NestJS 11 compat, single maintainer, low stars. First-party Restate has no official NestJS module. (2) Human tasks: awakeables work (confirmed via docs) but require building a custom signal endpoint - it is NOT turnkey. Temporal Signals/Updates/Queries are already wired via nestjs-temporal-core. (3) TypeSpec codegen: no TypeSpec-to-Restate emitter exists; the generator-first claim is aspirational. A codegen target would need to be built from scratch. (4) Drizzle/Postgres inside steps: works normally (confirmed pattern), no integration friction. (5) Virtual-objects-as-actors: genuinely elegant for per-patient state, but Temporal Workflows already provide per-entity isolation via workflow ID namespacing - same pattern, already operational.
