verified.notes: Research value: high - decisive prior art and a codebase blocker found. Key findings: (1) PONYTAIL LADDER FIRES AT RUNG 2: Temporal 1.17.2 is already installed in workflow-core-service. A second durable-execution runtime is YAGNI until Temporal is proven insufficient for the specific use case. (2) BUSL on air-gap is an unresolved legal question, not a confirmed green. Distributing the Restate server binary inside a Zarf package to customer infra requires explicit legal sign-off on the Additional Use Grant text - which was not obtainable during research. (3) restate-nest adapter is community-only, June 2025 last commit, likely NestJS 11 untested. Any NestJS integration path would need either: the community adapter (maintenance risk), wrapping Restate as a plain HTTP handler without NestJS decorator sugar (loses the pitch), or building a first-party adapter (significant effort). (4) The virtual-object actor model IS genuinely superior to Temporal for pure per-entity K/V-backed state machines with no cross-entity coordination. If a future use case emerges where Temporal's event-history model is the bottleneck for per-patient actors at scale, revisit Restate then - with the license resolved and a first-party NestJS adapter available. (5) Kafka/Redpanda is already the CuraOS event backbone; Restate's built-in K/V store duplicates a persistence concern already owned by CNPG Postgres + Drizzle. Verdict: reject for now. File a future consideration issue gated on: (a) Temporal proven insufficient for a specific actor-model need, (b) BUSL Additional Use Grant confirmed in writing for air-gap distribution, (c) official NestJS adapter exists.

candidates.name: Activepieces (piece SDK + connector pattern)

candidates.license: MIT (core + piece SDK); Commercial (ee/ embed builder)

candidates.risks: EE embed builder is iframe + commercial license - do not use it in the product build. Community piece quality varies; need CuraOS connector-quality gate. Piece SDK API may evolve; pin semver.

candidates.name: Mastra (@mastra/core + @mastra/nestjs + @mastra/mcp)
candidates.what: Apache 2.0 TypeScript AI orchestration framework with graph-based workflow engine (.then/.branch/.parallel), agents + tool calling, MCPClient/MCPServer, memory, RAG, and a first-class NestJS adapter that exposes agents/workflows/MCPs as NestJS endpoints with native DI/guards/interceptors. Fills taxonomy_ids: workflow-automation.catalog.ai-llm-steps (fully absent locally - zero mcp/llm/aiagent matches in automation+workflow+plugin services) and workflow-automation.catalog.mcp-tooling-integration (also absent).
candidates.maintenance_health: ~22k GitHub stars, active June 2026 releases, @mastra/nestjs and @mastra/mcp published to npm, backed by a VC-funded team
candidates.stack_fit: Bun-compatible (deploys to any Node/Bun environment). @mastra/nestjs is a drop-in NestJS module. BYO LLM provider via @ai-sdk/* packages pointing at LiteLLM (workspace-standard). Zod-based tool schemas. No wrapper needed.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Both: self-host Mastra on-prem (no external service required); BYO model provider via LiteLLM gateway for any 3rd-party LLM. MCPClient consumes external MCP servers; MCPServer exposes CuraOS flows to external agents.
candidates.person_centric_fit: AI step framed as consent-gated assistant; person sees intent + plain results; management surface = model config, tool bindings, guardrails, Langfuse observability.

candidates.risks: Enterprise features (advanced memory, dedicated support) are source-available not OSS. Fast-moving API - pin @mastra/core to semver range. Requires Presidio PHI egress gate on LLM inputs (workspace rule already mandates this). LiteLLM proxy must be deployed before AI steps work in self-host mode.

candidates.name: Vercel AI SDK (ai npm package)
candidates.what: Apache 2.0 TypeScript LLM provider facade with generateText/streamText/tool-call/structured-output APIs, MCP tool support, and explicit NestJS examples. Fills the BYO-model requirement for workflow-automation.catalog.ai-llm-steps when paired with LiteLLM for self-hosted model routing.
candidates.maintenance_health: 20M+ monthly npm downloads, AI SDK 7 released mid-2026, Vercel-backed, extensive provider ecosystem (@ai-sdk/openai, @ai-sdk/anthropic, etc.)
candidates.stack_fit: Zero-friction NestJS integration (official cookbook example). Works in Bun. MCP tool interface aligned with @modelcontextprotocol/sdk. Mastra uses ai SDK internally - so adopting Mastra implicitly adopts this. Direct import also valid for lighter use-cases (single generateText call in a service).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: BYO-3rd-party natively (100+ providers); self-host by routing providers through LiteLLM proxy (workspace-standard gateway). No Vercel platform dependency.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables plain-language AI responses on person-facing surfaces; streaming to React frontend via NestJS SSE; tool calls drive automation steps.

candidates.risks: API surface changed significantly between SDK 5, 6, and 7 - pin major version. Streaming requires SSE or WebSocket setup in NestJS (doable, documented). Vercel AI Gateway is optional cloud upsell - route through LiteLLM instead to stay self-hosted.

candidates.name: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk (MCP TypeScript SDK)
candidates.what: MIT TypeScript SDK (Anthropic-owned) for building MCP servers and clients: McpServer, StdioServerTransport, Streamable HTTP transport, tools/resources/prompts primitives. Fills taxonomy_id: workflow-automation.catalog.mcp-tooling-integration - the primitive for making CuraOS connectors and flows MCP-addressable and consuming external MCP servers as automation steps.

candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables AI agents (internal and external) to invoke CuraOS workflows on behalf of a person; exposes person-consented tools to agent ecosystem.

candidates.risks: v2 spec still in flux until July 2026 stable - breaking changes possible. NestJS adapter must be hand-written (small). Adopt via Mastra abstraction where possible to isolate from spec churn.

candidates.name: Trigger.dev v4 (@trigger.dev/sdk + @trigger.dev/nestjs)
candidates.what: Apache 2.0 durable task execution platform: retried long-running jobs, schedules, event-triggered tasks, real-time observability dashboard, self-host via Docker/K8s. Fills the execution durability gap for automation-core-service scheduled triggers and plugin-runtime-service async jobs - both confirmed stubs. @trigger.dev/nestjs is the official NestJS module.

If BullMQ already handles recurrence cadence adequately for simple schedules, full Trigger.dev adoption may be overkill for those cases - use for multi-step durable flows only.

candidates.name: Infisical (@infisical/sdk)
candidates.what: MIT open-source secrets management platform with NestJS integration guide, @infisical/sdk npm package, and nestjs-infisical-sdk community module. Fills the connector OAuth vault secrets gap in integrations-core-service: securely stores and injects API keys, OAuth tokens, and credentials for connector auth strategies at runtime rather than baking them into env files.

Community NestJS module (nestjs-infisical-sdk) is not Infisical-official; prefer the official @infisical/sdk with a thin NestJS provider wrapper.

verified.name: Activepieces (piece SDK + connector pattern)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT for @activepieces/pieces-framework and all code outside packages/ee/. EE commercial boundary is code-path enforced (packages/ee/ dir), not a runtime license check. Safe for self-hosted + air-gap distribution when importing SDK only. Community Edition (MIT) can be used commercially without restrictions. Confirmed via github.com/activepieces/activepieces/blob/main/LICENSE and activepieces.com/docs/about/license.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. v0.85.4 released June 17 2026, active commits as of June 20 2026. v0.84.0 (May 2026) fixed the SSRF CVE-2026-12813. 22k+ stars, 270+ contributors, YC-backed. npm package @activepieces/pieces-framework v0.26.0 published 17 days ago (~1400 downstream dependents). Rapid release cadence (~weekly). Risk: cadence means piece SDK shape could drift - requires Renovate auto-PR + pinned semver per [[curaos-version-pinning-rule]].
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is already built. integrations-core-service/src/connectors/connector-framework.ts exists in main, defines ConnectorRegistry + Connector descriptor, and its own docstring says "The host app registers real Activepieces pieces against this registry at boot." The static connector-catalogue.fixture.ts (4 entries) is the acknowledged gap placeholder. The abstraction layer does NOT need to be rebuilt; it needs to be populated. Ponytail ladder stops at rung 2 (already in this codebase). The trial verdict is: validate that @activepieces/pieces-framework pieces can be mapped into the existing ConnectorRegistry descriptor shape before committing to the full port.
verified.integration_reality: Harder than claimed on one axis, straightforward on others. (1) TypeSpec/Zod alignment claim is WRONG: the pieces-framework uses its own Property.* DSL (backed by TypeBox, not Zod). A bridge layer converting PiecePropertyMap -> Zod schema is required and non-trivial - this is the main integration effort. (2) Bun/NestJS fit is real: it is a pure TS npm package, no native bindings, imports cleanly under Bun. (3) The CVE-2026-12813 SSRF (CVSS 6.3 medium, fixed in 0.84.0) is in the Activepieces server ENGINE (packages/server/engine/src/lib/variables/processors/file.ts), NOT in @activepieces/pieces-framework. If CuraOS only imports the SDK descriptor types and does not embed the Activepieces execution engine, the CVE is irrelevant. (4) Air-gap for the full Activepieces engine is EE-commercial; for the SDK-only pattern (npm packages bundled via Zarf), MIT applies cleanly. (5) API stability: createPiece/PieceAuth shape is stable across 0.80-0.85; server-config breaking changes do not affect the SDK API.
verified.notes: Downgrade from adopt to confirmed-trial on three grounds: (A) Zod claim is overstated - TypeBox-to-Zod bridge is unproven effort; validate one piece end-to-end through the existing ConnectorRegistry before broad adoption. (B) The candidate proposes creating @curaos/connector-sdk as a port of createPiece shape - but connector-framework.ts already IS that abstraction; the real task is writing the adapter that maps an Activepieces piece object to a Connector descriptor, not a new SDK package. Scope is smaller than proposed. (C) Community piece quality gate is unresolved - 400+ pieces with no CuraOS quality gate means the catalogue claim inflates real usable count. Trial gate: implement ONE real piece (e.g. HTTP or Notify) through connector-framework.ts using @activepieces/pieces-framework@0.26.0, prove the TypeBox-to-Zod bridge, prove Bun import works, prove air-gap bundle via Zarf. If all three pass, full adopt is warranted. Do NOT create a parallel @curaos/connector-sdk package - extend connector-framework.ts per [[curaos-reuse-dry-rule]].

verified.name: Mastra (@mastra/core + @mastra/nestjs + @mastra/mcp)
verified.license_confirmed: Partially confirmed. Apache 2.0 core is clean for self-hosted + air-gap distribution. Enterprise features (RBAC, SSO, ACL, Agent Builder in prod) are source-available under Mastra Enterprise License - not open source - and require a commercial agreement for production use. EE boundary is the `ee/` directory tree. Core-only use is license-safe; any production use of auth/RBAC/Agent Builder is not.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active but compromised-reputation. Last release is clean (1.42.0 forward-rolled after June 17 2026 incident). However: a North Korean state actor (Sapphire Sleet) successfully backdoored 143 @mastra/* npm packages for 88 minutes on June 17 2026 via a stale contributor credential, deploying a cross-platform RAT across 1.1M weekly download exposure. Malicious versions unpublished and clean versions republished, but no public confirmation that the full scope access audit is complete. Zero CVEs assigned during active exploitation - standard scanners had no detection surface. The scope remains a high-value state-actor target.
verified.simpler_alternative: Yes. CuraOS already has Temporal in workflow-core-service. AI steps as Temporal Activities + direct @ai-sdk/* (Vercel AI SDK) packages wired through LiteLLM cover the stated gap without a new framework layer. @ai-sdk/* is Zod-native, BYO-provider, TypeSpec-contract-friendly, and has no supply chain incidents. This satisfies the ponytail ladder at rung 4 (already-installed-adjacent) rather than importing a new framework with known friction and a fresh state-actor targeting history.
verified.notes: Four compounding blockers beyond the supply chain incident: (1) @mastra/nestjs is Express-only in v1 - hard bootstrap failure on any other adapter, constraining CuraOS adapter flexibility forward. (2) Confirmed Bun+Turborepo monorepo breakage (issue #9993): mastra build/dev hangs and redefines Node globals in a Bun+Turbo environment - exactly CuraOS's topology. (3) API churn is real and architectural: storage backend, harness singleton, and tracing identification model all broke in the 1.0 wave with codemods required - each migration is a codegen-pipeline event that fights [[curaos-generator-evolution-rule]]. (4) Generator-first target described in the proposal (adding AiAgentStep to automation.tsp then regenerating @curaos/automation-sdk) is achievable with @ai-sdk/* directly without Mastra as the intermediary - Mastra adds a framework lock-in layer on top of the ai-sdk primitives it already wraps. REFERENCE status: revisit when (a) Bun+Turbo issue is closed, (b) scope access audit is publicly confirmed complete, (c) Fastify adapter lands, (d) API stabilizes past 1.x churn phase. The underlying @ai-sdk/* approach should be trialed independently.

verified.name: Vercel AI SDK (ai npm package)
verified.license_confirmed: Confirmed Apache 2.0 (github.com/vercel/ai/blob/main/LICENSE). Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution and BYO-provider duality. No patent retaliation clause issues. Clean.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. AI SDK 7 released June 25, 2026. Issues actively triaged (16k+ total, recent activity June 2026). Vercel-backed. April 2026 Vercel breach was an internal OAuth supply chain incident - confirmed by Vercel + GitHub + npm + Socket Security that no npm packages were compromised. The "ai" package itself is clean. One pre-v7 filetype-bypass vuln existed (fixed in 5.0.52/6.0.0-beta - no CVE in v7 lineage). No unresolved critical CVEs on current major.
verified.integration_reality: REAL FRICTION - not optimistic. Critical blocker discovered: AI SDK v7 is ESM-only ("type": "module" required, no CommonJS exports). The automation-core-service uses "type": "commonjs" (confirmed in package.json). Migrating a NestJS 11 service to ESM is non-trivial: all local imports need .js extensions, swc/tsc emit config changes, and test harness alignment. The NestJS cookbook example exists and works - but only in an ESM project. Dynamic await import() is the escape hatch for CommonJS callers but is awkward in a DI-heavy NestJS module graph. LiteLLM routing via @ai-sdk/openai-compatible (custom baseURL) is well-documented and confirmed to work - that part of the claim holds. Mastra does use ai SDK internally (confirmed), so if Mastra is the primary agent layer, direct ai SDK adoption in automation-core-service is a separate decision, not a free implicit one. Bun 1.1+ is compatible as a runtime with ESM packages - no Bun-specific issues.
verified.simpler_alternative: Ponytail check: the codebase has no existing LLM call layer (automation-core-service has zero ai/mastra deps). The actual use case is generateText/streamText calls inside AiAgentStep handlers. If Mastra is already planned as the agent runtime, Mastra wraps ai SDK internally - direct ai SDK import in automation-core-service may be redundant for the agentic path. For non-agentic LLM steps (single generateText), fetch() against LiteLLM's OpenAI-compatible endpoint with a Zod-validated response is a zero-dep option that avoids the ESM migration cost entirely. That is the ponytail ladder rung to test first before committing to ai SDK in CJS services. For the full streaming + tool-call + structured-output surface (the full value prop), fetch() is not a substitute - ai SDK earns its keep there.
verified.notes: Trial (not adopt) because of two real gates that must be resolved first: (1) ESM migration of automation-core-service (or scoping ai SDK to a new ESM-only package within the monorepo) must be planned as explicit work, not assumed free. (2) Mastra adoption decision should be settled first - if Mastra is the agent layer, the direct ai SDK surface in services shrinks to streaming SSE to frontend, which may justify a thin ESM wrapper package rather than migrating the whole service. Recommended path: create a new ESM package (@curaos/ai-llm-client) that wraps ai SDK and is imported by automation-core-service via dynamic import(), or migrate automation-core-service to ESM first. Pin at v7.x.x (major pin mandatory - three major breaking rewrites in 18 months confirms this risk is real). Do not import @ai-sdk/vercel gateway package - route all providers through self-hosted LiteLLM proxy to stay off Vercel's cloud upsell path.

verified.name: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk (MCP TypeScript SDK)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. No CLA, no copyleft. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution and local-vs-3rdparty duality. No issues here.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: MIXED. Active: 57k+ downstream packages, official Anthropic ownership, v1.29.0 current (published ~3 months ago per npm). CONCERN: last release cadence has slowed; v2 spec RC (2026-07-28) introduces breaking changes (stateless protocol, removes initialize handshake + Mcp-Session-Id). v1.x supported for min 12 months post-v2 GA. Formal deprecation policy now exists, which is positive. Open issue #2042: transitive deps (fast-uri, hono, express-rate-limit, ip-address) carry known vulns that break npm audit --audit-level=high. Workaround: package.json overrides exist but must be maintained manually.
verified.integration_reality: HARDER than claimed. Bun: confirmed compatible (runs on Node/Bun/Deno, bun add works). Zod: compatible at schema layer but NOT first-class - SDK uses its own JSON Schema primitives; Zod integration requires manual bridge or a codegen layer. TypeSpec: no native MCP emitter exists in official tooling yet; third-party OpenAPI-to-MCP generators (Kubb, Speakeasy, harsha-iiiv/openapi-mcp-generator) can bridge OpenAPI output from TypeSpec, but this is a multi-hop chain that must be built and maintained. NestJS 11: NO official adapter. Candidate claims "~50 LOC thin wrapper" but reality is 3+ community adapters of varying maturity: rekog-labs/MCP-Nest (most cited), bamada/nestjs-mcp, @nestjs-mcp/server (adrian-d-hidalgo). All are community-maintained, not official. None carry the stability guarantees needed for a generator-first codegen target - the codegen emitter would be coupling to a community wrapper, not Anthropic. This is the primary integration gap: the generator-first target (mcp-server entity emitter in tools/codegen) would need to emit code against one of these community NestJS adapters, meaning generator stability depends on an unmaintained third-party.
verified.simpler_alternative: Not applicable for the core use case (protocol interop with the MCP ecosystem is the point; you cannot substitute it). However, ponytail challenge: if the actual need is internal workflow automation addressability, a simpler REST/OpenAPI endpoint from NestJS already handles it with zero new deps and works today. MCP adoption is justified ONLY if external AI agents (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.) need to invoke CuraOS workflows - i.e., the protocol interop value is real. If the near-term use is internal-only, defer. If Mastra is adopted as the automation orchestrator, @mastra/mcp already wraps this SDK - direct adoption of the raw SDK is then redundant (YAGNI). Resolve the Mastra decision first.
verified.notes: THREE active security concerns that must be mitigated before production: (1) CVE-2026-25536 (CVSS High, cross-client data leak via StreamableHTTPServerTransport instance reuse; fixed in >=1.26.0 - pin to >=1.26.0, currently satisfied by 1.29.0). (2) Transitive dep vuln cluster (fast-uri/hono/express-rate-limit/ip-address in 1.29.0) - add package.json overrides per issue #2042; breaks npm audit CI until fixed upstream. (3) STDIO transport RCE design flaw (OX Security, April 2026, 30+ CVEs ecosystem-wide) - Anthropic declined protocol-level fix, responsibility is developer-side sanitization. CuraOS must never pass user-controlled input into the STDIO command field; codegen emitter must enforce this via allowlist. For air-gap: no runtime external calls, SDK works offline. For generator-first: TypeSpec -> OpenAPI -> MCP codegen chain requires building a custom emitter step; this is medium effort, not S. Verdict downgraded from adopt to trial: adopt only after (a) Mastra adoption decision is settled (if Mastra wins, consume via @mastra/mcp not directly), (b) a NestJS adapter is chosen and locked (recommend rekog-labs/MCP-Nest as most cited, but audit its own dep tree), (c) package.json overrides applied to clear audit, (d) STDIO path sandboxed in codegen emitter. Do NOT use as a v1 GA blocker dependency in the current zero-fault wave; file as post-v1 or v1.1.

candidates.name: rrule-temporal
candidates.what: RFC-5545 + RFC-7529 recurrence rule expansion returning Temporal.ZonedDateTime; backend recurrence engine for CuraOS calendar_event.rrule storage and slot expansion. Fills gap: recurring event expansion in calendar-core-service and scheduling-service (taxonomy: calendar.recurring-events). Direct successor to unmaintained rrule.js, Temporal-native, tz-correct by construction.
candidates.stack_fit: Drop-in NestJS import, no wrapper needed. Works with Bun runtime. Requires @js-temporal/polyfill on Node < Temporal-native; Node 22 may have partial native support. Pair with Luxon (already in calendar-core) for display formatting. Store RRULE strings in Drizzle text column, expand at read time.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves first-party self-host
candidates.maintenance_health: v1.4.5 (2024), active; created by rrule.js contributors as the explicit Temporal-era successor. ~low weekly downloads but fills a clear technical gap. rrule.js (the predecessor) has 1.7M downloads/week but last released 3 years ago - migration pressure builds.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Powers patient-facing 'my appointments' recurring view, clinician recurring schedule blocks, and hr leave accrual recurrence. Invisible infrastructure; surfaces as correct dates on person-app calendar.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg - @curaos/calendar-sdk (expand rrule strings in SDK layer so all consumers get tz-correct occurrences without re-implementing expansion)
candidates.risks: Temporal API polyfill adds ~60KB browser bundle cost; mitigate by keeping expansion server-side only. Low contributor count. If Temporal ships universally in Node 24+ the polyfill dep drops automatically.

candidates.name: rrule (jkbrzt/rrule)
candidates.what: De-facto JS recurrence rule library, RFC-5545 RRULE parsing and date expansion. Still widely used (1.7M weekly downloads) but last published 3 years ago (v2.8.1). Open issues accumulating since 2025 with no maintainer responses. Reference for migration path - existing codebases using it should plan migration to rrule-temporal.
candidates.stack_fit: Works with Bun/NestJS. No resource changes needed. But timezone handling has known bugs and Date-based (not Temporal). The CuraOS calendar-core schema already uses Luxon for IANA tz - rrule.js Date objects create an impedance mismatch.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves first-party self-host
candidates.maintenance_health: Effectively unmaintained. v2.8.1 last published 3 years ago. 3700 GitHub stars. Issues open since Nov/Dec 2025 unanswered. Drupal community has already flagged it as unmaintained.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Would serve same use cases as rrule-temporal but with tz bugs that affect cross-timezone patient scheduling.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg
candidates.risks: Unmaintained. Timezone correctness bugs well-documented. No RSCALE support. Will not receive security patches.

candidates.name: react-big-calendar
candidates.what: GCal/Outlook-style React calendar component: month/week/day/agenda views, drag-drop, multi-date-library adapters, events management. Fills the CalendarBoard/SchedulingBoard UI archetype gap in @curaos/ui (taxonomy: ui-kit-archetypes). Person-facing calendar views (patient-app my-appointments, clinician daily schedule) and management calendar (scheduling admin, CRM activity calendar).
candidates.stack_fit: React-native, no wrapper needed. TypeScript types via @types/react-big-calendar (v1.16.3). Adapts to date-fns or Luxon (already installed). Drop into @curaos/ui as the CalendarBoard primitive - generators can emit the CalendarBoard archetype wired to calendar-sdk hooks. No resource view (multi-provider/room) natively.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves first-party self-host
candidates.maintenance_health: v1.20.0 published 24 days ago. 8500+ stars, ~500k weekly downloads. Actively maintained by jquense. MIT fully open.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Patient-app: my-appointments calendar, clinician-app: daily/weekly schedule. CRM: activity timeline calendar view. Personal-calendar app: full calendar view. Strong person-centric fit for all journey surfaces.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit (@curaos/ui CalendarBoard component wrapping react-big-calendar, wired to calendar-sdk event feed; ui-app-emit emits CalendarBoard archetype for calendar-connected entities)
candidates.risks: No built-in multi-resource (provider/room) view - requires FullCalendar premium or Schedule-X premium for that specific archetype. For basic event calendar this is fully sufficient.

candidates.name: FullCalendar (standard, MIT plugins)
candidates.what: Mature multi-framework calendar: month/week/day/agenda/timegrid views, drag-drop, recurring events via rrule plugin, TS support. Standard plugins are MIT. Premium plugins (Timeline/Resource) are commercial ($480/dev). For CuraOS: standard tier covers CalendarBoard archetype and most scheduling views. Resource timeline (multi-provider, multi-room) needs premium.
candidates.stack_fit: @fullcalendar/react wraps core cleanly. TS types built-in. Heavier bundle than react-big-calendar or schedule-x. Premium resource views require per-developer license + OEM license if distributed to tenants. Standard tier is the safer adoption scope.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves first-party self-host
candidates.maintenance_health: ~1M npm downloads/week. Adam Shaw maintaining, long-running v6 release cadence. 19k+ stars. Most mature calendar library in the JS ecosystem.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Strong for clinician-app schedule views (timegrid week is the standard clinical scheduling view). Standard tier covers person-facing appointment list/calendar. Resource views (multi-room booking for management surface) need premium.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit (@curaos/ui SchedulingBoard component for the management multi-resource view; CalendarBoard reuses react-big-calendar for person surface)
candidates.risks: Standard MIT is safe. Premium resource plugins at $480/dev + OEM pricing for tenant distribution creates commercial dependency for the multi-resource management surface. Split: use react-big-calendar for person CalendarBoard, FullCalendar standard for richer management views, evaluate premium only if multi-resource management is prioritized.

candidates.name: schedule-x
candidates.what: Modern lightweight event calendar: day/week/month views, drag-drop, dark mode, RTL, i18n, React/Vue/Svelte. MIT for base calendar. Resource scheduler is a premium (paid per-project) feature. ~12k weekly downloads, v4.1.0. Alternative to react-big-calendar with better i18n/RTL baseline and lighter bundle.
candidates.stack_fit: React package @schedule-x/react. TypeScript first. Smaller bundle than FullCalendar. Temporal-polyfill used internally. Resource scheduler requires separate premium license per project.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves first-party self-host
candidates.maintenance_health: v4.1.0 last published ~3 months ago. Active development. Lower download count than react-big-calendar (12k vs 500k). Solo maintainer risk.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing calendar views with RTL and i18n are a fit for CuraOS's localization requirements. Base tier adequate for personal-calendar-service UI.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit (alternative CalendarBoard, especially for RTL/i18n-heavy deployments)
candidates.risks: Lower ecosystem footprint. Resource scheduler commercial creates same risk as FullCalendar premium but at lower price point. Solo/small maintainer team.

candidates.name: Cal.com (AGPL v4.9.3 self-hosted)
candidates.what: Full scheduling platform: booking pages, availability rules, calendar sync (Google/Outlook), payment, webhooks, multi-user teams. Could serve as the BYO-3rd-party scheduling provider for tenants who want a managed booking page. Fills: external booking link for CuraOS patient-facing appointment self-scheduling (taxonomy: scheduling.patient-self-schedule). AGPL v4.9.3 is the last open-source version; future releases closed-source.
candidates.license: AGPL-3.0 (v4.9.3 last open version; future releases proprietary)
candidates.stack_fit: Cal.com is a Next.js + Prisma + PostgreSQL monolith - not NestJS-native. Integration path: deploy Cal.com v4.9.3 as a sidecar service (per [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]] BYO mode), integrate via webhooks into CuraOS Kafka/Redpanda event stream. @calcom/atoms and @calcom/embed-react npm packages call api.cal.com (NOT self-hosted) - not viable for self-hosted-first. Webhook-only integration is the clean path.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: BYO-3rd-party (tenant brings their Cal.com/Cal.diy instance; CuraOS holds event reference via webhook)
candidates.maintenance_health: 41k+ stars. Went closed-source May 29 2026. v4.9.3 is frozen AGPL. No security patches will come for self-hosted going forward. Cal.diy (MIT) is personal-use only. High deployment complexity (Next.js monolith, Redis, email, calendar OAuth).
candidates.person_centric_fit: High for patient self-scheduling (person books their own appointment on a beautiful booking page). Management surface needs CuraOS scheduling-service to hold ground truth. Dual-surface: Cal.com handles the booking UX, CuraOS owns the appointment entity.
candidates.generator_first_target: api-adapter (webhook receiver in scheduling-service: cal.com booking.created -> scheduling.appointment.booked event on Kafka)
candidates.risks: AGPL service boundary: CuraOS serves Cal.com's modified code to users = must publish modifications. Frozen at v4.9.3 = no CVE patches. High operational complexity. @calcom/atoms embed requires Cal.com cloud account in 2026. Recommend: support as BYO-3rd-party option only, not as the self-hosted primary scheduling engine.

candidates.name: PostgreSQL tstzrange + EXCLUDE constraint (native platform feature)
candidates.what: DB-level double-booking prevention using PostgreSQL exclusion constraints on tstzrange. Eliminates the need for an application-level scheduling engine for conflict detection. Fills: resource conflict guard in scheduling-service and business-scheduling-service (any multi-resource booking). Example: EXCLUDE USING gist (resource_id WITH =, tstzrange(start_at, end_at) WITH &&) prevents overlapping bookings at the DB layer.
candidates.stack_fit: Drizzle ORM supports custom SQL for exclusion constraints via sql`` template. CNPG runs standard PostgreSQL with btree_gist extension (required for tstzrange exclusion). Zero new npm deps. Emit the exclusion constraint from the Drizzle schema generator.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves first-party self-host
candidates.maintenance_health: PostgreSQL core feature, fully stable. btree_gist ships with PostgreSQL. CNPG enables it by default.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Invisible to person - prevents the bad experience of double-booked appointments. Management surface: booking conflict errors surface as clear 409 responses.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (drizzle schema emitter adds tstzrange exclusion constraint for any entity with resource_id + time range fields)
candidates.risks: Requires btree_gist extension enabled (default on CNPG). Drizzle custom SQL needed - fold into the schema emitter once, then free for all services.

candidates.name: Luxon (already installed)
candidates.what: Timezone-aware date manipulation library, already in use in calendar-core-service (defaultTzid IANA resolution). No new dep. For backend scheduling logic: slot generation, duration arithmetic, IANA timezone conversion, DST-safe period calculations. Fills: tz-correct slot computation in scheduling-service.
candidates.stack_fit: Already installed in calendar-core. Immutable, IANA tz via Intl API, Bun-compatible. Use for slot generation arithmetic in scheduling-service. Pair with rrule-temporal (which returns Temporal.ZonedDateTime) via a thin adapter or migrate slot logic to Temporal directly.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves first-party self-host
candidates.maintenance_health: Actively maintained. Stable v3.x. Industry standard for backend tz-heavy work.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Powers correct appointment times in the person's local timezone on patient-app.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (already wired into calendar-core; extend to scheduling-sdk slot generation)
candidates.risks: None. Already present. Upgrade path to native Temporal is clean when ecosystem matures.

candidates.name: Cal.diy (MIT)
candidates.what: Personal booking page, stripped-down MIT fork of Cal.com. Single-user booking page concept: no team features, no app store, no multi-tenant. MIT licensed. Officially 'personal and non-production use' per Cal.com team. Could provide reference patterns for booking page UI and availability logic.
candidates.stack_fit: Not integrable as a library. Next.js app. Patterns only. The availability engine and booking form patterns are reference-grade for CuraOS's scheduling-service slot API design.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: BYO-3rd-party
candidates.maintenance_health: v6.1.0 exists. MIT. Actively developed but scoped to personal use. Not production-grade for multi-tenant platform use.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Reference for simple patient self-booking UX patterns.
candidates.generator_first_target: reference-only
candidates.risks: Not recommended for production by Cal.com team. Single-user only. No webhook API parity with full Cal.com.

candidates.name: Ottehr scheduling engine
candidates.what: Open source, production EHR (MIT-style) with TypeScript appointment scheduling, multi-location, provider/group availability, telemedicine vs in-person. Reference for healthcare-specific scheduling data model: appointment types, provider availability windows, location constraints, patient self-booking flow.
candidates.stack_fit: Tightly coupled to Oystehr FHIR platform (Zambdas serverless functions, Fhir client). Not extractable as a standalone npm SDK. Port-adapt the data model and availability algorithm patterns into CuraOS scheduling-service.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves first-party self-host
candidates.maintenance_health: Active (masslight/ottehr), production-scale (millions of patient visits/month). TypeScript, React, FHIR-R4 native.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Excellent reference for the patient self-scheduling journey (person picks type, location, provider, time slot). Management surface: provider availability config, multi-location scheduling admin.
candidates.generator_first_target: contract-typespec (scheduling-service.tsp: appointment-type, availability-window, slot, booking-request, confirmation - port-adapt Ottehr's data model)
candidates.risks: Not importable as a library. Reference-only for data model and UX patterns. Oystehr coupling means no code can be lifted verbatim.

verified.name: rrule-temporal
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. Compatible with self-hosted distribution, air-gap (Zarf bundles npm tarballs), and local-vs-3rdparty duality. No patent or copyleft risk. Candidate claim: accurate.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Candidate version claim is WRONG: stated v1.4.5 (2024); actual current is v1.5.3 (April 2026), v1.5.2 (June 26 2026). Active - recent release 3 days ago as of research date. Single maintainer (1 npm collaborator, 1 GitHub owner). No CVEs or security advisories found. Weekly downloads: ~80K (Socket.dev data) - candidate claim of "~low weekly downloads" is materially false, likely stale. rrule.js open issue #450 "Additional maintainers?" still unresolved; rrule-temporal inherits same single-maintainer bus-factor risk. rSchedule (the other alternative) is last published 7 years ago - genuinely abandoned.
verified.integration_reality: Bun: NO native Temporal API (GitHub issue #15853 open; Bun does not ship Temporal as of June 2026). Polyfill mandatory on Bun and Node < 26. Node 26 ships Temporal natively (unflagged, May 2026) but CuraOS targets Node 24 LTS (still requires --harmony-temporal flag or polyfill) - polyfill is NOT optional yet for the stack. Polyfill cost: candidate claims ~60KB browser; actual @js-temporal/polyfill is 160KB minified / 45KB gzipped. Mitigated if server-side only (NestJS service, not browser bundle) - candidate acknowledges server-side expansion, so server cold-start cost only, acceptable. CRITICAL MISMATCH: @js-temporal/polyfill objects are instanceof-incompatible with temporal-polyfill objects. If any frontend/FullCalendar component or SDK peer uses temporal-polyfill, cross-boundary ZonedDateTime objects will fail instanceof checks. Workaround: .toString() + re-parse across boundaries. A fork of rrule-temporal with temporal-polyfill support exists on npm but is not the main package - adds dependency risk. NestJS/Drizzle/Zod/TypeSpec integration: no native glue needed; it is a pure date computation library. Store RRULE strings as Drizzle text, call expand at read time - correct. No generator-first conflict: it is a compute utility, not a scaffold target.
verified.simpler_alternative: PostgreSQL-side: postgres-rrule extension (Thoughtbot pattern) can expand occurrences inside the DB via generate_series + PL/pgSQL. Simpler for pure read-time expansion with no JS dependency, but loses Temporal tz-correctness guarantees and requires a Postgres extension in every deployment (complicates air-gap and CNPG). Not a drop-in win. No stdlib path: JS Date is tz-broken for recurrence; no stdlib alternative. Ponytail verdict: no simpler installed option covers RFC-5545 tz-correct expansion. The problem is real and the library is the minimum solution - but trial before full SDK embedding is warranted given the polyfill-compatibility landmine.
verified.notes: Three corrections to the candidate brief: (1) Version is v1.5.3 not v1.4.5; (2) weekly downloads are ~80K not low; (3) polyfill cost is 160KB minified not ~60KB. The instanceof-incompatibility between @js-temporal/polyfill and temporal-polyfill is a real integration hazard for the SDK layer target (@curaos/calendar-sdk) - any consumer that also uses FullCalendar's temporal-polyfill peer dep will hit this. Mitigation: either pin the SDK to temporal-polyfill fork of rrule-temporal, or serialize ZonedDateTime to ISO string at SDK boundary and re-parse on the other side. Recommend trial scoped to calendar-core-service server-side expansion only, NOT SDK embedding yet. Validate polyfill compatibility shim before SDK surface commit. Node 26 LTS (October 2026) will eliminate the polyfill dependency entirely - the timing is favorable for a trial-now, simplify-in-6-months arc.

verified.name: react-big-calendar
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. Permissive. Safe for self-hosted + air-gap (Zarf/Helm) distribution and local-vs-3rdparty duality. No CLAs, no premium tiers on the core package.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Partially confirmed with caveats. v1.20.0 published ~24 days ago (June 2026). Repo migrated from jquense/ to bigcalendar/ org - governance transfer, not a fork. ~1.16M weekly downloads (Snyk reports healthy). Active. BUT: React 19 support issue #2701 is open and unresolved per search results as of late 2024/early 2025 - CuraOS runs React 19.1.0. Also: @types/react-big-calendar is a separate DefinitelyTyped package (NOT bundled) last updated Sep 2025, with a known "Calendar cannot be used as a JSX component" TypeScript error in React 19 context (DefinitelyTyped discussion #71657). These two issues together represent a live integration risk against CuraOS stack.
verified.simpler_alternative: No stdlib or native browser API covers this use case. However, the candidate submission's claim that date-fns or luxon are "already installed" is FALSE - neither @curaos/ui nor personal-calendar package.json has either library. Adding react-big-calendar requires adding a date localizer (date-fns or luxon) as a net-new dependency. That is two new deps, not one. schedule-x (@schedule-x/react, v4.6.0, MIT core, a month old) is a credible alternative: framework-agnostic JS-first, built-in React adapter, better TypeScript (first-party types), no React 19 JSX compat issues reported. Its free core covers month/week/day/agenda views. The resource-scheduler plugin is premium (@sx-premium/resource-scheduler) - same ceiling as react-big-calendar, but schedule-x's premium is a known-quantity premium vs react-big-calendar's "just build it yourself" gap.
verified.integration_reality: More glue than claimed. The generator-first target requires emitting a localizer instance (not just JSX props) into the CalendarBoard wrapper - the localizer must be created from the date lib adapter at module init time, which is non-trivial for codegen templates. The @types/react-big-calendar React 19 JSX error means generated code may not typecheck without workarounds (likely a tsconfig or wrapper cast). The lodash CVE-2025-13465 (prototype pollution via _.unset/_.omit) is present in the transitive dep tree; lodash 4.17.23 is the fix, but v1.20.0's changelog does not confirm this bump was applied. Air-gap: the library bundles cleanly from npm with no external CDN calls - no issue there. Multi-resource view (clinician room/provider grid, scheduling admin board) is entirely absent from the free tier; that archetype cannot be generator-emitted from this library.
verified.notes: Downgrade from "confirmed-adopt" to "confirmed-trial" on three grounds: (1) React 19 compat is unconfirmed - the open issue #2701 and the @types JSX error need to be verified in a real CuraOS Next.js 15 + React 19.1.0 build before committing the CalendarBoard archetype to this lib. (2) Two net-new deps (react-big-calendar + a date localizer), not one. (3) The multi-resource scheduling archetype - critical for HealthStack clinician scheduling and admin boards - has no free path in this library; the generator would need to emit two different calendar primitives (react-big-calendar for simple views, something else for resource views), which creates a split-archetype problem in the codegen. Recommended trial scope: install react-big-calendar + date-fns in a single spike app (personal-calendar is the right surface), wire the React 19 peer dep, confirm @types compat, render a basic week view against calendar-sdk event feed. If green, adopt for the simple-event archetypes (patient my-appointments, clinician daily schedule, CRM activity calendar) and track the resource-view archetype as a separate decision (schedule-x premium or FullCalendar Standard). Do NOT gate the full CalendarBoard generator on this trial. Security: run `bun audit` in the spike; if lodash is still at 4.17.21, override to 4.17.23 via resolutions field.

verified.name: FullCalendar (standard, MIT plugins)
verified.license_confirmed: PARTIALLY confirmed with a hard split. Standard plugins: MIT, confirmed accurate, no restrictions on self-hosted or air-gap distribution. Premium plugins (Timeline/Resource): tri-licensed commercial. Critical gap the candidate understates: distributing to tenants requires an OEM license (custom pricing, not the $480/dev tier). The $480 tier is per-developer-seat for internal use; shipping the premium component to external tenants or as SaaS requires a separate OEM agreement with custom pricing (confirmed via fullcalendar.io/license/premium-oem). This is a binary blocker for multi-resource management views in CuraOS multi-tenant SaaS and on-prem distribution. License key enforcement is client-side only (console warning, no outbound network call), so air-gap deployments are not broken technically - but legally the OEM requirement still applies. The candidate correctly calls this out; the risk is understated in severity.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed healthy. v6.1.21 released June 21, 2026 (8 days ago). Active v7 beta series in parallel (v7 now fully React-native including SSR and StrictMode, no longer Preact wrapper). ~1M npm downloads/week. No CVEs found against the core library (a 2022 CVE was against the separate WP FullCalendar WordPress plugin, not the JS library). Single maintainer (Adam Shaw) is a concentration risk but the project has sustained that model for 10+ years. v7.1 roadmap item includes virtual rendering for resources - meaningful for large clinical datasets. v7 is not yet tagged "latest" on npm, so Turborepo/Renovate will pin to v6 by default unless explicitly targeted.
verified.integration_reality: React integration is straightforward for standard tier: @fullcalendar/react wraps core, TS types built-in, no glue code needed against Bun or NestJS (it is a pure frontend component - backend is irrelevant). No Bun-specific issues found. The candidate's claim that it integrates cleanly with Bun/NestJS/Drizzle/Zod/TypeSpec is accurate but misleadingly framed - it is a React UI component, so NestJS/Drizzle/Zod/TypeSpec are not integration points at all; those are backend concerns. The only real integration concern is bundle size (~100KB gzipped with plugins loaded) and generator-first fit. For generator-first codegen, FullCalendar renders opinionated DOM and offers render hooks but no headless/data-only mode - the codegen target must wrap the component and pass data props, which is the standard pattern and poses no structural problem. Turborepo tree-shaking handles plugin splitting adequately.
verified.simpler_alternative: react-big-calendar (v1.20.0, released June 5, 2026, fully maintained, MIT, ~1.16M downloads/week) covers month/week/day/agenda views and drag-drop for the person-facing CalendarBoard archetype. It is already a stronger fit for that surface: lighter bundle, pure MIT with zero commercial tier ambiguity, and no split-brain license management. For the management multi-resource surface (multi-provider, multi-room), react-big-calendar has no resource axis - FullCalendar standard also lacks this. schedule-x is a lighter modern alternative but has significantly less adoption (~50K/week) and fewer clinical scheduling integrations. The candidate's proposed split (react-big-calendar for person surface + FullCalendar standard for richer management views) is the correct call and avoids the premium OEM trap for the initial trial scope.
verified.notes: Verdict rationale: adopt the standard MIT tier for trial, strictly scoped to management scheduling views where react-big-calendar lacks capability (richer timegrid, recurrence UI via rrule plugin). Do NOT adopt premium resource/timeline plugins without resolving OEM licensing for multi-tenant distribution - the OEM tier is custom-priced and legally required before any tenant ships with it. Action items for trial: (1) pin @fullcalendar/react to v6 (stable/latest tag) until v7 exits beta; (2) use react-big-calendar for all person-facing CalendarBoard surfaces (lighter, simpler, pure MIT); (3) gate any premium plugin evaluation behind an explicit OEM license conversation; (4) the generator-first codegen target (ui-kit SchedulingBoard component) should accept data props and wrap FullCalendar standard - this is straightforward. No lock-in risk at standard tier: the component is replaceable. The one unresolved question is whether the Turborepo ui-kit build pipeline handles FullCalendar's CSS import requirements cleanly - that is a one-hour spike, not a blocker.

verified.name: PostgreSQL tstzrange + EXCLUDE constraint (native platform feature)
verified.license_confirmed: Confirmed safe. PostgreSQL License is BSD-2-Clause equivalent - permissive, no copyleft, no distribution restrictions. Self-hosted + air-gap fully compatible. No caveats.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed. This is a PostgreSQL core feature (GiST exclusion constraints since PG 9.2; btree_gist ships as a bundled contrib module). PostgreSQL 18 released 2025-09-25 adds cleaner WITHOUT OVERLAPS syntax. CNPG 1.29 (current) deploys PG 18.3 by default. No CVEs or open critical issues against this specific mechanism. Fully stable.
verified.integration_reality:
Partially optimistic. Three real friction points the candidate understates:

1. DRIZZLE HAS NO NATIVE EXCLUSION CONSTRAINT API. Issues #2813 and #3388 are still open (no merge, no timeline as of June 2026). The emitter must emit raw SQL via a manual migration file or a sql`` DDL snippet. Drizzle Kit will not detect or introspect the constraint on subsequent `drizzle-kit push` runs - it will ignore it or potentially drop it on schema drift reconciliation. This is a generator-first risk: the codegen emitter MUST own the raw-SQL migration fragment and Drizzle Kit must be configured to not clobber it. Non-trivial to get right.

2. CITUS SHARDED TABLES: Citus columnar storage explicitly does not support GiST indexes. For distributed (sharded) tables, exclusion constraints must include the distribution column - meaning resource_id must BE the shard key or the constraint must be prefixed with tenant_id. Cross-shard conflict detection does NOT work. For the CuraOS multi-tenant Citus topology (DB-per-tenant with CNPG), this is manageable IF each tenant DB is a plain PG instance rather than a sharded Citus cluster, but this must be verified per deployment model.

3. PG 18 WITHOUT OVERLAPS is strictly cleaner but also not yet in Drizzle. The candidate frames "btree_gist ships by default" - confirmed available, but it requires explicit CREATE EXTENSION btree_gist per database. CNPG does not auto-enable it; the service seed or migration must do it. The candidate's claim "CNPG enables it by default" is incorrect - it is AVAILABLE, not ENABLED.

Concurrency protection is real and correct: exclusion constraints use predicate locking that closes the race window that application-level checks leave open. This is the strongest argument for adoption.
verified.simpler_alternative: No simpler option exists for this specific guarantee. Application-level conflict checks (SELECT + INSERT in a transaction) have a TOCTOU race even at SERIALIZABLE isolation for new-row conflicts. Advisory locks work but require application coordination and don't compose with ORM transactions cleanly. The constraint IS the simplest correct solution - ponytail approves. The only question is the Drizzle glue cost, which is real but one-time in the emitter.
verified.notes:
VERDICT: confirmed-trial, not confirmed-adopt. The core mechanism is correct, stable, licensed, and fits the stack. Downgrade from adopt because:

(a) The Drizzle integration claim is optimistic - no native API, raw SQL migration required, Drizzle Kit drift handling must be explicitly designed into the emitter, not assumed.

(b) The "CNPG enables btree_gist by default" claim is false - must be seeded per DB.

(c) Citus sharded topology incompatibility not mentioned - needs explicit scoping to per-tenant single-node PG instances.

TRIAL path: implement in scheduling-service first (single tenant, plain CNPG PG 18 instance). Emit the constraint as a raw SQL migration fragment from the codegen emitter with a btree_gist extension guard. Validate that drizzle-kit push does not clobber it on re-run (this is the make-or-break test). Once that pattern is confirmed green, promote to adopt and wire into the schema emitter for all resource-booking entities.

PG 18 WITHOUT OVERLAPS note: if CNPG 1.29 is the deployment target (PG 18.3), prefer WITHOUT OVERLAPS over the GiST EXCLUDE syntax for new tables - it is cleaner DDL and has identical semantics. Still requires raw SQL in Drizzle until native support lands.

Sources used: PostgreSQL docs, CNPG release notes, Drizzle ORM GH issues #2813/#3388, Citus DDL docs, PostgreSQL License (OSI), BetterStack PG18 temporal guide, Hashrocket PG18 post.
verified.candidate: PostgreSQL tstzrange + EXCLUDE constraint (native platform feature)

candidates.name: TigerBeetle (tigerbeetle-node)
candidates.what: Purpose-built financial transactions DB with two-phase debit/credit transfers and ACID guarantees at 10k+ TPS. Fills erp-accounting-gl (taxonomy: erp.accounting.general-ledger) as the atomic posting hot-path engine for the accounting-core-service's transfer execution layer, freeing Drizzle/Postgres to own CoA, journals, reports, and fiscal metadata.
candidates.stack_fit: tigerbeetle-node npm package, TypeScript types included, Node >= 18, Bun-compatible. NOT a Drizzle/SQL surface: fixed accounts+transfers schema only; Drizzle stays for CoA/journals/tax/fiscal metadata. Runs as a separate sidecar binary (Linux production, macOS dev). Wrapper needed to translate accounting-core domain events into TB account/transfer calls.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (single binary, air-gap compatible, no cloud offering yet). No BYO-3rd-party SaaS option. Satisfies self-host lane; BYO lane covered by Postgres Drizzle-based double-entry as fallback.
candidates.maintenance_health: v0.17.7 released June 15 2026. ~11k GitHub stars. Backed by TigerBeetle Inc (TechCrunch-covered). Weekly releases. Jepsen-tested (v0.16.11 passed strong serializability). Production-ready on Linux since 2024.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Invisible to person; enables real-time accurate balances that power 'my account balance', 'payment posted instantly', and copay estimate accuracy across healthstack-billing and accounting-core.
candidates.generator_first_target: accounting-core-service SDK (wraps TB client behind accounting.tsp Transfer operation); posting-rules.ts maps domain events to TB createTransfers calls.
candidates.risks: Linux-only production (no Windows/macOS in prod - fine for K8s air-gap target). Fixed schema: no SQL queries, no ad-hoc reporting - must mirror to Postgres for reports. Cloud rollout still baking. Second DB engine adds ops complexity. Not a drop-in Drizzle replacement.

candidates.name: dinero.js v2
candidates.what: Immutable, tree-shakeable, bigint-native money arithmetic library with 168 ISO 4217 currencies and compile-time currency mismatch detection. Directly fills @curaos/currency package gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.multi-currency) and replaces hand-rolled SafeMinorNonNeg/int64 patterns throughout accounting-core, sales-core, commerce-core, and healthstack-billing.
candidates.stack_fit: Zero-dep npm import, full TypeScript generics, tree-shakeable. Bun-native. Drizzle stores int64 minor units (unchanged); Dinero wraps at service boundary for arithmetic and formatting. FX gain/loss computed as Dinero difference before posting to accounting-core GL. No wrapper needed - import directly into @curaos/currency package.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (offline, no external API). FX rate feed is BYO (ECB/Frankfurter free API or Open Exchange Rates). Satisfies both lanes.
candidates.maintenance_health: v2.0.0 stable shipped March 2026 after 17 alpha releases. ~9k GitHub stars. MIT. Active single maintainer (Sarah Dayan). 17 alpha hardening cycle = stable semantics.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables accurate multi-currency display in 'my invoices', 'my balance', 'my copay estimate' across all person-facing apps with locale-aware formatting built in.
candidates.generator_first_target: @curaos/currency package (new); accounting.tsp FX gain/loss entry generation; all money-path services import via @curaos/currency.
candidates.risks: v2 is a breaking API change from v1 (different function-based API). Single maintainer. FX rates still need a separate feed (not bundled). Minor units storage discipline must be enforced by team convention.

candidates.name: node-sales-tax (sales-tax npm)
candidates.what: Offline international tax rate calculator covering EU VAT (MOSS), Canada GST, 50+ countries with optional online VAT number fraud check. Partial fill for @curaos/tax-engine gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.tax). Covers simple flat-rate VAT/GST look-up; compound/repartition/withholding from Odoo account_tax model still needs port-adapt on top.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm import, TypeScript types built-in, zero deps, Bun-compatible. Plugs into @curaos/tax-engine as the rate-lookup primitive; compound/repartition/withholding logic wraps on top. No Drizzle interaction needed at rate-lookup layer.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (offline JSON rates, no API key). BYO lane: swap for TaxJar/Avalara SDK when tenant needs real-time nexus calculation. Satisfies both lanes via pluggable rate-provider pattern.
candidates.maintenance_health: v2.22.0 released within 6 days of research date (June 2026). MIT. Single maintainer (valeriansaliou). Tax rate updates released as PATCH versions. Long track record (5+ years).
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables accurate tax display on 'my invoice' before payment; correct copay tax on patient billing estimate.
candidates.generator_first_target: @curaos/tax-engine package; accounting.tsp tax computation step; sales-core invoice line tax resolution.
candidates.risks: Offline JSON rates require npm dep update for rate changes (no auto-refresh). Does NOT cover compound tax repartition, withholding, or reverse-charge - those need additional port-adapt from Odoo account_tax logic. Single maintainer. Nexus/economic-nexus rules (US state-by-state) not handled without TaxJar/Avalara BYO.

candidates.name: Lago (self-hosted billing engine)
candidates.what: Open-source metering + usage-based billing + subscription management + invoicing platform. Fills erp-invoice-subscription-recurring gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.invoicing, erp.accounting.subscriptions). Covers: event ingestion, usage metering, plan/charge definitions, subscription lifecycle, invoice generation, dunning, payment orchestration, revenue analytics.
candidates.language: Ruby on Rails (backend) + TypeScript Node.js REST SDK
candidates.stack_fit: AGPL: never import Lago code into CuraOS build. Integration via REST SDK (@getlago/lago-node). NestJS accounting-core-service calls Lago REST API as a billing-state sidecar. Helm chart available for K8s air-gap deployment. Lago manages billing state; CuraOS maps Lago invoices back to accounting-core GL via event subscription. Zod validation on Lago response DTOs needed.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Both: self-host Lago pod in same K8s cluster (self-hosted lane) OR configure to call Lago Cloud (BYO-3rd-party lane). Satisfies dual requirement from [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]].
candidates.maintenance_health: ~9k GitHub stars. AGPL-3.0. Ruby/Rails backend, React TS frontend. Helm charts maintained. Active releases (2026). YC S21 backed. Node.js SDK open-source.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person sees clean invoice + subscription self-management ('my plan', 'my usage this month', 'cancel or upgrade'). Lago's invoice PDF + webhook feed CuraOS personal-app billing view.
candidates.generator_first_target: accounting.tsp (subscription/invoice contract wraps Lago events); @curaos/lago-adapter package (thin NestJS module wrapping Lago Node SDK with Zod validation); codegen emitter for subscription-core-service.
candidates.risks: AGPL service boundary must be enforced - any code import requires AGPL compliance. Ruby/Rails adds a second runtime to ops. Heavy infra (Postgres, Redis, MinIO, Kafka optionally). Lago billing state is external to CuraOS DB-per-tenant model - FK references only, no joins. Clickhouse needed for some analytics. Initial integration effort is L.

candidates.name: Kill Bill
candidates.what: 10-year production-grade open-source subscription billing + payments platform. Alternative BYO billing sidecar to Lago. Covers same erp-invoice-subscription-recurring gap (taxonomy: erp.accounting.invoicing, erp.accounting.subscriptions) with stronger catalog/plugin model for complex tariff structures, native dunning, and built-in payment gateway plugins (Stripe, Adyen, Braintree).
candidates.stack_fit: Apache-2.0: could theoretically import, but Java runtime makes npm-import nonsensical - treat as sidecar REST service. Community Node.js client covers 250+ REST endpoints. NestJS wraps via HttpService. Helm/Docker available. Kaui admin UI built-in (no custom admin UI needed for billing ops).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host on K8s (primary). No managed cloud offering for BYO lane - pair with Kill Bill cloud if needed. Slightly weaker dual-lane story vs Lago.
candidates.maintenance_health: 10+ years production history. Apache-2.0. Java/Killbill Inc backed. Active GitHub. 4.5k stars. Capterra reviews 2025/2026 confirm active use.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same as Lago: person sees invoice + subscription self-management; Kill Bill webhooks feed CuraOS personal-app views.
candidates.generator_first_target: Same as Lago: accounting.tsp subscription/invoice contract; @curaos/killbill-adapter NestJS module.
candidates.risks: Java runtime = second language in ops. Plugin model is Java-only (no TS plugins). Smaller community than Lago in 2025-2026 trends. Community Node client less maintained than Lago's official SDK. Complex catalog XML config for advanced pricing.

candidates.name: ledgerstack-core
candidates.what: SQL-first double-entry accounting engine for Node.js targeting multi-tenant SaaS with Postgres/MySQL/SQLite support and worker-based processing. Candidate primitive for accounting-core-service GL execution layer (taxonomy: erp.accounting.general-ledger) without the complexity of adding TigerBeetle as a second DB.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm import, Postgres-native, multi-tenant aware, worker-compatible. Aligns with Drizzle ORM layer (uses its own SQL but wraps a compatible Postgres driver). Would slot into accounting-core-service posting-rules.ts as the journal entry executor.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (npm package, no external service). BYO lane not applicable.
candidates.maintenance_health: 2025 release (DEV Community article). Very early stage - single author, low stars, no production references found. License not confirmed in search results.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same as TigerBeetle: invisible; enables accurate balances for person surfaces.
candidates.generator_first_target: @curaos/currency package or accounting-core posting-rules.ts.
candidates.risks: Unknown license - legal review needed before import. Extremely early stage (single blog post, no production track record). API surface not validated against CuraOS posting-rules pattern. May not survive as a dependency; safer to port-adapt the GL logic from Odoo LGPL as planned in gap-analysis.

candidates.name: medici
candidates.what: Double-entry accounting system for Node.js + Mongoose/MongoDB, TypeScript, with voided-entry audit chain. Referenced as prior art for GL journal/book patterns. Does NOT fill the CuraOS Postgres gap directly, but the voided-entry pattern (mark as voided, add offsetting entries) is the same as what accounting-core needs for period-close reversal entries.
candidates.stack_fit: MongoDB-only - no Drizzle/Postgres fit. Integration_mode is port-adapt: take the journal book/entry/voiding pattern into accounting-core Drizzle schema design. Not importable as-is for Postgres stack.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (offline npm package).
candidates.maintenance_health: v7.2.0 published 2 months before research date (2026). 235 stars. MIT. Maintained by flash-oss.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Pattern-reference only; no direct person surface impact.
candidates.generator_first_target: accounting.tsp journal/entry/voiding pattern reference only.
candidates.risks: MongoDB-only: unusable without major adapter rewrite on Postgres. Low stars relative to complexity. Port-adapt value only.

candidates.name: Meteroid
candidates.what: Open-source billing + metering + invoicing platform in Rust with Postgres + Clickhouse + Kafka. Covers same erp-invoice-subscription-recurring gap as Lago but is higher-performance and earlier stage. AGPL-3.0 service boundary.
candidates.stack_fit: AGPL service boundary: call via REST/gRPC only. Helm chart for K8s. Requires Clickhouse (new DB engine CuraOS does not currently run) - significant infra expansion. Rust binary = no language runtime conflict but adds binary dependency.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host (Helm). Meteroid Cloud for BYO lane. Satisfies dual requirement.
candidates.maintenance_health: v1 RC announced 2026. 2 years old. AGPL-3.0. Rust/Clickhouse/Kafka stack. Small but growing community. Too immature for production adoption in 2026.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same as Lago: person sees subscription + invoice views.
candidates.generator_first_target: Same as Lago: accounting.tsp + @curaos/meteroid-adapter.
candidates.risks: Clickhouse adds major new infra dependency. AGPL boundary. v1 RC only - pre-stable. Smaller Node.js SDK ecosystem vs Lago. Kafka overlap with CuraOS Redpanda (compatible but redundant). Adopt Lago first; revisit Meteroid when Clickhouse infra is warranted.

candidates.name: Medusa v2 (commerce modules)
candidates.what: Modular TypeScript commerce platform with isolated modules: product, pricing (price rules, currency, tiers), orders, inventory (reservations, stock locations), fulfillment, promotions. Covers erp-trade-supply-gaps (taxonomy: erp.commerce.pricing, erp.inventory.fulfillment) as reference for the pricing-rule resolution algorithm and inventory reservation pattern.
candidates.stack_fit: MIT npm modules, TypeScript, Postgres-native. Medusa v2 modules are independently importable. Pricing module's `calculatePrices` logic and inventory module's `reserveInventory` pattern are directly adaptable to commerce-core-service and inventory-core-service in CuraOS. However Medusa uses its own ORM (MikroORM via @mikro-orm) - bridging to CuraOS Drizzle requires adapter layer. NestJS integration documented. Wrapper needed for ORM compatibility.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (npm modules, no external service). No BYO lane distinction - it's a library.
candidates.maintenance_health: 31k+ GitHub stars (most starred TS commerce OSS). MIT. Active Medusa Inc backing. v2 production-ready 2026. Weekly releases.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Pricing rules enable accurate 'your price' display on personal-shop; inventory reservation prevents oversell on 'add to cart'; fulfillment status feeds 'my orders' tracking.
candidates.generator_first_target: commerce-core-service specs (PriceRule/PriceResult in commerce.tsp); inventory-core-service specs (reserve/ATP ops); reference for codegen price-rule resolver.
candidates.risks: Medusa's ORM is MikroORM - bridging to CuraOS Drizzle requires adapter or dual-ORM in commerce service (violates one-persistence-stack rule). Better used as a reference/port-adapt source than direct import. Full Medusa import brings framework opinions (their DI container, module system) that conflict with CuraOS NestJS patterns.

verified.name: TigerBeetle (tigerbeetle-node)
verified.license_confirmed: Confirmed Apache-2.0, no CLA, no additional restrictions. Safe for self-hosted + air-gap distribution. The separate tigerbeetle-node repo was archived in 2022; the Node.js client is now shipped from the main tigerbeetle repo under the same license and published as the npm package "tigerbeetle-node" (latest 0.17.7, June 12 2026).
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. Weekly releases; v0.17.7 shipped June 12 2026 (one week before the claimed June 15 date - minor date discrepancy in the candidate, not material). ~11k GitHub stars. Jepsen 0.16.11 (June 2025) found ONLY two non-critical issues (multi-predicate query results + a debugging API timestamp) - both fixed by 0.16.45. Strong serializability confirmed. One open concern: indefinite client retry on node failure (not yet resolved). WAL helical-fault permanent cluster disable risk was raised in earlier community discussion but not confirmed in the official Jepsen report. No public CVEs found.
verified.integration_reality: Bun: tigerbeetle-node uses a native Node-API (.node binary) addon. Bun implements 95% of Node-API surface; async_hooks and uv_* threadpool semantics are the gap zones. TigerBeetle's client uses a background I/O thread and async callbacks - exactly the gap zone. No confirmed Bun compatibility evidence found; all documented usage is Node.js. This is a real risk, not a footnote. NestJS: no first-class DI module exists; a custom NestJS module wrapping the client would be hand-rolled (not generator-emittable from TypeSpec directly - TB's schema is fixed debit/credit integers, not a TypeSpec-describable REST surface). Drizzle/Postgres sync: TB offers no built-in CDC to Postgres. The candidate's "mirror to Postgres for reports" is a bespoke outbox-or-polling bridge you write and operate. That is non-trivial glue: event ordering, at-least-once delivery, schema mapping from TB's integer account IDs to CoA codes, and a separate reconciliation job. CRITICAL: The existing accounting-core-service already implements a complete, correct double-entry ledger in Drizzle/Postgres with: balanced-entry enforcement at service AND DB trigger layers, idempotent event consumption with unique-key row-lock, durable outbox (write + event in one tx), append-only immutability trigger, posting-rules seam, and auto-seeded CoA. This is not a stub - it is a production-grade implementation covering all three correctness invariants TB is cited for. TPS ceiling: Drizzle/Postgres with CNPG + Citus handles the GL posting hot-path at CuraOS scale (healthcare/ERP tenant volumes are not fintech interchange scale). TB's 10k+ TPS advantage is irrelevant until a profiler names Postgres GL inserts as the bottleneck, which has not happened.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - already installed and fully implemented. accounting-core-service already has a complete double-entry engine on Drizzle + Postgres (CNPG). Ponytail ladder rung 2: the codebase already has it. Adding TB introduces: a second stateful binary to operate and upgrade (TB version must match client exactly - tight coupling), a required Postgres mirror bridge for all reporting (every query goes to Postgres anyway), unconfirmed Bun napi compatibility, a bespoke NestJS wrapper not supported by any generator, and a two-DB consistency problem (TB + Postgres must stay in sync across crashes). The local-vs-3rdparty duality rule (curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule) requires both a self-hosted AND a BYO-3rd-party option. TB has no BYO-3rd-party/SaaS path today (cloud offering is invitation-only and immature as of mid-2026). The fallback being "Postgres Drizzle-based double-entry" is not a BYO lane - it is the same self-hosted stack. The rule is not met.
verified.notes: Downgraded from "trial" to "downgrade-reference" (keep as a named option in the ADR, do not integrate now) for three compounding reasons: (1) ponytail ladder kills it - the need is already met by the existing implementation; (2) curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule is not satisfied - no real BYO-3rd-party lane exists; (3) Bun napi compatibility is unconfirmed for TB's async I/O model specifically. Revisit only if a production profiler shows Postgres GL insert latency as the actual bottleneck at real tenant load AND Bun napi compatibility is explicitly confirmed by the TB team or a community production report. At that point the integration pattern is: TB owns hot-path balance snapshots only, Postgres remains source of truth for CoA/journals/reports, outbox relay bridges the two, and the NestJS wrapper is generator-first via a posting-rules.ts emitter added to curaos/tools/codegen.

verified.name: dinero.js v2
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. No CLA, no dual-license, no copyleft. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution. Permissive - no restrictions on bundling into Zarf artifacts or private deployments. Safe.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: PARTIALLY confirmed - with caveats. Stable v2.0.0 shipped March 2026 (confirmed via blog + SourceForge mirror + search result snippets showing "2.0.2 last published 3 months ago"). 17 alpha hardening cycle is real. BUT: single maintainer (Sarah Dayan, Nuxt team), prolonged alpha runway (2021-2026 = 5 years), npm shows old v1.9.1 as the visible "latest" in several search result snippets suggesting the v2 stable npm tag may not be cleanly set. No known CVEs. GitHub issue #764 ("What's the status with v2?") existed publicly - signals past momentum concerns. Weekly downloads ~294k (v1 era numbers; v2 adoption curve unknown). Not battle-proven at scale yet in v2 stable form. Bus factor = 1.
verified.simpler_alternative: REAL ALTERNATIVE EXISTS and partially covers the use case. The codebase already stores amounts as bigint minor units in Drizzle (amount_minor BIGINT + text currency_code). For arithmetic: native BigInt operators cover add/subtract/compare at service boundaries with zero deps. For locale formatting: Intl.NumberFormat (stdlib, Bun-native) covers all 168 ISO 4217 currencies with locale-aware display - no library needed. What dinero adds on top: (1) currency-mismatch type safety at compile time, (2) allocation/splitting (banker's rounding), (3) FX conversion scaffolding. Those three are real gaps stdlib cannot close. So the honest ladder answer: stdlib + native BigInt covers ~70% of the claimed value; adopt dinero only if compile-time currency safety and allocation math are explicit requirements for the @curaos/currency package.
verified.integration_reality: Integrates cleanly with the existing stack. Bun runs it natively (ESM, no Node-isms). NestJS: pure function import, no DI friction. Drizzle layer unchanged - store bigint minor units as today, wrap at service boundary only. Zod: no native Zod schema shipped, team must write a thin Zod validator for Dinero objects at API boundaries (small, ~10 lines). TypeSpec: no native TypeSpec emitter - money fields stay as int64 scalars in .tsp, Dinero is internal-only; no contract exposure needed. Generator-first: dinero import belongs in @curaos/currency package; codegen templates emit 'import { add, subtract, toDecimal } from dinero.js' - straightforward template change, no generator redesign. Claim of 'no wrapper needed' is optimistic: a thin @curaos/currency facade is still correct (isolates the dep, owns the Zod schema, owns the bigint-to-Dinero conversion). That facade is ~50 lines, not a heavyweight wrapper. v2 API is function-based (not chainable), which is generator-friendly. Multi-package structure consolidated into subpath imports (dinero.js/bigint, dinero.js/currencies) - single install, no multi-package coordination debt.
verified.notes: VERDICT RATIONALE - trial not full adopt: The value proposition is real (compile-time currency safety, allocation math, FX scaffolding) and the integration is clean. But three friction points prevent immediate 'adopt': (1) bus factor 1 with a 5-year alpha history - a v2.x patch pace needs 3-6 months of post-stable observation before betting accounting-core on it; (2) the npm 'latest' tag confusion (v1.9.1 vs v2 stable) is a minor ops hazard for Renovate auto-PR and needs explicit version pinning; (3) 'no wrapper needed' undersells the required @curaos/currency facade - it IS needed for Zod schemas, bigint bridge, and generator isolation, but it is small. RECOMMENDED TRIAL SCOPE: implement @curaos/currency package with dinero.js v2 for accounting-core-service FX gain/loss and allocation paths only. Pin exact version. Validate over 1-2 sprints before rolling to sales-core and commerce-core. Do NOT adopt for healthstack-billing until trial proves out - healthstack copay rounding has regulatory implications and the bus-factor risk is higher there. PONYTAIL NOTE: if the only need is display formatting + basic add/subtract, drop dinero and use native BigInt + Intl.NumberFormat. Adopt dinero only when currency-mismatch type safety or allocation math is on the critical path.

verified.name: node-sales-tax (sales-tax npm)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed via GitHub. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution and local-vs-3rdparty duality. No concern.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: PARTIALLY confirmed - with a catch. Latest is v2.23.0 (candidate stated v2.22.0; already stale). Active: PATCH releases track real rate changes (Estonia 22->24%, Slovakia 20->23%, Israel 17->18%, all 2025). Single maintainer (valeriansaliou) = bus-factor-1, but 5+ year track record and consistent cadence. No known CVEs against sales-tax itself in GitHub Advisory Database. HOWEVER: the runtime dependency cross-fetch (used for optional online VAT number validation) is effectively unmaintained since ~Dec 2024 (last release v4.1.0, no PRs/issues activity, multiple open unresolved issues). This is a hidden dependency risk the candidate brief did not disclose.
verified.integration_reality: Harder than claimed. The "zero deps" claim in the candidate brief is FALSE. sales-tax carries cross-fetch as a runtime dependency for the online VAT number fraud-check feature. cross-fetch is a stale shim that proxies to node-fetch under Node and was introduced specifically to patch a Node 22 incompatibility (issue #67). Bun ships native fetch; cross-fetch is redundant and potentially a source of future friction as the ecosystem drops polyfill shims. The offline rate-lookup path (the part CuraOS actually needs) does not use cross-fetch at all - it is pure JSON file reads - so the dep is only pulled in if the optional VAT fraud-check API is called. Air-gap deployments must NOT call the VIES endpoint anyway, making cross-fetch dead weight in the bundle. TypeScript types are built-in (no @types/ needed). NestJS injection is trivial (service wrapper). Drizzle has no interaction at the rate-lookup layer. Zod validation wraps cleanly on top. TypeSpec contract for the tax-computation step is unaffected. Generator-first target (@curaos/tax-engine package) is a clean fit provided the wrapper never calls checkTaxNumber() in production paths.
verified.simpler_alternative: Partially. For EU-only coverage: ibericode/vat-rates or benbucksch/eu-vat-rates are pure static JSON with no runtime dep and no code surface. They are narrower (EU only, no Canada GST, no 50+ country coverage) and have no computation logic - callers must implement rate resolution themselves. For CuraOS scope (ERP + HealthStack patient billing across multiple jurisdictions), sales-tax's rate coverage and the minimal computation API are worth the marginal weight over a raw JSON file, provided cross-fetch is quarantined.
