The ponytail ladder stops here: no already-installed dep covers 50-country tax rate lookup + Canadian GST; stdlib does not; the package is justified but must be adopted with the cross-fetch caveat addressed.
verified.notes: Adopt as trial with three hard conditions: (1) Wrap inside @curaos/tax-engine so no service ever calls sales-tax directly - the pluggable rate-provider pattern the candidate describes is mandatory, not optional, both for BYO-TaxJar/Avalara lane and to isolate the cross-fetch surface. (2) Never call checkTaxNumber() / the online VAT fraud check in any production or air-gap path - gate it behind a capability flag that is OFF by default; air-gap tenants must never trigger the VIES HTTP call. This eliminates the cross-fetch risk at runtime. (3) Pin the sales-tax version in Renovate with auto-PR on PATCH bumps; each PATCH is a tax rate update and must be auto-merged after CI green (offline JSON only changes). The compound/repartition/withholding gap acknowledged by the candidate is real and material for the Odoo account_tax port - size that as a separate story before marking @curaos/tax-engine done. Nexus/economic-nexus (US) is also out of scope without TaxJar/Avalara, which the BYO lane must document explicitly in the tax-engine AGENTS.md. Downgrade to reject if the generator-first wrapper cannot fully hide sales-tax's API surface from service code.

verified.name: Lago (self-hosted billing engine)
verified.license_confirmed: AGPL-3.0 confirmed. The critical clarification: calling Lago via REST API from a proprietary NestJS service does NOT trigger AGPL copyleft. AGPL Section 13 fires only when you distribute or network-serve a MODIFIED version of Lago itself. CuraOS's integration pattern (REST adapter calling Lago as an external sidecar service, no source import) is clean. Zero obligation to open-source CuraOS code. The candidate's "agpl-service-boundary" verdict and "never import Lago code" constraint are correct and sufficient. No commercial license needed for this pattern. One residue risk: if CuraOS ever forks/patches lago-api source and self-hosts that modified version, AGPL triggers - enforce a hard rule that Lago source is never modified locally.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. Latest release: v1.48.1, June 11, 2026. Regular cadence throughout 2026 (v1.x series). ~9k GitHub stars confirmed. YC S21, enterprise customers (Mistral AI, Algolia, GitHub cited). SDK: lago-javascript-client v0.56.0-beta is the active package (lago-nodejs-client deprecated at v0.26.0-beta, 2 years stale - the candidate's "@getlago/lago-node" reference is outdated; correct package is lago-javascript-client). Helm charts maintained at charts.getlago.com. One open issue flagged: race condition in payment processing allowing credit application before payment confirmation (issue #4326, Sep 2025) - no confirmed fix date surfaced, relevant for financial correctness.
verified.integration_reality: More complex than claimed. Confirmed: lago-javascript-client works with Fetch API, ESM, TypeScript types - Bun-compatible in practice (Bun's Node.js compat covers Fetch + ESM). NestJS REST adapter pattern is sound. Zod wrapping on response DTOs is straightforward. However, three claims need correction: (1) SDK name is lago-javascript-client, not @getlago/lago-node - update the generator target. (2) Infrastructure floor is Postgres + Redis mandatory, ClickHouse optional (wiki: "Using ClickHouse to scale an events engine" - it is a scaling add-on, not baseline). A docker-compose.light.yml exists for minimal deployment. (3) Kafka/Redpanda are NOT mandatory at baseline - they feed ClickHouse for high-volume paths only. This materially reduces the ops surface for initial trial. The billing state isolation concern is real: Lago owns its own Postgres schemas; CuraOS DB-per-tenant model cannot join across. FK references + event subscription for GL mapping is the only viable pattern - confirmed correct. Generator target: lago-adapter NestJS module + TypeSpec subscription/invoice contract wrappers + Zod DTOs is realistic and generator-first compatible.
verified.simpler_alternative: No credible simpler alternative covers the same surface. Flexprice (Go backend, AGPL-3.0 backend / Apache 2.0 frontend, ~3.5k stars) is younger and thinner. Kill Bill is Java/Apache 2.0 but architecturally heavier and decade-old design. Building usage metering + subscription lifecycle + invoice generation + dunning from scratch in NestJS is a multi-month L effort with high correctness risk (billing math, proration, tax, idempotency). Lago at trial scope (REST sidecar + adapter module) is the minimum-viable path. Ponytail ladder stops at rung 5: already-available dependency that solves it, integrated via thin adapter. No custom billing engine needed.
verified.notes: Corrections to candidate before trial: (1) SDK package name: lago-javascript-client (not @getlago/lago-node). Update codegen generator target. (2) ClickHouse and Kafka are scaling add-ons, not baseline requirements - trial starts with Postgres+Redis only, adds ClickHouse when event volume demands it. (3) Race condition issue #4326 (payment/credit ordering) needs a compensating guard in the CuraOS accounting-core-service event handler - do not assume Lago's payment webhooks are atomic. (4) AGPL boundary enforcement: add a linting rule or CI check that no lago-api source files are ever imported into the CuraOS monorepo. REST-only calls only. (5) Air-gap path: Lago Helm charts exist and are maintained; Zarf bundling is feasible for the Postgres+Redis+Lago pod set. ClickHouse adds complexity to Zarf bundle - defer to when volume requires it. Trial scope: deploy Lago pod in dev cluster, wire lago-javascript-client adapter in accounting-core-service, map one subscription type end-to-end through to GL event. Downgrade to reference if trial surfaces the Ruby/Rails runtime as an operational burden at scale.

candidates.name: EDIFlow (@ediflow/core + @ediflow/hipaa-x12-005010)
candidates.what: TypeScript-native X12/HIPAA EDI library: parse + generate 837P, 837I, 835, 270/271 and 11 more HIPAA transaction sets. Fills gap taxonomy_ids: healthcare.revenue.insurance-claims, healthcare.revenue.eligibility, healthcare.revenue.revenue-cycle. Covers the @curaos/x12-sdk package gap identified in gap-analysis.json (ABSENT). Clean Architecture: Domain layer is format-agnostic; X12 is an adapter, so adding new transaction sets requires no Domain changes.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host only (pure library, no SaaS)
candidates.maintenance_health: Active: v0.3.0 released May 2026, 13 npm packages, 711 passing tests, 90% coverage, 0 TS errors. Solo maintainer (early-stage risk), no corporate backer yet.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm-import into Bun/NestJS. Zero external dependencies. Wrap in @curaos/x12-sdk: TypeSpec contract -> EDIFlow emitter/parser -> service. No NestJS-specific wrapper needed; call as a pure library from service methods.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Back-office EDI; person surface = claim status timeline ('submitted to payer', 'adjudicated, you owe $X'). EDIFlow supplies the engine; CuraOS contracts + events supply the person-facing projection.
candidates.generator_first_target: sdk-package (@curaos/x12-sdk wrapping EDIFlow emitters/parsers, folded into codegen mold so every claim service variant extends the SDK)
candidates.risks: Solo maintainer - if abandoned, 837/835/270 segments are ANSI standard so migration to node-x12 or scratch is feasible. v0.3.0 is pre-1.0; API surface may change. 270/271 support confirmed in HIPAA set but verify segment completeness against CMS payer requirements before committing.

candidates.name: Twenty CRM (self-hosted API sidecar)
candidates.what: Full CRM pipeline system: leads, contacts, accounts/households, deals/opportunities, pipelines, activities, email sync, reporting. Covers crm-insurance-broker.sales.* and crm-insurance-broker.service.* taxonomy IDs. Fills the CRM gaps in crm-core-service (leads absent, thin deal model, no pipeline catalog, no cases). Two usage modes: (A) self-host Twenty alongside CuraOS and call its GraphQL/REST API from crm-core (api-adapter, BYO-3rd-party path); (B) reference its NestJS + metadata-driven architecture to accelerate crm-core-service build (reference-only).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: BYO-3rd-party (run Twenty as a sidecar, call its API; or self-host for the operator's own CRM use)
candidates.maintenance_health: ~45k GitHub stars, $5M seed (YC S23, Nov 2024), 300+ contributors, 60 releases, 10,900+ commits. v0.42.x as of mid-2026. Actively maintained, well-funded.
candidates.stack_fit: Twenty backend = NestJS + GraphQL + PostgreSQL - identical to CuraOS stack. twenty-sdk npm package exists for extending Twenty's own model. As an API adapter: thin NestJS service wraps Twenty GraphQL calls behind a crm.tsp-defined interface. No Bun-specific issues (REST/GraphQL client). Wrapper needed to bridge TypeSpec contracts to Twenty's metadata-driven schema.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Twenty is entirely org/rep-centric (Salesforce alternative framing). Person-centric reshape requires the same re-centering as the source-map analysis - mine its entity completeness, not its UX. Its metadata-driven object model is a strong reference for CuraOS crm-core contract design.
candidates.generator_first_target: api-adapter (NestJS crm-core-service wraps Twenty REST/GraphQL as BYO-3rd-party CRM backend; crm.tsp contract is the stable internal interface)
candidates.risks: AGPL-3.0: any modification of Twenty code distributed externally triggers source-disclosure. Internal self-host for operator's own CRM = safe. Distributing CuraOS with bundled Twenty modifications = risky. Preferred usage is pure API adapter (no code modification). Twenty's metadata-driven model diverges from CuraOS TypeSpec-first; keeping the adapter thin is critical to avoid coupling. As of 2026 Twenty is pre-1.0 on some APIs.

candidates.name: EspoCRM (BYO sidecar + REST client)
candidates.what: Full-featured CRM with clean JSON entity definitions (Lead, Contact, Opportunity, Case, Meeting, InboundEmail) that map 1:1 to the gap-analysis crm-insurance-broker taxonomy. GPL-3.0 PHP backend. Fills crm-insurance-broker.sales.leads, .contacts, .opportunities, .service.cases-tickets via BYO-3rd-party path. Primary CuraOS value: (A) run as BYO-3rd-party CRM sidecar for operators who already use EspoCRM; (B) its entityDefs JSON are the cleanest reference data models for CuraOS crm.tsp contract design (directly cited in source-to-local-map.json as best_source for Lead, Opportunity, Case).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: BYO-3rd-party (self-hosted EspoCRM instance; CuraOS adapts via REST API)
candidates.maintenance_health: Active: 7k+ stars, v9.x as of 2025, PHP backend, documented Node.js API client. No corporate backer; community-maintained. Stable for 10+ years.
candidates.stack_fit: PHP backend - not importable into NestJS. Integration is via documented REST API + the official Node.js client (espocrm/api-client-javascript). Thin NestJS adapter service wraps the client behind crm.tsp interface. No Bun issues for the HTTP client.
candidates.person_centric_fit: EspoCRM is org-centric. Value is as a BYO operator sidecar and as a data-model reference. Person-centric surface entirely on CuraOS side.
candidates.generator_first_target: api-adapter (crm-core-service BYO adapter module; entityDefs JSON as reference for contract-typespec crm.tsp design)
candidates.risks: GPL-3.0: entity definitions are facts (not copyrightable), safe to reference for TypeSpec design. PHP backend - cannot npm-import; REST-only integration. Dependency on EspoCRM being self-hosted by operator. Node.js client is thin and unmaintained by EspoCRM core team - may need a wrapper to handle auth + retry.

candidates.name: Ballerine (KYC/KYB document collection + workflow)
candidates.what: Open-source risk management infrastructure: document collection SDK flows, KYC/KYB workflow engine, case management back office, 3rd-party plugin system. Fills the document-collection/KYC gap for insurance policy onboarding and lead qualification (crm-insurance-broker.sales.leads, insurance policy application intake). Covers both self-host (on-prem data ownership) and BYO-3rd-party (Ballerine cloud). Backend uses NestJS + TypeBox, directly stack-compatible.
candidates.license: MIT (web SDK/flows) + AGPL-3.0 (backend case management service)
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: both (self-host Ballerine backend for on-prem data ownership; web SDK imported as npm package for document collection flows)
candidates.maintenance_health: ~8k stars, YC-backed, active as of 2025-2026. NestJS + TypeBox backend. Multiple npm packages published. Last release within 2025.
candidates.stack_fit: Web SDK (MIT) is a React component - embeds in CuraOS React apps directly. Backend (AGPL) runs as a sidecar NestJS service - calls via REST API from crm-core or a dedicated KYC service. TypeBox + NestJS alignment means the backend API shape is immediately familiar. Bun can run the web SDK in dev; no Bun-specific issues on the NestJS backend sidecar.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Directly person-centric: the document collection flow is the person's onboarding journey (upload ID, provide proof of address, etc.). The back-office case management is the management surface. Strong fit for person-centric lens - the person owns their document submission journey.
candidates.generator_first_target: react-component (web SDK flows embedded in crm-app / policy-onboarding app via ui-app-emit); api-adapter (NestJS crm-core calls Ballerine case management REST API for review workflow)
candidates.risks: Backend is AGPL-3.0: same service-boundary constraint as Twenty. Self-host = safe; distributing modified Ballerine backend = source disclosure required. SDK (MIT) = fully safe to import. Risk: Ballerine is fintech-focused (KYC for payments/marketplaces) - insurance-specific document types (policy applications, proof of insurability) need custom flow definitions. Workflow engine JSON config may not cover all insurance-specific decision trees without extension.

candidates.name: node-x12 (ASC X12 parser/generator)
candidates.what: MIT JavaScript/TypeScript library for parsing, generating, querying, and mapping X12 EDI with stream support. Fallback option for @curaos/x12-sdk if EDIFlow is too immature. Fills same gap as EDIFlow: healthcare.revenue.insurance-claims, healthcare.revenue.eligibility, healthcare.revenue.revenue-cycle.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host only (pure library)
candidates.maintenance_health: Last published ~2022 (4 years ago). 8 downstream packages use it. Not actively maintained. Solo maintainer (abandoned). Stars unknown but low.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm-import. TypeScript types included. Works in Bun. No NestJS wrapper needed. Lower-level than EDIFlow: requires more code to build 837P/I segment loops from scratch.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same as EDIFlow - pure back-office EDI engine; person surface is claim status timeline.
candidates.generator_first_target: sdk-package (@curaos/x12-sdk using node-x12 as the segment-level primitive)
candidates.risks: Abandoned: no 2025/2026 updates, no HIPAA-specific transaction set schemas. Would require maintaining the library internally or forking. EDIFlow is strictly better for new work. Use node-x12 only if EDIFlow has a blocking bug.

candidates.name: aposin/openinsuranceplatform (insurance data model reference)
candidates.what: Java insurance platform with a system-wide data model covering policy management, party management, claims, commission, product definition, reinsurance, and collection/disbursement. Apache-2.0 licensed. Reference-only for insurance-specific data model concepts: Policy, Coverage, Premium, Commission, Reinsurance, Product Definition. No TypeScript, no npm package.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host (reference for contract design only)
candidates.maintenance_health: ARCHIVED: aposin association closed. No active maintainers. Last activity 2022-2023. Java codebase only.
candidates.stack_fit: Not importable. Reference-only: data model entities (Policy, Coverage, Commission, Product) inform TypeSpec contract design for insurance-overlay services. No wrapper possible.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Data model is org-centric (carrier/broker perspective). Person-centric reshape required for any entity mined from this source.
candidates.generator_first_target: contract-typespec (inform insurance-overlay TypeSpec contract design for Policy/Coverage/Commission entities)
candidates.risks: Archived project: no support, potential bit-rot in data model vs current insurance standards. Commission model may reflect European insurance market specifics. Validate any mined concepts against current regulatory requirements.

candidates.name: openIMIS (insurance policy data model reference)
candidates.what: Python/Django open-source health insurance management system covering beneficiaries, policies, premiums, contributions, claims, providers, and accounting. Active in 14 countries, 38M+ beneficiaries. FHIR R4 interop. Reference-only for insurance-specific data model: Policy (product, contribution schedule, coverage period), Claim lifecycle, premium collection. Not TypeScript, not npm.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host (reference for contract design; GraphQL + FHIR REST API available if BYO-3rd-party path needed for health insurance operators)
candidates.maintenance_health: Active: multiple repositories, Docker deployment, used in production at national scale. Swiss SDC + Bluesquare backed. Python/Django backend, React frontend.
candidates.stack_fit: Python backend - no npm import. REST API (FHIR R4 + GraphQL) available for BYO-3rd-party adapter if CuraOS HealthStack operators already run openIMIS. Data model (Product, Policy, Beneficiary, Claim) is the richest permissive reference for insurance-specific TypeSpec contract design in CuraOS.
candidates.person_centric_fit: openIMIS is beneficiary-centric (close to person-centric by design for health financing). Its beneficiary/policy model is a good reference for person-owned insurance policy entities.
candidates.generator_first_target: contract-typespec (inform insurance-overlay TypeSpec contract design for Policy/Beneficiary/Premium/Claim entities)
candidates.risks: Python-only: no TypeScript artifacts to import. FHIR R4 API is health insurance focused (not general P&C/life insurance). Commission and broker-side features are limited.

candidates.name: Commission calculation engine (scratch build)
candidates.what: No viable open-source TypeScript commission calculation engine exists on npm with meaningful adoption. Commercial SaaS (CaptivateIQ, Performio, Core Commissions) dominate; none are self-hostable. The @curaos/commission-engine package must be built from scratch. Gaps covered: commission calculation for sales (crm-insurance-broker.sales.commissions) and insurance broker commissions (premium-based, tiered, split, clawback on cancellation). The aposin openinsuranceplatform commission module and EspoCRM/SuiteCRM commission concepts serve as data model references only.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host (build @curaos/commission-engine; BYO-3rd-party = commercial SaaS like CaptivateIQ via API)
candidates.maintenance_health: N/A - no viable open-source candidate found. Market gap confirmed by search across npm, GitHub, and commercial landscape.
candidates.stack_fit: Scratch build: TypeSpec contract (Commission rule: type enum flat/tiered/split, rate, clawback window) -> Drizzle schema -> pure TS calculation engine -> NestJS service. Inputs from sales-core (deal closed, amount), insurance-overlay (policy premium, effective date). Fold every edge case back into the generator per [[curaos-generator-evolution-rule]].
candidates.person_centric_fit: Dual surface: person = the agent/broker sees their earned commission statement ('my earnings this month, this quarter, clawback alerts'); management = admin commission console (rule config, batch calc, payout batch, override, audit). Same commission engine, two re-centered surfaces.
candidates.generator_first_target: sdk-package (@curaos/commission-engine: rule model + calculation + clawback; folded into codegen so crm-core and insurance-overlay services share one canonical calc path)
candidates.risks: Financial calculation: commission math is a money path requiring the same fail-closed integer-minor discipline as billing. Clawback on policy cancellation needs precise attribution window logic. Audit trail mandatory. Regulatory risk in insurance contexts (broker commission disclosure rules vary by jurisdiction).

verified.name: EDIFlow (@ediflow/core + @ediflow/hipaa-x12-005010)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed via GitHub org page and DEV.to posts. No contradictions found. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution and local-vs-3rdparty duality: pure library, no SaaS call-home, no GPL contamination. License claim in candidate is accurate.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: PARTIALLY confirmed with caveats. v0.3.0 released May 2026, TypeScript, MIT, last GitHub update May 15 2026 - all match candidate claims. Confirmed solo maintainer, no corporate backer, no team, no funding - maintainer says so explicitly in DEV.to posts. Bus factor = 1, project started January 2026 (5 months old as of this review). 711 tests / 90% coverage / 0 TS errors: self-reported, not independently verifiable from search results. Weekly download count: not retrievable (npm page redirect blocked). Zero external dependencies: claimed but not independently verified from package.json. No CVEs found specific to EDIFlow - but the package is new enough that vuln databases have not indexed it significantly. The npm ecosystem had a major supply-chain attack (chalk/debug) in Sep 2025 affecting packages with billions of downloads; a low-traffic solo package carries different but real supply-chain risks (maintainer account compromise). Critical gap: no independent production postmortems, no case studies, no community usage reports outside the maintainer own DEV.to blog series.
verified.integration_reality: Worse than claimed. Candidate says 'no NestJS wrapper needed; call as pure library from service methods' - technically true but understates the integration work. Real integration path for CuraOS: TypeSpec contract definition -> @curaos/x12-sdk package -> EDIFlow emitter/parser calls -> NestJS service method. The TypeSpec-to-EDIFlow binding is custom glue that does not exist yet. EDIFlow has no TypeSpec plugin, no AsyncAPI event emitter, no Drizzle model binding, no Zod schema export. Every segment mapped to a TypeSpec type and every Drizzle insert has to be hand-authored once per transaction set. The candidate flags this as 'wrap in @curaos/x12-sdk' but that wrapper is the entire implementation effort - EDIFlow provides the segment parse/emit kernel only. Bun compatibility: EDIFlow is pure TypeScript with zero native deps, so Bun runtime is fine. Generator-first fit: neutral - EDIFlow does not fight the generator, but it also does not help it; the codegen mold still needs new .tsp.hbs templates, sdk-emit hooks, and service-seed-emit fixtures regardless of whether EDIFlow or scratch code is underneath. Air-gap: zero-dependency pure library packages fine in Verdaccio/Zarf artifact store. No lock-in beyond the @curaos/x12-sdk wrapper surface.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is already documented in the codebase. The gap-analysis.json (/.ai-analysis/gap-analysis.json, id: x12-revenue-cycle) explicitly recommends: best_source = OpenEMR (GPL) X125010837P.php + X125010837I.php + ParseERA.php + EDI270.php; suggested_mode = 'E port-adapt (X12 logic) into a NEW @curaos/x12-sdk generator-first package'. X12 segments are ANSI standard (not copyrightable), so porting logic from OpenEMR to fresh TypeScript carries no GPL contamination. This path: (a) was already evaluated and chosen before this candidate appeared, (b) produces the same @curaos/x12-sdk generator-first package, (c) starts from battle-tested segment loop logic used in production EHRs, (d) eliminates the v0.x API-change risk entirely since the SDK owns the contract, (e) eliminates bus-factor dependency on a 5-month-old solo project. EDIFlow is a reference for how to structure the TypeScript adapter layer (clean architecture: Domain format-agnostic, X12 as adapter) - not a dependency to adopt. Additionally, for 270/271 eligibility specifically, Stedi offers a production-grade managed API option that satisfies the local-vs-3rdparty duality rule as the BYO-3rd-party-provider path, while the @curaos/x12-sdk covers the self-hosted path.
verified.notes: Verdict rationale: downgrade to reference, not adopt. Three converging reasons. (1) Internal prior art wins: gap-analysis.json already recommended port-adapt from OpenEMR as the implementation path. That decision pre-dates this candidate and should not be overridden by a newer external option without a concrete advantage. EDIFlow does not offer one - it is the same TypeScript adapter work either way, but with an external bus-factor-1 dependency added. (2) Pre-1.0 on a 5-month-old solo project is a real risk for a compliance-graded surface. X12 5010 segment completeness for CMS payer-specific companion guides (HETS 270/271 005010X279A1, UHC 005010X221A1 835 quirks, etc.) is unverifiable from public sources. CMS payer requirements include loop/segment constraints beyond the base spec that only show up in production integration. (3) X12 008060 is published (late 2025) and federal rulemaking for mandatory adoption is in progress. A pre-1.0 library's roadmap alignment to 008060 is unknown. Ponytail ladder position: rung 4 (already-installed dep) is not satisfied - EDIFlow is NOT already installed. Rung 7 (minimum code that works) is satisfied by the port-adapt path the gap analysis already specified. USE EDIFlow as: architecture reference for the TypeScript clean-architecture adapter pattern (Domain layer format-agnostic, X12 as infrastructure adapter - this is the right shape for @curaos/x12-sdk). DO NOT adopt as a runtime dependency until: maintainer gains a second committer or corporate backer, version reaches 1.0 with stable API, and a production user with CMS payer integration posts a postmortem. 270/271 segment completeness against real payer companion guides must be verified against CMS HETS companion guide (005010X279A1) before any adoption decision.",

verified.name: Twenty CRM (self-hosted API sidecar)
verified.license_confirmed: FAIL - open-core dual-license, not plain AGPL-3.0. twenty-ee files (SSO/SAML/OIDC, RBAC, row-level permissions) are under a separate commercial license requiring a paid subscription for production use. Air-gap Zarf bundle distribution of Twenty (even unmodified) obligates operators to commercial license terms for any twenty-ee features. Mode A (sidecar) is blocked until legal confirms scope-limited to AGPL-only files with no twenty-ee surface. Mode B (reference-only, no Twenty code shipped) is clean.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: PASS with caveats. v2.15.0 shipped 2026-06-24, actively maintained. BUT: CVE-2026-46624 (CRITICAL RCE via SQL injection + OS command injection in groupBy REST endpoint, affecting 1.7.7-1.16.7) is patched in >1.16.7 / current v2.15. CVE-2026-27023 (SSRF bypass, high) patched. Open GitHub issue #21071: axios dep proxy-bypass SSRF (CVE-2026-44492/4/5) - patch status unconfirmed at research time. Running Twenty as a production sidecar means owning its CVE lifecycle independently - the candidate does not budget this operational overhead.
verified.integration_reality: Harder than claimed. Twenty's metadata-driven schema (runtime object definitions auto-generating REST+GraphQL) is the inverse of CuraOS's TypeSpec-contract-first approach. A crm-core adapter wrapping Twenty's GraphQL must translate between Twenty's dynamic schema and the stable TypeSpec crm.tsp interface - this is a schema-translation layer, not a thin adapter, and must track Twenty's schema churn across every release. Upgrade path has documented DB schema mismatch failures between minor versions (Cloudron forum). Bun runtime for the wrapper itself is fine (pure HTTP client, no native bindings). NestJS stack match is genuine but does not reduce the TypeSpec-vs-metadata-model impedance.
verified.simpler_alternative: Yes. Generator-first extension of crm-core-service (emit leads + pipeline catalog + cases via curaos/tools/codegen producing NestJS service + TypeSpec contract + Drizzle schema + seed) avoids license ambiguity, CVE monitoring obligation, sidecar ops overhead, and schema-translation glue entirely. Ponytail ladder: the generator is already installed; the missing entities are well-defined domain objects; Mode A sidecar fails rungs 1 and 5 (YAGNI complexity + existing capability path). Mode B (reference-only) passes rung 5: mine Twenty's entity model to accelerate TypeSpec contract design for crm-core, do not run it.
verified.notes: Mode B (reference-only) is confirmed-adopt: Twenty's entity completeness (leads, pipeline stages, activities, cases) is strong prior art for crm-core TypeSpec contract design. Mine it. Mode A (api-adapter sidecar) requires: (1) legal sign-off that deployed scope excludes all twenty-ee files, (2) CVE monitoring SLA for Twenty releases, (3) schema-translation layer budget. If those three are satisfied it becomes confirmed-trial. Current recommendation: use Twenty as a reference architecture, extend crm-core-service generator-first for the missing entities.

verified.name: EspoCRM (BYO sidecar + REST client)
verified.license_confirmed: FAIL on claimed license. Candidate says GPL-3.0; actual license is AGPLv3 (changed December 2023, applies from v8.x onward). This is a material difference. AGPLv3 closes the ASP loophole: any networked service built on or distributing modified EspoCRM source must release its source. HOWEVER: CuraOS's BYO-3rd-party integration model never imports EspoCRM source into the NestJS codebase. The adapter calls EspoCRM over its documented REST API. REST API calls do not create a derivative work. The entityDefs JSON files (Lead.json, Opportunity.json, etc.) are data/configuration structures expressing domain-common CRM facts; they are not copyrightable as creative expression and can be used as reference for TypeSpec contract design without license obligation. Verdict on license: AGPLv3 is safe for the declared BYO-sidecar + REST-adapter integration mode. The claimed license string in the candidate is simply wrong and must be corrected in source-to-local-map.json.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. EspoCRM is genuinely maintained: v9.0 shipped January 2025, v9.2 shipped September 30 2025, v9.x continues with PHP 8.5 support added in early 2026. Regular blog releases every 1-2 months. 7k+ GitHub stars is accurate. Community-maintained with no single corporate backer is accurate and a real risk for a 10+ year old PHP CRM, but the cadence is not stale. CVE surface: 1 CVE in 2024, 6 in 2025 (HTML injection, open redirect, password hash sort leak, iframe sandbox gaps) - all disclosed and patched in subsequent minor releases (9.0.5, 9.0.7, 9.0.8). No unpatched critical/RCE found. For a BYO-sidecar model the operator carries the patching burden, which must be documented in the adapter contract.
verified.integration_reality: Harder than claimed, but not a blocker. Key friction points: (1) The official JS client (espocrm/api-client-js) is NOT a published npm package. It is copy-paste snippet code in the docs. There is no @espocrm/client on npmjs.com. The adapter will need to be written against raw fetch/axios or a thin hand-rolled wrapper - roughly 80-120 lines. The candidate's claim of "official Node.js client" is misleading; it is documentation-only sample code with no version, no changelog, no test suite. (2) Auth: EspoCRM supports API Key header auth and HMAC-SHA256 signed requests. Both work fine from NestJS/Bun via native fetch. No Bun compatibility issues. (3) NestJS adapter pattern: thin BYO module behind crm.tsp interface is correct and achievable. No generator-first concern because this is an external REST adapter, not a generated service scaffold. Generator touches only the CuraOS crm-core-service interface, not EspoCRM internals. (4) No Drizzle/Zod/TypeSpec friction: EspoCRM data never touches CuraOS ORM layer directly. Zod shapes the inbound API responses; TypeSpec defines the crm.tsp contract surface. All clean.
verified.simpler_alternative: Checked ponytail ladder. No already-installed CuraOS module covers Lead/Opportunity/Case BYO-CRM sidecar integration today. The crm-core-service is a gap in the current service inventory (crm-insurance lane). Rolling a generic REST adapter for ANY external CRM is ~100-150 lines of NestJS module + Zod response shapes. That is likely comparable effort to an EspoCRM-specific adapter given the JS client is not packaged. However, the entityDefs reference value (best_source for TypeSpec contract design) is real and hard to replicate without a reference implementation. Alternative: skip EspoCRM as a sidecar target entirely and design crm.tsp contracts from first principles using industry-standard CRM field taxonomies (Salesforce SOQL, HubSpot v3 API, or IATA standards). That path removes the AGPLv3 dependency on the reference entirely and produces a more portable contract. That is worth noting but is a design question above this candidate's scope.
verified.notes: Three corrections required in source-to-local-map.json before this proceeds to implementation: (1) license field: GPL-3.0 -> AGPLv3. (2) js-client claim: "official Node.js client" -> "documentation-only code snippet, no npm package, write a thin fetch wrapper". (3) maintenance risk: add explicit note that operator carries EspoCRM patch cadence burden for CVEs; adapter contract must surface this. Effort estimate S (small) is correct for the adapter shell. entityDefs JSON reference use is legally clean. BYO-sidecar model is architecturally sound for the [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]] duality. Downgrade from recommended 'trial' to confirmed-trial: proceed to adapter design with corrections applied. Do not advance to confirmed-adopt until a working probe of EspoCRM v9 REST API (Lead CRUD + auth round-trip) is verified locally per [[curaos-verify-before-build-rule]].

verified.name: Ballerine (KYC/KYB document collection + workflow)
verified.license_confirmed: Partially confirmed, with a material correction. Web SDK (@ballerine/web-sdk, @ballerine/workflow-browser-sdk) is MIT - safe to import. Backend workflows-service is AGPL-3.0 - confirmed. Self-hosting the unmodified backend is legally safe for internal use; distributing a modified backend requires source disclosure. However the candidate framed this as "service-boundary safe" which is accurate ONLY if CuraOS never ships a modified Ballerine backend to customers. For CuraOS on-prem/hybrid deployment models (§4 of AGENTS.md - customer infra), any modification to the AGPL backend that is deployed to customer infrastructure triggers AGPL copyleft disclosure requirements. This is a tighter constraint than "agpl-service-boundary" implies for the CuraOS distribution model.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: FAIL - significantly worse than claimed. The README explicitly states: "The open source repository is currently undergoing a major rebuild and is not actively supported at this time." Multiple independent sources confirm this. The company has pivoted to agentic merchant fraud detection (Scam and Fraud Detection API launched April 2026, Agenticom.org launched May 2026) - a completely different product from document-collection KYC/KYB flows. The original @ballerine/web-sdk is last published 3 years ago. @ballerine/workflow-browser-sdk (v0.6.8) shows recent activity (~8 days ago at search time) but represents the rebuild track, not the stable OSS track. Stars (~8k) are historical, not a maintenance signal for 2026. YC-backed status is real but the company's commercial energy is on their SaaS merchant-risk product, not the OSS infrastructure. OSS adopters are directed to email oss@ballerine.com, which is a support-deprioritization signal.
verified.simpler_alternative: Yes, for the actual CuraOS use case (insurance policy onboarding document collection + lead qualification review workflow). The CuraOS workflow/BPM engine (the core platform primitive per AGENTS.md §5.0) is the correct host for KYC document-step orchestration - it already owns human task + automation + SLA. Document upload steps are form steps in a BPM flow, not a separate KYC infrastructure. The back-office review surface is the crm-insurance-broker app built on the App/Site Builder. The 3rd-party provider slot (per curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule) is better filled by a purpose-built identity verification vendor API (Persona, Onfido, Sumsub, or their self-hosted equivalents) called from a thin NestJS adapter in crm-core, rather than running a second full NestJS service with a competing Prisma schema. Insurance-specific document types (proof of insurability, policy applications) would need custom Ballerine flow definitions anyway - that work lands in CuraOS BPM config instead, which is the generator-first target.
verified.integration_reality: Much harder than claimed. Three concrete blockers: (1) ORM conflict - Ballerine backend uses Prisma (confirmed via issue #2340 Prisma migrations in workflows-service), not Drizzle/MikroORM. Running a sidecar with a competing Prisma schema alongside CuraOS's Drizzle+MikroORM+CNPG stack means two ORM migration systems, two connection pool configurations, and schema ownership ambiguity - this is not "immediately familiar", it is a second persistence stack, which AGENTS.md §9 (single persistence stack rule) forbids. (2) Web SDK is Svelte-compiled, not React - embedding uses a JS mount() call against a DOM element or iframe, not a React component import. This is workable but it is a cross-framework boundary that the generator (ui-app-emit) cannot emit as a native React component; it emits a wrapper div + SDK init script, which breaks the generator-first contract for the UI layer. (3) No Helm/Zarf/air-gap deployment story found - self-hosted is docker-compose only. CuraOS requires Helm + Zarf for every service per curaos-orchestration-rule and curaos-airgap-rule. Adding Ballerine means owning and maintaining the Helm chart and Zarf bundle for a third-party service in a rebuild phase.
verified.notes: Research value: high -- Three disqualifying findings confirmed from independent sources that the candidate's summary understated or missed. (1) Maintenance risk is acute: the OSS repo is in an officially-unsupported rebuild phase while the company has commercially pivoted to merchant fraud SaaS. (2) ORM mismatch (Prisma vs Drizzle/MikroORM) violates the single-persistence-stack rule. (3) No air-gap/Helm story means CuraOS would own the operationalization cost for a project in flux. The correct action is to keep Ballerine on the radar as a reference architecture for KYC flow UX patterns and workflow JSON config shape, but not adopt it. The integration points it would fill (document collection step, review queue) are expressed more cleanly through: BPM engine (flow steps) + a thin NestJS adapter to a BYO identity-verification provider API (Persona/Onfido/Sumsub self-hosted) + the existing crm-insurance-broker app surface. Revisit if Ballerine ships a stable v1 of the rebuilt OSS track with Helm charts and the pivot back to infrastructure is confirmed.

candidates.name: Documenso (self-hosted e-sign service) + @documenso/sdk-typescript
candidates.what: Full-featured self-hosted DocuSign alternative with PKCS#12 digital signatures, multi-signer envelopes, embedded signing, webhooks, and eIDAS/UETA compliance. Fills the gap where esign-core-service already owns the contract layer but needs a BYO-3rd-party provider option (local_vs_3rdparty dual requirement). The sdk-typescript (MIT) is the api-adapter target. Covers taxonomy: erp.recurring.contracts esign surface + contract.signed_status projection.
candidates.license: Service: AGPL-3.0 (Community) / Commercial $250/mo Platform tier for AGPL escape. SDK (@documenso/sdk-typescript): MIT.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: run Documenso container (AGPL CE, PKCS#12 signing passphrase + PostgreSQL required). BYO-3rd-party: point same sdk-typescript at Documenso Cloud or DocuSign/HelloSign via api-adapter shim in esign-core. Dual requirement satisfied.
candidates.maintenance_health: 17k+ stars on main repo, MIT SDK at v0.3.1 (Jul 2025), v2 API in beta, commercial backing (YC), active releases. Requires PostgreSQL - heavier deploy than DocuSeal.
candidates.stack_fit: sdk-typescript is a plain fetch-based TS SDK - direct npm import in NestJS esign-core-service with no wrapper needed. Service deploy = Helm chart + PostgreSQL (CNPG already present). Zod validation wraps SDK response types at the service boundary.
candidates.person_centric_fit: In-app 'Review & sign' step exposed via esign-core ops for the My Agreements / contract-renewal person journey. Shareable self-serve signing link for non-account counterparties. Clinician/admin envelope console for multi-signer status, void, chain-of-custody audit.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: @documenso/sdk-typescript injected via esign-core-service NestJS module as the 'remote provider' branch of the PdfRendererProvider port pattern already established in reports-service. Contract: esign.tsp already owns the SigningRequest/Signature shapes; the sdk bridges to the external service without changing the TypeSpec.
candidates.risks: AGPL service boundary: cannot embed Documenso code in CuraOS build without commercial license. SDK (MIT) is safe to import. v2 API still in beta as of mid-2025 - pin sdk version. PostgreSQL hard-dep on service deploy (already have CNPG). $250/mo Platform tier required if embedding AGPL-escape for commercial tenants.

candidates.name: DocuSeal + @docuseal/api
candidates.what: Lighter-weight self-hosted e-sign alternative (Ruby on Rails backend, SQLite default). ~17k GitHub stars, TypeScript/JS SDK published as @docuseal/api (v1.0.23). Simpler deploy than Documenso; supports more field types, SMS delivery. AGPL-3.0 + Section 7(b) additional terms: API access and embedding require paid Pro On-Premises license ($20/user/month). Best fit as the BYO-3rd-party adapter option behind the esign-core provider port, paired with Documenso as the default self-hosted provider.
candidates.license: AGPL-3.0 + Section 7(b). Embedding/API require $20/user/month Pro On-Premises license.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host option: simpler deploy than Documenso (SQLite ok). BYO-3rd-party: point sdk at DocuSeal Cloud. However API is paywalled even on self-hosted - legal-review needed before production use.
candidates.maintenance_health: 17k stars, fork count 1.7k, npm package v1.0.23 actively maintained, TypeScript SDK active on GitHub (docuseal-js). Ruby stack lighter footprint than Documenso.
candidates.stack_fit: sdk (@docuseal/api) is plain TS, no framework coupling. NestJS module wraps it at provider level. Zod wraps response types. Service is a separate Docker container.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same person journey as Documenso. API covers template-based submission creation, embedded signing widget, webhook events. Field-type breadth (signature, date, text, checkbox, radio, image) supports clinical consent forms.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: @docuseal/api as alternative provider behind the esign-core 'provider port' - switchable via env config in NestJS DI (same contract, swap implementation).
candidates.risks: AGPL + Section 7(b) means API/embedding paywalled even on self-hosted. Community frustration documented (GitHub Discussion #273). Ruby stack is a foreign runtime in a Bun/NestJS shop - container isolation required. SDK depends on remote service availability.

candidates.name: Gotenberg (self-hosted PDF engine)
candidates.what: Docker-based stateless API wrapping Chromium + LibreOffice for HTML/CSS/Markdown -> PDF, DOCX -> PDF, PDF merge/split. Already the chosen PDF engine per ADR-0113 (InMemoryPdfRendererProvider stub confirms the real Gotenberg adapter is injected at modulith level). MIT license, 12k+ GitHub stars, version 8.32+ active. Fills: reports-service PDF rendering (erp.platform.documents 'present-strong'), AVS PDF render, CMS-1500/UB-04 paper claim forms, invoice PDF.
candidates.language: Go (service); HTTP API consumed from any language
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host primary: Helm chart + Docker image, air-gap safe (Zarf-packaged). BYO-3rd-party: swap the PdfRendererProvider NestJS port to point at any hosted HTML-to-PDF service (PDFBolt, WeasyPrint cloud) - the interface already exists in reports-service.
candidates.maintenance_health: 12k GitHub stars, MIT license, v8.32+ released mid-2025, actively maintained by a single author with commercial Gotenberg Cloud offering. Widely production-deployed.
candidates.stack_fit: Already integrated via the PdfRendererProvider interface in reports-service. gotenberg-js-client npm package exists but is Gotenberg v6-era; direct fetch() calls to /forms/chromium/convert/html are simpler and already the pattern. No wrapper needed - the provider interface is the wrapper.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Invisible infrastructure. Enables: patient AVS PDF download, invoice PDF email, CMS-1500 download in patient-app (transparency over own billing), clinical document export.
candidates.generator_first_target: Codegen-emitter-target: the reports-service GotenbergHttpAdapterProvider (the real adapter injected at modulith) should be emitted as part of service scaffold rather than hand-written each time. Port interface already in codegen mold shape.
candidates.risks: LibreOffice route is stateful (one instance per container) - horizontal scaling needs awareness. Chromium adds image size (~1.5GB). Custom fonts must be bundled into the image for HIPAA/clinical PDFs. gotenberg-js-client npm package lags Gotenberg v8 API - use fetch() directly or the official Go client pattern.

candidates.name: @pdfme/generator (JSON-template PDF generation)
candidates.what: MIT-licensed TypeScript-native PDF generator from JSON templates. Works in Node.js and browser. WYSIWYG template designer UI (@pdfme/ui) for business users to edit templates. Actively maintained: v6.1.6 released ~Jun 2026. Covers: form-fillable PDF templates for invoices, discharge summaries, consent forms, quotes - the 'template engine' slot separate from Gotenberg's HTML-render-to-PDF path. Taxonomy: erp.platform.documents template authoring + esign document prep.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (npm import - no service dependency). No BYO-3rd-party equivalent needed; the template JSON stays in CuraOS storage.
candidates.maintenance_health: 3.4k+ GitHub stars, v6.1.6 (Jun 2026 - very recent), weekly downloads ~33k. MIT, community-driven. Requires Node >=16 (Bun compatible).
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm import in NestJS reports-service or a dedicated template-generator-service. `generate(template, inputs)` returns Uint8Array PDF bytes - pipe to SeaweedFS storage via storage-sdk. @pdfme/ui ships as a React component for the admin template designer - plugs directly into the ui-kit / admin web apps.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables: tenant-branded invoice PDFs, discharge summary templates, consent form templates with fillable fields. Template designer in admin surface; person gets correctly branded, pre-populated document.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: @pdfme/generator imported in reports-service behind a TemplateRendererProvider port (complement to PdfRendererProvider). Template JSON stored per-tenant in storage-core. @pdfme/ui React component emitted by ui-app-emit for document-template admin screen.
candidates.risks: Font embedding requires correct setup (Unicode/CJK needs extra font registration). WYSIWYG designer is React-only (fine for CuraOS ui-kit). Not suitable for pixel-perfect HTML/CSS designs - that stays with Gotenberg. Large PDF generation (100+ pages) may be slower than Gotenberg Chromium for complex layouts.

candidates.name: @react-pdf/renderer (React-component PDF generation)
candidates.what: MIT-licensed React-component-based PDF generator. 15.9k stars, v4.5.1 (2025), React 19 support since v4.1.0. Enables building PDF documents with JSX + familiar CSS-subset styling. Server-side rendering via renderToStream/renderToBuffer. Fills: programmatic PDF generation where the template is code (e.g., AVS report, complex multi-section clinical document) rather than a JSON template. Complement to pdfme for code-driven documents.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (npm import).
candidates.maintenance_health: 15.9k stars, 150+ contributors, v4.5.1 published ~Apr 2025, 860k weekly downloads. Well-maintained, React 19 compatible.
candidates.stack_fit: Imports in NestJS service or dedicated PDF worker. renderToBuffer() returns Buffer - pipe to storage-sdk. No DOM required - server-side safe. Known packaging quirks with Bun (not official support but community reports working). Wrap in a try/catch with fallback to Gotenberg for complex rendering.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Best for: AVS (After-Visit Summary) PDF with dynamic clinical sections, patient discharge reports, branded certificates. Code-driven = easy to internationalize (RTL) and add conditional sections.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: imported in reports-service alongside Gotenberg. AVS Composition template renders via react-pdf; standard HTML invoice via Gotenberg. The PdfRendererProvider port can dispatch by document type.
candidates.risks: Not officially Bun-supported (test carefully). CSS subset only - no full CSS3. External font files must be loaded manually. Complex table layouts can be verbose. Bun polyfill may be needed for canvas APIs. GitHub issue #2624 documents packaging/SSR problems worth tracking.

candidates.name: Carbone (document template engine)
candidates.what: npm-importable report/document generator: injects JSON into ODT/DOCX/XLSX/PPTX/PDF templates, outputs any of those formats. Core concept: template files with {{variable}} markers, rendered server-side. Fills: contract/invoice/report template generation from DOCX templates (non-developer business users manage templates in Word/LibreOffice). v3.8.2 on npm (Jun 2026), actively maintained.
candidates.license: Carbone Community License (CCL) - free for internal use and embedding in value-added products. FORBIDDEN as a hosted document-generator-as-a-service offering (i.e., selling Carbone generation as a service).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: npm import into NestJS service (CCL allows embedding in product). BYO-3rd-party: Carbone Cloud API (commercial). CCL restriction: CuraOS as a SaaS must confirm it is not reselling document generation as the primary product offering - legal review needed.
candidates.maintenance_health: GitHub carboneio/carbone active, v3.8.2 Jun 2026, backed by Carbone.io commercial entity. n8n + MCP integrations published 2025-2026. Strong ecosystem.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm import in NestJS. `carbone.render(template, data, options, callback)` - wrappable as an async provider. DOCX/ODT templates stored in storage-core. LibreOffice required for DOCX-to-PDF conversion (already in Gotenberg container - can reuse via Gotenberg's LibreOffice route instead of bundling LibreOffice separately).
candidates.person_centric_fit: Business template rendering for: contracts, insurance policies, quotes. Business users manage templates in Word without developer involvement. Person receives a branded, personalized PDF document.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: Carbone imported as a 'DocxTemplateRenderer' provider alongside pdfme (JSON templates) and Gotenberg (HTML-to-PDF). Template storage managed by documents-core-service.
candidates.risks: CCL is not OSS - legal review required for SaaS resale boundary. LibreOffice dependency for DOCX-to-PDF (mitigate by routing through Gotenberg's existing LibreOffice module). Template format locked to Office XML / ODF - not portable to web-native rendering. Callback-based API (older style) - needs promisify wrapper.

candidates.name: Unstructured (self-hosted OCR/document ETL API)
candidates.what: Open-source Python/FastAPI service for partitioning 26+ file formats (PDF, DOCX, images, HTML, PPTX) into structured JSON elements with layout analysis, table detection, and multi-strategy OCR (hi_res/fast/ocr_only). Official unstructured-js-client (TypeScript) on npm. Self-hosted via Docker. Fills: document-core-service (clean slate) ingestion pipeline, OCR extraction for uploaded patient documents, consent form parsing, lab result PDF ingestion.
candidates.license: Apache-2.0 (open source core). Unstructured Platform (enterprise) is commercial SaaS.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: unstructured-api Docker container on K8s. BYO-3rd-party: point js-client at Unstructured Platform API (SaaS). Dual satisfied.
candidates.maintenance_health: unstructured: 9k+ stars, Apache-2.0, actively maintained 2024-2025, commercial backing (Unstructured). unstructured-js-client on GitHub, npm published. Docker image: downloads.unstructured.io registry.
candidates.stack_fit: unstructured-js-client is a plain TypeScript HTTP client - direct import in NestJS document-core-service. Service URL is env-configurable (local or Platform API). Partition results are JSON arrays of typed elements (Title/NarrativeText/Table/Image) - Zod schemas wrap them at ingestion boundary.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables: ingest scanned patient records into structured data (problems, meds, allergies from faxed/uploaded documents), parse insurance cards, extract lab values from PDF results for the patient-experience read model.
candidates.generator_first_target: Service: document-core-service (currently clean slate) becomes the owner of the Unstructured provider port. SDK pkg: @unstructured-io/unstructured-client (or js-client) imported as the ingestion provider. Emits document.partitioned events to Kafka for downstream consumers.
candidates.risks: Python service is a foreign runtime (container isolation required - same pattern as Gotenberg). hi_res strategy requires GPU or significant CPU. Image is large (~2-3GB with inference models). Unstructured's open-source vs Platform API feature split is moving target - some enrichments (table structure, VLM OCR) require Platform. Air-gap: models must be pre-downloaded.

candidates.name: Tesseract.js (pure-JS OCR, lightweight)
candidates.what: WebAssembly port of Tesseract OCR engine, pure JavaScript/TypeScript, 100+ languages, no native deps. 36k GitHub stars, MIT license. Fills: lightweight OCR for CuraOS in environments where Unstructured container is overkill (e.g., extracting text from uploaded images in personal-app, processing single-page insurance cards, extracting handwritten field values from scanned forms).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (npm import, WASM). No network dependency.
candidates.maintenance_health: 36k stars, Apache-2.0, v5.x active, Node >=16 required, 2025-maintained. Does NOT support PDF input directly (requires PDF-to-image step first).
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm import in document-core-service or inline in any NestJS service that needs quick OCR. Works in Bun (WASM). Accuracy significantly lower than Unstructured hi_res for complex layouts. Use only for single-column text / simple image extraction.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Edge case: extract text from scanned insurance card photo uploaded via personal-app. Not suitable for clinical document ingestion pipeline (use Unstructured for that).
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: secondary OCR provider behind the document-core-service provider port (fast/simple path, Unstructured as hi_res path).
candidates.risks: Does not parse PDFs directly. Poor on complex layouts/tables. No layout analysis. Accuracy gap vs transformer-based models for clinical/structured documents is significant. Only appropriate as a lightweight fallback or for simple image->text use cases.

candidates.name: pdf-lib / @cantoo/pdf-lib (PDF manipulation)
candidates.what: Pure JavaScript library for creating and modifying existing PDFs (fill AcroForms, merge, overlay, embed fonts/images). MIT license. Fills: PDF form filling for CMS-1500/UB-04 (overlay field values onto existing government form PDFs), PDF merging for report assembly, watermarking signed documents. Distinct from generation (Gotenberg/pdfme) - this is for surgical manipulation of existing PDF bytes.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (npm import).
candidates.maintenance_health: CAUTION: original pdf-lib (Hopding) last published Nov 2021, maintainer unreachable (HN thread 2025). @cantoo/pdf-lib fork: also inactive per Snyk (no npm release in 12 months). pdfme/pdf-lib fork exists but newer. Community consensus: use for PDF manipulation (merge/overlay) where the task is simple; migrate if maintenance stays dead.
candidates.stack_fit: Import in @curaos/x12-sdk or reports-service for CMS-1500/UB-04 box-coordinate overlay. No framework coupling. Bun compatible (pure JS). For complex form filling, pdfme's form support may be a better maintained alternative.
candidates.person_centric_fit: CMS-1500/UB-04 box fills for billing-admin; patient gets download of their own submitted claim form. Watermarking signed contracts before storage.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg: imported in @curaos/x12-sdk (claim-forms emitter) for CMS-1500/UB-04 overlay, and optionally in reports-service for PDF merge/assembly.
candidates.risks: CRITICAL: original project unmaintained since Nov 2021. @cantoo fork also inactive. pdfme/pdf-lib fork is newer but small. Evaluate @cantoo/pdf-lib v2.5.3 security posture before use. For new development, prefer pdfme's form-fill capabilities or route CMS-1500 through Gotenberg HTML-to-PDF (government form HTML templates exist publicly). Use pdf-lib only for precise box-coordinate overlay where HTML rendering is impractical.

verified.name: Documenso (self-hosted e-sign) + @documenso/sdk-typescript
verified.license_confirmed: Partially confirmed. SDK (@documenso/sdk-typescript) is MIT -- clean. Service is AGPL-3.0 CE -- confirmed. BUT: the "safe AGPL service boundary" framing understates risk. AGPL network copyleft triggers when tenants interact with the Documenso service over a network (embedded signing iframes, direct API from tenant frontend). Multi-tenant commercial SaaS use requires either: (a) no direct tenant-to-Documenso network interaction (proxy-only via esign-core-service), or (b) $250/mo commercial Platform license. Additionally, embedded signing is an EE (proprietary, non-AGPL) feature -- not available under AGPL CE without commercial license. A formal legal review is required before any commercial tenant deployment. Air-gap: AGPL is compatible with distribution, no additional obligation beyond source availability. No SPDX mismatch found.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Service: confirmed active. v2.11.0 released May 2026; regular cadence throughout 2025-2026. API v2 went stable November 2025. CONCERN: v2.11.0 Docker image was NOT published to GHCR (issue #2951 open) -- latest container image is v2.10.1. This is a Helm/air-gap ops reliability gap. SDK: borderline. @documenso/sdk-typescript last release v0.3.1 was July 1 2025 -- ~10 months behind the service (v2.11.0). API features added post-July 2025 require raw HTTP, not SDK calls. No Documenso-specific critical CVEs confirmed; CVEs attributed in search (2026-29045, 2026-22817, 2026-22818) are Hono framework CVEs, not Documenso product CVEs. No official Helm chart exists -- only a community chart at v0.0.6 (rizkyfaza20/documenso on ArtifactHub).
verified.simpler_alternative: DocuSeal (AGPL-3.0, 17k+ stars) covers most of the same surface: REST API, embeddable widget, self-hosted, air-gap capable, SQLite-default (no mandatory PostgreSQL), ~256 MB RAM floor vs ~500 MB for Documenso. Lacks PKCS#12 digital signatures and formal eIDAS compliance. If PKCS#12/eIDAS is a hard requirement for the esign lane, DocuSeal does not substitute. If it is not (simple electronic signatures suffice), DocuSeal should be evaluated first -- it is strictly simpler to deploy and has no unsettled AGPL/EE license ambiguity. Check eIDAS requirement against esign-core Requirements.md before committing to Documenso.
verified.integration_reality: SDK is fetch-based with confirmed Bun compatibility (native Bun.file support documented). NestJS module wrapping is straightforward -- no incompatibilities found. Zod boundary wrapping is still real work (SDK response types need mapping to TypeSpec/Zod shapes). SDK v0.3.1 lags service by ~10 months, meaning newer API endpoints (bulk get-by-ID, search envelopes) require raw HTTP calls, not SDK calls. The generator-first pattern is not impeded -- SDK is an npm package, no codegen lock-in. The effort estimate of 'M' is optimistic: add official Helm chart authoring (none exists), AGPL legal review, and SDK gap coverage via raw HTTP for post-July-2025 API surface.
verified.notes: Downgrade from 'adopt' to 'confirmed-trial' because: (1) AGPL commercial-tenant legal review is a required pre-condition before any live deployment -- not a minor risk; (2) no official Helm chart exists, making air-gap/Zarf deployment a first-class build task; (3) SDK is 10 months stale relative to the service; (4) embedded signing (the primary UX feature cited in 'person_centric_fit') is an EE feature requiring $250/mo commercial license. Trial path: wire the MIT SDK in esign-core-service against the BYO-3rd-party branch first (zero AGPL exposure). Run legal review on multi-tenant AGPL self-host scenario in parallel. Only promote to full adopt after (a) legal sign-off obtained, (b) official or CuraOS-owned Helm chart written and Zarf-packaged, (c) SDK gap inventory completed against current v2 API surface. If PKCS#12 is not a hard requirement, re-evaluate DocuSeal before trial.

verified.name: DocuSeal + @docuseal/api
verified.license_confirmed: AGPL-3.0 + Section 7(b) confirmed accurate. API access, embedding, SSO, and webhooks are ALL paywalled even on self-hosted - require Pro On-Premises license at $20/user/month plus $0.20/document for API completions. Community (free self-hosted) tier is UI-only. This is NOT a usable self-hosted API integration without paid seats. Additionally confirmed: Pro tier ships as a private Docker image (not buildable from public source), which creates a hard air-gap blocker - CuraOS cannot bundle a private proprietary image in Zarf packages. AGPL copyleft also triggers if DocuSeal source is modified and served; unmodified use for internal signing is cleaner but the API paywall still applies. License is materially worse than claimed framing of "self-hosted option".
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active: v3.1.1 released June 22, 2026. Regular cadence confirmed. 17k stars, 1.7k forks accurate. CVE details page exists on cvedetails.com but no critical exploited vulnerabilities surfaced in 2025-2026 searches. Ruby on Rails + Sidekiq + Redis runtime confirmed - three containers required for production (app + postgres + redis), not the lightweight "SQLite ok" framing. SQLite is dev-only; production concurrency requires PostgreSQL + Redis. Maintenance health is fine; the framing of "lighter footprint" is misleading for production use.
verified.integration_reality: SDK (@docuseal/api v1.0.23) is pure TypeScript with no native bindings - Bun-compatible. NestJS DI wrapping is straightforward. Zod can wrap responses. No generator-first conflict at the SDK layer. BUT: the SDK is useless without a Pro license key to call the API against. No official Helm chart for K8s deployment (unlike Documenso which has a tracked issue but no chart either). Air-gap is doubly blocked: Pro image is private + license key validation mechanism in offline mode is undocumented (Cloudron forum confirms Pro requires custom Docker app, not public image). Ruby on Rails is a foreign runtime requiring container isolation and Redis as a mandatory sidecar - not a Bun/NestJS-native fit at the service level. Integration claim is optimistic: SDK works, but everything above it (license, air-gap, K8s ops, Ruby sidecar) fights the stack.
verified.simpler_alternative: Documenso is the strictly better self-hosted default: AGPL-3.0 with NO Section 7(b) paywall, full API access free on self-hosted, TypeScript/Next.js native stack (closer to Bun ecosystem), TypeScript SDK exists (github.com/documenso/sdk-typescript), PostgreSQL-native, community Helm chart issue open (#1345). Documenso satisfies the local-vs-3rdparty duality without a license key gate. DocuSeal can remain documented as the BYO-3rd-party cloud option ONLY (point SDK at DocuSeal Cloud for teams that already pay), but should not be the self-hosted provider behind the esign-core port.
verified.notes: Candidate recommendation was "trial" with self-hosted as primary. Downgraded to reference because: (1) API paywall on self-hosted makes it non-functional as a self-hosted provider without paid seats - violates local-vs-3rdparty-rule's self-hosted-first requirement; (2) private Pro Docker image is a hard Zarf/air-gap blocker per curaos-airgap-rule; (3) Ruby+Redis sidecar adds foreign runtime ops cost with no offsetting benefit over Documenso; (4) Documenso covers the same use case without these constraints. DocuSeal is valid as a REFERENCE entry in the esign-core provider registry for the BYO-3rd-party cloud path (teams already paying DocuSeal Cloud can plug in their API key). Do not use as the default self-hosted provider. The generator-first SDK wrapper can accept DocuSeal as a named provider behind the same esign-core port, but only for cloud-pointed use. Legal review is still required before enabling the self-hosted path given Section 7(b) ambiguity around embedding in a multi-tenant SaaS context (AGPL copyleft + additional terms compound).

verified.name: Gotenberg (self-hosted PDF engine)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed via GitHub (candidate and GitHub agree). ADR-0113 §4.9 says "Apache 2.0" - minor doc inconsistency, both are safe-permissive, no SaaS distribution restriction either way. Air-gap and local-vs-3rdparty distribution: clear.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. v8.32 released 2026 (exact date not pinned in search results but confirmed post-CVE-cluster). Single maintainer (bus factor 1) - real risk, mitigated by: commercial Gotenberg Cloud offering creating sustainability incentive, 12k+ stars, production use at scale. Four critical CVEs disclosed in 2026 (CVE-2026-42589 CVSS 9.8 RCE, CVE-2026-40281 CVSS 10.0, CVE-2026-42596 CVSS 9.4 SSRF, CVE-2026-27018 auth bypass) - ALL fixed in v8.32. v8.32 also reverts SSRF protection defaults back to permissive; operators must explicitly set denyPrivateIps=true + denyPublicIps flags - mandatory for internal K8s deployment. Pin to >= 8.32 and set SSRF flags; digest-pin the image per [[curaos-version-pinning-rule]].
verified.integration_reality: Clean. PdfRendererProvider port (reports-service/src/providers/pdf/pdf-renderer.provider.interface.ts) and InMemoryPdfRendererProvider stub confirmed in codebase. The real Gotenberg adapter is NOT yet written - stub is in place. Integration = one fetch()-based NestJS provider implementing renderPdf(): multipart POST to /forms/chromium/convert/html, receive PDF bytes. No gotenberg-js-client npm package needed (v6-era, lags v8 API); raw fetch() is already the established pattern per the interface. Codegen target confirmed: GotenbergHttpAdapterProvider should be emitted by the reports-service scaffold emitter, not hand-written. ADR-0113 §4.9 is the formal decision record. No new npm deps required.
verified.simpler_alternative: No. PDFBox (already chosen) covers programmatic structured PDFs but cannot render JS/charts. WeasyPrint: no JS support, 100s latency on complex docs (rejected in ADR-0113). wkhtmltopdf: unmaintained. For HTML+chart fidelity PDF at the reports-service + AVS + CMS-1500 + invoice use cases, Gotenberg (Chromium) is the minimum viable choice, not over-engineering.
verified.notes: TRIAL not ADOPT because: (1) GotenbergHttpAdapterProvider is not yet written or emitted by codegen - adoption is partial until the real adapter lands and codegen emits it. (2) The 2026 CVE cluster is a yellow flag on the security track record; a single-maintainer project with four critical CVEs in one year warrants a trial period with digest pinning and a Renovate auto-PR gate. (3) v8.32 SSRF defaults are permissive by default - internal K8s deployment MUST set denyPrivateIps=true + appropriate denyPublicIps flags in Helm values or the service is SSRF-vulnerable from inside the cluster. (4) LibreOffice path (DOCX->PDF) requires one replica per concurrent conversion - irrelevant for primary HTML path but must be documented in ops runbook. (5) Chainguard hardened image exists (catalog.redhat.com + chainguard.dev confirmed) - prefer chainguard/gotenberg over gotenberg/gotenberg:latest for HIPAA deployments. (6) Community Helm chart (MaikuMori/helm-charts) is not official - evaluate for Zarf packaging; alternative is a first-party Helm chart in curaos/ops/. Upgrade path to confirmed-adopt: codegen emits GotenbergHttpAdapterProvider, ops Helm chart is first-party or vetted, SSRF flags confirmed in staging Helm values, 30-day clean CVE window on >= 8.32.

verified.name: @pdfme/generator (JSON-template PDF generation)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution. npm import only - no SaaS dependency. Cloud service is optional/separate; the npm package is unconstrained MIT. Safe-permissive verdict holds.
verified.maintenance_confirmed:
Confirmed active. v6.1.6 published ~Jun 2026 (search result: "11 hours ago" at search time). Weekly downloads ~33k confirmed. GitHub stars 3.4k-4.3k range (some search results differ; ballpark confirmed). Active release cadence verified.

SECURITY FLAGS - two advisories found, both relevant:

1. CVE-2025-53626 / GHSA-54xv-94qv-2gfg: Sandbox escape + prototype pollution in expression evaluator. Affects @pdfme/common 5.2.0-5.4.0. Fixed in 5.4.1+. CVSS 3.1 (low-medium). XSS + prototype pollution via dynamic expression feature.

2. GHSA-pgx6-7jcq-2qff (SSRF): Unvalidated URL fetch in getB64BasePdf when basePdf is attacker-controlled. Fixed in @pdfme/common 5.5.10+. SSRF attack surface is real in SSR/NestJS context - the service fetches arbitrary URLs if templates accept user-controlled basePdf values.

Both are PATCHED in v6.x. But they reveal a pattern: the expression evaluation and template URL fetching features are active attack surfaces if CuraOS ever lets tenants or end-users author templates directly. The SSRF advisory is the more dangerous one for a server-side NestJS service generating documents from user-supplied templates.
verified.integration_reality:
Partially optimistic. Realistic assessment:

BONA FIDE: `generate(template, inputs)` returns Uint8Array - pipes to SeaweedFS storage-sdk with no glue. NestJS DI wrapping is trivial. Bun runs Node-compatible npm packages; no known Bun incompatibility found for pdfme.

GAPS vs CLAIM:

1. local-vs-3rdparty rule FAIL: pdfme has no swappable provider abstraction. The PDF engine is pdf-lib, hardwired. There is no "BYO 3rd-party PDF template provider" path. CuraOS rule requires every integratable area to expose a provider interface (e.g., PdfTemplateProvider) with a local and a 3rd-party implementation. The candidate claims a TemplateRendererProvider port - that port does NOT exist in pdfme; it must be hand-built as a CuraOS wrapper. The candidate is proposing CuraOS build this abstraction over pdfme, which is valid, but the claim should be explicit: pdfme is the LOCAL impl; CuraOS must still define the provider interface and wire a "BYO 3rd-party" slot (e.g., DocuSign template API, HubSpot PDF API).

2. @pdfme/ui is React-only and pulls in the full React runtime. The ui-kit already uses React so this is not a blocker, but it cannot be used in any server-side or non-React context. Admin template designer = fine. Mobile = not applicable.

3. SSRF threat model for CuraOS: consent forms and discharge summaries will have templates authored by clinical admins (trusted, not end-users). If the basePdf field is populated from DB records under admin control only, SSRF risk is low. If tenants can upload arbitrary template JSON from external sources, need input sanitization on basePdf before calling generate(). HIPAA context amplifies the blast radius of any SSRF or data exfiltration.

4. Expression evaluation: If CuraOS templates use the {} expression syntax with user-provided inputs, the prototype pollution surface (CVE-2025-53626) is relevant even in patched v6, as expression evaluators are historically re-exploitable. Recommend disabling dynamic expressions for PHI-bearing templates.

5. TypeSpec/Zod: no native pdfme Zod schema or TypeSpec contract. CuraOS must wrap generate() behind a typed service boundary and validate template JSON with Zod before passing to pdfme. Template JSON schema is pdfme-proprietary; TypeSpec cannot describe it without a custom scalar. Not a blocker but means template storage format is pdfme-vendor-locked.
verified.simpler_alternative: Gotenberg (already in CuraOS stack per candidate taxonomy) covers HTML-to-PDF with full CSS pixel-perfect fidelity. If the use case is templated documents, a simpler path is: Handlebars/Mustache HTML templates stored in storage-core, rendered to PDF by the existing Gotenberg service. No new dependency, no expression-eval attack surface, no React designer constraint. Tradeoff: no WYSIWYG admin designer (business users get a code template editor, not a drag-drop canvas). If the WYSIWYG designer is a real user requirement (clinical admins authoring consent form layouts without developer involvement), pdfme fills a genuine gap Gotenberg cannot. If templates are developer-authored and rarely changed, Gotenberg + HTML templates is simpler and already present.
verified.notes:
CONDITIONAL TRIAL, not full adopt. Conditions before landing in generator-first codegen:

1. Wrap behind a PdfTemplateProvider interface in reports-service (or template-generator-service). Local impl = pdfme. 3rd-party slot = explicit stub (satisfies local-vs-3rdparty rule; even an unimplemented ExternalPdfTemplateProvider stub + config toggle is sufficient to not violate the rule).

2. Validate basePdf fields from DB before passing to generate() - never pass raw user-supplied URLs. Add a Zod allowlist check on basePdf format (data URI or internal storage URL only).

3. Disable expression evaluation ({} syntax) for any template that touches PHI fields. Document this restriction in the template-generator-service AGENTS.md.

4. @pdfme/ui React component is fine for admin designer screen - emit via ui-app-emit with an explicit flag in the generator template so it's not silently bundled into all apps.

5. Pin to v6.1.6 exact. SSRF and sandbox-escape advisories are patched here; do not float ~6.x without Renovate alert review.

candidates.name: Meilisearch (Community Edition)
candidates.what: Rust-based full-text + hybrid (BM25 + vector/semantic) search engine with typo tolerance, facets, tenant tokens for multi-tenancy. Fills taxonomy gaps: platform.search (search-service FHIR-param mapping enrichment from weaker-than-source-enrichments), patient/record search UX (patient-search), and the FilterBar/SearchShell ui-kit archetype contract lint gate (ui-kit-archetypes). Covers the `search-service` local module.
candidates.license: MIT (Community Edition, v1.28+ binary). Enterprise Edition (sharding only) = BUSL-1.1, production-requires commercial. CE is safe-permissive for self-host.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~50k GitHub stars. v1.28.0 released May 2026. Active weekly releases. Backed by Meilisearch SAS (Paris). Dual-binary split (CE/EE) added in v1.28.
candidates.stack_fit: Official `meilisearch` npm package (TypeScript). `nestjs-meilisearch` v3.0.0 NestJS module available. Self-hosted single binary or Docker. Multi-tenant via signed tenant tokens (JWT, scoped filter_by rules). Hybrid search (BM25 + vector via OpenAI/HuggingFace embeddings) is stable since v1.9+; semanticRatio tunable. Integrates directly with search-service NestJS module via HTTP SDK. Drizzle/Postgres stays source of truth; Meilisearch is the index layer.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted CE (first-party, Zarf/Helm/K8s compatible). BYO: Meilisearch Cloud SaaS at meilisearch.com. Both supported.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Patient/user-facing instant search (sub-50ms) with plain-language typo tolerance. Tenant tokens scope each person to their own data. FHIR search-param semantics mappable on top of the index layer.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/search-sdk wrapping meilisearch-js) + codegen-emitter (search-emit: index definition per .tsp entity, FHIR search-param mapping table, FilterBar contract lint gate)
candidates.risks: EE-only feature is sharding (only needed beyond ~100M docs per tenant). Hybrid vector is production-stable but semantics need embeddings sidecar (OpenAI or local model). License split (CE MIT / EE BUSL) requires tracking which binary is deployed.

candidates.name: pgvector (PostgreSQL extension)
candidates.what: First-class vector search (HNSW + IVFFlat indexes) inside existing CNPG/PostgreSQL. Fills: RAG/semantic search on clinical records, FHIR-param hybrid ranking, terminology similarity, CDS cohort embedding queries. Gaps covered: vector dimension of search-service (weaker-than-source-enrichments FHIR search-param) and future CDS embeddings (cds-rules-engine analog). Taxonomy: platform.search, healthstack.quality (CDS embedding).
candidates.license: PostgreSQL License (permissive, equivalent to MIT/BSD). Extension hosted under pgvector MIT.
candidates.maintenance_health: v0.9.0 (early 2026) added sparse vector support and HNSW improvements. Actively maintained by Andrew Kane. CNPG supports pgvector extension natively. Widely deployed in production at 1M-5M vector scale.
candidates.stack_fit: Zero new service: enabled as a CNPG PostgreSQL extension. Drizzle ORM supports vector column via drizzle-orm/pg-core vector() type. NestJS service calls Drizzle as normal. HNSW index: p95 ~2-5ms at 100K vectors, ~8-15ms at 1M vectors on 4vCPU/8GB. Sparse vector support (v0.9) enables BM25+dense hybrid within Postgres. No new operator required.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted: CNPG extension (first-party). No BYO-3rd-party option needed - it IS Postgres. RDS/Neon/Supabase also offer it if cloud option is wanted.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables semantic 'find similar records', 'did you mean', personalized ranking on patient/user data without leaving Postgres boundary. PHI stays inside CNPG isolation.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (add vector() column to .tsp entities that opt in via @vectorEmbedded decorator; drizzle schema emit adds HNSW index migration)
candidates.risks: Beyond 5M vectors per tenant, consider Qdrant sidecar. Embedding generation requires a model sidecar (local or remote). Sparse+dense hybrid via pgvector 0.9 is newer; test recall vs Qdrant for FHIR-param semantics.

candidates.name: Apache Superset (embedded analytics)
