candidates.what: Self-hosted BI/dashboard platform with embedded SDK (iframe + guest token + RLS). Fills: reports-service Superset-embed path (already referenced locally but gaps exist in multi-tenant RLS wiring + NestJS token minting). Covers taxonomy: platform-reporting-builder (Superset-embed slice of reports-service), healthcare.platform.reports-dashboards, crm-insurance-broker.platform.reporting. Does NOT replace the ReportDefinition/field-catalog generator gap - that stays in reports-sdk.
candidates.maintenance_health: 60k+ GitHub stars. Active Apache top-level project. Backed by Preset.io (managed offering). Regular monthly releases. Large contributor base.
candidates.stack_fit: NestJS backend calls POST /api/v1/security/guest_token/ with service-account credentials, returns short-lived JWT scoped to tenant dashboards + RLS clause (e.g. tenant_id='X'). Frontend embeds via @superset-ui/embedded-sdk iframe. Wrapper needed: NestJS SupersetService that mints tokens per request. Does NOT plug into Drizzle/TypeSpec directly - it reads from its own DB (Postgres) that mirrors or connects to the data warehouse layer.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted: Docker/Helm/K8s (Zarf-packageable). BYO: Preset.io managed Superset. Both viable.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing 'my analytics' views (patient outcome trends, claim history charts, invoice aging) served as scoped guest-token dashboards. Management surface: full BI explorer for admin/clinical users. Same Superset instance, different guest token scopes.
candidates.generator_first_target: service/ui-kit (SupersetService in reports-service + ui-app-emit ReportViewer archetype wired to embedded-sdk)
candidates.risks: Operational weight (Python/Redis/Celery/Postgres stack). Multi-tenant RLS must be wired per dashboard - easy to misconfigure. Superset UI shows org-centric analytics by default; person-facing re-centering is done at the tenant token + RLS layer, not in the Superset UI itself. Not air-gap friendly without bundling Python deps.

candidates.name: Typesense
candidates.what: High-QPS in-memory fuzzy search engine with scoped API keys for multi-tenancy, native vector + hybrid search, and Raft-based HA clustering. Alternative to Meilisearch for higher-throughput search (10K+ QPS) or when HA clustering is the primary constraint. Covers same taxonomy as Meilisearch (platform.search, patient-search). Reference/trial over adopt because Meilisearch is the better default for CuraOS's scale and license position.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~22k GitHub stars. Latest v30.2 (April 2026). Active. Self-funded company (Typesense Inc). No outside VC pressure visible.
candidates.stack_fit: Official `typesense` npm client (TypeScript, v3.0.6). NestJS integration via community packages or direct HTTP. Scoped API keys (HMAC-signed with embedded filter_by) = production-grade multi-tenancy. Hybrid search: keyword + vector in one query. HA via Raft (3-node). GPL-3 server: OK to self-host as a service, but must not link server code into CuraOS build.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted binary/Docker (first-party). Typesense Cloud SaaS as BYO option.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same as Meilisearch. Scoped keys let person search only their own records. Higher QPS headroom useful if patient/user concurrency is high.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (same @curaos/search-sdk abstraction layer that can swap Meilisearch for Typesense backend)
candidates.risks: GPL-3 server means cannot include Typesense binary in CuraOS images without verifying GPL compliance chain. Lower GitHub star count vs Meilisearch. Single-node write (Raft = HA but not write-sharded). If Meilisearch CE meets QPS needs, Typesense adds no value.

candidates.name: OpenSearch
candidates.what: Apache 2.0 fork of Elasticsearch 7.10 by AWS. Full-text + analytics + ML (neural search plugin) at large scale. Relevant for: FHIR search-param semantics at clinical scale, clinical document indexing, and the 'open search analytics' dimension of reports-service (if Superset is insufficient for operational analytics). Covers: search-service FHIR-param mapping, platform-reporting operational logs queries. Best when data volume, complex aggregations, or FHIR-native search behavior (like `_includes`, compartment search) are needed.
candidates.maintenance_health: Backed by AWS. OpenSearch 2.x active. Large contributor ecosystem (AWS, Aiven, SAP). @opensearch-project/opensearch npm client TypeScript-generated. Well-maintained docs.
candidates.stack_fit: npm client `@opensearch-project/opensearch` Apache-2.0. NestJS wraps via service class. Multi-tenancy via index-level access control + document-level security plugin (Apache 2.0 in OpenSearch, unlike Elasticsearch's paid tier). JVM overhead significant: each node 2-4GB RAM minimum. Use when Meilisearch hits limits (complex aggregations, FHIR search `$everything`, analytics queries).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted Docker/K8s (Zarf-packageable). AWS OpenSearch Service as BYO cloud option. HAPI-FHIR natively integrates with both.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing clinical search ('my records', 'find my encounter') at FHIR compartment scope. Management: clinical document search across patient population, quality measure cohort queries.
candidates.generator_first_target: service (optional search-service backend adapter for OpenSearch; search-sdk abstracts Meilisearch vs OpenSearch behind same interface)
candidates.risks: Heavyweight: JVM, significant Helm/ops complexity vs Meilisearch single binary. Multi-tenancy security plugin requires careful config. For CuraOS's current scale, Meilisearch CE is simpler. OpenSearch is the upgrade path, not the starting point.

candidates.name: Qdrant
candidates.what: Rust vector DB with payload filtering, sparse+dense hybrid search, and tenant isolation via payload index. Fills the vector search gap when pgvector is insufficient (>5M vectors, higher recall requirements, dedicated vector service). Covers: taxonomy platform.search (semantic dimension), CDS embedding queries, terminology similarity beyond SNOMED tree. Upgrade path from pgvector, not the starting point.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~22k GitHub stars. v1.18 client (2026). Actively developed by Qdrant GmbH (Berlin). Native cloud at cloud.qdrant.io. Single binary, Docker-friendly.
candidates.stack_fit: Official `@qdrant/js-client-rest` (Apache-2.0, TypeScript). NestJS wrapper as a thin service module. Payload filtering is part of the indexing pipeline (unlike Weaviate post-filter) = fast filtered vector search. Operates alongside Postgres: Postgres is source of truth, Qdrant is the vector index. Benchmark P99 at 10M vectors: ~12ms vs Weaviate ~16ms, Milvus ~18ms.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted single binary/Docker/Helm (Zarf-packageable). Qdrant Cloud as BYO cloud option.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Semantic 'find similar clinical notes', 'recommended care actions', 'similar patients' (PHI-gated). Person-facing: 'related health topics' search. Management: cohort similarity for quality measures.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/vector-sdk wrapping @qdrant/js-client-rest, switchable with pgvector adapter)
candidates.risks: Adds a second persistence system alongside Postgres. For <5M vectors, pgvector is operationally simpler with no loss. Only adopt Qdrant when pgvector benchmarks show insufficient recall or latency in profiling.

candidates.name: Cube (semantic layer / headless BI)
candidates.what: Open-source TypeScript+Rust semantic layer: defines metrics, dimensions, joins once in code; exposes SQL, REST, GraphQL, and MCP APIs to downstream consumers (Superset, embedded charts, AI agents). Fills: the field-catalog-from-contracts generator gap in platform-reporting-builder (ReportDefinition field resolution) and the semantic consistency layer between reports-service and Superset. Taxonomy: platform-reporting-builder (semantic/field-catalog slice).
candidates.license: Apache 2.0 (Cube Core). Cube Cloud = commercial SaaS.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~18k GitHub stars. Cube 2.x actively developed. Backed by Cube Dev Inc (YC W20). Long-term support (LTS) for Cube Core announced 2025. SQL/REST/GraphQL/MCP APIs all production stable.
candidates.stack_fit: NestJS calls Cube REST/SQL API to resolve metric queries. Cube data model in TypeScript (cube.js files) defines entities and joins from the same Postgres DB that Drizzle manages. Pre-aggregations cached in CubeStore (Rust). Superset can connect to Cube via SQL API as a data source. No Drizzle-native integration but Postgres compatibility means Cube reads same tables. TypeSpec contract -> Cube data model generation is a generator gap that can be filled.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted Docker/Helm (Zarf-packageable). Cube Cloud as BYO managed option.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing KPI home ('my metrics': my outstanding balance, my care gaps count, my order status) served via Cube metric definitions scoped by tenant/user. Management: full BI metric governance preventing definitional drift across services.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (cube-emit: generate .js cube data model stubs from .tsp entity contracts; wires into reports-service field-catalog generator closure)
candidates.risks: Additional service to operate (CubeStore + Cube API). Value only materialises once ReportDefinition + field-catalog generator closure exists. If reports-service stays Superset-only, Cube is premature. Start only if semantic consistency across 40+ services is a pain point.

candidates.name: cql-execution (CQF/HL7 JS CQL engine)
candidates.what: JavaScript/TypeScript library for executing Clinical Quality Language (CQL) expressions against ELM JSON artifacts. Direct fill for ecqm-quality-engine gap (taxonomy: healthcare.ancillary.public-health-reporting, healthstack-quality-service). Covers eCQM measure execution, population-criteria eval (IPP/DENOM/NUMER/DENEX/etc), and FHIR-based CQL logic via cql-exec-fhir PatientSource. Identified in gap-analysis as the TS port target for the pophealth/HDS Ruby engine.
candidates.maintenance_health: Maintained by HL7 CQFramework community. v3.3.0 on npm. Active: CMS eCQM 2025/2026 content repositories use it. Not backed by a commercial vendor - community/HL7 governance. Stable but not prolific-release cadence.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm import into healthstack-quality-service. `cql-execution` + `cql-exec-fhir` (FHIR PatientSource) compose to evaluate any eCQM measure against FHIR R4 patient data. No NestJS wrapper needed beyond a QualityMeasureService class. Pairs with cqf-ruler (Java, run-as-background-service) for HQMF import and QRDA export - exactly the C-mode pattern the ecqm gap-analysis prescribes.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: npm-import (runs inside NestJS process). No self-hosted service needed for evaluation. CQF-Ruler (Java sidecar, Docker) handles HQMF/QRDA conversion per gap plan.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing: 'my care gaps' card (consent-gated, derived from same quality engine). Management: population dashboard with IPP/DENOM/NUMER breakdowns, QRDA III submission, provider/practice quality scores.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (encapsulate cql-execution + cql-exec-fhir in @curaos/quality-sdk; codegen-emitter adds measure contract stubs per .tsp quality entity)
candidates.risks: CQL ELM JSON must be pre-compiled from CQL text (requires cql-to-elm Java tool or CQF-Ruler as sidecar). Measure definitions need to track CMS annual updates. Community governance (no vendor SLA). FHIR PatientSource performance at large cohort scale untested - may need batching strategy.

candidates.name: Evidence (BI-as-code, embedded reports)
candidates.what: SQL + Markdown framework for building version-controlled, embedded data reports and dashboards. Fills: platform-reporting-builder's scheduled/static report delivery slice (ReportDefinition scheduled rendering), and the 'reports as code' pattern for internal/clinical operational reports. Alternative to fully dynamic Superset for the subset of reports that are best expressed as code artifacts (eCQM reports, billing summaries, operational KPIs). Taxonomy: platform-reporting-builder (scheduled/static slice).
candidates.license: MIT (core framework). Evidence Studio (web IDE) = commercial.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~4.5k GitHub stars. Active development. Evidence Studio launched 2026. MIT core ongoing. Small but focused team.
candidates.stack_fit: Reports authored as .md + SQL files; compiled to static site or embedded via Evidence SDK. Row-level security and API-driven session management for per-tenant embedding. Deploys to any static host or own infra. Does NOT plug into Drizzle directly - queries Postgres via SQL. Weaker fit for dynamic user-authored reports (Superset is better there); stronger fit for developer-defined BI artifacts checked into version control alongside service code.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (static deploy or Node server). Evidence Cloud as BYO option.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing static reports: my encounter summary, my lab trends. Less suited for interactive ad-hoc queries. Management: quality measure reports, billing summaries checked into code alongside QRDA output.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (report-emit: scaffold .md report templates per service contract entity; wired into reports-service scheduled job output)
candidates.risks: Static/build-time model limits interactivity. Evidence Studio (web IDE) is commercial. Limited star count vs Superset. For fully interactive ad-hoc analytics, Superset is the better choice. Evidence is the 'reports-as-code' complement, not a Superset replacement.

candidates.name: Metabase
candidates.what: Self-hosted BI tool with a visual query builder and dashboards. Listed in research seeds for comparison. Covered here to document the rejection verdict. AGPL-3 license on the open-source version triggers legal review when embedded in a closed-source SaaS product. Multi-tenancy and white-labeling require the Enterprise plan (significant annual cost). No TypeSpec/contract-first integration path. Superset (Apache 2.0) + Cube (Apache 2.0) covers the same surface without the license and cost risks.
candidates.license: AGPL-3 (open source). Commercial Enterprise license for embedded/white-label.
candidates.maintenance_health: 38k GitHub stars. Large user base. Metabase Inc backed. Active. But AGPL + multi-tenant cost model disqualifies it.
candidates.stack_fit: No NestJS SDK. Embedded iframes similar to Superset but multi-tenant RLS needs Enterprise plan. No generator path. Heavier Java/Clojure runtime vs Superset Python.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted or Metabase Cloud. Both options exist but both have the license concern for embedded use.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Good BI UX but org-centric by default. No person-journey re-centering support. Superset + guest tokens achieves the same with better license.
candidates.generator_first_target: n/a - rejected
candidates.risks: AGPL-3 requires legal review for embedded closed-source use. Enterprise license pricing ($500/month+) for multi-tenancy. Switching cost after adoption is high.

verified.name: Meilisearch (Community Edition)
verified.license_confirmed: PARTIALLY confirmed, with a material discrepancy. CE binary is MIT-licensed (confirmed via GitHub LICENSE-MIT). EE binary (sharding only) is BUSL-1.1. The CE/EE split introduced in v1.28 is real and the CE MIT claim is accurate for self-hosted production. However, air-gap Zarf bundling of the CE Docker image is legally clean under MIT - no redistribution restriction. The candidate's license claim is substantively correct. Flag: you must track which binary (CE vs EE) is in your Zarf package; the candidate correctly calls this out as a risk. No BAA or HIPAA certification found for self-hosted CE - PHI stored in Meilisearch indexes would be your compliance burden, not theirs. Meilisearch must be treated as an index layer only; PHI stays in Postgres (already CuraOS doctrine).
verified.maintenance_confirmed: PARTIALLY confirmed, with a significant staleness flag. The candidate claims v1.28.0 (May 2026) as the latest. Actual latest is v1.48.2 released June 22-28, 2026 - the project is 20 minor versions ahead of the candidate's claim, meaning the candidate's research is already stale. Release cadence is confirmed active (monthly minor releases). CRITICAL: Two actively patched CVEs exist in recent versions - CVE-2026-57823 (information disclosure via tenant tokens, allows a token holder to infer document existence outside their search scope) and CVE-2026-57824 (privilege escalation via index-scoped API key able to execute global actions). Both fixed in v1.48.2 / v1.47.1. No evidence of exploitation in the wild, but the tenant token CVE (CVE-2026-57823) directly touches the multi-tenancy claim made for patient/record search. The nestjs-meilisearch wrapper (lambrohan) last published v3.0.0 in February 2025 - 16 months ago, NestJS 11 compatible but low activity since. Bun runtime is explicitly NOT in the official meilisearch-js test matrix.
verified.integration_reality: Moderately optimistic. The first-party meilisearch npm package (TypeScript HTTP client) works with any fetch-compatible runtime and is actively maintained. Bun is not officially tested but the client is fetch-based so it is likely compatible - requires a proof-of-concept run, not assumed safe. The nestjs-meilisearch wrapper is a thin module (dependency injection config) that is 16 months stale; it wraps the first-party client and NestJS DI, so breakage risk is low but ownership risk is real - CuraOS would need to own a fork or write a minimal NestJS module directly (10-20 lines, no wrapper needed per ponytail ladder). Drizzle stays source-of-truth; Meilisearch is the read index. Sync (write-through or CDC) must be built as part of the search-service codegen emitter - this is non-trivial glue code that the candidate correctly scopes to the generator-first target. TypeSpec -> index definition mapping is custom work; no existing TypeSpec emitter for Meilisearch exists. The FilterBar/SearchShell contract lint gate is pure internal work. The FHIR search-param mapping is not provided by Meilisearch - it is application-layer translation work on top of the index. Integration is feasible but the candidate understates the Bun uncertainty and overstates the NestJS wrapper readiness.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - and it is already in the stack. PostgreSQL (CNPG, already deployed) with pg_search (ParadeDB BM25 extension) + pgvector delivers hybrid search (BM25 + vector) without adding a new stateful service. Benchmarks show PG GIN/BM25 at P50 4ms latency for boolean queries. ParadeDB pg_search is production-stable as of 2025, runs inside CNPG, and CNPG already has Citus for multi-tenancy. This eliminates: a new binary to air-gap, a new Helm chart, a sync layer, tenant token JWT management, an embeddings sidecar, and the CE/EE binary tracking burden. The ponytail ladder stops here (rung 4: native platform feature / already-installed dependency). The case for Meilisearch must justify why pg_search + pgvector inside existing CNPG is insufficient - specifically typo tolerance and sub-50ms instant-search UX. pg_search does not provide typo tolerance (fuzzy match via trigrams is possible but inferior). For patient-facing instant search with typo tolerance, Meilisearch's UX delta is real. For internal FHIR parameter search and FilterBar contract linting, pg_search likely covers it.
verified.notes: Verdict rationale - confirmed-trial, not confirmed-adopt. Adopt is blocked by: (1) CVE-2026-57823 directly undermines the tenant token isolation claim for patient data - must be confirmed resolved at the pinned version before patient-facing deployment; (2) Bun runtime not officially tested - needs a passing integration test before the search-service generator emits code that depends on it; (3) nestjs-meilisearch wrapper is 16-month-stale community code - should be replaced with a minimal NestJS module wrapping the first-party client (ponytail: ~20 lines, no wrapper needed); (4) the candidate's version claim (v1.28.0) is 20 minor versions behind actual latest (v1.48.2) as of June 2026, suggesting the research was not verified against live sources before submission; (5) the ponytail ladder question is live - pg_search + pgvector should be prototyped first for the FHIR-param and FilterBar use cases before committing to a new stateful service; Meilisearch is justified only for the patient-facing instant-search UX path where typo tolerance is a product requirement. Trial gates: pin to v1.48.2+; run Bun fetch-client smoke test; write a minimal NestJS DI module in-house; scope embeddings sidecar as a separate decision; confirm pg_search covers internal search lanes before adding Meilisearch for those.

verified.name: pgvector (PostgreSQL extension)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT. Confirmed by multiple independent sources (linkgo.dev FAQ, instaclustr 2026 guide, official GitHub). The candidate conflated "PostgreSQL License" and "MIT" - they are both permissive and correct; MIT is the actual SPDX identifier. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution. No CLA, no SSPL, no usage-based restriction. Safe.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: MIXED. Latest confirmed release is v0.8.3 (June 2026 per Windows build mirror; v0.8.2 officially announced 2026-02-26 fixing CVE-2026-3172). The candidate's claimed "v0.9.0 with sparse vector support" is UNVERIFIED - no v0.9 appears in any release page or changelog found. The candidate overstates the version. v0.8.x is actively maintained by Andrew Kane with regular cadence. CVE-2026-3172 is real: buffer overflow in parallel HNSW index builds (affects 0.6.0-0.8.1); fixed in 0.8.2+. CNPG must pin to 0.8.2 or newer. No unfixed critical CVEs found in current release.
verified.integration_reality: MOSTLY ACCURATE with one critical gap. Drizzle ORM: confirmed first-class vector() column type + cosineDistance/l2Distance operators + HNSW index support (drizzle-orm v0.31+ / 0.36+). pgvector-node supports Bun + Node.js + Deno (confirmed on GitHub topics and NestJS guide). CNPG integration: confirmed via CNPG Recipe 18 (June 2025) and the cloudnative-pg/postgres-extensions-containers project - pgvector ships as an official CNPG extension image, declaratively installable. Air-gap: packagable as a CNPG ImageVolume extension container; no Zarf-specific guide found but the container image approach is Zarf-compatible (standard OCI image). TypeSpec generator target (@vectorEmbedded decorator) is SPECULATIVE - no TypeSpec pgvector emitter exists externally; this is custom codegen work the candidate describes as if ready. The generator-first integration is a build task, not a free pick-up. Performance numbers (p95 ~2-5ms at 100K, ~8-15ms at 1M on 4vCPU/8GB): plausible for HNSW but unverified at those exact specs; benchmarks show p95 climbs to 80-140ms at 5M vectors depending on ef_search, which is above the candidate's 8-15ms claim for 1M. LOCAL-VS-3RDPARTY DUALITY: the workspace rule (curaos_local_vs_3rdparty_rule.md line 30) ALREADY names "pgvector / Qdrant self-hosted | Pinecone / Weaviate Cloud / Qdrant Cloud" as the vector DB duality. pgvector is the self-hosted leg of an already-planned provider pair. The VectorProvider abstraction must still be built - pgvector does not self-satisfy the duality requirement alone.
verified.simpler_alternative: PostgreSQL full-text search (tsvector/tsquery + pg_trgm) already in CNPG covers keyword and fuzzy search without a new extension. For the CDS embedding and FHIR semantic ranking use case described, there is no simpler already-installed alternative - tsvector does not do dense vector similarity. pgvector is the minimum viable addition. Ponytail ladder stops here: no stdlib covers 768-dim cosine similarity at scale.
verified.notes: Three concrete corrections to the candidate submission: (1) v0.9.0 does not exist as of 2026-06-29; latest is v0.8.3 - "sparse vector support in v0.9" is an inaccurate claim; sparse vector (halfvec/sparsevec) was added in 0.7.x-0.8.x range, not a 0.9 feature. (2) The TypeSpec @vectorEmbedded decorator + codegen emitter is future build work, not available today - estimate should reflect this gap. (3) Performance at 1M vectors: third-party benchmarks show p95 latency higher than claimed (80-140ms range at 5M, not 8-15ms) for default ef_search settings; the 8-15ms figure likely requires careful tuning or reflects smaller datasets. Verdict is trial (not full adopt) because: the v0.9 fabrication signals the submission research was partially hallucinated; the VectorProvider abstraction is a real build cost not acknowledged; and HNSW performance at the FHIR-scale (per-tenant multi-million vector scenario with multi-tenant CNPG) needs validation in the actual stack before committing. Pin to CNPG with pgvector >= 0.8.2 to clear the CVE. Adopt the extension; treat the generator target and provider abstraction as explicit tracked work items.

verified.name: Apache Superset (embedded analytics)
verified.license_confirmed: Apache 2.0 confirmed via ASF project page and LICENSE.txt in repo. No GPL/LGPL dep conflicts reported by the Apache project. Compatible with self-hosted distribution and BYO-Preset.io managed path. Clean for air-gap bundling (license terms only; ops complexity is separate).
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Actively maintained. v6.0.0 shipped January 2026, v6.1.0 followed with 1500+ PRs and 136 contributors. Preset.io publishes monthly community updates. @superset-ui/embedded-sdk v0.4.0 published to npm within the last week (as of 2026-06-29). Top-level Apache project. Maintenance health claim is accurate.
verified.integration_reality: NestJS integration is real but requires two HTTP round-trips per user session: first fetch a CSRF token from Superset, then POST to /api/v1/security/guest_token/ with service-account credentials + RLS clauses. A documented NestJS + Superset pattern exists (ceegees blog). The SupersetService wrapper is ~50 lines - not heavy glue. Frontend uses @superset-ui/embedded-sdk (iframe + postMessage). No Drizzle/TypeSpec/Zod involvement - Superset reads its own Postgres sidecar, not CuraOS schemas. Generator target is realistic: NestJS SupersetService + ui-app-emit ReportViewer archetype. Dashboard definitions live outside CuraOS codegen (in Superset's own DB), so the generator boundary is the glue layer only. RLS is passed as string clauses in the guest token - correctness depends entirely on SupersetService minting them correctly per tenant.
verified.simpler_alternative: No drop-in simpler alternative exists for this specific use case. Grafana targets ops/metrics, not SQL-explorer BI; its multi-tenant embedded guest story is weaker. Metabase is simpler to operate but carries LGPL (commercial use requires paid Enterprise tier) and its embedded multi-tenant support is less mature. Scratch-build is infeasible at comparable feature depth. Superset is the correct pick on the build-vs-buy axis. The ponytail ladder stops here: no already-installed dep, no stdlib, no native platform feature covers embedded multi-tenant BI dashboards.
verified.notes:
DOWNGRADE FROM ADOPT TO TRIAL. Three issues make "adopt" premature:

1. RLS security track record is non-trivial in a HIPAA-adjacent system. OX Security found an RLS bypass via subquery injection in v4.1.3 (users can reference tables outside their authorized scope). GitHub issue 26374 (RLS not honored when caching enabled, cross-tenant data leak) was reported fixed in v3.0 but the pattern recurred. In HealthStack and any PHI-adjacent surface, this pattern requires mandatory mitigations before promoting to adopt: pin to v6.x, enforce GUEST_TOKEN_VALIDATOR_HOOK server-side, disable Superset-level caching on embedded dashboards (or accept cache-keying per RLS clause), and run Renovate with a short patch window on apache-superset.

2. CVE cadence is high. 14 CVEs in 2024, 6 in 2025, CVE-2026-23983 (data exposure) already in 2026. Fixes land quickly but you must be on the latest minor. This is manageable with Renovate + version pinning but it is a real ops cost.

3. Air-gap ops weight is understated. The full production stack is: Superset app + Celery worker + Redis + Superset Postgres (separate from CuraOS CNPG cluster) + OS-level build deps (libssl, libffi, libpq, libsasl2). Zarf can bundle the Helm chart and images but the Python wheel tree with native C extensions makes the Zarf package substantially heavier than a Bun/NestJS service. Not a blocker, but the candidate's "Zarf-packageable" claim needs a proof-of-build before production.

Trial path: wire crm-insurance-broker.platform.reporting first (non-PHI surface). Validate GUEST_TOKEN_VALIDATOR_HOOK enforcement, no-caching behavior, and Zarf bundle size. Pass those gates, then promote to adopt for healthcare.platform.reports-dashboards. The ReportDefinition/field-catalog generator gap in reports-sdk is correctly excluded from this candidate's scope - that stays separate.

verified.name: Typesense
verified.license_confirmed: GPL-3.0 server confirmed (Apache-2.0 clients confirmed). Risk is understated in candidate: distributing the Typesense binary inside an OCI image (Zarf air-gap bundle to customer infra) is distribution under GPL-3, requiring source availability. Meilisearch CE is MIT -- zero distribution obligation. This is a material compliance burden for CuraOS's self-hosted+air-gap model that the candidate does not flag adequately.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. v30.2 released April 2026. Regular release cadence in 2025-2026. No CVEs or public security advisories found. Self-funded (Typesense Inc.), no VC distress signals. Maintenance is not a concern.
verified.integration_reality: typesense npm (v3.x, TypeScript, Apache-2) works on Bun without issues -- pure HTTP client, no native addons. NestJS integration is community-only (hainguyen27798/typesense-nestjs, not Typesense Inc.-maintained). No first-party NestJS module, no TypeSpec binding, no Zod integration published. Integration effort is identical to Meilisearch, not better. The @curaos/search-sdk abstraction layer (generator-first target) correctly handles backend swapping and avoids lock-in.
verified.simpler_alternative: Meilisearch CE (MIT, already designated platform.search primary): independent 2026 benchmarks show Meilisearch outperforms Typesense at the relevant QPS range -- 1200 QPS saturation vs 800 QPS for Typesense on identical hardware (c5.xlarge), with 3.2x lower p99 latency at 500 QPS (28ms vs 91ms). The candidate's 10K+ QPS throughput-advantage premise is from Typesense's own marketing page, not third-party data, and is inverted by current evidence. Meilisearch also has scoped API key multi-tenancy (filter_by scope), same HA read-scaling story, and zero GPL distribution overhead. PostgreSQL tsvector on CNPG covers CuraOS's current scale with zero new service.
verified.notes: Sole differentiators claimed by candidate -- higher QPS, Raft HA -- are either refuted (QPS) or overstated (Raft gives HA but not write-sharding; Meilisearch has equivalent read HA). Typesense's Raft limitation (entire dataset replicated per node, no horizontal write-sharding) is a real ceiling for very large corpora, but this is identical to Meilisearch CE. Both hit the same wall at the same place. Helm charts for Typesense are community-maintained (3 separate community repos, no first-party chart), same situation as Meilisearch. Reconsider only if: (1) a profiler shows Meilisearch CE saturating at measured production QPS, (2) GPL-3 compliance process is pre-cleared with legal for Zarf distribution, and (3) write-sharding is not yet needed (or Meilisearch Enterprise sharding is evaluated first). Until all three hold, Typesense adds cost (compliance process) with no demonstrated benefit.

candidates.name: Martin (maplibre/martin)
candidates.what: Blazing-fast PostGIS-native vector tile server (auto-discovers geometry tables/functions, serves MVT from PostGIS, PMTiles, MBTiles). Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.tiles: the fleet/map UI needs real-time vector overlays from the same PostGIS instance geospatial-core-service already owns.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host; BYO tile CDN (MapTiler, Esri) covered by same MapLibre frontend pointing at a different tile URL
candidates.maintenance_health: Active under MapLibre org (Linux Foundation). v0.15.x released Sept 2025, latest release within last weeks. Community active on GitHub discussions. Backed by MapLibre umbrella contributors.
candidates.stack_fit: No Node wrapper needed. Add as Docker sidecar (ghcr.io/maplibre/martin) reading the same CNPG PostGIS DB via DATABASE_URL. NestJS geospatial-core-service emits geometry tables; Martin auto-discovers them. Zero-config for most setups. Helm chart available; Zarf-compatible static binary for air-gap.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables real-time map rendering (patient location, delivery tracking, facility finder, geofence overlays) in both personal-* apps and business management dashboards without building a tile pipeline from scratch.
candidates.generator_first_target: service/geospatial-core-service: generator emits Drizzle geometry columns + GiST index + publishes Martin config snippet; ui-app-emit MapView archetype references tile URL env var
candidates.risks: Rust binary, not npm - deploy as sidecar. For very large global planet tiles, Martin + PostGIS is memory-intensive; PMTiles fallback for bounded read-only datasets. DB must have PostGIS 3.1+.

candidates.name: MapLibre GL JS + react-map-gl
candidates.what: MapLibre GL JS (BSD-3, npm: maplibre-gl) is the open-source WebGL map renderer; react-map-gl v8 (MIT, npm: react-map-gl) wraps it with idiomatic React/TypeScript components. Together they cover taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.maps for the ui-kit MapView archetype and all 24 web + 2 mobile apps that need interactive maps.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: serves both: self-hosted tiles from Martin/PMTiles/SeaweedFS OR BYO 3rd-party tile provider (MapTiler, Esri, HERE) by swapping tile URL - zero code change
candidates.maintenance_health: ~7M weekly npm downloads for maplibre-gl. react-map-gl v8.1.1 published ~2 months ago. MapLibre GL JS v5 released Jan 2025 with globe view + deck.gl integration. Backed by Linux Foundation MapLibre charter. No vendor lock-in unlike Mapbox GL JS.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm-import into @curaos/ui. react-map-gl/maplibre peer requires maplibre-gl>=4. Full TypeScript, no @types needed. Bun-compatible. Map component wraps in ui-kit MapView archetype; ui-app-emit generates it from contract archetype hint 'map'.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Powers patient/customer-facing location UX (find nearby facility, live delivery tracking on personal-shop, SOS location pin on personal-tracking app) AND management fleet map dashboard. Same component, two surfaces.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit component (MapView, MapMarker, GeofenceOverlay) + ui-app-emit archetype 'map' -> emits MapView usage with tile URL + source from contract
candidates.risks: WebGL rendering requires browser GPU. Mobile React Native needs maplibre-react-native (separate package, separate integration path). Large GeoJSON overlays need deck.gl for performance.

candidates.name: deck.gl
candidates.what: WebGL data visualization layers (ScatterplotLayer, TripsLayer, HeatmapLayer, PathLayer) composited over MapLibre GL. Covers the dense-dataset visualization gap in curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.fleet-tracking: fleet live positions (hundreds of vehicles), delivery route paths, geofence heatmaps in the management dashboard.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: frontend rendering only - agnostic to tile source; works with self-hosted or BYO
candidates.maintenance_health: Maintained by vis.gl (Uber open-source). v9.x active, Jan 2025 MapLibre v5 globe integration shipped. ~3M npm weekly downloads. Well-funded OSS ecosystem.
candidates.stack_fit: npm-import into @curaos/ui alongside react-map-gl. DeckGL React component wraps into MapView archetype when 'fleet-density' or 'trip-viz' hint is set. No server-side changes. Bun-compatible.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Primarily management surface (fleet dispatcher heatmap, route density analytics). Person surface limited to own trip history replay - marginal but non-zero.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit MapView archetype variant 'fleet-density' or 'trip-layer' -> emits DeckGL layers atop MapLibre base
candidates.risks: Additional bundle weight. YAGNI if only showing a handful of markers; only add when density/animation is required. Not needed for basic pin map.

candidates.name: Valhalla routing engine
candidates.what: Open-source OSM routing engine with multi-modal routing, isochrones, time-aware turn restrictions, map matching, matrix queries, and optimized routes. Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.location (routing leg of location service) and fleet-core dispatch (ETA, route optimization). `@iwpnd/valhalla-ts` (MIT, npm) is a typed HTTP client.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: self-host via Docker; BYO via HERE Routing, Google Routes, Mapbox Directions API - same @curaos/routing-sdk interface
candidates.maintenance_health: ~8k GitHub stars, Linux Foundation member, active maintainers from Mapbox/HERE/Meta. Multi-modal + isochrone features unique in OSS. Backed by professional GIS community.
candidates.stack_fit: Deploy as Docker sidecar. NestJS geospatial-core-service calls Valhalla HTTP API via `@iwpnd/valhalla-ts` (v1.0.29, MIT, 71 weekly downloads - thin but typed). Wrap into @curaos/routing-sdk to abstract self-host vs BYO provider. fleet-core-service emits dispatch.route.calculated events from routing results.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Powers patient/customer journey: 'get directions to clinic', 'track my delivery ETA', 'how long until driver arrives'. Management surface: fleet dispatcher sees optimal routes, driver app gets turn-by-turn. Isochrones enable 'show facilities within 30 min' person surface.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/routing-sdk) + service geospatial-core-service adds /routing endpoint wired to Valhalla; fleet-core-service consumes routing-sdk for ETA
candidates.risks: C++ build; Docker image ~2-4GB. OSM planet tile preparation takes 15-20 min for a country. @iwpnd/valhalla-ts is community, not official. For air-gap: OSM extracts must be bundled in Zarf. OSRM is faster for simple A-to-B without dynamic costing needs.

candidates.name: Traccar
candidates.what: Open-source GPS tracking server supporting 200+ protocols and 2000+ GPS hardware device models. REST API + WebSocket for live position events. PostgreSQL backend. Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.fleet-tracking: the hardware-ingestion layer that fleet-core-service cannot replicate without enormous protocol effort.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: self-host (Docker, PostgreSQL backend); BYO-3rd-party: Samsara, Here Fleet, Teltonika Cloud, Google Maps Fleet - same adapter contract
candidates.maintenance_health: ~5k GitHub stars, most active OSS GPS server on GitHub per community consensus. Apache-2.0 commercial-safe. 2025: OAuth2 support added, geofence ML improvements. Actively maintained Java codebase.
candidates.stack_fit: Deploy as Docker sidecar with its own PG DB (separate from CuraOS CNPG tenants). NestJS fleet-core-service consumes Traccar REST API (GET /api/positions, GET /api/events, WebSocket /api/socket) via a thin @curaos/traccar-adapter. Position events forwarded as fleet.position.updated Kafka events. fleet-core geofence logic in PostGIS owns geofence evaluation (Traccar geofences are duplicates - skip). No Bun/NestJS changes needed beyond the adapter.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Driver app (personal-tracking-service) sends positions to Traccar via existing protocol OR directly to fleet-core API. Traccar handles hardware GPS devices (ambulance, delivery van, field worker device) that personal-tracking cannot handle. Person (patient/customer) sees derived delivery ETA and SOS response location, not raw Traccar data.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK package (@curaos/traccar-adapter) adapter between Traccar REST+WebSocket and fleet-core-service position ingestion contract
candidates.risks: Java runtime - separate JVM process. Traccar has its own user/device model that must be mapped to CuraOS party/vehicle model. Does NOT provide order management or dispatch workflows (that stays in fleet-core). BYO alternative for customers with existing telematics platforms.

candidates.name: Photon (komoot)
candidates.what: OpenStreetMap geocoder built on OpenSearch (search-as-you-type, typo-tolerant, forward + reverse geocode). Covers taxonomy_id curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.location: the geocoding leg of geospatial-core-service (convert address <-> coordinates). GraphHopper publishes weekly updated Photon database dumps, drastically reducing import burden.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: self-host via Docker + OpenSearch; BYO: Google Geocoding API, HERE Geocoder, Mapbox Geocoding - same @curaos/geocoder-sdk interface
candidates.maintenance_health: Backed by Komoot (production-scale navigation app). Apache-2.0. Weekly updated database dumps from GraphHopper. Widely deployed in production mapping stacks. Simpler ops than Nominatim or Pelias (no Elasticsearch cluster needed beyond what CuraOS already runs).
candidates.stack_fit: CuraOS already runs Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for search-service. Photon uses the same OpenSearch index. NestJS geospatial-core-service calls Photon HTTP REST via thin @curaos/geocoder-sdk wrapper. Zarf-bundleable. No npm package needed - plain HTTP.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Powers 'find my nearest clinic', facility search, delivery address autocomplete, SOS address resolution in person apps. Management surface: driver address lookup, geofence definition by address.
candidates.generator_first_target: service geospatial-core-service: generator emits /geocode and /reverse endpoints wired to @curaos/geocoder-sdk; geocoder-sdk has self-hosted Photon + BYO provider implementations
candidates.risks: Java runtime + OpenSearch index storage. Weekly dump update schedule means addresses lag reality by up to 7 days. For real-time address accuracy (new buildings), BYO premium provider is the escape hatch. Air-gap requires bundling the Photon DB dump in Zarf (can be large for planet coverage).

candidates.name: PMTiles (protomaps)
candidates.what: Single-file archive format for vector/raster tiles served via HTTP range requests from any S3-compatible object store (including SeaweedFS). Zero tile server needed for bounded/static datasets. Covers the same curaos_cross_cutting.geospatial.tiles taxonomy_id as Martin but as a simpler deployment path for pre-built tile archives (country basemaps, regional overlays).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party self-host on SeaweedFS; BYO-3rd-party: Protomaps CDN, MapTiler CDN - same PMTiles URL in MapLibre style
candidates.maintenance_health: Actively maintained by Protomaps (Brandon Liu, production-grade). MapLibre GL JS natively supports PMTiles since v4. Widely adopted in self-hosted mapping stacks. No server process to operate.
candidates.stack_fit: npm package `pmtiles` for MapLibre integration (no import on NestJS needed). SeaweedFS already deployed - upload .pmtiles basemap file, set CORS headers, done. Martin handles dynamic PostGIS tiles; PMTiles handles static basemap archives. Complementary, not competing.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables offline-capable basemap for mobile apps (personal-tracking, personal-shop delivery view). Air-gap deployments get a bundled basemap Zarf artifact with zero runtime tile server.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit MapView archetype: tile source switches between Martin (dynamic) and PMTiles (static) via env config; no code change
candidates.risks: Static only: cannot generate live query-based tiles. Region/country coverage must be pre-selected and regenerated for updates. Not suitable for dynamic tenant-specific overlays.

candidates.name: Drizzle ORM PostGIS geometry type (built-in + wkx)
candidates.what: Drizzle ORM v0.31+ ships native `geometry` column type for PostGIS points (SRID 4326, mode: xy). Polygon/multipolygon/linestring requires a `customType` wrapper with `wkx` npm package for WKB-to-GeoJSON conversion. Together this covers the data-layer contract gap in geospatial-core-service: no external library needed beyond what is already in the stack.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: first-party only (DB layer)
candidates.maintenance_health: Drizzle ORM: actively maintained, v0.31 released 2024, comprehensive docs. wkx (npm): stable utility, MIT, widely used for WKB parsing. Both are already depended on or trivially addable.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct drop-in for geospatial-core-service Drizzle schema. geometry('location', { type: 'point', srid: 4326 }) for vehicle/facility positions; customType + wkx for geofence polygons. GiST index via sql template in drizzle migration. Generator folds geometry column + GiST index into codegen mold for any entity with 'location' field hint.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables proper spatial storage and queries (ST_DWithin for 'nearby', ST_Contains for geofence entry) backing both person (find nearby clinic) and management (geofence alert) surfaces.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter: add geometry column + GiST index emission when contract entity has @geo decorator; wkx added to SDK package template
candidates.risks: PostGIS polygon support is not native in Drizzle - the customType pattern is manual boilerplate. Must ensure PostGIS extension is pre-installed in CNPG (already in stack per inventory). Complex spatial queries (ST_Intersection, ST_Union) require raw sql() calls.

candidates.name: Fleetbase (AGPL-3.0)
candidates.what: Full open-source logistics OS (dispatch, order management, driver app, warehouse, proof-of-delivery, routing). Much broader than Traccar. Laravel/PHP backend. Would cover fleet-core dispatch workflow surface comprehensively.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: both (self-host or managed Fleetbase cloud)
candidates.maintenance_health: Active GitHub, 2026 blog active, $2500 implementation support tier. Commercial license available. Stack mismatch (PHP vs NestJS) is the primary integration friction.
candidates.stack_fit: AGPL means any modification must be open-sourced under AGPL or a commercial license purchased. PHP/Laravel stack does not compose into the NestJS/Bun monorepo. Would need full REST API adapter layer. Weight far exceeds the dispatch workflow value CuraOS needs.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Driver app (Navigator) is strong; but CuraOS personal-tracking-service already handles driver-side positioning. Overlap creates duplication.
candidates.generator_first_target: reference-only
candidates.risks: AGPL service boundary if CuraOS ships modifications. PHP runtime incompatible with Bun/NestJS monorepo. Scope far exceeds what fleet-core-service needs (70% overlap with existing CuraOS modules). Commercial license cost unclear.

verified.name: Martin (maplibre/martin)
verified.license_confirmed: Confirmed MIT OR Apache-2.0 dual license. Clean, permissive, safe for self-hosted + air-gap + OEM distribution. No copyleft concerns. Candidate's claim is accurate.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active and accelerating. v1.0 shipped Nov 2025 after years of pre-1.0 development. v1.5.0 through v1.10.1 shipped April-May 2026 (nine releases in two months). Under MapLibre/Linux Foundation governance. No known CVEs or public security advisories found. Maintenance health claim is accurate - this is a healthy project.
verified.simpler_alternative: Already decided. ADR-0206 (Accepted 2026-05-24) explicitly resolved tile serving as: PMTiles file archive + HTTP range-request proxy from MinIO/S3 + NestJS thin proxy + MapLibre GL JS client-side render. The existing Requirements.md for geospatial-core-service encodes this. The ponytail ladder stops at rung 2: the solution already exists in the codebase/ADR. Martin is a new process, new sidecar, new operational surface - none of which is needed given the resolved architecture.
verified.integration_reality: Martin is a standalone Rust sidecar, not a NestJS library. Integration with Bun/NestJS is not code-level - it is purely network-level (DATABASE_URL env + reverse proxy). This is straightforward but the claim of "zero glue" requires a correctly wired proxy for auth (Martin has NO built-in auth - JWT/bearer must be added at the proxy/CDN layer, confirmed). CORS is served as wildcard by default (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *), which conflicts with CuraOS's multi-tenant isolation posture. No official Helm chart exists for Martin - community K8s examples exist but an official chart is absent, so the candidate's "Helm chart available" claim is overstated; a custom chart would be needed. Multi-tenant RLS: Martin connects via a single DATABASE_URL and auto-discovers ALL geometry tables. It has no per-tenant session variable injection, so PostGIS RLS (which CuraOS uses via schema-per-tenant, ADR-0101) would require a custom SQL function layer or per-tenant Martin instance - neither is zero-config. The wildcard CORS + no-auth + no RLS awareness is a material gap for a multi-tenant healthcare platform.
verified.notes: Hard reject on architectural grounds, not quality grounds. Martin is an excellent tool - fast (2-3x faster than nearest competitor in benchmarks), well-maintained, correct license. The rejection is because ADR-0206 (Accepted 2026-05-24) already resolved tile serving to PMTiles + range proxy with no tile server process. That decision is locked. Adopting Martin would: (1) contradict a resolved ADR without a superseding decision, (2) add a new Rust sidecar process and operational surface, (3) require custom auth proxy wiring for every deployment including air-gap, (4) require custom RLS handling for multi-tenant schema-per-tenant isolation, (5) require a custom Helm chart. The PMTiles approach is simpler (static file, HTTP ranges, no server process, CDN-cacheable) and already covers the real-time overlay use case via pre-built regional extracts on a nightly cron (OSM lifecycle is already specced). If dynamic PostGIS geometry overlays (not OSM tiles) become a concrete need - e.g. live patient or vehicle positions - the right path is to raise a new ADR that explicitly weighs PMTiles-static vs Martin-dynamic with real traffic data, then supersede ADR-0206's tile-serving section. Do not adopt as a side decision without that process.

verified.name: MapLibre GL JS + react-map-gl
verified.license_confirmed: CONFIRMED with one caveat. maplibre-gl is BSD-3-Clause (verified via GitHub LICENSE.txt and npm). react-map-gl is MIT. Both are safe-permissive: no copyleft, no attribution-in-UI requirement, compatible with self-hosted + air-gap + proprietary SaaS distribution. No gotchas for Zarf bundle. One caveat: the candidate says maplibre-gl>=4 is required for react-map-gl/maplibre - true, but the current react-map-gl v8.1.1 pairs with maplibre-gl v5.x (latest: 5.24.0, published ~April 2026). Locking to v4 would be a downgrade; pin to v5 + react-map-gl v8.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: CONFIRMED. maplibre-gl v5.24.0 published ~April 2026 (2 months ago). ~7M weekly npm downloads verified. react-map-gl v8.1.1 published ~2 months ago. Linux Foundation MapLibre charter - TSC-governed, backed by Amazon/Meta/Microsoft/Esri/StadiaMaps. Martin tile server v1.0 released late 2025. No CVEs found in Snyk for maplibre-gl. RISK: react-map-gl v8 had a breaking pixelsToGLUnits issue when upgrading from v7 to MapLibre GL v5, but this is RESOLVED in v8 via the new react-map-gl/maplibre import endpoint. The v7-to-v8 upgrade is breaking (import path change). Going in at v8 directly avoids this pain entirely.
verified.simpler_alternative: No. Ponytail ladder check: no map library exists in the codebase (grep confirms zero references to maplibre, leaflet, openlayers, mapbox, deck.gl). Leaflet is the only lighter alternative - it IS simpler but disqualified for fleet/health use cases requiring 10k+ real-time markers; Leaflet DOM rendering degrades sharply above ~1k markers per published benchmarks. Native platform (<iframe> embed, static image tiles) does not cover interactive vector maps with geofence overlays, live tracking pins, and zoom/filter interaction. MapLibre GL is the minimum viable choice for the stated use cases; no installed dep covers it.
verified.integration_reality: MOSTLY ACCURATE, one understatement flagged. Web layer: react-map-gl/maplibre is a clean React/TS npm import, no NestJS/Drizzle/Zod/TypeSpec touch - it is a pure frontend rendering concern. Bun-compatible (browser bundle, no Node-specific APIs). Generator-first fit is real: the component API is declarative and prop-driven, making it straightforward to emit MapView archetype usage from ui-app-emit given tile URL + layer contract. Air-gap: PMTiles + Martin self-hosted tile server confirmed production-grade (Martin v1.0 GA). Tile URL swap for 3rd-party providers is a single config string - local-vs-3rdparty duality holds. UNDERSTATEMENT: Mobile path (maplibre-react-native) is a SEPARATE package, separate native build pipeline, separate version cadence, and v10 took 29 alphas + 21 betas to ship. The candidate notes this risk but calls effort S for ALL 24 web + 2 mobile apps. Web effort = S. Mobile effort = M minimum, possibly L depending on native CI/Zarf bundle complexity. The mobile integration path must be scoped and verified separately before committing to the 2 mobile apps.
verified.notes: ADOPT web layer (MapLibre GL JS v5.24 + react-map-gl v8.1.1 from react-map-gl/maplibre) as confirmed-trial, not full adopt, because: (1) react-map-gl v8 is relatively young (v8.0 released within the last ~6 months) and the MapLibre v5 API surface has active open bugs (globe-related issues #6872, #6892, #6902 from Dec 2025); (2) mobile path is NOT covered by this pair and must be a separate trial. Concrete constraints to enforce: pin maplibre-gl to ^5.24.0 and react-map-gl to ^8.1.1; always import from react-map-gl/maplibre (not the legacy mapbox path); tile URL must be injected via contract config (not hardcoded) to satisfy local-vs-3rdparty duality; large GeoJSON overlays (fleet heatmaps) should gate on deck.gl integration before assuming MapLibre alone handles them. Mobile apps: file a separate candidate evaluation for @maplibre/maplibre-react-native before committing. Generator-first target (MapView + MapMarker + GeofenceOverlay in ui-kit, archetype hint in ui-app-emit) is valid and should be the integration entry point, not per-app manual wiring.

verified.name: deck.gl
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed via GitHub visgl/deck.gl LICENSE file and OpenJS Foundation hosting. Air-gap compatible: pure client-side WebGL rendering, zero required external network calls at runtime. Tile source is pluggable; self-hosted PMTiles or Martin tile server satisfies CuraOS air-gap constraint. Distribution is clean for both self-hosted and BYO-3rd-party models per curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. v9.3.5 published June 26 2026 (3 days before evaluation). Multiple releases per month in the 9.3.x cycle. No CVEs found in CISA, NVD, or GitHub security advisory searches. Active bug triage visible in GitHub issues (fixes shipped Dec 2025 - Feb 2026). ~3M weekly npm downloads. vis.gl / OpenJS Foundation governance is stable.
verified.integration_reality: More complex than claimed. Three unlisted costs: (1) MapLibre GL JS is NOT currently installed in fleet-manager or ui-kit - adopting deck.gl means adopting MapLibre + deck.gl simultaneously, not layering on an existing dep. (2) Next.js 15 App Router SSR hazard: deck.gl and its d3 dependency are ESM-only and will throw on server-side import without explicit dynamic import with ssr:false or a 'use client' boundary. The generator MapView archetype template MUST encode this guard or every emission is a defect. (3) Air-gap tile source: the Zarf package must bundle or env-configure a self-hosted tile source (PMTiles/Martin); this work is not in the S-effort estimate. No Bun bundler-specific issues found (fully ESM since v9.0, Bun bundles ESM cleanly). The DeckGL React component wrapping pattern is straightforward once MapLibre is installed.
verified.simpler_alternative: MapLibre GL JS native heatmap layer and native clustering cover the low-to-mid density scenario (hundreds of vehicles as markers, geofence heatmaps via style layers) without deck.gl. MapLibre is uninstalled too, so both options have the same zero-dep baseline today. The ponytail ladder says: install MapLibre first and use its native layers. Escalate to deck.gl only when live-update repaint rate causes measurable jank (MapLibre is documented as slow on frequent repaints) or when TripsLayer animated replay is a hard requirement. deck.gl earns its keep at the animated/high-density tier only.
verified.notes: Staged adoption path: (1) Add MapLibre to ui-kit as the MapView base - this is the real first decision. (2) Implement fleet live positions and geofence heatmaps using MapLibre native layers. (3) If live dispatcher repaint rate jank is measured, add @deck.gl/core + @deck.gl/layers + @deck.gl/react as the escalation layer, emitted via the generator MapView 'fleet-density' archetype variant. The generator template must include the dynamic import SSR guard and a tile-source env variable. Bundle weight for full deck.gl metapackage is ~500-600 KB minified / ~150-170 KB gzip; selective sub-package imports tree-shake materially lower. No critical open issues or security advisories found.

verified.name: Valhalla routing engine
verified.license_confirmed: MIT verified. Confirmed by Interline Technologies page: "Valhalla, and all of the projects under the Valhalla organization, use the MIT License." No copyleft, no AGPL trap. Safe for self-hosted + air-gap distribution and BYO-provider dual-path. Claim accurate.
verified.maintenance_confirmed:
Active. Latest confirmed release: 3.6.1 (November 2025), last repo commit June 26 2026. Funded contributors from Mapbox, HERE, and community. One risk removed: nilsnolde/docker-valhalla (third-party wrapper) was archived July 22 2025, but its code migrated to the official repo at ghcr.io/valhalla/valhalla with Docker setup at valhalla/valhalla/blob/master/docker/README.md. The @iwpnd/valhalla-ts client has ~71 weekly downloads (confirmed thin), last publish ~late 2025/early 2026. No CVEs found specific to Valhalla in NVD or GitHub Advisory Database searches.

Downgrade risk for the candidate claim: "Linux Foundation member" - NOT confirmed. Sources only confirm it is open-source MIT; no Linux Foundation membership found. Overstated provenance.
verified.simpler_alternative: Two alternatives worth flagging: (1) @routingjs/valhalla (from nilsnolde/routingjs, gis-ops maintained) is a better-maintained multi-engine TypeScript client with a unified interface across Valhalla, OSRM, GraphHopper, and ORS - it directly enables the BYO-provider duality the curaos-local-vs-3rdparty rule requires, making it a stronger pick than @iwpnd/valhalla-ts. (2) OSRM is faster for pure A-to-B ETA queries with no dynamic costing, but lacks isochrones and multi-modal - insufficient for 'show facilities within 30 min' requirement. No already-installed CuraOS dependency or stdlib covers geospatial routing. Valhalla is the correct OSS engine; the client library choice should be upgraded.
verified.notes:
**Research value: high** - Multiple independent sources confirm prior art and real deployment patterns.

ADVERSARIAL FINDINGS (net verdict: confirm-trial, not full adopt yet):

1. LICENSE - PASS. MIT confirmed, compatible with self-host + air-gap + dual-provider.

2. MAINTENANCE - CONDITIONAL PASS. Engine (valhalla/valhalla): strong, v3.6.1 Nov 2025, commit June 2026. CLIENT (@iwpnd/valhalla-ts): WEAK - 71 weekly downloads, solo community maintainer. Propose swap to @routingjs/valhalla (nilsnolde/routingjs, multi-engine, gis-ops-backed) which also directly supports the BYO-provider interface the curaos-local-vs-3rdparty rule mandates.

3. NESTJS/BUN INTEGRATION - OPTIMISTIC in candidate. Valhalla exposes a plain HTTP JSON API; any fetch client works. The typed client layer is thin and replaceable. No NestJS-specific friction. Zod wrapping of Valhalla responses is straightforward. TypeSpec contract generation for the routing SDK is feasible (HTTP wrapper, not C++ FFI). No Bun incompatibility. Integration claim is accurate but the specific client library choice (@iwpnd/valhalla-ts) is the weakest link.

4. GENERATOR-FIRST LOCK-IN - PASS. Valhalla is a sidecar HTTP service. @curaos/routing-sdk wraps it. The generator emits the SDK package + NestJS service wiring + TypeSpec contract. No lock-in: swap the provider URL, keep the SDK interface. This pattern directly serves the self-host vs BYO duality.

5. PONYTAIL LADDER CHECK - The candidate passes. No stdlib covers geospatial routing. No already-installed CuraOS dep provides it. The service boundary is correct: HTTP sidecar, not embedded C++ lib. The client is thin (~few HTTP calls). Simplest working integration: @routingjs/valhalla wrapping a Docker sidecar.

KEY RISKS TO TRACK IN TRIAL:
- Docker image: runtime image ~500MB-1GB (run-only, no build toolchain). OSM tile build for a country: 15-20 min + 15-20 GB disk for North America. Zarf bundle must include pre-built tiles, not just the binary, or OSM extract + tile-build job. This is the primary air-gap complexity; it is solvable but non-trivial.
- Tile build is a one-time ops step per region, not a dev-loop cost.
- Switch @iwpnd/valhalla-ts to @routingjs/valhalla before the SDK package is generated - this is a generator input decision, not a post-gen patch.
- "Linux Foundation member" claim in the candidate is unverified; weight accordingly.

VERDICT RATIONALE: Trial (not full adopt) because the Docker + tile-build ops path for air-gap needs a proof run before committing the generator. The engine itself is proven; the CuraOS-specific Zarf bundle + pre-built tile strategy is not yet validated locally.

candidates.name: shadcn/ui + Radix UI primitives
candidates.what: Copy-paste component distribution built on Radix headless primitives + Tailwind. Already partially adopted in @curaos/ui (10+ Radix packages installed). Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes`: provides battle-tested accessible primitives (Dialog, Select, Tabs, Tooltip, DropdownMenu, Command/cmdk, Combobox, DatePicker, etc.) that become the foundation for all 16 missing archetypes. Components are copied into the repo - no external runtime dependency.
candidates.maintenance_health: 115k+ GitHub stars as of March 2026, #1 JS project of 2024 per JS Rising Stars. Backed by shadcn (Vercel employee). Active changelog. Now supports both Radix and Base UI (MUI) as primitive layers. Radix itself has slowed (WorkOS acquisition), but shadcn/ui has decoupled from it.
candidates.stack_fit: Native fit: Tailwind already in stack, Radix already installed, React 19, TypeScript 5. ui-app-emit already emits shadcn-style components. Zero wrapper needed.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (code copied into repo = zero external runtime). No 3rd-party SaaS. Fully air-gap safe.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Command palette (cmdk), accessible dialogs, drawers, and date pickers are person-facing UX primitives used in patient-app, personal-* apps, and clinician surfaces equally. RTL-capable via Radix internals.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit package (@curaos/ui) - already the integration point; shadcn CLI pulls components into src/components/
candidates.risks: Radix maintenance slowdown is a real signal; mitigated by shadcn/ui adding Base UI as alternate primitive layer in 2025. Destructuring breakage from Radix internal APIs if Radix drops maintenance entirely. Monitor.

candidates.name: TanStack Table v9 + TanStack Virtual
candidates.what: Headless table/grid engine (sorting, filtering, grouping, pagination, column pinning, row selection, server-side) + row virtualization. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` DataTable and `platform-reporting-builder` grids. v9 (beta as of 2026) uses prototype-chain row/column APIs - 90% less memory vs v8, handles 10-16M rows before hitting 4 GB. TanStack Virtual pairs for virtualized rendering of those rows.
candidates.maintenance_health: TanStack org (Tanner Linsley). Query: 46k stars, active 2026 releases. Table: v9 in public beta 2026, v8 stable and proven. Virtual: 19.7M weekly downloads. Strong backing, framework-agnostic design.
candidates.stack_fit: React 19 compatible. No wrapper needed. Already aligns with existing data-table.tsx in ui-kit. TanStack Query v5 (server state) pairs naturally with Table for server-side fetch patterns used across NestJS APIs.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted (npm package, no SaaS). Air-gap safe.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing apps (patient-app, personal-finance) need paginated/virtualized lists of clinical results, invoices, and transactions. Same table primitives serve both management grid and person timeline list.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit (codegen emitter) - archetype hint 'data-grid' maps to TanStack Table wrapper in @curaos/ui
candidates.risks: v9 still in beta - destructuring breaking change (methods must be called on object, not destructured). Recommendation: pin v8 stable now, migrate to v9 when stable. v8 handles 1M rows fine for current needs.

candidates.name: TanStack Query v5 + TanStack Form v1
candidates.what: Query: async server-state cache (fetch, cache, sync, background refetch, optimistic updates). Form: headless cross-framework form state with Standard Schema (Zod 4 native, no extra resolver package). Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` form binding. @curaos/ui already ships react-hook-form v7 + @hookform/resolvers; TanStack Form v1 is an alternative for new complex forms with field-level async validation and nested arrays.
candidates.maintenance_health: Query v5: released Oct 2023, active 2026 releases (Jun 23, 2026 per search). 46k stars. Form v1: stable May 2025, shadcn/ui integration guide published. Actively maintained by TanStack org.
candidates.stack_fit: React 19, TypeScript 5. Query integrates directly with NestJS REST/SSE endpoints. Form v1 supports Zod 4 natively via Standard Schema - exact match with curaos/ui's Zod 4.4.3 dep. react-hook-form already installed; Form v1 is additive for new wizard/multi-step forms.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted npm. Air-gap safe.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Query powers person-facing real-time data (patient vitals, invoice status, appointment schedules). Form v1 handles multi-step patient intake wizards, consent forms, and preference screens with async field validation against NestJS endpoints.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit - codegen emits useQuery hooks from @curaos/*-sdk + TanStack Query as the data-fetching layer
candidates.risks: Form v1 is a parallel choice to react-hook-form; avoid running both for same form type. Recommendation: keep RHF for existing forms, adopt TanStack Form for net-new multi-step wizard archetype where nested field arrays and async validation per-field matter.

candidates.name: Recharts
candidates.what: Declarative SVG chart library built on D3 and React components. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` ReportViewer + `platform-reporting-builder` chart widgets. Chart types: Line, Bar, Area, Pie, Radar, Scatter, Treemap, RadialBar, ComposedChart. Composable component API; ResponsiveContainer for layout. Needed for: vitals trends (patient-app), billing AR aging, quality dashboards, KPI panels, financial GL reports.
candidates.maintenance_health: 27k GitHub stars, 48.9M weekly downloads, MIT. Active community - most critical issues addressed within days. Recharts 2.x stable, v3 preview. Practical default for React dashboards.
candidates.stack_fit: Pure React + SVG, zero canvas complexity. ~290KB bundle (SVG path rendering included). Tree-shakeable per chart type. No server component conflict. Direct drop-in to @curaos/ui ReportViewer wrapper.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted npm. Air-gap safe. No telemetry.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing: vitals trend chart (patient-app), spending over time (personal-finance-app), donation history (fundraising-app). Management: quality measure population dashboards, AR aging bars, revenue cycle funnel.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit package (@curaos/ui) - ReportViewer + KpiCard components wrap Recharts; ui-app-emit emits chart archetype for contract fields typed as time-series
candidates.risks: Bundle size ~290KB adds weight - mitigate with dynamic import per chart component. D3 version pinning can cause peer dep conflicts. visx is lower-level alternative if custom chart needs arise.

candidates.name: @dnd-kit/core + @dnd-kit/sortable
candidates.what: Modular, accessible drag-and-drop toolkit. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` KanbanBoard component. Provides: Pointer/Mouse/Touch/Keyboard sensors, screen-reader live regions, ARIA announcements, sortable list and multi-container presets. react-beautiful-dnd is deprecated (Atlassian dropped it); dnd-kit is the 2026 community standard replacement. Needed for: CRM Kanban pipeline, task boards, scheduling drag-and-drop, order-of-operations step reordering.
candidates.maintenance_health: MIT, 1.64M weekly downloads (@dnd-kit/accessibility), active releases (latest @dnd-kit/react 0.5.0 published 17 days ago). Clauderic Demers (original author) still active. Community fork the-metrics/dnd-kit also active.
candidates.stack_fit: React 19 compatible, TypeScript-first. No CSS or style assumptions - pairs with Tailwind naturally. @dnd-kit/react is the new React-specific package (separate from legacy @dnd-kit/core) aligned with React 19 concurrent mode.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted npm. Air-gap safe.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing: drag-to-reorder personal tasks, care plan steps, workout schedule. Management: CRM deal pipeline kanban, work order scheduling board, approval queue ordering.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit package (@curaos/ui) - KanbanBoard component; ui-app-emit emits kanban archetype for contracts with status/stage field + ordered list
candidates.risks: @dnd-kit/react (new React-19-native API) is at 0.5.x - still maturing. Use @dnd-kit/core (stable 6.x) for production now; migrate to @dnd-kit/react when 1.0 ships. Keyboard DnD requires explicit announcements config for full a11y.

candidates.name: react-i18next + i18next
candidates.what: De facto React i18n stack: runtime key lookup, pluralization, interpolation, namespace splitting, lazy loading, ICU message format plugin. Covers `ui-kit-archetypes` RTL/i18n requirement for all 16 archetype components + all 24 web apps. CuraOS requires full i18n + RTL per NFR. Already used in NestJS backend (i18n-node standard); react-i18next aligns frontend to same message catalog format.
candidates.maintenance_health: i18next: 17.6M weekly downloads, MIT. react-i18next: 6.99M weekly downloads. Both under i18next org, actively maintained since 2014. Massive plugin ecosystem (backend loaders, language detectors, formatters). LinguiJS is the compile-time alternative with smaller bundle (~3KB vs 22KB) but requires build-step; i18next simpler for runtime-loaded translations from CMS/DB.
candidates.stack_fit: React 19 hooks (useTranslation). Turborepo: shared i18n namespace packages per domain. Bun runtime: i18next backend loads JSON from /public/locales. RTL: pairs with Tailwind dir utilities and Radix RTL support.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted: translation files in repo + optional backend loader from DB/storage-service. No SaaS required. Air-gap safe.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Patient-app and personal-* apps must serve non-English speakers and RTL languages (Arabic/Hebrew for MENA deployments). Clinician apps need locale-aware date/number formatting for lab values and medication dosages.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit (codegen emitter) - generated screens auto-wrap string literals in t() calls; namespace convention baked into generator template
candidates.risks: 22KB bundle cost (i18next + react-i18next). Runtime JSON loading adds one network roundtrip per locale - mitigate with bundled default locale. If compile-time guarantee matters more than runtime flexibility, evaluate LinguiJS at that point.

candidates.name: FullCalendar (standard/MIT) + react-big-calendar
candidates.what: Two MIT-licensed options for CalendarBoard/SchedulingBoard archetype (gap `ui-kit-archetypes`). FullCalendar Standard: month/week/day/list views, event drag-resize, resource grouping (non-resource views are MIT; Timeline/Resource views are premium $480/dev/yr). react-big-calendar: simpler, no premium tier, 1.20.0 actively maintained (last publish 24 days ago). Needed for: appointment scheduling (HealthStack), staff/resource scheduling, calendar-based task views.
candidates.maintenance_health: FullCalendar: 19k stars, 1M+ weekly downloads. react-big-calendar: active (v1.20.0, Jun 2026). schedule-x: modern alternative (~2023, growing). Bryntum/DHTMLX: commercial only.
candidates.stack_fit: Both React 19 compatible. FullCalendar @fullcalendar/react is MIT wrapper. react-big-calendar uses moment.js or date-fns as localizer - use date-fns to avoid moment bundle weight. RTL support limited in both; needs wrapper.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted npm. Air-gap safe for MIT tier.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Patient-facing: my appointments calendar, medication schedule. Provider-facing: scheduling board with resource columns (exam rooms, providers). Management: staff roster calendar.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit package (@curaos/ui) - CalendarBoard component wraps one of these; ui-app-emit emits calendar archetype for contracts with dateTime + assignee fields
candidates.risks: Resource/Timeline views require paid FullCalendar license - evaluate whether react-big-calendar covers enough (it lacks multi-resource columns). Premium tier is per-developer subscription ($480/dev/yr). Recommendation: start with react-big-calendar for basic calendar views; add FullCalendar Standard (MIT) for richer event management if needed; only buy premium if multi-resource scheduling is a firm v1 requirement.

candidates.name: RJSF (react-jsonschema-form) v6
candidates.what: JSON Schema + uiSchema -> React form renderer with validation (AJV8 default, swappable). Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` schema-driven form generation and `platform-reporting-builder` dynamic field configuration. Direct path to emitting forms from TypeSpec-derived JSON Schema contracts: TypeSpec -> OpenAPI schema -> JSON Schema subset -> RJSF renders the form automatically. Needed for: dynamic workflow form steps, admin config screens, report builder field selectors.
candidates.maintenance_health: v6.6.1 published 1 day ago (Jun 2026), actively maintained by rjsf-team. MUI theme adapter available (@rjsf/material-ui). shadcn/ui theme adapter community-available.
candidates.stack_fit: TypeSpec contracts produce JSON Schema compatible output - direct pipeline to RJSF. Zod 4 schemas can be converted to JSON Schema via zod-to-json-schema. Needs a shadcn/ui theme adapter (build once in @curaos/ui). AJV8 validator swappable for lighter custom validator.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted npm + schema files in repo. Air-gap safe.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing: dynamic intake forms (patient registration, insurance enrollment, loan application) generated from service contracts without per-form UI code. Management: admin config forms for workflow steps, report definitions, connector settings.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter-target: ui-app-emit can emit RJSF-based screen instead of hand-coded form when contract is purely data-capture (no complex UX logic)
candidates.risks: Default widget set is not shadcn/ui styled - requires custom widget registry (1-2 day effort, build once). AJV8 adds ~30KB to bundle. Schema-to-UX fidelity limited: complex conditional logic (show field X if Y=Z) requires uiSchema customization. Not suitable for wizard/stepper screens with async step validation - use TanStack Form there instead.

candidates.name: AG Grid Community
candidates.what: Headless-optional data grid with enterprise-grade features in free tier: infinite scroll, server-side row model, pivot, column grouping, export (CSV), cell editing, row virtualization, master-detail rows. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` high-density ledger/billing grid screens (claims list, AR aging, GL journal entries) where TanStack Table v8 custom virtualization would be significant work. Community = MIT/Apache-2; Enterprise = $999/dev/yr (Excel export, range select, advanced pivot, row grouping tree).
candidates.maintenance_health: AG Grid Ltd backing, long-term stable product, 13k GitHub stars. Community version has been stable since 2016. Actively maintained 2026. Enterprise pricing: $999/dev/yr subscription (no perpetual license - stopping payment disables enterprise features in prod).
candidates.stack_fit: React 19 adapter available. Works with Tailwind for theming via CSS variables. No NestJS coupling needed. Heavy bundle (~500KB) - use dynamic import for ledger-only screens. Pairs with TanStack Query for server-side data fetching.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Community: self-hosted npm, air-gap safe. Enterprise: license key required, not air-gap friendly without prior arrangement.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Primarily management surface: billing ledger, claims list, GL journal, inventory grid. Person-facing uses lighter components (TanStack Table + Virtual). Recommendation: use AG Grid Community only for the densest management data grids where TanStack Table + Virtual is not sufficient out of the box.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit package (@curaos/ui) - LedgerGrid component wraps AG Grid Community; ui-app-emit emits ledger archetype for contracts with financial line-item fields
candidates.risks: Enterprise lock-in if features like Excel export or advanced pivot are needed (kill-switch on payment stop). Community lacks row grouping tree and Excel export. TanStack Table v9 (when stable) closes most gaps. Ponytail: start with TanStack Table; only add AG Grid Community if a specific gap is proven by a failing user story.

candidates.name: cmdk (Command Menu)
candidates.what: Headless, accessible command palette React component. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` FilterBar/SearchShell archetype. Used by Linear, Vercel, Raycast, Sourcegraph. Provides: fuzzy search, keyboard navigation, ARIA roles, roving focus, useSyncExternalStore-based rendering. shadcn/ui ships it as `<Command>` - already composable with the existing shadcn pattern in @curaos/ui.
candidates.maintenance_health: Maintained by Vercel (pacocoursey). De facto standard. React 18/19 compatible (uses useId, useSyncExternalStore). shadcn Command component is a thin wrapper around it.
candidates.stack_fit: Already the basis for shadcn Command. Pull via shadcn CLI into @curaos/ui. Zero extra integration work beyond theming.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted npm. Air-gap safe.
