candidates.person_centric_fit: Person-facing: global search in patient-app (find appointment, find medication, navigate to record). Management: admin global command palette for power users. Dual-surface with same component.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit package (@curaos/ui) - SearchShell archetype component wraps cmdk; ui-app-emit emits search-shell for any contract with a search/list endpoint
candidates.risks: Minimal. Single-focused primitive. Only risk is if cmdk's fuzzy search is too simple for complex multi-entity search; augment with Fuse.js if needed (ponytail: add when a profiler says so).

verified.name: shadcn/ui + Radix UI primitives
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. LICENSE.md in shadcn-ui/ui repo is standard MIT. Copy-paste distribution model means copied source files become repo-owned - no runtime dependency on any external service, no license propagation risk. Air-gap safe: no outbound calls at runtime. Radix UI individual packages are also MIT. Note: shadcn has historically downplayed attribution but MIT does require copyright notice in copies; keep the file-level comment intact when the generator copies components in.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active as of June 2026. Last upstream push 2026-06-10. CLI v4 shipped March 2026 (agentic-era redesign). February 2026: unified radix-ui package support. January 2026: first-class RTL shipped. Weekly-to-biweekly release cadence confirmed. Radix primitive slowdown is real (WorkOS acquisition, original maintainers departed), but mitigated two ways: (1) shadcn/ui added Base UI as alternate primitive layer in late 2025 - you can scaffold per-component with Base UI from `npx shadcn@latest create`; (2) 130M monthly npm downloads means WorkOS has strong commercial incentive to keep Radix alive. One real open issue: Radix Screen-reader aria-live suppression bug (aria-hidden on app root when modal open) has been open since issue #1820 - relevant for patient/clinician surfaces that need screen-reader announcements alongside modals. Track it.
verified.simpler_alternative: Already adopted - ponytail ladder rung 2 applies. The ui-kit package at curaos/frontend/packages/ui-kit already has 11 @radix-ui/* packages installed and wraps them in shadcn-style CVA+Radix components (button.tsx comment explicitly says "shadcn-style cva + Radix Slot"). cmdk, vaul (drawer), react-day-picker are NOT yet in package.json - those are the gap packages covering the missing archetypes (Command palette, Drawer, DatePicker). Adding them is extending the already-adopted pattern, not a new dependency class.
verified.integration_reality: Tight native fit confirmed by codebase inspection. @curaos/ui already uses: CVA, clsx, tailwind-merge, react-hook-form + @hookform/resolvers, Zod 4, Tailwind 3, React 19, TypeScript 5, Bun 1.2+. All are the exact shadcn/ui peer/dependency stack. Zero glue needed for the existing 11 Radix packages. Gap packages (cmdk, vaul, react-day-picker + date-fns) are small, tree-shakeable, MIT. Generator-first: shadcn CLI writes source into repo - this IS the generator pattern; ui-app-emit can template imports from @curaos/ui rather than calling the shadcn CLI at runtime. No conflict. RTL: officially shipped January 2026 via CLI transform (logical CSS classes). Confirmed relevant for Arabic/Hebrew target locales. Security note: registry injection attack (RCE via malicious registry devDependencies) applies ONLY when running `shadcn add` from untrusted registries at dev time - irrelevant once components are copied in and committed. CLI v4 --dry-run/--diff flags mitigate for any future additions. One open Radix a11y bug (aria-live suppression) should be tracked as a known ceiling for modal+screen-reader combos.
verified.notes: Three gap packages to add for missing archetypes: cmdk (Command/command-palette), vaul (Drawer), react-day-picker + date-fns (DatePicker). All MIT. Effort S as claimed. Recommend adding Base UI as the primitive layer for any NET-NEW components going forward (not migration of existing ones) - it has Combobox and multi-select built-in and the team overlaps with Radix. Mark the Radix screen-reader aria-live bug as a ponytail ceiling: // ponytail: Radix aria-hidden-on-root bug open (#1820) - upgrade to Base UI Dialog when fixed or when screen-reader coverage is gated in CI.

verified.name: TanStack Table v9 + TanStack Virtual
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. GitHub repo (https://github.com/TanStack/table/blob/main/LICENSE) and npm metadata for @tanstack/react-table 8.21.3 and 9.0.0-beta.19 both show MIT. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution and local-vs-3rdparty duality - no SaaS call-home, no usage telemetry, no dual-license trap. Safe-permissive claim holds.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Mixed. v8 stable (8.21.3, last published April 2025 - over a year ago, no patch activity since). v9 is active: beta.17 on 2026-06-20, beta.19 on 2026-06-25. Active release cadence on v9 but it is still beta as of 2026-06-29. CRITICAL: May 2026 supply-chain attack (CVE-2026-45321 / GHSA-g7cv-rxg3-hmpx) hit 42 @tanstack/* packages via GitHub Actions OIDC token hijack by TeamPCP. @tanstack/react-table and @tanstack/virtual were NOT among the 42 compromised packages - they are in the confirmed-clean families. All currently-published versions are safe post-remediation. Hardening applied: SHA-pinned Actions refs, pull_request_target workflow restructure, repository_owner guards. Risk is residual trust-in-org, not an active vuln in table/virtual specifically.
verified.integration_reality: Bun: no issue - Bun installs npm packages natively, TanStack Table has no Node-specific native bindings. NestJS/Drizzle/Zod: no friction - table is a pure frontend concern, zero backend coupling. TypeSpec: irrelevant; table consumes OpenAPI-shaped response types, no contract-layer change needed. React 19 + React Compiler: REAL PROBLEM. v8 useReactTable() returns an internally-mutable instance, which React Compiler flags as incompatible (issues #33057, #5567, #6137 on TanStack/table). The only v8 workaround is 'use no memo' directives, disabling Compiler optimization on every table component. v9 explicitly fixes this (state management rewritten to follow official React patterns) but is still in beta with multiple breaking changes per release cycle (beta.10 changed row model factories, beta.17 changed prototype-chain APIs). Generator-first: fits cleanly - the 'data-grid' archetype hint can emit a stable wrapper around the Table instance in @curaos/ui. The wrapper boundary insulates app code from v8->v9 migration. Air-gap: safe, npm-import only.
verified.simpler_alternative: Yes, and it already exists. The codebase has a hand-rolled DataTable at curaos/frontend/packages/ui-kit/src/components/data-table.tsx (147 lines, zero deps). It covers: sortable columns, row selection (controlled + uncontrolled), aria-sort, numeric alignment, generic over row type. It does NOT cover: pagination, server-side fetch, column pinning, grouping, virtualization of large row counts. For the 24 web apps in current scope the existing component likely covers 80% of tables. The gap cases are: (a) platform-reporting-builder grids needing 1M+ row virtualization, (b) management grids needing column pinning + grouping. Those two use cases justify TanStack Table - but NOT across all 24 apps immediately. Ponytail ladder: rung 2 applies for most tables (existing DataTable). TanStack Table is rung 4/7 only for the virtualization + advanced grid subset.
verified.notes: Verdict is confirmed-trial not confirmed-adopt for three reasons: (1) v8 has a confirmed React 19 Compiler incompatibility with no clean fix - 'use no memo' is acceptable short-term but is a smell for a stack that will adopt the Compiler widely. (2) v9 resolves the Compiler issue but is mid-beta with breaking changes still landing weekly (beta.10, beta.17, beta.19 all had API breaks); pinning v8 now and migrating to v9-stable later is the correct sequencing but means two migrations. (3) The existing data-table.tsx already covers simple cases - adding TanStack for the full surface would be over-engineering per ponytail rung 2. Recommended scoped adoption: pin @tanstack/react-table 8.21.3 + @tanstack/react-virtual latest stable for the specific archetypes that need virtualization or advanced grid features (reporting-builder, high-volume management grids). Do not replace the existing DataTable for simple sorted lists. In generator: map 'data-grid' archetype to TanStack wrapper; leave 'data-table' archetype on existing component. Migrate to v9 when it exits beta (watch for v9.0.0 stable tag). Supply-chain note: @tanstack/react-table and @tanstack/react-virtual were confirmed clean in the May 2026 incident; pin exact versions in package.json and verify SHA on install in air-gap environments per version-pinning rule.

verified.name: TanStack Query v5 + TanStack Form v1
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed for both repos. No CLA, no copyleft. Compatible with self-hosted, air-gap (Zarf), and local-vs-3rdparty duality. No distribution restrictions.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Query v5: v5.101.1 released June 29 2026 (hours before this check), multiple releases/month throughout 2026, active. Form v1: stable since May 2025, Zod 4 support confirmed March 2026. One known rough edge: z.coerce.* fields change inferred input types under Zod 4 Standard Schema; workaround is .default().catch() pattern, not a blocker. Both actively maintained.
verified.integration_reality: SPLIT verdict between the two candidates. Query: already integrated - all 24+ frontend apps pin @tanstack/react-query@5.101.0 and curaos/tools/codegen (ui-app-emit.ts, ui-app-files.ts, hooks-reconcile-emit.ts) already emits useQuery hooks. Generator-first is already satisfied. This is adoption-confirmed, not a trial. Form v1: additive-only. RHF v7.79.0 + @hookform/resolvers@5.4.0 already in @curaos/ui-kit. Form v1 has no codegen emitter path yet; adding it requires a new generator archetype for multi-step wizard forms. No glue needed for Bun/NestJS/Zod - Form v1 uses Standard Schema natively with no resolver package. TypeSpec -> OpenAPI -> queryOptions generation is viable via Hey API or openapi-react-query-codegen if the generator needs to grow beyond current hand-rolled hooks.
verified.simpler_alternative: For Query: no simpler alternative - already the standard, no change needed. For Form v1 specifically: RHF v7 (already installed) covers multi-step wizards via useFormContext + FormProvider. 2026 independent consensus from Formisch, splitforms, LogRocket, MakersDen: default to RHF; reach for TanStack Form only when nested dynamic field arrays + per-field async validation state are the core requirement. Bundle sizes nearly identical (RHF ~10.7 KB gzipped, TanStack Form ~9.6 KB gzipped). Ponytail ladder: Form v1 does not clear rung 5 for general use - RHF covers it. It clears rung 7 only for the specific multi-step wizard archetype where RHF's form-wide validation timing is a real constraint.
verified.notes: SECURITY FLAG (resolved): May 11 2026 supply chain attack (CVE-2026-45321, GHSA-g7cv-rxg3-hmpx) hit 42 @tanstack/* packages via GitHub Actions cache poisoning. @tanstack/query* and @tanstack/form* are confirmed clean per multiple independent sources (Snyk, Wiz, Orca Security, StepSecurity). Attack was scoped to the router repo CI pipeline. Post-incident hardening published. Risk is real-but-resolved for these specific packages; pin versions and SHA-verify tarballs per curaos_version_pinning_rule. SPLIT RECOMMENDATION: Query v5 = confirmed-adopt (already adopted, verdict is retroactive confirmation). Form v1 = confirmed-trial, scoped strictly to net-new multi-step wizard archetype where field-level async validation and nested arrays are the primary requirement. Keep RHF for all existing forms and simple new forms. Do not run both for the same form type. Generator gate: Form v1 adoption is blocked on ui-app-emit gaining a wizard-archetype emitter - no hand-rolled forms, generator-first per curaos-generator-evolution-rule.
verified.candidate: TanStack Query v5 + TanStack Form v1

verified.name: Recharts
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. Copyright 2015-2026 Recharts Group. npm package.json + snyk both confirm. Air-gap safe (pure npm, no telemetry, no CDN calls). Self-hosted distribution: no restriction. Compatible with CuraOS dual local-vs-3rdparty model.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: v3.9.0 released 2026-06-23 (within 6 days of evaluation). Release cadence: v3.6 Dec 2025, v3.7 Jan 2026, v3.8 Mar 2026, v3.9 Jun 2026 - monthly to bi-monthly. Snyk: no known CVEs in recharts itself; prototype-pollution in old transitive deps predates v3 and is resolved. CRITICAL flag: v2.x officially deprecated 2026-05-24 - npm install warns. All sub-3.0.0 versions deprecated. Candidate cited "2.x stable, v3 preview" - this is STALE and WRONG. v3 is the only maintained line. Any adoption must pin v3.
verified.integration_reality: Fits the React 19 + Bun stack cleanly. No SSR/RSC conflict (pure client SVG). Peer deps: react + react-dom ^18||^19 - matches curaos React 19. No NestJS/Drizzle/Zod surface: charts are pure frontend, no backend glue needed. TypeSpec contracts produce typed time-series fields; Recharts consumes plain arrays - generator can emit typed data adapters. ui-kit (@curaos/ui) currently has zero chart deps - clean insertion point. Generator-first path is viable: ReportViewer + KpiCard wrappers in @curaos/ui, ui-app-emit emits chart archetypes for time-series contract fields. No lock-in risk: SVG component API is thin, wrappable, swappable. Bundle reality check: candidate claimed ~290KB - Bundlephobia data for v3.8.1 shows ~150KB minified (gzipped ~50KB), significantly better than claimed. Dynamic import per chart type is valid mitigation regardless.
verified.simpler_alternative: No chart utility exists in stdlib, Radix, Tailwind, or any already-installed dep (@curaos/ui has none). Scratch SVG is non-trivial for the required types (RadialBar, ComposedChart, Radar). visx (@visx/* packages, ~15KB modular) is the lower-level alternative but requires D3 knowledge and 2-3x implementation time per chart - not appropriate for generator-first emission. Nivo has better out-of-box WCAG 2.1 AA (all SVG charts include ARIA + keyboard nav) but heavier bundle and less ergonomic wrapper pattern. Recharts v3 accessibilityLayer is now default-on, closes most of the gap, though ARIA role choices (img vs graphics-symbol) remain imperfect. For CuraOS HealthStack (patient-app vitals, clinical dashboards) WCAG AA is a legal compliance requirement (ADA Title II healthcare deadline active as of 2026). This is the one real risk: Recharts accessibility is opt-in-improved, not guaranteed AA, requiring per-component augmentation.
verified.notes: DOWNGRADE from adopt to trial. Three reasons: (1) Candidate description is stale - v2 is deprecated, not stable; adoption must be v3-pinned from day one. (2) WCAG 2.1 AA gap: HealthStack patient-app and clinician-app face legal healthcare accessibility requirements (ADA Title II). Recharts accessibilityLayer (default-on in v3) is a step forward but known ARIA role mismatches (img vs graphics-symbol) cause axe/Lighthouse failures without manual augmentation per chart. Nivo is the only drop-in alternative with AA-closer defaults. (3) Generator-first integration is sound but needs a proof-of-concept emit before broad adoption - the wrapper pattern is well-established (shadcn uses Recharts) but the ui-app-emit -> typed archetype path must be validated on one chart type first. Recommended path: add recharts v3 to @curaos/ui, implement ReportViewer + one KpiCard archetype, run Lighthouse accessibility audit, confirm axe passes before wiring remaining chart types. If AA gap proves unacceptable on clinical views, evaluate Nivo for HealthStack overlay charts only while keeping Recharts for management/ops dashboards.

candidates.name: Puck (@puckeditor/core)
candidates.what: MIT React visual editor that outputs clean JSON to any backend; drag-drop composition of your own React components; multi-zone DropZone/slot model; plugin API; theming via CSS custom properties. Directly covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` (builder-studio surface emission) and `platform-reporting-builder` (dashboard/workspace surface type + SavedLayout). Generator-first target: ui-app-emit / builder-core-service surface type.
candidates.maintenance_health: 12.9k stars; v0.22.x releasing weekly in 2026; backed by Measured (funded studio); active docs + blog cadence; no evidence of stall
candidates.stack_fit: Pure React component - drops into any React app with zero wrapper. JSON output stored in any Postgres table via NestJS endpoint. No Bun/Drizzle/Zod friction. Plugin model extends without forking. Slot fields replace old DropZone API (minor migration note in v0.22 docs).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only - no cloud dependency; data plane is your own backend (NestJS + Drizzle). Satisfies curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule: first-party self-hosted by default, no 3rd-party option needed since there is no vendor cloud to BYO.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Builder-studio is the management surface for tenant admins authoring pages/layouts. Person-centric surfaces (patient-app, personal-* apps) consume the rendered JSON output. Dual-surface: admin authors with Puck -> person sees generated layout. Satisfies no-feature-loss: full admin control retained, person gets published result.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit + builder-core-service (SavedLayout entity stores Puck JSON; surface type 'dashboard' added to builder)
candidates.risks: v0.22 still pre-1.0 (breaking API changes possible); DropZone->slot migration in progress; no out-of-box auth/RBAC (must layer on top); limited built-in component library (you supply your own from @curaos/ui).

candidates.name: xyflow / @xyflow/react
candidates.what: MIT node-based canvas library for React (React Flow). Covers gap `workflow-automation-primitives` (WorkflowCanvas archetype) and `ui-kit-archetypes` (WorkflowCanvas component). Used by Windmill, n8n, Retool for their flow editors. Custom node types map to CuraOS BPM step types (human-approval, connector, code-step, AI-agent). Generator-first target: ui-kit WorkflowCanvas component + workflow-designer surface in builder-core.
candidates.maintenance_health: 37.3k stars; ~2.8M weekly npm downloads; docs updated April 2026; xyflow team active with Svelte Flow sibling; Pro subscription = examples only, no feature gates; sustainable via subscription model.
candidates.stack_fit: React component; TypeScript-native with exported Node/Edge/OnConnect types. Zero backend coupling - graph JSON stored in Drizzle. MiniMap, Background, Controls ship out of box. Custom node renders any React component, so BPM step palette maps directly to @curaos/ui components.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only; no cloud dependency. First-party self-hosted. Pro examples subscription is optional and does not affect runtime.
candidates.person_centric_fit: WorkflowCanvas is a management surface (admins design BPM flows). Person sees the workflow as a journey timeline card, not the canvas. Management retains full canvas control; person gets 'waiting on you' inbox item derived from the same flow definition.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-kit WorkflowCanvas component (wraps xyflow) + workflow-core-service flow definition JSON schema stored via Drizzle
candidates.risks: Large bundle if not tree-shaken (~80KB gzipped for core); requires CSS import; node layout algorithms are manual (no auto-layout in MIT tier - dagre integration is documented but requires extra dep). Pro examples cost ~$14-30/mo for access to advanced templates.

candidates.name: GrapesJS (@grapesjs/react)
candidates.what: BSD-3-Clause web builder framework rewritten in TypeScript; official React wrapper @grapesjs/react. Covers `ui-kit-archetypes` gap for email/notification template authoring and page template builder surface inside builder-core. Plugin ecosystem for blocks/panels/commands. Generator-first target: notify-service NotificationTemplate authoring UI + builder-core template surface.
candidates.maintenance_health: 25.8k stars; v0.22.16 current; team ships commercial Studio SDK (v1.0.62 in 2026) proving sustainable business model; regular core commits.
candidates.stack_fit: React 18/19 compatible via @grapesjs/react wrapper. Outputs HTML/CSS or JSON. Heavier than Puck (~200KB). Better for HTML email template editing (notify-service) than for app-page composition where Puck is cleaner. No Bun/NestJS/Drizzle coupling.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host; core is BSD, no cloud required. Studio SDK is an optional commercial upgrade for a richer no-code experience.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Template authoring is a management surface (admins author email/SMS/notification templates). Person receives the rendered output. Full template variable/locale management preserved on management surface; person gets correctly personalized comms.
candidates.generator_first_target: notify-service NotificationTemplate (drag-drop template editor) + builder-core HTML template surface type
candidates.risks: Heavier bundle than Puck; HTML/CSS output model is less clean than Puck JSON for app-surfaces; Studio SDK is commercial (must track version boundary). React wrapper less mature than core. Avoid for general page/app builder where Puck wins.

candidates.name: Form.io (formio/formio.js renderer)
candidates.what: MIT JSON-powered form renderer + drag-drop form builder React component. Covers gap `ui-kit-archetypes` (Wizard/Stepper, multi-step forms) and `workflow-automation-primitives` (FormTrigger binding in workflow-core). JSON schema form definition aligns with TypeSpec contract-first: form schema derived from entity contract. Generator-first target: ui-app-emit Wizard archetype + workflow-core FormTrigger.
candidates.license: MIT (formio.js renderer); OSLv3 (formio server); Enterprise Form Builder module is commercial
candidates.maintenance_health: Active; formio/react GitHub maintained; Docker self-host documented; enterprise tier gives team sustainability. OSLv3 server is weak copyleft but only relevant if you run/modify the server, not just the renderer.
candidates.stack_fit: formio/react provides React component wrapper. JSON schema maps cleanly to Zod-validated entity models (generator can emit form schema from .tsp entity). NestJS backend serves form definitions from Drizzle. No Bun friction on renderer side. Server (OSLv3) can be run as a self-hosted sidecar or replaced with NestJS endpoint.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host renderer (MIT) + own NestJS backend. Server sidecar optional. Enterprise Form Builder module = commercial BYO option if richer builder needed.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Multi-step forms are both person-facing (patient intake, consent capture, KYC) and management-facing (admin form designer). Same JSON schema renders in both contexts. Person-centric: journey-style wizard. Management: full form designer with conditional logic, validation, calculated fields.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit Wizard/FormTrigger archetype (codegen emits form schema from entity model) + workflow-core FormTrigger binding
candidates.risks: OSLv3 server license is non-standard; must not copy/modify and redistribute server without open-sourcing changes. Enterprise Form Builder module required for embeddable drag-drop designer (commercial). Renderer alone is MIT and sufficient for render-only use cases. TypeScript types less complete than Puck/xyflow.

candidates.name: dnd-kit (@dnd-kit/core + @dnd-kit/sortable)
candidates.what: MIT drag-and-drop primitive toolkit for React. Not a full builder - the low-level foundation that Puck and other builders sit on. Covers `ui-kit-archetypes` gap for KanbanBoard and any sortable list archetype needing custom drag behavior beyond what Puck's DropZone provides. Generator-first target: @curaos/ui KanbanBoard, ApprovalInbox sortable columns.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~2.8M weekly downloads; community standard in 2025-2026; Atlassian's Pragmatic DnD is the only credible alternative for 1000+ item lists; react-beautiful-dnd deprecated.
candidates.stack_fit: Pure React hooks; 6KB core; no CSS opinion; TypeScript-native. Fits directly into @curaos/ui component library. Puck already uses it internally so no version conflict if Puck is also adopted.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host; no cloud.
candidates.person_centric_fit: KanbanBoard/ApprovalInbox used on both management (admin pipeline, task board) and person (my-tasks board, my-requests queue) surfaces from the same component with different data.
candidates.generator_first_target: @curaos/ui KanbanBoard + ApprovalInbox components (archetype vocabulary in ui-app-emit)
candidates.risks: Collision detection at 1000+ droppables becomes measurable; upgrade path is Pragmatic DnD for that scale. No built-in tree/virtualized list support.

candidates.name: Activepieces (self-hosted service)
candidates.what: MIT workflow automation platform (Zapier alternative). Covers gap `workflow-automation-primitives` (connector registry, AI-agent step, MCP servers, pause-resume, test-step). 280+ pieces auto-become MCP servers. createPiece pattern is the direct prior art for @curaos/connector-sdk. NOT an npm-importable React SDK - embedding is via iframe or API. Generator-first target: @curaos/connector-sdk port of createPiece pattern; automation-core-service connector registry.
candidates.maintenance_health: High activity; 280+ pieces; AI agent + MCP server integration added 2024-2025; Docker/K8s/Helm self-host; $5-20/mo VPS cost for self-host.
candidates.stack_fit: Can run as a self-hosted sidecar automation engine (BYO-3rd-party mode) OR its createPiece/pause-resume source is port-adapted into @curaos/connector-sdk (first-party self-hosted mode). Iframe embed for the visual builder. REST/webhook API for workflow trigger from NestJS. No native NestJS SDK but REST coupling is straightforward.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Both: self-host Activepieces as the automation engine (BYO-3rd-party option) OR port createPiece into @curaos/connector-sdk for a fully first-party connector registry. curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule satisfied.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person sees 'my connections' + 'my automations' + 'waiting on you' inbox derived from automation runs. Management sees full flow designer + connector catalog + run logs. Person-centric: pre-built journey templates; management: full no-code canvas.
candidates.generator_first_target: @curaos/connector-sdk (port createPiece pattern) + automation-core-service AiAgentStep/McpServer contracts
candidates.risks: iframe embed is not a native React SDK; for embedded flow designer in builder-studio, must iframe Activepieces or build own canvas on xyflow. createPiece port is the primary value path, not iframe embed. AI agent step requires LiteLLM + Langfuse + Presidio PHI-scrub wiring per gap-analysis.

candidates.name: Appsmith (Apache 2.0, self-hosted)
candidates.what: Apache 2.0 internal-tool/admin-panel builder. Covers `platform-reporting-builder` gap (ad-hoc report/query builder, per-user dashboard layout) as a self-hosted sidecar. Not an npm-import; embedding via iframe. 25+ DB connectors; drag-drop widget canvas; custom React component support via iframe widget. Generator-first target: reports-service ReportDefinition surface (BYO-3rd-party mode = Appsmith; first-party = Puck-based builder-core).
candidates.maintenance_health: Active; large community; cloud $15/user/mo but self-hosted community edition is free with no per-user fees; Docker/K8s self-host well documented.
candidates.stack_fit: Runs as a Docker/K8s sidecar. NestJS services registered as REST datasources. Iframe embed into builder-studio. Not React-component native. Best as a BYO-3rd-party option for tenants who already use Appsmith, while Puck-based builder-core is the first-party path.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Both modes: self-hosted Appsmith as BYO-3rd-party admin builder + Puck/builder-core as first-party. Satisfies dual-option requirement.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Appsmith is management-surface-only by design (internal tools). Person surfaces remain in the CuraOS React apps. No person-facing use case for Appsmith directly.
candidates.generator_first_target: reports-service ReportDefinition (Appsmith as optional BYO viewer for power users; first-party ReportDefinition entity feeds Gotenberg/Superset per gap-analysis)
candidates.risks: iframe embed has postMessage API for bidirectional comms but is not native React component. Enterprise tier required for SSO/SAML/audit on cloud; self-hosted community is permissive. Heavy service (~1-2GB RAM). Overlap with Puck-based builder-core; use only as BYO-3rd-party fallback.

candidates.name: Craft.js (@craftjs/core)
candidates.what: MIT React drag-drop page editor framework. Same target as Puck (ui-kit-archetypes / builder-studio surface). Predates Puck; more low-level; slower maintenance cadence.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~7k stars; last npm publish Feb 2025; community issues (#483, #507) flag stalled v2 and slow response; no backing org. Falling behind Puck (12.9k stars, weekly releases).
candidates.stack_fit: React only; no Bun/NestJS friction but more boilerplate than Puck for the same outcome.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host; no cloud.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same dual-surface potential as Puck but maintenance risk makes it a liability.
candidates.generator_first_target: N/A - rejected
candidates.risks: Stalled v2; single maintainer; Puck is strictly superior in maintenance health, API clarity, and release cadence. No reason to choose Craft.js over Puck.

candidates.name: Plasmic (AGPL Studio + MIT SDK)
candidates.what: Visual builder + headless CMS. Studio is AGPL; SDK is MIT. The editor itself is partly closed-source (codegen engine); self-hosting the full studio is limited. Covers same ui-kit-archetypes / builder-core gap as Puck.
candidates.maintenance_health: Active; well-funded; but studio self-hosting is constrained - 'self-hosted = generated code, not the codegen engine or editor UI'.
candidates.stack_fit: SDK integrates with React but editor requires Plasmic cloud or limited self-hosted mode. AGPL studio creates service-boundary risk for CuraOS's builder-studio embedding use case.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Fails first-party self-hosted requirement: editor engine is not fully self-hostable under permissive terms.
candidates.person_centric_fit: N/A - rejected on license/self-host grounds.
candidates.generator_first_target: N/A - rejected
candidates.risks: AGPL studio cannot be embedded in CuraOS builder-studio without triggering service-boundary AGPL requirements. Editor engine is partly proprietary. Puck is strictly better for the self-hosted embedding use case.

candidates.name: ToolJet (AGPL)
candidates.what: AGPL open-source internal tool / admin-panel builder. Same target as Appsmith. Has embed docs but AGPL license means embedding in CuraOS builder-studio (a non-AGPL product) requires commercial license purchase.
candidates.maintenance_health: Active; large community; Docker/K8s self-host; AI app generation added 2025.
candidates.stack_fit: AGPL blocks embedding without commercial license. Appsmith (Apache 2.0) is a drop-in alternative that avoids this risk entirely.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host possible but license cost required for non-AGPL products.
candidates.person_centric_fit: N/A - rejected on license grounds.
candidates.generator_first_target: N/A - rejected
candidates.risks: AGPL embedding trap: changed from GPL to AGPL specifically to require commercial license when embedded in SaaS products. Appsmith is strictly better-licensed for CuraOS's use case.

verified.name: Puck (@puckeditor/core)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. Safe for self-hosted + air-gap distribution. No CLA, no proprietary runtime required for core editor. Puck Cloud/AI tiers are optional SaaS add-ons; core is fully offline-capable. No license risk.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. v0.22.0 published 2026-06-28 (one day ago). Weekly release cadence verified. Backed by Measured (funded studio). 12.9k GitHub stars. HN thread active June 2026. No stall signal. HOWEVER: still pre-1.0 with documented breaking API changes across every minor version (plugin API rewrite in v0.21, DropZone deprecated and removed in favor of Slots in v0.19, data model mutation requiring migrate() helper). No schema version field in the JSON payload - consumers must track Puck version to know how to read stored data.
verified.integration_reality: Overstated. The candidate calls integration "zero friction," but three real friction points exist: (1) DropZone is deprecated; the Slots data model stores nested components as inline arrays rather than a global zones object - any SavedLayout stored under DropZone schema is broken on upgrade unless migrate() is run against every persisted row; that is a data migration burden the generator must own. (2) Puck has no Zod schema for its JSON output - the shape is TypeScript-inferred from the library internals, not a published JSON Schema or TypeSpec model; integrating with CuraOS TypeSpec contracts requires a hand-authored Zod shape that will drift on each Puck minor. (3) The builder-core-service SavedLayout entity would need to store raw Puck JSON alongside or instead of the existing DSL format - adding a second JSON schema language to the surface model, not replacing one.
verified.simpler_alternative: BLOCKING - ponytail ladder rung 2 fires: GrapesJS (v0.22.13) is already installed AND already integrated in builder-studio. A full working SitesCanvas component (storage-adapter.ts + SitesCanvas.tsx + basic-blocks.ts) exists, wired to the live builder-core-service via BuilderClient, with a real grapesjs JSONB field in the SurfaceInput DSL already persisted to Postgres. Adding Puck would introduce a SECOND visual editor with a different JSON schema, a different drag-drop model, and a different component registration API into the same app. That is net negative: more bundle, two data model dialects in SavedLayout, doubled maintenance surface, and a breaking migration for any surface already authored in GrapesJS. GrapesJS also ships a Style Manager, Device Manager, and Asset Manager out of the box - capabilities Puck explicitly does NOT include, requiring @curaos/ui components to supply everything. For the dashboard/reporting-builder surface type gap, GrapesJS's existing plugin API and block registration covers the same composition need. Extend the existing integration; do not add Puck.
verified.notes: Local-vs-3rdparty rule (curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule) requires every integratable area to offer BOTH a first-party self-hosted option AND a BYO-third-party-provider option. Puck is self-hosted-only by design; there is no third-party managed provider option to BYO. This is a structural mismatch with the rule's duality requirement. GrapesJS has the same profile but is already present - the question is moot for an already-adopted dependency. For any net-new visual editor adoption the rule would require either a managed-provider pairing or a documented waiver. Separately: the Puck Cloud AI tier (AI page generation, versioned datastore, multiplayer) calls Measured's servers at runtime - if that tier is ever adopted it breaks the air-gap/self-hosted constraint. Core MIT tier does not phone home. The candidate's local-vs-3rdparty analysis (claiming "no 3rd-party option needed since there is no vendor cloud") is incorrect under the rule as written; the rule requires offering a BYO path regardless of whether a vendor cloud exists. This is a secondary concern given the primary rejection reason (GrapesJS already installed and integrated).

verified.name: @xyflow/react
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. No runtime feature gating. proOptions.hideAttribution is a single client-side prop with no network check. Self-hosted and air-gap distribution fully compatible - MIT permits unrestricted use, modification, and redistribution including bundled offline artifacts.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. v12.11.1 released 2026-06-22 (days before this analysis). Prior releases: 12.10.2 (2026-03-27), 12.8.4 (2025-08-20), 12.6.0 (2025-04-17). Continuous cadence. No xyflow-specific CVEs. React RSC CVE-2025-55182 is unrelated (NestJS RSC deserialization; xyflow is client-side canvas only). 2.8M weekly npm downloads. Svelte Flow sibling maintained in parallel - team is not single-product fragile.
verified.simpler_alternative: None. No installed dep or stdlib covers a node-based canvas. d3 requires ~10x surface area to re-implement what xyflow ships. Ponytail ladder halts at rung 4: xyflow is ALREADY the committed dep per ADR-0121d (Accepted 2026-05-24, RESOLVED in RESOLUTION-MAP). This is not a new adoption decision - it is a confirmation of an existing accepted ADR.
verified.integration_reality: React 19 + Bun ESM compatible (ships ESM; Bun resolves it natively; React 19 support confirmed Oct 2025). TypeScript-native. Zero backend coupling - xyflow renders client-side only; CuraOS IR JSON is the canonical store in Drizzle; xyflow is the renderer layer. No NestJS/TypeSpec/Zod surface touched. Generator target is well-defined: ui-kit WorkflowCanvas wrapper emits proOptions={{ hideAttribution: true }} by default. CSS import required (one line). Bundle: ~80KB gzipped for core per candidate claim; Bundlephobia page was inaccessible but this aligns with v12 published data. Dagre is unmaintained - ELK.js (already named in ADR-0121d, EPL license, layout-only, not runtime-bundled) is the correct layout engine. Windmill reference: they use @xyflow/svelte (not @xyflow/react) - structural confirmation of fitness, minor claim inaccuracy in the candidate writeup.
verified.notes: Decision already made at ADR-0121d. This verification confirms the ADR's claims hold under 2026 scrutiny. One generator emission fix needed: proOptions={{ hideAttribution: true }} must be in the WorkflowCanvas wrapper emitted by codegen, not left to per-app config. Dagre dep should not be added - use ELK.js (already co-selected in ADR-0121d). ELK handles non-tree graphs and complex layouts; dagre is documented as unmaintained by xyflow's own docs.

verified.name: GrapesJS (@grapesjs/react)
verified.license_confirmed: CONFIRMED BSD-3-Clause for grapesjs core (v0.22.16, also v0.23.2 on npm as of late June 2026). Studio SDK is a separate commercial product under a proprietary license that requires domain registration, forbids watermark removal, and embeds license-validation code - this is NOT BSD-3 and is NOT the adoption target. The boundary is clear: core = BSD-3, Studio SDK = commercial. Self-hosted and air-gap use of the core is clean. Air-gap of Studio SDK is blocked by domain-license-validation requirement - incompatible with [[curaos-airgap-rule]]. Candidate must be scoped to core + community plugins only.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Core grapesjs: actively maintained. v0.22.16 released April 2026, v0.23.2 published ~24 days before June 29 2026 (so ~June 5 2026). 34 open issues, 22 open PRs, commits through June 25 2026. MJML plugin updated June 15 2026. No GrapesJS-specific CVEs found. Sustainable via Studio SDK commercial revenue. BUT: the WRAPPER situation is a red flag. The candidate names @grapesjs/react (official, v2.0.0, last published ~October 2024 per 8-months-ago signal from npm search). React 19 support was added to the Studio SDK, NOT confirmed in the open-source @grapesjs/react v2.0. The community package grapesjs-react v4.0.x is what the codebase already uses - and the production code in builder-studio/SitesCanvas.tsx documents a CJS/ESM interop bug requiring a require() runtime workaround.
verified.simpler_alternative: PONYTAIL LADDER HIT: grapesjs (core) + grapesjs-react are ALREADY INSTALLED in the monorepo (curaos/frontend/apps/builder-studio, grapesjs@^0.22.13, grapesjs-react@^4.0.2) and already in production use for the SitesCanvas surface. This is rung 2 - already in codebase. No new dependency needed. The candidate's @grapesjs/react wrapper (official, ~3700 weekly downloads, lower maturity) should be evaluated against the already-in-use grapesjs-react@4.0.x before switching. EmailBuilder.js (MIT, usewaypoint, 468 stars) is a simpler JSON-first alternative specifically for email/notification templates - lighter, no interop bugs, clean JSON output schema that fits notify-service better than HTML/CSS blob. Worth evaluating for the notify-service use case specifically before extending GrapesJS there.
verified.integration_reality: React 19 incompatibility with @grapesjs/react is a CONFIRMED open issue (GrapesJS/react #18). CuraOS runs React 19.1.0. The existing production code in builder-studio already hit this and works around it via a require() runtime hack documented in SitesCanvas.tsx comments (CJS/ESM interop mismatch where the bundle exposes GrapesjsReact named export, not default). This is a real integration tax, not theoretical. The generator-first target (notify-service NotificationTemplate editor) adds a second integration point against NestJS/Drizzle: GrapesJS stores HTML+CSS blobs or its own JSON snapshot format. Neither maps cleanly to a TypeSpec-defined NotificationTemplate schema or Drizzle column type without custom serialization. Storing raw GrapesJS JSON as JSONB works but locks the template format to GrapesJS internals - migration risk if GrapesJS is ever swapped. Bun compatibility: no specific issues found; GrapesJS is a browser-side library and Bun only touches it at install/bundle time where ESM support is fine. ~200KB bundle weight confirmed heavier than Puck.
verified.notes: 1. SCOPE CORRECTION: Candidate names @grapesjs/react (official wrapper, ~3700 downloads/week, last release ~Oct 2024, React 19 NOT confirmed fixed in OSS wrapper). Codebase uses grapesjs-react (community wrapper, v4.0.3 in node_modules). These are different packages. Adoption evaluation should target grapesjs-react@latest (already installed) not @grapesjs/react. 2. USE-CASE SPLIT: For builder-core HTML template surface - already adopted, no action needed (rung 2 of ponytail ladder). For notify-service NotificationTemplate authoring - consider EmailBuilder.js (MIT, JSON-first, stateless, maps cleanly to a typed schema) before extending GrapesJS to a second use case. GrapesJS email output is HTML blobs; notify-service needs structured template variables, locale slots, and Drizzle-storable format. 3. STUDIO SDK BOUNDARY: Must be tracked explicitly in ai/rules. Any team member touching builder-studio must know the Studio SDK is commercial and off-limits for air-gap tenants. The gjs.market comparison pages are vendor-adjacent (gjs.market sells GrapesJS plugins/services); weight those sources lower. 4. VERDICT RATIONALE: trial (not confirmed-adopt) because: (a) the React 19 interop bug is real and the workaround in SitesCanvas is a technical debt marker, (b) the notify-service extension is a new use case that needs its own evaluation vs simpler alternatives, (c) the wrapper package identity needs resolving (@grapesjs/react vs grapesjs-react). The core library itself is solid; the integration surface has sharp edges.

verified.name: Form.io (formio/formio.js renderer)
verified.license_confirmed: PARTIALLY WRONG. The candidate claims formio.js renderer is MIT - this is CONTESTED. The formio/formio.js GitHub repo currently shows OSLv3 in the repo license field (same as the formio server), not MIT. The @formio/react wrapper package on npm does list MIT. The distinction is blurry and disputed (a 2020 GitHub issue confirmed formio.js staying MIT, but current repository metadata contradicts this). OSLv3 is a network-copyleft license: deploying a modified OSLv3 work as a SaaS/self-hosted service to external users triggers source-disclosure obligations - identical to AGPL in practice. This directly conflicts with CuraOS's self-hosted + air-gap + BYO-provider distribution model. The Enterprise Form Builder Module (drag-drop builder embed) is a separately licensed commercial module requiring a unique library license on top of the platform license - it is NOT in the MIT/OSLv3 open source package. License claim "MIT renderer, OSLv3 server only" is optimistic at best, unverified at worst. Legal review required before any adoption.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. @formio/js v5.4.1 published ~10 days ago (as of late June 2026). @formio/react v6.2.0 published ~2 months ago, 18k weekly downloads. Enterprise v9.7.0 released Feb 2026. CVE-2025-67718 (CVSS 8.7) found in formio server (path-handling auth bypass, affects server package not renderer). TypeScript types had breaking regressions in the v4->v5 migration (missing ExtendedComponentSchema, BuilderInfo, ValidateOptions in @formio/js v5.0.0). Open issue count high across repos. The @formio/js package name itself changed from formiojs (legacy, frozen at 4.21.7) to @formio/js v5 - any existing formiojs references are on a dead package. Verdict: maintained but TypeScript quality is a liability for a TypeSpec-first codebase.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - already installed. @curaos/ui already ships react-hook-form 7.79.0 + @hookform/resolvers 5.4.0 + zod 4.4.3 + Radix UI primitives. Multi-step wizard = a state machine over RHF steps + Radix Tabs/Dialog - no new dependency. TypeSpec entity -> Zod schema -> RHF form is already the natural generator target and is TypeScript-native, tree-shakeable, and zero OSLv3 risk. RJSF (react-jsonschema-form, MIT, 46k weekly downloads, v5 stable, v6 in beta 2025) is the correct JSON-schema-to-form drop-in if a schema-driven renderer is truly needed - it does NOT require a commercial module for the builder, and its TypeScript types are complete. SurveyJS Form Library is MIT for rendering but survey-creator (drag-drop builder) requires a commercial developer license - same commercial trap as formio's Enterprise Form Builder Module.
verified.integration_reality: Overstated. The claim "no Bun friction" is unverified - no documented Bun+formio.js integration evidence found. @formio/js v5 is ESM but ships a large bundle (historical issue #1243); v5 claims optimization but no measured figures surfaced. The generator-first target (TypeSpec -> formio JSON schema) requires a custom emitter: TypeSpec uses its own schema dialect, formio uses its own JSON schema superset - these do not map 1:1 without a non-trivial translation layer. Zod schema -> formio schema is also non-trivial (formio schema has formio-specific keys: validate, conditional, logic, etc.). The candidate frames this as "aligns cleanly" but it actually requires a bespoke codegen emitter with ongoing maintenance. RHF + Zod already emit from the same Zod schema the codebase uses everywhere - no translation layer needed.
verified.notes: Primary kill reasons: (1) License ambiguity on the renderer itself (OSLv3 vs MIT unresolved; network-copyleft is a hard blocker for CuraOS's self-hosted SaaS distribution model). (2) The drag-drop builder - the stated use-case for the 'workflow-automation-primitives' gap - is commercial-only (Enterprise Form Builder Module), negating the open-source value proposition entirely. (3) Ponytail ladder rung 2 fires: react-hook-form + zod + Radix are already installed in @curaos/ui and cover wizard/multi-step without a new dependency. (4) TypeScript types regressed in v5 migration - a liability for a TypeSpec-contract-first codebase. Downgraded to reference: formio's JSON schema definition format and conditional-logic model are worth studying as prior art for the generator's form-schema design. RJSF is the correct trial candidate if schema-driven rendering (beyond what RHF covers) is actually needed after proving the gap with RHF first.

candidates.name: pocket-id
candidates.what: Passkey-only OIDC IdP (WebAuthn/FIDO2 exclusive, no passwords). Fills: identity-service OIDC front-end (taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles) + SMART-on-FHIR scope issuer. Already deployed locally. BSD-2-Clause Go binary, SQLite default, single container. Provides JWKS endpoint for NestJS JWT guard consumption.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~5.7k stars, active releases, community org (no major backing org), BSD-2, Go + Svelte frontend. Rapid growth since 2024. Suitable for homelab/self-host use case. Risk: small team.
candidates.stack_fit: NestJS consumes JWKS + standard OIDC tokens via @nestjs/passport + passport-jwt (no wrapper needed). Pocket-id REST API for user/group management. LDAP sync available. No NestJS-specific SDK but standard OIDC protocol = zero wrapper friction.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party self-host only (no BYO-3rd-party option -- no cloud variant). Meets the local-first charter; BYO-3rd-party would use Keycloak or Zitadel path instead.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Passkey-first auth is the best person-centric auth UX (no password, phishing-resistant). Persons authenticate once via Face ID/Touch ID; identity flows downstream to patient-app, business apps, clinician-app via OIDC. Aligns with person-owns-their-identity directive.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/identity-sdk) -- codegen emitter should emit JWKS-aware JWT guard decorator + SMART scope decorators from TypeSpec contract rather than per-service hand-coded guards
candidates.risks: Single-factor only (passkey); no password fallback for legacy/accessibility scenarios. Small maintainer team. SQLite default (swap to Postgres for production HA). No cloud/BYO variant -- must be paired with Keycloak for orgs that need SAML/LDAP federation.

candidates.name: Keycloak
candidates.what: Full-featured OIDC/OAuth2/SAML IdP with passkey/WebAuthn (native since 26.4, GA), LDAP/AD federation, fine-grained authz services, multi-realm multi-tenant. Fills: BYO-3rd-party OIDC path + SAML federation + enterprise SSO + SMART-on-FHIR scope enforcement. Covers taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles (enterprise/SAML tier).
candidates.maintenance_health: Red Hat backed, 20k+ stars, v26.x active (26.4 passkeys GA Sep 2025), decades of production use. JVM overhead: 1.25GB RAM baseline for a single pod -- heavy for air-gap/homelab. The de-facto enterprise OSS IdP.
candidates.stack_fit: nest-keycloak-connect npm adapter (guards, decorators, resource/scope protection). Multi-tenant via realm-per-tenant or per-org realm resolver. JWKS endpoint for standard JWT validation. Wrapper needed for multi-tenant realm routing but documented pattern exists.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Both: self-host (Helm chart, K8s operator) AND can be used as BYO-3rd-party (managed Keycloak from Red Hat, cloud IAM providers). Satisfies [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]] dual requirement.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Passkey/FIDO2 native (26.4+), social login, self-service account console for persons to manage their credentials, MFA. WebAuthn conditional UI now in default login forms. Directly supports person-owns-their-auth-devices.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/identity-sdk) -- emitter should generate Keycloak-compatible resource+scope declarations from TypeSpec contract ops, not hand-code resource_access claims per service
candidates.risks: JVM overhead (1.25GB+) is heavy for homelab/air-gap. Memory issues reported in 26.5+ (GC tuning needed). Java/Quarkus operational complexity. nest-keycloak-connect adapter lags behind Keycloak releases. Multi-realm complexity at scale. SAML upstream brokering available (unlike Zitadel v3).

candidates.name: Zitadel
candidates.what: Go-based API-first OIDC/OAuth2 IdP with native multi-org model, passkey/WebAuthn, immutable event-sourced audit trail, and TypeScript Actions. Strong BYO-3rd-party candidate. Fills: enterprise OIDC IdP + multi-tenant org management. Taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles (BYO tier).
candidates.maintenance_health: Active org, v3+ (AGPL since March 2025), 9k+ stars, Go binary (low RAM vs Keycloak), no JVM. Removed CockroachDB in v3 (Postgres only). Enterprise commercial license available. Immutable audit trail built-in.
candidates.stack_fit: OIDC standard tokens consumed by NestJS via passport-jwt/JWKS. gRPC management API has Go/TypeScript SDKs. TypeScript Actions for lifecycle hooks. No native NestJS module but standard OIDC = minimal wrapper. Multi-org maps cleanly to CuraOS tenant model.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Both: self-host (Helm, K8s) + managed Zitadel Cloud. Satisfies dual requirement -- but AGPL means if CuraOS modifies Zitadel source and exposes as SaaS, disclosure required.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Passkey-first, self-service account management, GDPR-ready event log. Multi-org means each patient/customer org can have their own IdP branding/settings.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/identity-sdk) -- emit Zitadel resource registration API calls from TypeSpec contract during codegen scaffold
candidates.risks: AGPL-3.0 since v3 (March 2025) -- if org ever modifies Zitadel and offers as SaaS, source must be open. Running unmodified = safe. Legal review needed. SAML upstream IdP consumption not supported (IdP-only SAML). No multi-tenancy in OSS tier of Kratos (Ory), but Zitadel's org model is built-in. CockroachDB dropped in v3 -- CNPG Postgres fits perfectly.

candidates.name: @simplewebauthn/server
candidates.what: TypeScript-first WebAuthn/FIDO2/passkey library for Node.js. Handles registration ceremony (attestation parsing, CBOR decoding) and authentication ceremony (assertion verification, signature check) entirely in-process. Fills: passkey WebAuthn in identity-service for non-pocket-id deployments or local dev. Taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles (authn primitive).
candidates.maintenance_health: De-facto Node.js WebAuthn standard, actively maintained, works on NestJS/Express/Fastify/Hono/Cloudflare Workers. Has Drizzle-compatible passkey schema example. Paired with @simplewebauthn/browser for frontend.
candidates.stack_fit: npm-import into identity-service. Drizzle schema for passkey credential table documented. NestJS + PostgreSQL demo repo exists. Zero wrapper needed -- pure TypeScript, Bun-compatible. Plug into existing identity-service NestJS guards.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party self-host always (in-process library, no external service). Complements pocket-id (which also uses FIDO2 but as a standalone IdP).
candidates.person_centric_fit: Enables passkey-first authentication (Touch ID, Face ID, hardware keys) as the primary person login method -- no password, phishing-resistant, best-in-class UX.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/identity-sdk) -- codegen should emit passkey registration/authentication endpoint stubs + Drizzle credential table schema as part of identity-service scaffold
candidates.risks: Library only -- storage, session, challenge management are application responsibility (use Drizzle + Redis/memory for challenges). Requires HTTPS in production (WebAuthn spec). Must be paired with a fallback authn method for accessibility/legacy.

candidates.name: Cerbos
candidates.what: Open-core policy decision point (PDP) sidecar for ABAC+RBAC+ReBAC. Policies in YAML/Rego, evaluated via gRPC/HTTP. Externalizes authorization from every service. Fills: shared authz policy engine replacing per-service roles.guard sprawl. Taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles (authz layer), workflow-automation.ops.rbac, crm-insurance-broker.platform.role-based-access.
candidates.maintenance_health: 4,448 stars (Jun 2026), v0.45.x, active releases, Cerbos Ltd backed, Apache 2.0 PDP, open-core (Hub is commercial). NestJS official demo + npm SDK. Used by production B2B SaaS.
candidates.stack_fit: cerbos npm SDK + nestjs-cerbos interceptor pattern. NestJS guard calls Cerbos PDP over gRPC with principal attributes + resource attributes; PDP evaluates YAML policy. ABAC lets policies use tenant context, clinical role, HIPAA consent flags. No wrapper beyond the SDK guard pattern (already documented).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host PDP (open-source, Docker/Helm). Hub (policy management UI) is SaaS-optional; air-gap deployments use local bundle endpoint. Meets dual requirement: self-host PDP always available, Hub as optional BYO-cloud control plane.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Centralizes who-can-see-what logic, enabling privacy-by-policy (clinician only sees consented patient records, person only sees their own data, HIPAA field-level rules as policy YAML). Simplifies adding consent-aware access rules without touching every service.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter-target -- TypeSpec contract resource ops should generate Cerbos resource definitions + policy scaffolds (derived resource.yaml per service), injected into service scaffold by ui-app-emit / emitServiceLive
candidates.risks: PDP sidecar = extra hop per authz check (latency, though sub-ms for cached policies). Hub internet dependency for air-gap policy bundle distribution (solvable with local bundle server). ABAC policy authoring learning curve. Cerbos Hub is commercial for full policy-management UI.

candidates.name: OpenFGA
candidates.what: CNCF Incubating Zanzibar-inspired ReBAC authorization engine (Auth0/Okta donated). Models authorization as relationship tuples + authorization model DSL. Strong for object-ownership hierarchies (document viewer, org member, folder editor). Fills: fine-grained ReBAC authz layer for document/record ownership and org hierarchy. Taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles (ReBAC tier).
candidates.maintenance_health: CNCF Incubating, Okta/Grafana maintained, @openfga/sdk npm v0.9.3 (published monthly), Apache 2.0. @rxap/nest-openfga community NestJS module. Active ABAC+ReBAC roadmap. Watch API for cache invalidation.
candidates.stack_fit: @openfga/sdk npm + @rxap/nest-openfga module. NestJS guard delegates to OpenFGA check API. Tuples stored in Postgres (which CNPG already manages). Fits well for document/care-plan/consent record ownership. ABAC support is newer and less mature than ReBAC.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host (Docker/Helm) + FGA.dev managed cloud. Satisfies dual requirement.
candidates.person_centric_fit: ReBAC naturally encodes person-owns-their-record, patient-can-read-their-own-clinical-document, org-member-can-access-shared-resource. Consent as a relationship tuple is a clean model.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter-target -- TypeSpec resource types should emit OpenFGA type definitions; SDK emit should generate NestJS FGA guard + tuple write helpers per resource type
candidates.risks: ABAC is secondary (Cerbos is stronger there). Two-service authz stack (Cerbos for ABAC, OpenFGA for ReBAC) adds complexity -- pick one primary. @rxap/nest-openfga is community-maintained. Zanzibar consistency model requires careful tuple management.

candidates.name: SpiceDB
candidates.what: Most Zanzibar-faithful open-source permissions database. Zed schema language, caveated relationships (ABAC+ReBAC hybrid), Watch API for cache invalidation, strong consistency. Fills: fine-grained permissions with ABAC caveats if Zanzibar fidelity is required. Taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles (Zanzibar tier).
candidates.maintenance_health: Authzed-backed, Apache 2.0, authzed-node npm (official), active releases, used by OpenAI ChatGPT Enterprise at tens-of-billions scale. Most mature Zanzibar impl. zed CLI for schema management.
candidates.stack_fit: authzed-node npm (gRPC client). No native NestJS module -- requires manual guard wiring. Zed schema language has TypeScript type generator tools (community). More operational complexity than Cerbos (schema migrations, consistency tokens).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host (Docker/Helm) + Authzed cloud managed. Satisfies dual requirement.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Caveated relationships can encode consent state as an attribute directly in the authorization tuple -- patient consent revocation immediately invalidates access without policy rebuild.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter-target -- TypeSpec types could emit Zed schema fragments; SDK emitter generates NestJS SpiceDB guard + WriteRelationships helpers
candidates.risks: ABAC via caveats is powerful but complex to operate correctly. No native NestJS module. Higher schema migration overhead than Cerbos YAML. Authzed commercial cloud is the comfortable path -- air-gap on self-hosted is viable but requires more ops. Overkill if Cerbos ABAC satisfies requirements.

candidates.name: better-auth
candidates.what: TypeScript-first in-process auth framework. Plugin-based: passkey, OIDC provider (now OAuth 2.1 provider in v1.5+), multi-tenant organizations, Drizzle adapter, NestJS integration documented. Could replace or complement identity-service session/token layer. Fills: lightweight embedded auth for personal-* services or dev environment. Taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles (lightweight embedded tier).
candidates.maintenance_health: MIT, rapid growth (12k+ stars signals by 2026), v1.6+ active, framework-agnostic, Drizzle adapter native, NestJS integration page exists. OAuth 2.1 provider plugin (replaces deprecated OIDC plugin) is production-ready as of v1.5. Community-maintained NestJS module.
candidates.stack_fit: Native Drizzle adapter (import from 'better-auth/adapters/drizzle'). NestJS integration: disable NestJS body parser, mount better-auth handler. Passkey plugin + OAuth 2.1 provider plugin cover authn + IdP needs in one npm package. Bun-compatible (framework-agnostic design).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party self-host always (in-process). No cloud variant -- all data stays in your Postgres via Drizzle. Perfect for air-gap.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Passkey-first (pre-auth registration in 1.6), magic-link, social login all available. OAuth 2.1 provider means CuraOS can issue its own tokens to patient-app and business apps without a separate IdP service for simpler deployments.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/identity-sdk) -- codegen scaffold for identity-service should emit better-auth initialization + Drizzle schema migration + NestJS auth module wiring as optional embedded-auth mode
candidates.risks: NestJS integration is community-maintained (not official). OAuth 2.1 provider plugin maturity: replaced OIDC plugin in v1.5 -- still relatively new. Not a full IdP (no SAML, no LDAP federation, no admin UI for enterprise). Best for dev/personal-tier; pocket-id or Keycloak for production enterprise IdP.

candidates.name: Casbin (node-casbin + nest-authz + casbin-drizzle-adapter)
candidates.what: In-process policy enforcement library (ACL/RBAC/ABAC via policy model + adapter pattern). nest-authz provides NestJS guard/decorator. casbin-drizzle-adapter loads/saves policies from Drizzle-managed tables. Fills: lightweight in-process authz without a sidecar if Cerbos is overkill. Taxonomy: healthcare.platform.rbac-clinical-roles (in-process authz).
candidates.maintenance_health: Apache project, Apache 2.0, mature (10+ years), millions of downloads. nest-authz npm active. casbin-drizzle-adapter v1.1.1 last published ~1yr ago -- low cadence. Casbin itself healthy but Drizzle adapter is community with thin maintenance.
candidates.stack_fit: npm-import into any NestJS service. casbin-drizzle-adapter lets policy table live in same Postgres schema. No sidecar. However, no centralized policy management across services -- each service loads its own policy (vs Cerbos single PDP).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party self-host always (in-process). Air-gap friendly.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Neutral -- policies can encode person-owns-their-data rules but no built-in consent-awareness or clinical-role semantics; those must be hand-coded in policy model.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter-target -- could emit Casbin policy model + adapter init per service, but centralized Cerbos PDP is a better generator target
candidates.risks: Policy sprawl: each service maintains its own policy table (no central PDP). casbin-drizzle-adapter maintenance is thin. Filtered policy loading needed for multi-tenant (supported but requires explicit filter calls). No audit log of policy decisions. Worse than Cerbos for multi-tenant ABAC at scale.

verified.name: pocket-id
verified.license_confirmed: BSD-2-Clause confirmed. No license change found. Permissive, air-gap safe, no copyleft constraint on CuraOS distribution.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. Latest: v2.9.0 (2026-06-16). Regular release cadence through 2025-2026. Small team (community org, no major backer). CVE-2026-28513 (CVSS 8.5) was patched in v2.4.0 -- but a revocation-bypass fix was merged then reverted (2026-05-18); status in v2.9.0 unclear. Maintainer responsiveness exists but team depth is thin for a security-critical auth component.
verified.integration_reality: NestJS integration is straightforward: standard OIDC JWKS endpoint consumed by jwks-rsa + passport-jwt -- no pocket-id-specific SDK needed, no lock-in. Custom claims via group/user config work for group/role propagation. PostgreSQL backend is production-supported (DSN env var). No generator-first impedance: TypeSpec can emit JWKS-aware JWT guard decorators and SMART scope decorators independently of the IdP. Integration claim is accurate for the NestJS+JWKS path.
verified.simpler_alternative: No simpler option covers the full passkey-OIDC-IdP surface from an already-installed dependency. However, Keycloak (already named as the BYO-3rd-party fallback) covers a superset including SMART-on-FHIR scopes natively. Pocket-id is the simpler self-host path, NOT a complete replacement for the dual-IdP architecture required by [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]].
verified.notes: Three concrete findings the candidate understated: (1) SMART-on-FHIR scope claim is optimistic. No evidence pocket-id supports SMART App Launch Framework scope semantics (scp, fhirUser, patient context, launch parameter) natively. Custom claims can approximate it but require out-of-band engineering. Do not treat pocket-id as a SMART-on-FHIR issuer without explicit extension work. (2) local-vs-3rdparty rule gap: pocket-id alone fails [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]] -- no BYO-3rd-party path exists. Pocket-id is valid as the self-host leg only; Keycloak or Zitadel must be the BYO leg. The dual-IdP architecture must be designed explicitly before either is wired into identity-service. (3) Healthcare accessibility gap: passkey-only with no fallback auth may conflict with HIPAA contingency access controls and accessibility requirements (WCAG, ADA, Section 508) for clinical users who cannot use biometric/hardware passkeys. A one-time-code or email fallback path is needed before clinical deployment. Verdict: confirmed-trial, not confirmed-adopt. Use pocket-id for the homelab/operator-auth self-host leg now (already deployed, low risk for that scope). Block promotion to clinical patient/clinician auth path until: (a) the revocation-bypass status in v2.9.0 is verified resolved, (b) SMART scope extension work is scoped, (c) fallback auth strategy for clinical users is designed, (d) Keycloak or Zitadel BYO path is architected alongside it to satisfy the duality rule.

verified.name: Keycloak
verified.license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 confirmed. No SSPL, no BUSL, no per-MAU fee. CNCF Sandbox project. Air-gap compatible via Bitnami Helm chart + Zarf bundle. Permissive for self-hosted + BYO-3rdparty distribution. License claim is accurate.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Maintained but operationally turbulent. Latest: 26.6.x (June 2026), Red Hat backed, active release cadence (4 minor/year). HOWEVER: 26.5.0 introduced a startup memory regression causing OOMKilled pods in K8s (issue #45459, #45662); 26.5.x patch train ran to 26.5.7. Active CVE surface: 10+ CVEs in 2025-2026 including token issuance for disabled users (CVE-2025-14559), privilege escalation (CVE-2026-3121), UMA policy injection (CVE-2026-4636), and redirect URI bypass (CVE-2026-3872). Red Hat patches promptly but the CVE rate on an auth system is a cost center. JVM baseline: 1,250 MB RAM minimum per official sizing docs (requests: 3 CPU / 1,250 Mi). The 1.25 GB claim in the candidate is confirmed accurate and low-balled for production HA.
verified.integration_reality: Worse than claimed. The primary NestJS adapter (nest-keycloak-connect) last published v1.10.1 in August 2024 - ~22 months ago. NestJS 11 support was filed as issue #197 in January 2025 and remains unresolved; install requires --force. Bun compatibility is undocumented. Alternatives exist (bannaarr01/nestjs-keycloak-auth, custom passport-jwt + jwks-rsa) but add glue work. Real integration path = bypass nest-keycloak-connect and wire JWKS validation manually via jose + passport-jwt, which is non-trivial for per-tenant realm routing. Multi-realm tenant routing requires a custom realm resolver (not zero-glue). Keycloak Organizations (GA in 26.0) scales better than realm-per-tenant, but requires a different multi-tenant wiring model than what the candidate describes. TypeSpec integration is entirely handrolled - no code generator emits Keycloak resource/scope declarations from TypeSpec today. The generator-first target named in the candidate (@curaos/identity-sdk emitting Keycloak resource+scope from TypeSpec) does not exist and must be built. SMART-on-FHIR requires community extension JARs (zedwerks/keycloak-smart-fhir) - not built-in, requires Java SPI development.
verified.simpler_alternative: ADR-0120 already made this call: CuraOS identity-service v1 = pure NestJS, NO Keycloak. The identity-service Requirements.md (43.5 KB, last updated 2026-06-05) specifies Better Auth + SimpleWebAuthn + node-saml + samlify + scim-patch + fhirclient-js + jose + OPA/Cerbos/OpenFGA. Passkeys (FR-003, SimpleWebAuthn), SAML (node-saml/samlify), SMART-on-FHIR (ported zedwerks logic), SCIM, multi-tenant per-schema, and three-layer AuthZ are ALL already designed and specified. The candidate is proposing a Java JVM sidecar to replace what the project already committed to building in TypeScript. ADR-0120 explicitly defers Keycloak to v2/v3 only if specific enterprise customers demand it.
verified.notes: Rejection is on architectural fit grounds, not on Keycloak quality. Keycloak is a legitimate enterprise IdP. The rejection reasons: (1) ADR-0120 is a binding RESOLVED decision - 'No Keycloak in v1', violating it requires an ADR amendment, not a lane adoption vote. (2) The identity-service is already deeply specified with a pure-NestJS/Bun-native stack; Keycloak would be a parallel track, not a complement. (3) JVM overhead (1.25 GB+ RAM minimum) conflicts with homelab/air-gap constraint where the whole stack must coexist. (4) The NestJS adapter is effectively unmaintained (22 months stale, NestJS 11 broken without --force). (5) SMART-on-FHIR requires community Java SPI extensions, not native. (6) Multi-realm complexity at the CuraOS CNPG/schema-per-tenant scale is an unresolved operational problem. IF a v2 enterprise customer explicitly demands Keycloak as their IdP (BYO-3rdparty path), the identity-service provider abstraction (ADR-0154) is designed to accept it as a federation upstream - that is the correct integration point, not a lane adoption.

verified.name: Zitadel
verified.license_confirmed: CONFIRMED AND CORRECTLY STATED. AGPL-3.0 effective v3 (March 31, 2025). Core + Console + hosted-login are AGPL; proto definitions, APIs, and SDKs remain Apache 2.0. Running unmodified = zero disclosure obligation, confirmed by Zitadel's own FAQ. Modifying Zitadel source and offering as SaaS = must open modifications. CuraOS tenant customers running unmodified Zitadel are not affected. The AGPL risk is real only if CuraOS ever forks Zitadel internals and ships that fork as SaaS -- which it should not do given the generator-first rule. Commercial license available as escape hatch. Assessment: low practical risk for intended use pattern (self-host-service, unmodified).
verified.maintenance_confirmed: CONFIRMED ACTIVE. v4 GA shipped August 2025. v4.15.x released May 4, 2026. Terraform provider 3.2.2 released June 21, 2026 (within the last 8 days). Release cadence is roughly biweekly minor + weekly patch. Three 2026 CVEs found: CVE-2026-55671 (SSRF/denylist bypass, CVSS 4.3 low, patched), CVE-2026-44671 (LDAP injection, patched), CVE-2026-33132 (incorrect authz, patched). All patched promptly. No unpatched critical CVEs found. Security response posture is acceptable. One operational friction note: v4.15.2 introduced an aggressive RFC1918 denylist that blocked legitimate in-cluster Actions v2 targets (GitHub issue #12326) -- relevant for air-gap where Actions call internal services.
verified.simpler_alternative: PARTIAL OVERLAP WITH EXISTING DEPLOYMENT. Pocket-ID is already in production at *.example.com as the live OIDC broker (confirmed in live-ops-substrate-rule, session-47 handover, and local-project-inventory: 'auth: OIDC + PKCE (Pocket-ID broker)'). Pocket-ID covers: passkey-first OIDC, single-binary, SQLite, zero-JVM, air-gap trivial. What Pocket-ID CANNOT do: multi-org hierarchy (each tenant org = first-class entity with own IdP/branding/policy), SCIM provisioning, per-org user pools, Actions v2 lifecycle hooks, RBAC clinical roles modeling, enterprise IdP federation (OIDC upstream). Zitadel fills the gap Pocket-ID intentionally does not -- multi-tenant org management plus enterprise IAM depth. These are additive, not duplicates. Ponytail verdict: Pocket-ID stays for simple consumer-facing OIDC; Zitadel is justified only for the multi-tenant B2B enterprise tier. Do not rip out Pocket-ID.
verified.integration_reality: REALISTIC BUT NOT ZERO-GLUE. Positive: official Zitadel NestJS docs + example repo (July 2025, updated). Standard path is openid-client + @nestjs/passport + JWKS endpoint -- all already-installed dependencies in the CuraOS NestJS stack, no new npm deps needed for token validation. gRPC management API has @zitadel/node SDK (Apache 2.0) for resource provisioning. Actions v2 = external HTTP webhook, meaning NestJS can serve the action endpoint natively -- clean fit. Negative signals: (1) Actions v2 with RFC1918 targets broken in v4.15.2 (issue #12326) -- relevant for in-cluster action calls in air-gap. (2) No native TypeSpec emitter exists; the @curaos/identity-sdk generator target must handwrite Zitadel resource API calls or wrap @zitadel/node -- not code-gen hostile, but not zero-effort. (3) Air-gap requires manual image mirroring (no Zarf bundle); Helm chart supports private registry override but needs explicit image list. (4) SAML inbound federation has a per-instance EntityID limitation -- federation metadata aggregators (common in healthcare enterprise) fail; this is a real gap for hospital AD/SAML scenarios. (5) Actions v2 V1 sunset in v5 means any V1 usage must migrate.
verified.notes: ADOPT FOR ENTERPRISE TENANT TIER ONLY, NOT AS POCKET-ID REPLACEMENT. Trial scope: (1) Run Zitadel alongside Pocket-ID -- Pocket-ID handles consumer/developer OIDC, Zitadel handles enterprise org/multi-tenant tier. (2) Wire @zitadel/node (Apache 2.0) into @curaos/identity-sdk as a provider implementation under the local-vs-3rdparty-rule's provider abstraction pattern -- CuraOSEnterpriseIdPProvider wrapping Zitadel, interchangeable with the Pocket-ID path. (3) Defer SAML inbound federation until Zitadel resolves per-instance EntityID limitation (GitHub discussion #8942 is open). Flag as known gap for hospital SAML AD scenarios. (4) Air-gap: build image list from Helm chart, mirror to local registry, test Zarf bundle manually -- no shortcut exists. (5) Fix the Actions v2 + RFC1918 denylist issue (upgrade to patch past v4.15.2 or configure allowlist per issue #12326) before any in-cluster webhook. (6) Legal: confirm with org counsel that unmodified AGPL self-host is clear for the SaaS tenant model before GA. Verdict rationale: Zitadel is the strongest OSS candidate for multi-tenant org-first IAM in 2026; the multi-org model maps cleanly to CuraOS tenancy; Go binary + CNPG Postgres is the right operational shape; the NestJS integration is standard OIDC with no exotic glue. The trial gate is the air-gap validation + SAML gap documentation + legal AGPL sign-off.

verified.name: @simplewebauthn/server
verified.license_confirmed: MIT, confirmed. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap + BYO-3rd-party duality. No copyleft, no SaaS restriction, no attribution burden beyond standard MIT text.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. Latest stable: 13.3.1 (pinned in identity-service package.json). Patch 13.3.2 released ~June 29 2026 (Dependabot PRs observed in the wild). Single maintainer (MasterKale) - confirmed bus-factor risk; external contributions closed. GHSA-6hxq-p678-4hr2 (CVSS v4 Low/2.0) fixed in 13.3.2: verifyRegistrationResponse() allowed a malicious x5c to chain to a self-signed root instead of the RP trust anchor. HOWEVER: identity-service uses attestationType:'none' (line 154 of webauthn-registration.service.ts) - the vulnerable code path is never entered. Still: bump to 13.3.2 immediately as hygiene (a single dep bump, trivial).
verified.integration_reality: Already integrated - not a candidate, an incumbent. identity-service/package.json has "@simplewebauthn/server": "13.3.1" in dependencies. Full ceremony pair (generateRegistrationOptions, verifyRegistrationResponse, generateAuthenticationOptions, verifyAuthenticationResponse) is wired into NestJS services (webauthn-registration.service.ts, webauthn-authentication.service.ts). Drizzle passkey credential table schema is in identity-schema.ts. Challenge management uses Valkey (valkey-glide 2.4.0) via consumeLatestChallenge/saveChallenge - not in-memory, correct for horizontally-scaled k8s. TypeSpec contract emits OpenAPI via bunx tsp compile specs/auth.tsp (confirmed in package.json scripts). The library wraps behind WebAuthnRegistrationServer / WebAuthnAuthenticationServer interfaces (injectable), so it is already generator-first-injectable and swappable. Zero glue needed. Bun-compatible: library uses WebCrypto + Uint8Array, no Node-only Buffer or crypto module dependency since v10 refactor.
verified.simpler_alternative: None. WebAuthn ceremony (CBOR decode, COSE key parse, attestation verify, assertion verify, challenge consume) cannot be reduced to a stdlib one-liner. The only alternative is pocket-id (already in use as standalone IdP) which delegates the ceremony to an external process rather than in-process - that adds a network hop and a service dependency for the passkey path, worse not better. No already-installed dep covers the ceremony; jose (installed) covers JWT only.
verified.notes: ACTION REQUIRED - bump 13.3.1 -> 13.3.2 in identity-service/package.json. One-line dep bump, no API changes. attestationType:'none' makes the CVE unexploitable in this codebase right now, but running a patched version is non-negotiable. Bus-factor (single maintainer, closed contributions) is the real long-term risk; mitigated by: (a) library wraps behind injectable interface already, so replacement is a single provider swap; (b) JSR mirror published; (c) WebAuthn spec is stable (L3 CR March 2025), so the library is feature-complete, not evolving rapidly. The candidate claim that "NestJS + PostgreSQL demo repo exists / zero wrapper needed" understates the truth: CuraOS already ships the full integration with the injectable abstraction layer - the generator-first target (emit passkey stubs in identity-service scaffold) is the real open work, not adoption.
