candidates.name: OpenTelemetry JS/TS SDK + @opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core
candidates.what: Distributed tracing, metrics, and log correlation for every NestJS service. Fills taxonomy gap workflow-automation.ops.observability (partial) and the scaffold-only backend/packages/observability package. The official OTel NestJS plugin auto-instruments controllers, guards, interceptors, BullMQ workers, and HTTP layers.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~160k weekly downloads for @opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core; v0.65.0 published June 2026 (bi-weekly OTel cadence); CNCF-graduated project with hundreds of contributors.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct npm-import into NestJS services. Bun requires a --preload otel-init.ts file (no --require flag) - documented 2026 pattern from oneuptime.com. Needs a thin wrapper module for NestJS DI (see nestjs-otel below). Generator-first: live-emit Handlebars main.ts template should import otel-init first.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: route OTLP spans to self-hosted Grafana LGTM stack (Tempo + Mimir + Loki). BYO-3rd-party: swap OTLP endpoint to Datadog, Honeycomb, Grafana Cloud, etc. Zero code change.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Backend infra - enables SLO alerting that protects patient and user journey reliability (scheduling, billing, consent latency budgets from ops/slo).
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (live-emit Handlebars main.ts + otel-init.ts template) + SDK pkg (@curaos/observability package fills scaffold-only gap)
candidates.risks: Bun preload must run before any instrumented import; codegen templates must enforce order. OTel semantic conventions for HTTP/Kafka still evolving (semver unstable namespaces).

candidates.name: nestjs-otel (pragmaticivan/nestjs-otel)
candidates.what: NestJS DI-aware wrapper around the OTel SDK: OpenTelemetryModule, metric histogram decorators, async context propagation. Fills the same workflow-automation.ops.observability gap - provides the NestJS-idiomatic layer on top of raw OTel so services get metrics/traces without boilerplate per-service.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~15k weekly downloads; actively maintained; Amplication uses it in production NestJS microservices.
candidates.stack_fit: Direct import into NestJS app module. Wraps @opentelemetry/sdk-node; works alongside instrumentation-nestjs-core. No Bun-specific issues beyond the preload pattern. Generator-first: the module import goes into the codegen app-module template.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Both - OTLP exporter target is configurable at runtime via env vars.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Backend infra; reduces per-service instrumentation boilerplate so the team focuses on domain features not observability plumbing.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (app-module.ts.hbs template injection)
candidates.risks: Secondary dependency on pragmaticivan (single maintainer); if abandoned, drop to raw @opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core directly. Low blast radius.

candidates.name: GlitchTip
candidates.what: Self-hosted error tracking with 100% Sentry SDK wire protocol compatibility. Fills error tracking gap (curaos_error_tracking_rule names GlitchTip prod + Sentry SaaS dev). MIT licensed, 4 containers, 256-512MB RAM. GlitchTip 6 (2026) added improved stacktraces.
candidates.language: Python (Django backend); @sentry/nestjs SDK on the client
candidates.maintenance_health: Active GitLab project; v6 released 2026; K8s Helm chart available. Handles up to ~20M events/month on single Postgres node before write saturation.
candidates.stack_fit: Client side: @sentry/nestjs SDK (unchanged) pointed at GlitchTip DSN. Zero NestJS code change. OTel-mode Sentry v8 SDK also works: same OTel pipeline feeding GlitchTip as error sink and LGTM as trace/metrics backend. Helm chart for K8s deploy.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host primary (GlitchTip self-hosted on homelab/k3d). BYO-3rd-party: swap DSN to Sentry SaaS or Bugsink - no client code change.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Catches patient-facing errors (scheduling failures, auth loops, billing crashes) before users report them; error rate feeds SLO burn-rate alerting.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/observability fills scaffold-only; includes Sentry/GlitchTip init) + codegen-emitter (otel-init.ts Handlebars includes error reporting init)
candidates.risks: Postgres write saturation above 20M events/month (upgrade path: self-hosted Sentry). Django runtime separate from NestJS ecosystem. No Session Replay or profiling vs full Sentry.

candidates.name: Langfuse
candidates.what: LLM observability, evals, prompt management, and cost tracking for AI/automation-core and CDS steps. Fills workflow-automation.catalog.ai-llm-steps (absent) observability sub-gap and the LLM eval dimension of curaos_agent_eval_obs_rule. MIT core; EE features (SCIM, audit log, data retention) need license key.
candidates.license: MIT (core) + commercial EE for advanced features
candidates.maintenance_health: YC W23; ~30k GitHub stars; JS SDK v5 released March 2026; Helm chart (langfuse/langfuse-k8s). OTel-native SDK v4 is thin wrapper on OTel API - same pipeline as service tracing.
candidates.stack_fit: JS SDK: npm install langfuse. OTel-mode: point OTel OTLP exporter to Langfuse ingest endpoint - zero extra instrumentation code if OTel is already set up. NestJS: inject LangfuseService as provider. Self-host requires platform >= 3.95.0. Helm chart for K8s.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Helm on homelab (2 containers + Postgres + Redis). BYO-3rd-party: Langfuse Cloud (GDPR-compliant EU region available).
candidates.person_centric_fit: LLM steps in CDS, automation-core, and AI workflow nodes affect patient safety and user journeys; Langfuse's eval metrics (faithfulness, hallucination) catch regressions before they reach users.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg + codegen-emitter (ai-llm step template in automation-core and workflow-core services)
candidates.risks: EE features (audit log) need commercial license for regulated healthcare deployments; OSS core covers tracing + evals. Langfuse platform must be kept >= 3.95.0 for SDK compatibility.

candidates.name: Pyrra
candidates.what: SLO management for Prometheus: reads OpenSLO/CRD definitions, generates multi-window multi-burn-rate Prometheus recording rules and alerts, provides a UI. Fills ops/slo gap - CuraOS already has OpenSLO YAML templates; Pyrra is the K8s operator that activates them as live alerts.
candidates.maintenance_health: ~1.5k stars; v0.8.2 (Jan 2026); dependency updates Jan 2026. Small team maintainers ('free time'). Sloth is an alternative with similar features but less K8s-native UI.
candidates.stack_fit: K8s operator mode reads ServiceLevelObjective CRDs; filesystem mode reads YAML (matches ops/slo OpenSLO files). Helm chart available. No NestJS/Bun integration needed - pure ops plane. Generator: service-catalog.json already generates OpenSLO; Pyrra consumes them.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only (K8s operator or Docker). No managed cloud SaaS option.
candidates.person_centric_fit: SLO burn-rate alerts fire before SLO breach, protecting patient scheduling/billing journey P95 latency budgets defined in ops/slo.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (ops/observability generate.ts already emits OpenSLO; wire Pyrra CRD schema as the target format)
candidates.risks: Low maintenance velocity - if Pyrra stalls, Sloth or Google SLO Generator are drop-in alternatives with same Prometheus output. No AsyncAPI/event SLO support (event consumer-lag SLOs need custom PrometheusRules).

candidates.name: Grafana LGTM (Loki + Grafana + Tempo + Mimir) via lgtm-distributed Helm chart
candidates.what: Full self-hosted observability backend: Mimir (metrics), Loki (logs), Tempo (traces), Grafana (dashboards). Single Helm umbrella chart lgtm-distributed v3.0.1. Fills the backend sink for OTel spans/metrics/logs that CuraOS services emit. CuraOS ops/observability already generates Grafana dashboards - this is the deployment target.
candidates.license: Apache-2.0 (Grafana OSS) + AGPL for Grafana Enterprise features
candidates.maintenance_health: CNCF ecosystem; Grafana Labs backed; weekly releases. lgtm-distributed chart v3.0.1 on ArtifactHub.
candidates.stack_fit: NestJS services send OTLP to Alloy (Grafana Agent successor) which forwards to Mimir/Loki/Tempo. CuraOS ops/observability generate.ts already emits Grafana dashboard JSON - those dashboards import directly. Helm chart for homelab K8s.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: lgtm-distributed on homelab/k3d (monolithic mode for small cluster). BYO-3rd-party: Grafana Cloud (same OTLP endpoint, same dashboards).
candidates.person_centric_fit: Operational reliability for all person-centric journeys; Tempo distributed traces show which service in the patient scheduling or billing chain is slow.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (ops/observability generate.ts target format alignment with Mimir remote_write + Tempo OTLP endpoint)
candidates.risks: Resource-heavy for homelab monolithic mode: Mimir + Loki + Tempo + Grafana needs ~4GB RAM minimum. Grafana Enterprise features (RBAC, reporting) are AGPL/commercial; OSS tier sufficient for CuraOS dev/prod.

candidates.name: Pact + Pact Broker (pact-js + pact-broker-docker)
candidates.what: Consumer-driven contract testing for HTTP APIs and async messaging between CuraOS microservices. pact-js v13 MIT; self-hosted Pact Broker via pactfoundation/pact-broker Docker image. AsyncAPI plugin (pact-asyncapi-comparator) covers Kafka/Redpanda event contracts from CuraOS AsyncAPI specs. Bi-directional contract mode (BDCT) accepts OpenAPI from TypeSpec output directly.
candidates.maintenance_health: pact-foundation org; pact-js ~50k weekly downloads; pact-nestjs injection wrapper updated Feb 2026; Pactflow (commercial) is same foundation; community active.
candidates.stack_fit: pact-js npm-import in test files; pact-nestjs DI wrapper for provider verification. BDCT mode: TypeSpec -> OpenAPI -> feed to Pact Broker as provider contract, no need to write consumer test boilerplate. AsyncAPI plugin covers Redpanda event contracts. Self-hosted broker: 1 Docker container + Postgres.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: pact-broker-docker + Postgres (homelab). BYO-3rd-party: Pactflow SaaS (same CLI tooling, just different broker URL).
candidates.person_centric_fit: Contract tests catch breaking API changes between patient-app/clinician-app consumers and backend services before they reach users; can-i-deploy gate blocks deploy of incompatible service versions.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (generate provider contract verification test stub per service from TypeSpec + AsyncAPI contracts)
candidates.risks: AsyncAPI/event contract support via pact-asyncapi-comparator is newer and less mature than HTTP CDCT. BDCT mode requires generated OpenAPI to be uploaded to broker - needs CI step. Pactflow advanced features (webhooks, teams) need commercial plan.

candidates.name: Microsoft Presidio (Python service) + pii-vault (TS alternative)
candidates.what: PII/PHI detection and anonymization before LLM calls, log redaction, and audit scrubbing. Presidio = Python REST API (run as sidecar); pii-vault = pure TypeScript Presidio-compatible npm package (MIT, multi-language). Fills the PHI scrubbing requirement in curaos_agent_eval_obs_rule and the ConsentGuard/data-boundary rule before any LLM trace lands in Langfuse.
candidates.language: Python (Presidio server) / TypeScript (pii-vault client or presidio-anonymizer-nestjs wrapper)
candidates.maintenance_health: Presidio: Microsoft-backed, GitHub ~2k stars, active. pii-vault: newer, MIT, TS-native. presidio-anonymizer-nestjs NestJS wrapper exists on GitHub.
candidates.stack_fit: Presidio: run as K8s sidecar (2 containers: analyzer + anonymizer); call via HTTP from NestJS services before emitting LLM traces to Langfuse. pii-vault: npm-import directly into NestJS if pure-TS is preferred; no Python sidecar needed. Wrapper needed: inject as NestJS provider via presidio-anonymizer-nestjs or custom service.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Presidio containers or pii-vault in-process. BYO-3rd-party: Azure AI Language PII detection service (same interface).
candidates.person_centric_fit: Protects patient PHI from leaking into LLM telemetry/traces; critical for HIPAA compliance on HealthStack AI features and CDS steps.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@curaos/observability or @curaos/phi-boundary package - extend existing healthstack-phi-boundary package with redaction pipeline)
candidates.risks: Presidio NLP models (spaCy) are probabilistic - custom recognizers needed for domain-specific PHI (NPI, UMLS codes). pii-vault is newer with smaller community. In-process TS option trades recall for latency.

candidates.name: DeepEval
candidates.what: LLM evaluation framework with 50+ metrics (hallucination, faithfulness, answer relevancy, toxicity, bias). TypeScript SDK released 2026. Fills eval dimension of curaos_agent_eval_obs_rule alongside Langfuse. Used for CI-gated LLM quality checks on CDS rules, automation-core AI steps, and any LLM-generated content.
candidates.maintenance_health: confident-ai/deepeval; ~10k GitHub stars; active 2026 blog + guides; Confident AI cloud optional; self-run locally at $0.
candidates.stack_fit: Python SDK: run as CI step or NestJS subprocess call for eval. TS SDK: npm-import into test files (Vitest/Bun test). Integrates with OTel and LiteLLM. Langfuse + DeepEval combination is the canonical CuraOS agent-eval-obs stack per curaos_agent_eval_obs_rule. Wrapper needed: thin NestJS provider to invoke DeepEval eval endpoint in integration tests.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: run Python eval runner in CI (local). BYO-3rd-party: Confident AI cloud for centralized eval dashboard.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Eval guards prevent hallucinated CDS recommendations or incorrect medication information from reaching clinicians and patients.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (generate LLM-eval test stub alongside service integration tests for ai-llm-step services)
candidates.risks: TS SDK is new in 2026 and feature set lags Python. Python eval runner adds a CI dependency. 50+ metrics can be expensive (LLM-as-judge calls); subset selection needed.

candidates.name: Grafana k6 (v2.0)
candidates.what: Load/performance testing with SLO threshold codification. Already adopted in ops/perf with constant-arrival-rate baselines and SLO-mapped thresholds. k6 v2.0 (2026) adds AI-assisted test generation, Playwright browser module parity, and JSON summary output for CI consumption.
candidates.maintenance_health: Grafana Labs backed; k6 v2.0 released 2026; AGPL core with commercial Grafana Cloud k6; 24k GitHub stars; dominant load testing tool.
candidates.stack_fit: Already real-working in ops/perf. Tests run as standalone binary (not imported into services); AGPL service boundary does not affect CuraOS application code. Results stream to Prometheus/Mimir via k6 Prometheus remote_write output.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: k6 binary in CI + ops/perf scripts. BYO-3rd-party: Grafana Cloud k6 for distributed load generation.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Validates that patient scheduling, billing, and appointment booking endpoints stay within SLO latency budgets under load.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter (ops/observability generate.ts already emits k6 baselines per service-catalog.json; extend to emit Playwright browser scenario stubs)
candidates.risks: AGPL applies to k6 binary/server, not test scripts. Grafana Cloud k6 for distributed testing is commercial. k6 browser module still maturing for full Playwright parity.

verified.name: OpenTelemetry JS/TS SDK + @opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core
verified.license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 confirmed. CNCF graduated May 11, 2026. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution and local-vs-3rdparty OTLP endpoint duality. No restrictions on redistribution or commercial use.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active: v0.65.0 of instrumentation-nestjs-core published ~June 2026, bi-weekly cadence. Core SDK 2.0 shipped March 2025. CNCF-backed with hundreds of contributors. nestjs-otel wrapper (v8.0.3 May 2026) is lightly maintained (1 primary author, ~2k weekly downloads) - not abandoned but carries dependency-chain risk. One open CVE: CVE-2026-54285 in @opentelemetry/core (unbounded memory in W3C Baggage propagation, DoS vector, mitigated by internal OTLP endpoint + ingress header size limits). One open probable regression: issue #3458 MaxListenersExceededWarning/EventEmitter memory leak in NestJS+OTel combination (March 2026, unresolved).
verified.integration_reality: Partially confirmed, optimistic on Bun. (1) Bun issue #26536 (open, unresolved June 2026): OTel http/express/fastify instrumentation on Bun produces only generic span names - HTTP transport-level monkey-patches do not fire correctly because Bun's Node.js compat layer intercepts differently. The controller-level NestJS spans may survive (NestJS patches its own lifecycle), but HTTP attribute enrichment is degraded. (2) NestJS microservice/Kafka consumer handlers are NOT auto-instrumented by instrumentation-nestjs-core (issue #2510, PR opened March 2026, merged status unclear) - manual context propagation required for Kafka/Redpanda consumers. (3) BullMQ instrumentation requires a separate @opentelemetry/instrumentation-bullmq package, not covered by instrumentation-nestjs-core as the candidate implies. (4) Bun lacks --require so the init file must load via --preload or programmatic import-order discipline; this IS documented and workable but requires codegen template enforcement. (5) Semantic conventions: @opentelemetry/semantic-conventions/incubating is NOT semver-stable and may break on minor bumps; codegen templates must use only the stable surface with OTEL_SEMCONV_STABILITY_OPT_IN=http.
verified.simpler_alternative: No. The observability package is scaffold-only (just a README). Zero OTel deps exist in any curaos service package. Hand-rolling distributed tracing, metrics, and log correlation across 40+ NestJS services from scratch is far worse than OTel. No stdlib or already-installed dep covers this. Ponytail ladder stops at rung 5: OTel is the minimum viable solution for the stated problem.
verified.notes: Downgraded from adopt to confirmed-trial because the Bun HTTP instrumentation gap (issue #26536) is unresolved and directly affects CuraOS's Bun-primary NestJS runtime. Before baking into the generator as default-on: (1) Run a smoke test: deploy one NestJS service on Bun with instrumentation-nestjs-core and verify controller-level spans carry route attributes (not just generic GET/POST). (2) Confirm whether the microservice PR (b92e038) merged and covers Kafka transport. (3) Pin @opentelemetry/core to a version above the CVE-2026-54285 fix once available; track via Renovate. Generator-first targets are correct: codegen should emit otel-init.ts (loaded first via --preload in bun run scripts) + the @curaos/observability SDK package wrapping NodeSDK init. If the Bun HTTP transport gap persists, the generator must emit explicit manual span wrappers for controller routes as a fallback. The nestjs-otel DI wrapper is optional glue; the candidate should evaluate whether it is worth the extra dependency vs. a thin handwritten NestJS module (ponytail: one small module with no transitive risk beats a lightly-maintained third-party wrapper).

verified.name: nestjs-otel (pragmaticivan/nestjs-otel)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. Compatible with self-hosted, air-gap, and local-vs-3rdparty distribution. No concern.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. Latest v8.0.3 released 2026-05-15, v8.0.2 on 2026-01-21. v8 dropped NestJS < 11 support so peerDeps are @nestjs/common >= 11 < 12, which matches the CuraOS stack exactly. Weekly downloads are ~176k (search results diverge from the candidate's claimed 15k - actual number is higher, not lower). Immich (major OSS project) adopted it via Renovate PR to v8. No open CVEs found against nestjs-otel itself (CVE-2025-54782 is in @nestjs/devtools-integration, unrelated). Single maintainer (pragmaticivan) is a real bus-factor risk but not a blocker at trial grade; fallback path to raw @opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core is documented and low-blast-radius.
verified.simpler_alternative: Partially yes. Raw @opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core (CNCF-backed, actively published - 0.65.0 released ~11 days ago) covers automatic trace collection from controller handlers with zero wrapper. For traces-only across services, the ponytail ladder says prefer the official package. nestjs-otel earns its keep ONLY for the additive features the official package cannot do: (1) per-request WideEvent attribute bag (structured enrichment from any DI provider in the call chain without request-scoped injection), (2) @Span / @Histogram / @Counter method decorators for domain-level metrics. If CuraOS services need only HTTP-span traces, drop to instrumentation-nestjs-core. If they need metric decorators and structured span enrichment in service methods (the observability-quality lane implies they do), nestjs-otel adds real value with ~5 lines of app module wiring vs. 50+ lines of manual SDK + interceptor boilerplate per service.
verified.integration_reality: Integration surface confirmed realistic, not optimistic. The codegen already uses ts-morph with anchor comments (@curaos-codegen:auto-wire-import-statements, @curaos-codegen:auto-wire-imports) in app.module.ts for idempotent module injection. OpenTelemetryModule.forRoot() wiring via wireAppModule() is a straightforward call: one import statement + one imports[] entry. Generator-first is directly supported - no bespoke hand-edits needed. Bun caveat confirmed: Bun does not support --require / Node auto-instrumentation preload flags; a manual instrumentation init file preloaded via bunfig.toml [preload] is required. This is a one-time codegen emitter concern (emit a tracing.ts preload file alongside the service), NOT a per-service boilerplate problem. No Drizzle/Zod/TypeSpec conflicts found - nestjs-otel sits at the NestJS module layer and is orthogonal to ORM, validation, and contract layers. Fastify adapter note: @opentelemetry/instrumentation-fastify was removed from auto-instrumentations-node in March 2026; if CuraOS uses Fastify adapter, @fastify/otel must be added explicitly.
verified.notes: Trial grade, not full adopt, for two reasons: (1) Bun preload integration has not been externally validated against CuraOS's exact bun compile + k3d deploy pipeline - needs one service spike before fleet-wide codegen emission. (2) Single-maintainer bus factor is real; pragmaticivan has shipped consistently through v8 but the fallback path (strip to @opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core + hand-roll WideEvent interceptor) should be documented in the ADR before committing generator output to all 40+ services. Recommended action: wire one service (e.g. audit-core-service) manually via codegen, prove traces+metrics land in the local OTel collector, then promote the emitter template to fleet. Do NOT adopt MetinSeylan/Nestjs-OpenTelemetry as alternative - last visible activity was 2024 and it lags on NestJS 11 support. @amplication/opentelemetry-nestjs is a maintained fork but narrower; nestjs-otel has higher download volume and cleaner peer dep targeting. Fastify adapter gap: if Fastify is the HTTP adapter, add @fastify/otel to the emitter as a co-dependency.

verified.name: GlitchTip
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. Multiple independent sources (GitLab README, official site, European Purpose review 2026) agree. No AGPL components. The project explicitly distances itself from Sentry's license drift: GlitchTip is building glitchtip-cli as MIT replacement for sentry-cli after sentry-cli went non-free. Clean for self-hosted distribution and air-gap bundling under [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]].
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. GlitchTip 6 (2026-02-03) and 6.1 (2026-03-23) both landed in 2026. v6.1 adds logging support (with DuckDB option), MCP support (opt-in, off by default), and performance monitoring refactor supporting both transactions and spans. Helm chart is versioned (8.0.1 on 2026-02-04 per Artifact Hub tags). No CVEs found in NVD search - zero GlitchTip-specific entries returned. Bus factor is moderate-small: team-funded (no VC), not solo, but smaller than Sentry. Dropped features: basic uptime monitoring and old-style performance monitoring are no longer maintained per community reports - but this is noise for CuraOS because CuraOS already has LGTM for APM/uptime.
verified.integration_reality: SDK integration is real and shallow. @sentry/nestjs or @sentry/node pointed at a GlitchTip DSN is the entire client-side change: one DSN env-var swap. GlitchTip's own docs confirm this for Node.js. OTel-mode (Sentry v8 SDK in OTel passthrough) also confirmed compatible - same OTel pipeline, GlitchTip as error sink, LGTM for traces/metrics. No NestJS-specific glue code needed. Air-gap path: GlitchTip publishes images to Docker Hub (glitchtip/glitchtip); no pre-built Zarf bundle exists, but the 4-container set (web+worker+redis+postgres) is standard Zarf-bundleable via Helm chart. Helm chart 8.0.1 available; deploys web, worker, migration job, postgres and redis as optional sub-charts. No external network calls at runtime. Generator-first target is clean: otel-init.ts Handlebars template adds one DSN init block; no per-service bespoke code.
verified.simpler_alternative: LGTM stack (already in CuraOS observability) does NOT cover structured error grouping, issue deduplication, release tracking, or user-impact correlation the way GlitchTip does. The ponytail ladder check: (1) YAGNI? No - error_tracking_rule already names GlitchTip explicitly. (2) Already in codebase? No error sink exists. (3) Stdlib? Not applicable. (4) Native platform? OTel exceptions exist but no grouping/dedup UI. (5) Already-installed dep? LGTM handles traces/metrics/logs but has no Sentry-protocol error grouping. GlitchTip fills a genuine gap LGTM does not close. Verdict: not replaceable by what is already present.
verified.notes: Three real risks worth tracking: (1) Postgres write saturation above ~20M events/month - acceptable for CuraOS homelab scale; upgrade path is self-hosted Sentry (same DSN swap, zero client change). (2) No pre-built Zarf bundle - must author one; 4 well-known images make this straightforward, not blocking. (3) Uptime monitoring and old-style perf monitoring are de-emphasized in v6 - irrelevant because LGTM covers those roles in CuraOS. Bugsink is a viable simpler alternative (single container, SQLite, no Redis) if ops overhead becomes a concern, but GlitchTip's richer feature set and the existing rule citation make it the correct primary. The candidate's claims are accurate and not optimistic.

verified.name: Langfuse
verified.license_confirmed: MIT core confirmed accurate. EE paywall confirmed for: SCIM, extended audit logging, data retention policies, server-side data masking, org management API, project-level RBAC. EE features ship in the same container image but are gated by license key - no separate binary. CRITICAL for CuraOS: HIPAA-grade audit logs are EE-only. The OSS core covers tracing + evals freely, but any regulated healthcare deployment (HealthStack) will need a commercial license for the audit-log feature, or must bolt an external audit trail. This is understated in the candidate brief as a minor footnote; it is actually a blocker for HealthStack production use without a contract.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Confirmed active. YC W23, ~30k GitHub stars. JS SDK v5 released March 2026 (observation-centric model, propagateAttributes replaces updateActiveTrace). Platform v3 stable since December 2024 with ClickHouse as primary trace store. Active release cadence confirmed. Known open bugs: (1) OTLP spec violation - server returns application/json when client sends application/x-protobuf, causing deserialization failures in .NET/Java/PHP clients (issue #11550, January 2026, status unclear); (2) GenAI semantic conventions v1.37+ events-based prompts do not surface Input/Output fields (issue #12657, March 2026). Air-gap Helm image-registry override bug confirmed (#250, langfuse-k8s): images hard-pull from docker.io even when registry is overridden - active issue with workaround required. Bitnami registry restructure (August 2025) adds migration friction for air-gap mirror maintainers.
verified.integration_reality: Claim is optimistic on Bun. No confirmed Bun-specific validation found; OTel JS SDK v2 is the baseline and Bun supports OTel OTLP exporters in general, but no explicit Langfuse+Bun CI coverage documented. OTel-mode path (point OTLP exporter at Langfuse ingest) is the least-risky integration: if OTel is already wired (which CuraOS targets), adding Langfuse is an exporter endpoint change - zero SDK code in services. NestJS injection as a provider is a community pattern, not first-party; no official NestJS module exists. Generator-first impact: the codegen emitter (automation-core ai-llm step template) would need to emit the OTel span attributes (gen_ai.* semantic conventions) - one codegen change, not per-service glue. That is generator-first compatible. Self-host v3 resource floor is significantly heavier than the candidate brief implies: 2 containers (web + worker) + ClickHouse (2 CPU/8 GiB minimum, 3-node recommended) + Redis/Valkey + S3/MinIO. Total minimum: ~6 CPU / 17 GiB RAM for a lean deployment. Air-gap via Zarf: no official Zarf package; manual image mirroring required with registry override bug workaround. This is real friction, not a minor note.
verified.simpler_alternative: Ponytail check: OTel already in CuraOS stack. If the only need is LLM span tracing routed into the existing Grafana/Tempo pipeline, zero new service is needed - emit gen_ai.* semantic conventions from LLM call sites and they flow through the existing OTel collector. That covers the observability sub-gap with no new infrastructure. What Langfuse adds beyond that: structured evals (faithfulness, hallucination scoring), prompt management UI, cost tracking per trace, and dataset/experiment management. These are not covered by stdlib OTel + Grafana. So the simpler path handles tracing only; Langfuse is justified only if eval scoring and prompt management are real near-term requirements. Arize Phoenix (Apache 2.0, fully open, no EE paywall, OTel-native) is a cleaner air-gap alternative if audit-log EE requirement is a concern, though it lacks prompt management.
verified.notes: Verdict is trial, not adopt, for three reasons: (1) EE paywall on audit logs is a hard constraint for HealthStack regulated deployments - this must be contractually resolved before production rollout, not deferred; (2) air-gap image-registry override bug in langfuse-k8s is an unresolved open issue that directly conflicts with CuraOS Zarf/air-gap requirement - workaround exists but is unsupported; (3) ClickHouse dependency adds a significant infrastructure footprint (minimum 6 CPU/17 GiB RAM) to the homelab/on-prem profile - evaluate against available capacity before committing. Recommended trial scope: wire OTel-mode (OTLP exporter) from automation-core and one CDS step to a dev-cluster Langfuse self-hosted instance; validate Bun compatibility, OTel OTLP protobuf response bug impact, and air-gap image mirroring under Zarf before escalating to adopt. Do not ship Langfuse to HealthStack production until EE audit-log licensing is resolved.

candidates.name: Novu (@novu/framework + @novu/react)
candidates.what: Multi-channel notification orchestration (email, SMS, push, chat, in-app) with code-first TypeScript workflow SDK for NestJS and a composable React <Inbox> UI component with real-time WebSocket feed. Covers taxonomy: notify-core-service (orchestration layer), in-app inbox UI, provider abstraction (50+ providers), preference center. Gaps filled: unified channel routing, in-app notification feed, subscriber preference management.
candidates.license: MIT (core) + commercial Enterprise Edition (SSO, audit logs)
candidates.maintenance_health: 39k+ GitHub stars; v3.14.0 released June 2026; active weekly releases; backed by Novu Inc (Series A); 94 repos in org; 50+ provider integrations
candidates.stack_fit: @novu/framework/nest ships NovuModule.register() - zero wrapper needed for NestJS. @novu/react drops directly into CuraOS ui-kit. backendUrl+socketUrl overrides point React component at self-hosted API. Bun runtime works (bun compile is separate issue, not relevant for service usage). TypeSpec event contract can emit Novu workflow trigger shapes.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Docker Compose + community Helm chart (not official). BYO-3rd-party: Novu Cloud ($30/mo per 30k runs). Both paths use identical SDK + workflow code - full [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]] compliance.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Preference center per subscriber (person controls which channels/types they receive). In-app Inbox is person-facing real-time feed. Notification history queryable by subscriber. Strong dual-surface fit: person sees their feed; management surface triggers and monitors workflows.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@novu/framework): workflow definitions go in notify-core-service, emitted by codegen mold as .workflow.ts files alongside TypeSpec contracts. @novu/react plugs into ui-kit as InboxWidget.
candidates.risks: Hard MongoDB + Redis dependency (not replaceable as of June 2026 - GitHub issue #7566 open, no roadmap). Minimum 8GB RAM for self-host adds ops cost. MongoDB is a second DB engine alongside CuraOS's Postgres-only stance. Community Helm chart is unofficial. Enterprise features (SAML SSO, audit log export) require commercial license.

candidates.name: react-email (@react-email/render + @react-email/components)
candidates.what: JSX-to-HTML email rendering library: write email templates as React/TSX components, render to HTML string for any SMTP/HTTP provider. Covers email template authoring layer for notify-core-service and healthstack notification surfaces (appointment reminders, care plan updates, clinical alerts). Not a delivery service - pure rendering.
candidates.maintenance_health: 17k+ GitHub stars; v5.0 shipped 2025; 920k weekly downloads; @react-email/render has 8.2M weekly downloads; backed by Resend; 182 contributors; updated May 2026.
candidates.stack_fit: Pure npm import, zero framework coupling. Works with NestJS + nodemailer. Known issue: does not work with `bun compile` binary (issue #2585 open) - irrelevant for service mode (Bun-as-runtime works fine). Turborepo/monorepo compatible. Templates live in @curaos/email-templates package, shared across services.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Provider-agnostic by design: render to HTML, send via Postal (self-host), Resend, SES, or any SMTP. Covers both local and 3rd-party delivery.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Templates can be authored with person-centric language and dynamic data (patient name, appointment detail, care instructions). Components reusable across patient-app emails and business-side alerts with the same design system.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter-target: the ui-app-emit or a new email-emit step generates .email.tsx template stubs from TypeSpec entity contracts, the same way it generates page stubs.
candidates.risks: Owned by Resend (commercial email SaaS) - could deprioritize non-Resend paths. bun compile incompatibility is noted but non-blocking for service-mode usage. Requires a delivery layer on top.

candidates.name: Postal (self-hosted SMTP relay + HTTP API)
candidates.what: Self-hosted mail delivery platform: HTTP API + SMTP relay for transactional and bulk email with bounce processing, DKIM/SPF, click/open tracking, multi-org management UI, and webhook delivery events. Covers the local self-host SMTP tier for notify-core-service email channel - the 'first-party email server' option per [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]].
candidates.language: Ruby (server); any HTTP client for integration
candidates.maintenance_health: v3.3.6 released 2026-04-28; active maintenance; 14k+ GitHub stars; production-proven at many orgs; postalserver org has dedicated docs and PHP library.
candidates.stack_fit: No Node SDK needed - Postal exposes HTTP API; wrap in a thin NestJS provider (PostalProvider implements EmailProvider interface) using `fetch` or axios. Zarf-packageable as a container. Works alongside CuraOS's Postgres CNPG (Postal uses MySQL internally - no conflict, separate service).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Local: Postal self-hosted container. BYO-3rd-party: swap PostalProvider for ResendProvider/SES/Mailgun - same NestJS interface. Full dual compliance.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Delivery infrastructure only - person-facing value is reliable, tracked email delivery. Bounce/complaint suppression protects patient email from accidental spam-folder landing.
candidates.generator_first_target: service: notify-core-service EmailProvider port. Codegen emits PostalProvider class from a provider config schema in TypeSpec.
candidates.risks: Ruby runtime is a second language in the ops stack (but containerized, no build-time coupling). MySQL internal DB (separate from Postgres CNPG - no schema conflict, just another container). Relatively complex to warm up IP reputation for clinical email.

candidates.name: listmonk (self-hosted newsletter + transactional email)
candidates.what: Single-binary Go server (Postgres only) covering subscriber management, list segmentation, campaign scheduling, transactional email API, and drag-and-drop template builder. Covers lifecycle/marketing email lane (appointment campaign sequences, wellness check-ins, bulk patient communications) that Novu is not optimized for. v6.1.0 (Mar 2026) adds multipart plaintext body in transactional API.
candidates.maintenance_health: v6.1.0 released 2026-03-29; actively maintained by knadh (creator of many OSS tools); single-Postgres dependency; used by large orgs in production; 14k+ GitHub stars.
candidates.stack_fit: AGPL service boundary: CuraOS calls listmonk's REST API from notify-core-service, never imports code. Postgres-native (shares CNPG PostgreSQL cluster or separate instance). Zarf-packageable. No npm SDK but HTTP client wrapper is trivial - 50 lines in a NestJS ListmonkProvider.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host only for listmonk itself; but it relays via any SMTP/provider (Postal, SES, Mailgun). Covers local self-host path; BYO-3rd-party is the SMTP relay config.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Patient/customer opt-in/out management per list per person. Double opt-in flows protect patient autonomy. Subscription preference portal can be surfaced in patient-app as communication preferences. Unsubscribe is first-class, not an afterthought.
candidates.generator_first_target: service: notify-core-service ListmonkProvider and list-seeding scripts. TypeSpec contract emits campaign trigger events consumed by listmonk webhook handler.
candidates.risks: AGPL: must stay behind service boundary (HTTP API calls only, never import). Go binary means no native TypeScript SDK - thin HTTP wrapper needed. Not suitable for real-time in-app or push notifications (email/SMS only).

candidates.name: ntfy (self-hosted push notification server)
candidates.what: Pub/sub push notification server via plain HTTP PUT/POST. Covers mobile/desktop push without FCM/APNs vendor lock: ntfy relays to APNs/FCM transparently. Self-hosted Go binary with topic-based ACL. Fills push notification gap for clinical alerts (appointment reminders, lab result ready, care plan updates) on mobile apps. [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]]: self-hosted ntfy OR BYO FCM/APNs direct.
candidates.maintenance_health: Actively maintained by binwiederhier; official iOS + Android apps; strong homelab/self-host community; regular releases through 2025-2026; ntfy-me-mcp shows Node/TypeScript ecosystem engagement.
candidates.stack_fit: Trivial HTTP integration from NestJS: POST to ntfy topic = push sent. No SDK required; if desired, a 20-line NestJS NtfyProvider wraps it. Works as Novu provider adapter so Novu orchestrates the channel while ntfy handles delivery. Zarf-packageable (single Go binary container).
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: ntfy container. BYO-3rd-party: configure Novu's FCM/APNs provider instead - same NestJS interface. iOS relay mode in ntfy itself bridges to Apple APNs without requiring a self-hosted APNs cert.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Person installs ntfy app and subscribes to their patient-specific topic (scoped by tenant+patient ID with ACL). Push reaches them without CuraOS holding their FCM token. Topic-level unsubscribe is person-controlled.
candidates.generator_first_target: service: notify-core-service PushProvider port. Can also be a Novu custom provider definition.
candidates.risks: iOS requires relay to public ntfy.sh APNs bridge unless you run your own APNs cert (extra cert management). Topics are URL-guessable without ACL config - must configure per-tenant ACL. Not a clinical messaging system (push only, no E2E encryption).

candidates.name: Element/Matrix (Synapse + matrix-js-sdk)
candidates.what: Federated, E2E-encrypted, self-hosted secure messaging protocol. Covers secure clinical messaging lane: clinician-to-patient direct messaging, care team group chats, HIPAA-capable audit trail, federated inter-org communication. Used in production for German national healthcare TI-Messenger (millions of users, Matrix Conference 2025). matrix-js-sdk (MIT) provides TypeScript client.
candidates.maintenance_health: Synapse 11k+ GitHub stars; Element.io company backing; active Matrix Foundation; TI-Messenger national deployment 2025 proves production readiness at scale; matrix-js-sdk weekly releases.
candidates.stack_fit: matrix-js-sdk imports into NestJS for bot/bridge operations (programmatic message send from clinical workflows). Element Web embeds in CuraOS React frontend via iframe or as a standalone app. Significant ops footprint (Synapse + Postgres + media server). Not Zarf-trivial but Helm chart exists.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Synapse on CuraOS K8s. BYO-3rd-party: Matrix.org public homeserver or Element Cloud for teams that don't need on-prem. Federation means both options interoperate.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Patient owns their Matrix account (portable across homeservers). E2E encryption means even CuraOS cannot read messages at rest. Person-controlled key verification. Care team visible to patient as participants, not hidden from them.
candidates.generator_first_target: service: a new secure-messaging-service wraps matrix-js-sdk for server-side operations (room creation, bot messages for system notifications). UI: Element Web or a matrix-react-sdk embedding in patient-app.
candidates.risks: Significant ops complexity (Synapse Python, Postgres, coturn for VoIP). HIPAA compliance requires BAA configuration and careful homeserver hardening - not automatic. Heavy resource usage vs. simpler messaging. Clinical messaging is a v1.1+ feature for CuraOS scope.

candidates.name: Dittofeed (open-source lifecycle messaging)
candidates.what: Multi-channel lifecycle/marketing automation platform (email, SMS, push, WhatsApp, Slack) with user journey builder, segmentation, and broadcasts. TypeScript-native with Node.js + web SDKs. Postgres + ClickHouse + Temporal stack. Fills the customer-lifecycle-messaging lane that Novu (product notifications) and listmonk (bulk campaigns) don't fully cover - specifically behavioral-trigger journeys (e.g., patient onboarding sequence, care plan adherence nudges).
candidates.maintenance_health: 2.6k GitHub stars; YC S22; active PRs (1714 closed, 9 open as of 2026); self-host free, cloud from $75/mo; Node.js + web SDKs published.
candidates.stack_fit: TypeScript-native, Node.js SDK drops into NestJS as a service client. However, the self-host stack adds ClickHouse + Temporal on top of Postgres - significant infra expansion. For CuraOS v1 scope this is likely deferred. REST API usable without SDK.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host: Docker Compose / Helm (lite mode: Postgres only, no ClickHouse). BYO-3rd-party: Dittofeed Cloud. Both paths use same SDK.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Journey builder models patient/user lifecycle (onboarding, re-engagement, care adherence). Person-level opt-out and preferences. Segment by clinical attributes (diagnosis group, care plan status) for targeted communications.
candidates.generator_first_target: SDK pkg (@dittofeed/sdk-node): notify-core-service tracks events to Dittofeed for journey triggers. TypeSpec events map to Dittofeed track() calls in service-seed emit.
candidates.risks: ClickHouse + Temporal are heavy dependencies (though lite mode skips ClickHouse). 2.6k stars is lower confidence than Novu's 39k. Significant overlap with listmonk for bulk email. Recommended as v1.1 addition once Novu + listmonk cover v1 baseline.

candidates.name: jsx-email (Bun-native react-email alternative)
candidates.what: React-compatible JSX email rendering library forked from react-email with explicit Bun and edge runtime compatibility, plugin architecture, first-class Tailwind-to-inline-styles, and built-in Markdown rendering. Drop-in replacement for @react-email/render where bun compile compatibility matters. Same use-case as react-email but resolves the bun compile binary issue.
candidates.maintenance_health: Actively maintained; explicit Bun support; plugin ecosystem; last release 2025-2026 timeframe; smaller community than react-email but targeted at the exact Bun+edge gap.
candidates.stack_fit: Drop-in for @react-email/render. Works with Bun compile (the blocker react-email has). NestJS integration identical. Tailwind-to-inline solves email client compatibility without extra build step.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: N/A - rendering library, provider-agnostic.
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same as react-email: JSX components authored with person-centric content, shared with ui-kit design tokens.
candidates.generator_first_target: codegen-emitter-target: email-emit step in codegen mold generates .email.tsx stubs using jsx-email component primitives if Bun compile path is needed.
candidates.risks: Smaller community than react-email; API divergence from react-email could require migration effort if react-email resolves the bun-compile issue. Evaluate as react-email fallback rather than primary if bun compile is not on the critical path (service mode Bun works fine with react-email).

verified.name: Novu (@novu/framework + @novu/react)
verified.license_confirmed: PARTIAL. Core packages (MIT) confirmed. BUT: enterprise folder at repo root, apps/dashboard/src/ee, and apps/web/src/ee are covered by a SEPARATE commercial license. SAML SSO, audit log export, and advanced dashboard modules are NOT MIT. For CuraOS air-gap + HIPAA: the commercial EE boundary is tolerable only if those features are never needed unlicensed - but audit log export is exactly what HIPAA self-hosted deployments require, making the EE gate a real cost risk, not a footnote. Claim of "MIT (core) + commercial EE" is accurate but undersells the blast radius of the EE wall.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: CONFIRMED active. v3.14.0 confirmed June 2026, community Docker images available, SDKs shipping in parallel. Weekly cadence real. Series A + Novu Inc backing confirmed. No critical security CVEs found specific to Novu itself (a Helm 3.18.3 CVE exists in the Helm binary, not Novu). HOWEVER: the community Helm chart is NOT official - it is third-party authored. No official Helm chart from novuhq org. This is a real ops risk for CuraOS (uses Zarf/Helm for K8s + air-gap).
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - and this is the kill shot for "adopt". CuraOS already has: Kafka/Redpanda (durable event bus), NestJS (WebSocket gateway native), PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY (pg-notify NestJS transport exists as nestjs-pg-notify), and a Redis stack. A notify-core-service built on these existing primitives covers: multi-channel routing (emit typed Kafka events, per-channel consumers), in-app feed (PostgreSQL table + pg-notify + WS gateway already in stack), preference management (standard Drizzle table). The only genuine gap is the 50+ provider abstraction layer - but that maps to a thin adapter registry pattern, not a full external service. Novu forces MongoDB + Redis + 8GB RAM as a SECOND persistence engine alongside CuraOS's Postgres-only stance. Ponytail ladder rung 4 (already-installed dependency) covers 80% of what Novu delivers without the MongoDB engine conflict.
verified.notes:
FIVE VERDICTS BY AXIS:

1. LICENSE: Mostly fine for MIT core. EE gate on audit logs is a real HIPAA/compliance risk for self-hosted air-gap customers. Candidate overstated safety. Flag as cost risk, not blocker.

2. MAINTENANCE: Active, confirmed. No issue here.

3. INTEGRATION (NestJS/Bun/Drizzle/Zod/TypeSpec): NovuModule.register() for NestJS is real and documented. Bun compatibility is NOT explicitly confirmed by Novu - the claim that "Bun runtime works" is optimistic; Novu's server-side packages bundle Node-specific code (BullMQ worker pools, native MongoDB driver) that may need validation. @novu/react + ui-kit works fine (React is React). TypeSpec -> Novu trigger shapes is plausible but not a native integration - needs codegen mold. Drizzle/Zod: irrelevant, Novu does not expose a Drizzle/Zod surface; it IS the persistence layer for its own data, which is MongoDB, not Postgres.

4. GENERATOR-FIRST / LOCK-IN: Significant lock-in. Novu's workflow definitions (.workflow.ts files) are Novu-SDK-specific DSL. They cannot be expressed in TypeSpec AsyncAPI contracts natively - a codegen adapter must be built and maintained. If Novu changes its SDK shape (they have between v0, v1, v2, v3), every codegen-emitted file must be regenerated. This is a generator-first risk, not a benefit. Migration cost to exit Novu is high (rewrite every workflow definition + find a new in-app feed solution).

5. SIMPLER ALTERNATIVE (ponytail YAGNI): MongoDB is the decisive veto. CuraOS is Postgres-only (CNPG, Citus, Drizzle ORM, pgBouncer). Adding MongoDB for one service: (a) violates the single-DB-engine rule implicitly encoded in curaos_postgres_rule.md, (b) doubles ops surface for DBA/backup/CNPG migration procedures, (c) breaks air-gap (Zarf bundles must now include MongoDB images + storage operator), (d) adds 8GB RAM floor to a single service. The right path is a custom notify-core-service using Postgres + existing Kafka bus + NestJS WebSocket gateway. Reference Novu's provider abstraction design (50+ provider patterns) and in-app feed schema design without taking the MongoDB dependency. Use Novu Cloud as the BYO-3rd-party provider option per [[curaos-local-vs-3rdparty-rule]] - the SDK path still works for cloud-routed delivery without self-hosting Novu's server stack.

verified.name: react-email (@react-email/render + @react-email/components)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. @react-email/render latest publish April 28, 2026. Safe for self-hosted + air-gap distribution with no restriction on embedding or redistribution.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. v6.6.4 published June 2026 (2 days before evaluation). 17k+ stars, 8.2M weekly downloads on render package, 182 contributors, Resend-backed. No relevant CVEs: CVE-2025-55182 (React2Shell) affects React Server Components + Next.js, not react-email's renderToStaticMarkup path. Issue #2585 (bun compile binary) is open but confirmed non-blocking for service-mode (bun run) usage.
verified.integration_reality: Bun-as-runtime works: Resend ships a first-party react-email-turborepo-bun-example repo; Elysia (Bun-native) documents the integration. NestJS glue is ~10 lines (render() to HTML string, pass to nodemailer transporter). No framework coupling. Turborepo-compatible shared package pattern is established. CRITICAL: the candidate describes v5-era split packages (@react-email/render + @react-email/components as separate imports). v6 (April 2026) consolidates everything into a single react-email package - the split is deprecated. The @curaos/email-templates package must be authored against react-email v6 unified API, not the two-package shape described. This is a scoping error in the candidate, not a blocker.
verified.simpler_alternative: MJML (18k stars, industry standard, Bun-compatible, no React dep) covers responsive cross-client HTML but uses its own DSL - less generator-friendly for TypeSpec-to-TSX codegen stubs. Handlebars/EJS covers string templating but no component model. Neither matches the TSX-native generator-first pattern CuraOS needs. react-email is the right layer; no simpler already-installed option covers it.
verified.notes: Downgrade from adopt to trial for two reasons: (1) Vendor concentration risk - v6 added an embedded visual editor clearly targeting Resend SaaS customers; Resend's roadmap incentive to maintain provider-agnostic render path may weaken over time. MIT license is the backstop. (2) Package shape mismatch - candidate targets v5 split API; v6 unified package is a breaking change. Trial scope: implement notify-core-service integration with react-email v6 unified API (single react-email import), Postal SMTP delivery, and one TypeSpec-to-email-stub codegen emitter. Validate cross-client rendering against Litmus or equivalent before expanding to healthstack surfaces. Adopt full if trial passes.

verified.name: Postal (self-hosted SMTP relay + HTTP API)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed. No license change detected in 2024-2026. GitHub repo remains MIT. Safe for self-hosted + air-gap distribution and BYO-3rd-party duality. No SSPL, BSL, or AGPL trap.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active. v3.3.6 released 2026-04-28, v3.3.7 released 2026-06-03 (security release: SSRF prevention in outbound webhooks + SQL injection via GHSA-x2hq-rfpg-3xr5 patched). ~17k stars, 46 open issues (no critical unpatched). v3 dropped RabbitMQ dependency (2024). Release cadence: multiple per year, security issues are picked up and patched promptly - GHSA disclosed and patched same release cycle. Maintenance claim is confirmed.
verified.integration_reality: Partially optimistic. HTTP API is real and well-documented (POST JSON, API key auth, send/messages/webhooks endpoints) - thin fetch wrapper in NestJS PostalProvider is genuinely ~50 lines. The abstraction point is valid: EmailProvider interface already exists in notify-core-service (confirmed in m10-271 grill: interface-first, DI token, selector, no conditional branching in business logic). So the NestJS integration claim is accurate. TypeSpec codegen of PostalProvider from a provider config schema is plausible but needs a config schema not yet defined - that is speculative work, not existing glue. The claim "no Node SDK needed" is correct - raw fetch works. Zod validation on the request/response shape is trivial. No Drizzle interaction needed (Postal owns its own DB). HOWEVER: the ops claim "no conflict" with Postgres CNPG understates the real issue - Postal requires MariaDB (NOT MySQL - strictly MariaDB per docs), meaning a second stateful database engine enters the ops stack. For Zarf air-gap packaging this means bundling a MariaDB image + init, a Ruby-based container, and the Postal web/worker/SMTP containers (4+ containers). Zarf can handle this but it inflates the air-gap bundle and the ops surface significantly. The candidate calls this "M effort" - it is closer to L for the initial ops setup (IP warming, DKIM/SPF DNS records, MariaDB lifecycle, Postal web UI access controls, separate backup story for MariaDB vs CNPG).
verified.simpler_alternative: Yes, and worth naming explicitly. The local-vs-3rdparty rule already lists Postfix/Haraka as the self-hosted email example. Haraka (Node.js, MIT) would add zero new runtime languages and has an HTTP API plugin ecosystem. For the notify-core-service use case (transactional only, no mass campaigns, no web UI needed), Haraka + nodemailer SMTP relay is a lighter path. Stalwart (Rust, MIT, pre-1.0 but passed 2 independent security audits, single binary, no MariaDB) is a stronger air-gap story. Maddy (Go) is simpler still but less mature. None of these have Postal's management UI or bounce/click-tracking UI, which may be the actual differentiator. If those features are the requirement, Postal is the right pick. If the requirement is just reliable SMTP relay with bounce webhooks, simpler options exist.
verified.notes: Three concrete risks not fully surfaced in the candidate brief: (1) Security track record - GHSA-x2hq-rfpg-3xr5 was a SQL injection in the legacy API message lookup (blind, time-based, via condition keys). The fix landed in 3.3.7. This is a real historical vuln, not theoretical. SSRF in outbound webhooks also patched in 3.3.7. Both are patched, but this is a Ruby/Rails app and the surface is non-trivial. (2) MariaDB is a second DB engine, NOT a minor footnote. CNPG manages Postgres; MariaDB gets no such operator. Backup parity, schema migration lifecycle, and Zarf bundle size all increase. The candidate says "no conflict" which is true at the schema level but false at the ops level. (3) IP reputation for clinical email: the candidate flags this correctly. Clinical transactional email (appointment reminders, lab results notifications) failing spam filters is a patient safety issue, not just UX. A fresh IP needs weeks of warming. This is a launch-timeline risk for any new CuraOS deployment. Recommendation: downgrade from "adopt" to "trial" - validate the ops burden and IP warming story in a staging env before committing the generator codegen slot. The EmailProvider abstraction already in place means swapping in a lighter alternative later is low-cost if Postal proves too heavy.

verified.name: listmonk (self-hosted newsletter + transactional email)
verified.license_confirmed: AGPLv3 confirmed. Service-boundary isolation (HTTP API only, no import) is legally sound for SaaS and on-prem. No modification planned, so no source-publication obligation. Air-gap viable - single binary + Postgres, Zarf-packageable. License claim is accurate but the compliance burden is real: any fork or embedded patch must be published. Third-party Helm charts exist (redzumi/listmonk-chart, th0ths-helm-charts) but are community-maintained, not official. No official Helm chart from upstream.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Actively maintained - v6.1.0 released 2026-03-29. However, the security record is a concern: THREE CVEs disclosed in 2026 alone. CVE-2026-21483 (CVSS 8.0 High): stored XSS -> admin account takeover, affected <=5.1.0, fixed in v6.0.0. CVE-2026-34828 (High): active sessions not invalidated after password reset/change, fixed in v6.1.0. CVE-2026-34584 (CVSS 5.4 Medium): list permission bypass in multi-user environments, affected 4.1.0 to <6.1.0, fixed in v6.1.0. All three are now patched in v6.1.0 - but three auth/session/XSS vulns in one quarter from a tool that would hold patient communication preferences is a yellow flag. Open issue tracker shows ~92 open issues including campaign delivery stopping short (2000/5000 sent bug), re-subscription silently failing after unsubscribe, and silent API errors on permission failures. The delivery-stopping bug is particularly relevant for patient bulk campaigns.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - and this is the decisive ponytail ladder rejection. CuraOS already has: (1) notify-service with POST /notifications/batch bulk enqueue, campaignId/campaignName tracking fields, scheduled_for delivery, EmailProvider interface with CuraOSLocalEmailProvider (Haraka/Postfix SMTP) + ExternalEmailProvider (SendGrid/Postmark/Mailgun) - both local-first and BYO-provider duality satisfied. (2) Per-actor channel preferences (GET/PATCH /notify/preferences) with opt-in/opt-out per channel. (3) communications-store with delivery/opened tracking. The genuine gaps listmonk covers that notify-service does NOT: subscriber list management with SQL segmentation, double opt-in confirmation flows, drag-and-drop template builder UI, campaign analytics dashboard, bounce/unsubscribe management portal. These are real gaps - but they are OPERATOR-FACING UI gaps, not a missing service layer. Adding listmonk adds: a second Postgres schema to manage, a second binary to operate, Zarf package to maintain, a security surface with a recent CVE cluster, and AGPL boundary discipline forever. The correct path: extend notify-service with a mailing-list domain (subscriber lists, opt-in confirmation, segment queries) and add a campaign-builder UI in the app-builder. That stays generator-first (codegen emits the list/campaign module), stays in the existing CNPG cluster, and avoids the operational and license overhead.
verified.integration_reality: Integration is straightforward in principle (REST HTTP client in NestJS, ~50 lines) but the candidate's claim that this is effort=S is optimistic when the full picture is included: no official TypeScript/Node SDK (community libs only, unverified per listmonk docs), second Postgres instance or schema isolation decision, Zarf package authoring, SMTP relay config overlap with existing Haraka/Postfix stack, and ongoing security patch tracking for an AGPL binary. The generator-first target is valid (ListmonkProvider in notify-core-service) but adds a permanent external dependency that the generator must template, maintain, and conditionally toggle - complexity that fights the generator-evolution rule when listmonk itself changes its API (v6.0.0 added granular campaign permissions that would require provider updates). No native TypeScript/Bun SDK; HTTP wrapper is trivial but every breaking listmonk release propagates to the wrapper.
verified.notes: Downgrade to reference: listmonk is a well-built tool worth studying for its subscriber-list and double-opt-in patterns, but it should not be adopted as a CuraOS runtime dependency at this stage. Reasons in priority order: (1) Ponytail ladder rung 2 - notify-service already covers the bulk/campaign/preference/email-provider lane; the missing pieces (subscriber lists, double opt-in, template builder) should extend notify-service, not add a second service. (2) Three auth/session/XSS CVEs in Q1 2026, all now patched in v6.1.0 - acceptable for v6.1.0+ but the velocity of security issues in a tool holding patient communication opt-in data is a risk to track. (3) Campaign delivery reliability bug (campaigns stopping short) is unresolved. (4) No official Helm chart, no official TypeScript SDK - community-only ops and integration artifacts. (5) AGPL boundary discipline adds permanent review overhead. REFERENCE value: adopt listmonk's double opt-in confirmation flow design and SQL-segment subscriber model as patterns to implement natively inside notify-service. If a future milestone explicitly needs a standalone marketing-list UI for non-technical operators (e.g. wellness campaign managers) before the CuraOS app-builder is mature enough, re-evaluate then with v6.x security track record in hand.

candidates.name: next-runtime-env
candidates.what: Runtime injection of NEXT_PUBLIC_* and next.config-derived values without rebuild. Adds a PublicEnvScript component to root layout that reads process.env per-request (SSR), populates window.__ENV on client. Directly fixes the #840/ADR-0220 blocker: allowedOrigins, CSP nonces, and any NEXT_PUBLIC_ value become runtime-injectable without touching the build. Taxonomy: ui-app-emit generator gap - runtime config pattern.
candidates.maintenance_health: 580+ stars, active as of 2025, supports Next.js 14+; light dependency, widely adopted pattern for standalone Docker deploys
candidates.stack_fit: Drop-in for Next.js App Router. One PublicEnvScript in root layout.tsx, env() helper replaces process.env on client. Generator (ui-app-emit) emits it once in the layout template - zero per-route changes. No Bun/NestJS/Drizzle wrapper needed.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: First-party self-host only (reads from container env); no 3rd-party dependency
candidates.person_centric_fit: Invisible to end users; enables correct per-tenant config injection (tenant-specific API base URLs, CSP) which directly supports multi-tenant person-centric surfaces
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit (root layout.tsx template)
candidates.risks: Relies on server-side rendering to inject the script; pages that are statically exported will not benefit. Still a workaround, not a Next.js core fix - if Next.js changes App Router's root layout behavior it could break.

candidates.name: TanStack Start (full fleet migration)
candidates.what: Replace Next.js with TanStack Start across all 24 web apps. Addresses: runtime env (VITE_ vars still bake, but createServerFn escape hatch is cleaner); self-host/air-gap (bun build --compile = 132 MB alpine image vs 2-3 GB Next.js standalone); SSR/streaming (loaders + Suspense, 5.5x throughput benchmark vs Next.js); generator fit (file-based routes + routeTree.gen.ts is more emitter-friendly than App Router page.tsx conventions). Taxonomy: ui-app-emit generator target replacement.
candidates.maintenance_health: TanStack/router: 25k+ stars, v1.0 stable released March 2026, RSC support added April 2026, Tanner Linsley + active OSS community, production use: Inngest, Railway, 200+ routes each
candidates.stack_fit: Native Bun support (bun.sh official guide). Vite-based build fits Turborepo. No Nx official generator yet (GitHub discussion #34273 open as of March 2026 - community templates only). TanStack Query + Drizzle patterns well-documented. shadcn/ui official support. Auth cookie + NestJS JWT bridge documented. No wrapper needed for Bun runtime.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host first - Nitro or custom Bun server; no Vercel dependency. Bun compile to single binary for air-gap/Zarf. BYO hosting fully supported.
candidates.person_centric_fit: File-based routing with beforeLoad auth guards and loaders maps cleanly to person-centric journey flows (patient app, personal-* apps). RSC support (April 2026) enables server-rendered patient data without client-side waterfall.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit emitter - would emit src/routes/*.tsx files; Vite plugin auto-generates routeTree.gen.ts. More emitter-friendly than Next.js App Router because route discovery is explicit and generated file is stable.
candidates.risks: VITE_ vars still bake at build time (same problem as NEXT_PUBLIC_) - must use createServerFn pattern per variable, not automatic. No Nx generator plugin (CuraOS uses Nx + Turborepo) - ui-app-emit adapter must be written from scratch. 24-app migration at RC-to-v1 boundary = high risk window. RSC support is 2 months old (April 2026), not production-battle-tested. Migration cost: ~2 weeks per app without AI assist, likely 6-12 weeks for 24-app generator-first fleet even with codegen.

candidates.name: TanStack Start (selective - new apps only)
candidates.what: Use TanStack Start for net-new apps (not migrating the existing 24) while keeping the current Next.js fleet on next-runtime-env fix. Allows generator (ui-app-emit) to emit two targets: Next.js for existing apps, TanStack Start for greenfield. Lets the ecosystem/Nx generator situation mature before committing the fleet. Taxonomy: ui-app-emit codegen-emitter-target.
candidates.maintenance_health: Same as full migration entry. v1.0 stable March 2026. Ecosystem still maturing for Nx/monorepo tooling.
candidates.stack_fit: Same Bun + Turborepo fit. ui-app-emit would need a --framework flag (nextjs | tanstack-start). Two emitter templates to maintain in tools/codegen, but no migration burden on existing apps.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host first, same as full migration
candidates.person_centric_fit: Same as full migration. Allows person-centric new surfaces (e.g. patient-app v2, personal-health-app) to be built on TanStack Start with cleaner data loading patterns.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit (add tanstack-start emitter target alongside nextjs target)
candidates.risks: Two emitter targets in tools/codegen = maintenance surface doubles. Monorepo with mixed frameworks increases tooling complexity. Team must learn both mental models. Still no Nx generator to delegate to.

candidates.name: TanStack Router (SPA mode, no Start)
candidates.what: Use TanStack Router standalone (no TanStack Start SSR layer) for apps that do not need SSR - pure client-side SPAs served as static files. Runtime env is trivially solved: inject window.__ENV from a server-side config endpoint at container startup, or use a sidecar nginx that rewrites a config.js file. No build-time baking problem because there is no build-time SSR. Taxonomy: ui-app-emit for non-SSR apps.
candidates.maintenance_health: TanStack Router: 25k+ stars, stable v1, production-proven well before TanStack Start, used by thousands of SPAs
candidates.stack_fit: Excellent. Vite + Bun build. routeTree.gen.ts codegen. Turborepo-friendly. No SSR layer = simpler Helm/Zarf packaging (just a static asset bundle behind nginx). NestJS JWT auth via API calls, no cookie proxy complexity.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Static assets self-hosted (nginx/CDN); no managed dependency
candidates.person_centric_fit: Suitable for dashboard/admin apps where SEO is not a concern and interactivity is high. Not suitable for public-facing patient-facing pages where SSR matters for perceived performance.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit (SPA variant emitter - simpler than SSR, no server entry)
candidates.risks: No SSR = no server-side rendering for SEO or initial load performance. Not appropriate for public-facing surfaces. Mixed fleet (SPA + SSR) increases generator complexity.

candidates.name: Next.js with #840 fix (stay-nextjs path)
candidates.what: Keep all 24 apps on Next.js standalone; fix the NEXT_PUBLIC bake problem via next-runtime-env + generator-native PublicEnvScript in ui-app-emit root layout template; fix allowedOrigins/CSP by reading from process.env in a custom Next.js server wrapper (instrumentation.ts or server.js) that patches next.config at startup. This is the direct fix for the blocker that triggered the decision. No framework migration risk. Taxonomy: ui-app-emit generator template fix.
candidates.maintenance_health: Next.js: Vercel-backed, 128k+ stars, v15/v16 active, 8 years of ecosystem. NEXT_PUBLIC bake is unfixed by Vercel (intentional); next-runtime-env maintained independently.
candidates.stack_fit: Zero friction - existing 24 apps unchanged except generator template update. ui-app-emit adds PublicEnvScript to root layout.tsx template. allowedOrigins fix: move to runtime-read in a custom server.js that next() wraps. Bun standalone: Next.js standalone is NOT a single binary (requires node_modules dir); image size remains 2-3 GB unless manually slimmed.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-host (standalone output); no Vercel required. Existing Helm/Zarf packaging unchanged.
candidates.person_centric_fit: No change to person-centric surfaces. Existing App Router RSC pattern already in use for patient/personal apps.
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit (root layout.tsx template + custom server.js template)
candidates.risks: Standalone image size remains large (no single-binary option for Next.js). NEXT_PUBLIC bake is a workaround not a fix - next-runtime-env adds a runtime indirection layer. If Vercel changes App Router root layout behavior the workaround may break. Ongoing Vercel alignment tax: Next.js increasingly optimized for Vercel edge, self-host footprint grows each release.

candidates.name: Vite (standalone, framework-agnostic)
candidates.what: Use Vite directly (not via TanStack Start wrapper) as the build tool for custom SSR or SPA apps. TanStack Router plugs in via @tanstack/router-vite-plugin. Vite handles HMR, code-splitting, and asset bundling. Runtime env: no NEXT_PUBLIC analog - server reads process.env, passes to client however the app chooses. Reference point for understanding what TanStack Start adds on top. Taxonomy: build tooling for ui-app-emit.
candidates.maintenance_health: Vite: 70k+ stars, Evan You + VoidZero Inc, v6 stable 2025, de-facto standard for non-Next.js React apps
candidates.stack_fit: Excellent Bun compatibility. Turborepo pipeline integration well-documented. No Nx-specific plugin needed. Custom SSR entry point gives full control over env injection.
candidates.local_vs_3rdparty: Self-hosted build; no managed service
candidates.person_centric_fit: Neutral build tool; app architecture determines person-centric fit
candidates.generator_first_target: ui-app-emit (vite.config.ts template)
candidates.risks: Custom SSR requires more boilerplate than TanStack Start or Next.js. Not a framework - team must assemble auth, routing, streaming primitives themselves.

verified.name: next-runtime-env
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed via npm/snyk. Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution. No issue here.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: INACTIVE. Last release: v3.3.0, March 29 2025 (15 months ago). Snyk advisor marks maintenance status as "Inactive." No commits or PR activity post-March 2025. Peer dep declared for Next.js 14; Next.js 15 compatibility is anecdotal (no official release update), and Next.js 16 peer dep is unresolved - same pattern as better-auth, genkit, payload all broke on Next 16. CuraOS is on Next.js 15.3.5 today but the roadmap is Next 16 exposure risk.
verified.integration_reality: The candidate's core claim - that it "directly fixes #840/ADR-0220" - is OVERSTATED. ADR-0220 (Accepted 2026-06-29, v1.1 target) explicitly leaves the adopt-vs-in-house question OPEN at line 45: "Lean in-house for zero third-party runtime dep + exact control; decide in the spike child issue." The library solves only ONE of the four ADR-0220 decisions (client NEXT_PUBLIC_ injection via window.__ENV). The other three (allowedOrigins elimination via proxy header alignment, CSP at runtime via middleware, Zod boot validation) are independent of this package. Integration with Bun/NestJS/Drizzle is irrelevant - this is a Next.js RSC component only. Generator fit is fine (one layout.tsx template change in ui-app-emit). No Zod/TypeSpec/TypeScript surface conflict. BUT: the DIY pattern is ~25 lines of RSC + a 10-line isomorphic accessor - the entire mechanism is a server component that calls JSON.stringify on filtered process.env keys and emits a script tag. next-runtime-env is literally that, plus a @ts-ignore and a regex filter. The package.json adds a runtime dep that ships to production across all 21 apps; its implementation surface is trivial to own.
verified.simpler_alternative: YES - and ADR-0220 already names it. An in-house @curaos/runtime-config package (~35 LOC total: one RSC PublicEnvScript, one isomorphic env() accessor, one useRuntimeConfig() hook, one Zod boot validator) covers all four ADR-0220 decisions, not just the client injection. The DIY pattern is well-documented in Next.js discussions (#87229, #44628) and multiple blog posts; it is not novel. It lives in the curaos monorepo, is generator-emit-able from ui-app-emit like any other shared package, tracks Next.js upgrades on your schedule, carries no peer-dep lag risk, and removes a runtime external dep from all 21 production images. Ponytail verdict: the package IS fewer lines than re-implementing it carelessly, but the implementation is simple enough that an in-house package is the right rung - especially given the inactive maintenance and the v1.1 timeline where Next 16 exposure is real.
verified.notes: **Research value: high** -- ADR-0220 is already accepted and the open question (adopt vs in-house) is the exact verdict this audit addresses. Key facts: (1) next-runtime-env v3.3.0 is maintenance-inactive (15 months, no Next 15 official release, no Next 16 peer dep update). (2) The implementation is ~25 LOC of standard RSC + JSON.stringify - trivially owned. (3) ADR-0220 already leans in-house. (4) The candidate overstates scope: allowedOrigins is fixed by proxy header alignment (ingress annotation), not by this library. (5) No CVEs on the package itself; the Dec 2025 React/Next.js RCE (CVE-2025-55182/66478) affects Next.js core, not this library, but confirms staying close to the Next.js upgrade path matters. Recommended path: implement @curaos/runtime-config as a new frontend/packages/runtime-config package, emit from ui-app-emit generator, close ADR-0220 open question as "in-house." Reference next-runtime-env only as prior art for the implementation pattern.

verified.name: TanStack Start (full fleet migration)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed via TanStack org GitHub (table, query, router all MIT). Air-gap and self-hosted distribution: clean. No dual-licensing, no CLA, no AGPL trap. License claim holds.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active - 1.168.26 as of late June 2026, daily patch cadence. BUT: the "v1.0 stable" framing in the candidate is misleading - the blog post title is "v1 Release Candidate"; npm latest does not carry a separate stable dist-tag. RSC support (April 13, 2026) is 2.5 months old with zero production postmortems. Rapid version churn (1.129 to 1.168 in weeks) signals active but not settled. CRITICAL: CVE-2026-45321 (May 11, 2026) - 84 malicious versions across 42 @tanstack/* packages published via GitHub Actions OIDC chain attack (TeamPCP / Mini Shai-Hulud). Credential-exfiltrating malware at install time. All versions published after May 12 are reported clean. TanStack has hardened CI (removed pull_request_target, SHA-pinned actions, disabled fork caches). Current packages are safe to use WITH SHA-pinned lockfile + SBOM gate - but this is a non-trivial supply chain risk for a security-sensitive healthcare platform. The incident itself does not disqualify, but the version pinning rule becomes hard-mandatory, not advisory.
verified.integration_reality: Bun: real, first-party. Official bun.sh guide exists, start-bun example in TanStack/router repo, bun preset in app.config.js. Binary compile (132 MB alpine) is achievable but requires non-trivial Vite config (inlineDynamicImports: true, serveStatic: inline, single-chunk SSR bundle). Next.js 15 standalone + next-bun-compile adapter also achieves single-binary compile - the claimed 2-3 GB image comparison is apples-to-Docker-image not apples-to-compiled-binary. Gap is real but narrower than stated. NestJS/JWT bridge: documented (cookie -> Authorization header forwarding via SSR server), no first-party package needed, one DEV Community guide found. Drizzle/Zod: standard patterns documented, no glue needed. Nx generator: CONFIRMED MISSING - GitHub discussion #34273 open, no official plugin as of June 2026, only community templates. ui-app-emit adapter must be written from scratch. Virtual File Routes API (@tanstack/virtual-file-routes) is the correct integration path for generator-first - emitter writes physical route files OR supplies virtualRouteConfig; routeTree.gen.ts auto-regenerates. API is documented and stable enough, but no generator-first monorepo precedent at CuraOS scale found. VITE_ bake-at-build-time: confirmed same class as NEXT_PUBLIC_ - requires per-variable createServerFn escape hatch, manual per-request reads inside .handler(). For multi-tenant runtime config this is a design constraint, not a blocker, but it needs deliberate architecture.
verified.simpler_alternative: Ponytail ladder check: does this need to exist at all at full-fleet scale right now?
