No. Current 24-app fleet runs Next.js - migration is XL effort with no precedent at this scale. The specific wins claimed (image size, env-var ergonomics, generator fit) are real but not blockers in the current system. Simpler path: (1) adopt TanStack Start for NEW apps only via generator (zero migration cost, proves generator fit in isolation); (2) keep existing 24 apps on Next.js until at least one new app is in production and the Nx plugin situation resolves; (3) next-bun-compile covers the binary-size concern for Next.js apps if that becomes a real deployment constraint. Full-fleet migration at RC boundary + post-supply-chain-incident + no Nx plugin + 2.5-month-old RSC = three simultaneous risk factors. The ponytail verdict: ship TanStack Start as the generator target for net-new apps, leave the migration question for after 3-6 months of fleet-scale evidence.
verified.notes: Candidate recommendation was 'trial' - downgrading to 'downgrade-reference' (adopt for net-new via generator only; block full fleet migration). Reasons: (1) CVE-2026-45321 supply chain incident May 2026 - packages are clean NOW but air-gap/Zarf + version-pinning gate is mandatory before any adoption, not optional. (2) Nx generator gap is real and confirmed open - ui-app-emit adapter is a non-trivial build, not a 'write from scratch' footnote. (3) RSC is 2.5 months old with no production postmortems - claiming it as a generator-fit benefit for patient-centric SSR is premature. (4) 24-app fleet migration is XL effort with no comparable precedent; Inngest (one app, one engineer) and Railway (built their own tooling) do not de-risk a generator-first fleet migration. (5) The 'v1.0 stable' claim in the candidate overstates - it is an RC designation. Counter-evidence for keeping the candidate in scope at all: license is clean, Bun integration is first-party, NestJS JWT bridge is documented, file-based routing is genuinely more emitter-friendly than App Router, and production teams are shipping. The technology merits adoption as the forward generator target for new apps; it does not merit a full fleet migration today.

verified.name: TanStack Start (selective - new apps only)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed on npm for @tanstack/react-start and @tanstack/start-config. Permissive, compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution. No license issue.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: COMPROMISED. CVE-2026-45321 (GHSA-g7cv-rxg3-hmpx, Critical): on 2026-05-11, 84 malicious versions across 42 @tanstack/* packages were published via GitHub Actions cache-poisoning + Pwn Request + OIDC token extraction. @tanstack/react-start (versions 1.167.68, 1.167.71) and @tanstack/start-plugin-core are explicitly listed in the affected set - not the clean set. Malicious versions have since been deprecated and removed; TanStack hardened CI (SHA-pinned actions, removed pnpm cache) by 2026-05-20. Active releases exist (latest June 2026). However, Vite 8 SSR middleware bug (issue #7614, unresolved at time of research) causes dev server 404 on Vite 8 with no fix except manual node_modules patch. Additionally, no official Nx generator (open discussion #34273 since Jan 2026, still community-only).
verified.integration_reality: Does NOT integrate cleanly with CuraOS stack without significant glue. Blockers: (1) ui-app-emit.ts is 7,686 lines with ~150 hardwired Next.js references - adding a --framework flag and a second emitter target is not a minor flag, it is effectively forking the emitter, doubling the template surface, and doubling snapshot test count; (2) TanStack Start uses Vite + Nitro as bundler/server, orthogonal to the existing Next.js + next-runtime-env + standalone Docker pattern - two separate build pipelines in one Turborepo monorepo with different caching semantics; (3) No Nx generator to delegate to (open request only), so CuraOS tooling must own all scaffold templates end-to-end for both targets; (4) Active Vite 8 SSR middleware regression with no upstream fix, only a node_modules patch workaround; (5) React 19 required for Bun-native deployment mode per Bun docs - fleet is not confirmed on React 19 uniformly.
verified.simpler_alternative: Yes. The existing approach already covers the stated need: Next.js + next-runtime-env fix is the live fleet pattern (24 apps, zero migration cost). The candidate's own framing concedes no migration of existing apps. For new patient-facing or person-centric surfaces, Next.js App Router with TanStack Router (client-side only, already installed via @tanstack/react-query at pin 5.101.0) covers type-safe routing without the full TanStack Start server framework. Adding a full second SSR framework to ui-app-emit for greenfield apps that do not yet exist is speculative need (YAGNI). The Ponytail ladder stops at rung 1: this does not need to exist yet.
verified.notes: Three compounding blockers make this a reject rather than a downgrade-to-reference: (1) the supply chain incident directly hit @tanstack/react-start - the core integration package - meaning any version pinning audit must treat the entire package family as elevated risk until a full supply chain attestation (provenance + SLSA) is in place, which is not yet present; (2) the emitter fork cost is structural, not effort-estimable as L - 7.6k lines of Next.js-wired codegen need a full abstraction layer before any second target is viable, and that abstraction is unapproved scope; (3) the Vite 8 regression is unresolved and the workaround requires patching node_modules, which is incompatible with Bun compile single-binary targets and the air-gap Zarf packaging model (hermetic builds cannot tolerate post-install patches). Revisit when: Nx official generator ships, Vite 8 compat is fixed upstream, supply chain attestation (SLSA L2+) is published, AND a concrete greenfield app is approved that cannot be served by Next.js. File as future/v1.1 work.

verified.name: TanStack Router (SPA mode, no Start)
verified.license_confirmed: MIT confirmed (GHSA-g7cv-rxg3-hmpx advisory, npm registry, GitHub repo). Compatible with self-hosted + air-gap distribution. No CLA, no SSPL, no dual-license trap.
verified.maintenance_confirmed: Active release cadence through May 2026 (v1.170.x, releases every 1-3 weeks). HOWEVER: CVE-2026-45321 / GHSA-g7cv-rxg3-hmpx is a critical supply chain event (May 11 2026). 84 malicious artifacts published across 42 @tanstack/* packages including @tanstack/react-router explicitly, via CI token extraction from a compromised GitHub Actions runner. Malicious versions deprecated and removed from npm. Current 1.170.x versions confirmed safe. Hardening applied (pinned action SHAs, pull_request_target restructure, repo owner guards). Critical trust note: SLSA Build Level 3 provenance was legitimately issued for the malicious packages - attestation-as-proof-of-integrity failed structurally. For CuraOS air-gap / self-hosted posture with strict supply chain controls (curaos_airgap_rule, version-pinning with SHA-digest pins), this event is not yet enough time (6 weeks) to confirm the hardening is durable under the TeamPCP worm class of attack that also hit Bitwarden CLI and Aqua Trivy.
verified.integration_reality: Vite plugin integrates cleanly with Bun builds. routeTree.gen.ts codegen runs at Vite build time or via CLI watcher - no SSR server entry, no Next.js server runtime, Turborepo-friendly. NestJS/Drizzle/Zod are not touched (pure frontend). TypeSpec contracts consumed via @curaos/api-client SDK as in current apps - no change there. Helm/Zarf packaging is genuinely simpler as a static asset bundle. HOWEVER: ui-app-emit.ts (318KB) currently generates Next.js 15 App Router apps for ALL 24 web apps under ADR-0153 ui.react-next-page + ui.react-next-admin-shell recipes. Adding a TanStack SPA variant means maintaining two route paradigms, two Vite config shapes, two Dockerfile shapes, two Helm chart shapes in one massive emitter. The candidate describes this as a taxonomy addition but the generator impact is a fork-level change to a 318KB emitter.
verified.simpler_alternative: Already installed: Next.js 15 with output: 'export' in next.config.ts produces a fully static SPA bundle (no Node.js server, pure HTML/JS/CSS) served behind nginx. This is the ponytail ladder rung 2 - it is already in the codebase, already emitted by the generator, already has Helm/Zarf templates, already has Playwright e2e, already passes the full-surface sweep. For admin/dashboard apps where SSR is not needed, Next.js static export covers the use case with zero generator complexity delta and zero new supply chain surface. If type-safe route params are the specific pull toward TanStack Router, React Router v7 SPA mode (no framework mode, no Remix) is a lighter alternative with a smaller attack surface and no supply chain history to monitor.
verified.notes: Recommendation downgraded from trial to reference for three compounding reasons. (1) Supply chain event recency: CVE-2026-45321 was 6 weeks ago. SLSA attestation failure means the standard CuraOS SHA-digest pin of the npm tarball would not have caught the compromise because the CI was the attacker. The hardening is directionally correct but unproven under the next worm iteration. CuraOS air-gap rule requires trust in the artifact provenance chain; that chain failed for this exact package family in May 2026. (2) Generator-first conflict: ui-app-emit already emits a working recipe for all 24 apps. Adding a second paradigm is a generator fork, not a taxonomy addition. The generator-evolution rule requires the shared fix to be in the generator; here the shared fix is 'do not add the variant yet'. (3) Ponytail ladder: Next.js static export covers the SPA use case with zero new dependencies. The value delta of TanStack Router SPA mode over Next.js static export for internal admin apps is marginal type-safety on route params, not worth the supply chain monitoring burden, generator complexity, and mixed-fleet cognitive overhead. Revisit after: (a) 6+ months of clean TanStack release history post-hardening with no further TeamPCP incidents, (b) a concrete app in the fleet that genuinely cannot be served by Next.js static export or React Router v7 SPA mode, (c) generator team has capacity to maintain two ui-app-emit paradigms under the generator-evolution rule.
